topics.title: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET746 - LEAN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Lean Manufacturing

**Lean Manufacturing** (also called *Lean Production* or simply *Lean*) is a systematic, socio-technical methodology developed primarily for the elimination of **muda** (waste) within a manufacturing or service system, while simultaneously ensuring smooth *flow* of work, *pull*-based production triggered by actual customer demand, and a continuous pursuit of *perfection* through incremental improvements (**kaizen**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Essence):**
> Lean Manufacturing is a systematic approach to identifying and eliminating waste (non-value-adding activities) through continuous improvement, by creating value for the customer with fewer resources, less time, less space, and less human effort.

### Origin & Historical Context

- The roots of Lean Manufacturing lie in the **Toyota Production System (TPS)**, developed by **Taiichi Ohno**, **Eiji Toyoda**, and **Sakichi Toyoda** at Toyota Motor Corporation in post-World War II Japan (1940s–1970s).
- The term *"Lean"* was formally coined in the book **"The Machine That Changed the World"** (1990) by **James P. Womack, Daniel T. Jones, and Daniel Roos**, based on their five-year MIT study of the global automotive industry (IMVP – International Motor Vehicle Program).
- Originally called *Just-in-Time (JIT)* production, it evolved into a global management philosophy applied across aerospace, healthcare, software, and services.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Mapping for KTU Exams:**
> - Japanese: **Muda** = Waste (Non-value-adding activity)
> - Japanese: **Kaizen** = Continuous Improvement
> - Japanese: **Gemba** = The Actual Place (where work happens)
> - Japanese: **Kanban** = Visual Signal Card (pull trigger)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Plain-English Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen" 🍳**

Imagine a busy restaurant kitchen. The head chef (the *customer*) calls out an order: "One pasta, one salad!"

- **Traditional Mass-Production Kitchen:** The kitchen pre-cooks 50 plates of pasta at 11 AM, hoping customers will buy them. By 1 PM, 30 plates are thrown in the trash. The fridge is overflowing, the cooks are exhausted, and the chef's actual orders are delayed. This is **PUSH** production — pushing product regardless of demand.

- **Lean Kitchen:** Nothing is pre-cooked. The moment a customer orders pasta, a ticket (a **kanban** signal) is generated, ingredients are pulled from the fridge (just-in-time), the dish is cooked, plated, and served fresh. The fridge holds minimal stock, every step is visible, and any overcooking or burnt items are immediately spotted. This is **PULL** production.

**The core insight:** Don't make anything until the customer asks for it. Make it perfectly. Don't keep extra stock. Fix problems the moment they appear.

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this one-liner for KTU 2-markers:**
> *"Lean is about doing more with less — less time, less inventory, less space, less human effort — while giving the customer exactly what they want, exactly when they want it."*

---

## 1.3 The Five Core Principles of Lean (Womack & Jones, 1996)

The entire Lean framework is built on **5 principles** that any student MUST know for KTU:

| # | Principle | Meaning in Plain English |
|---|-----------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Value** | Define value from the *customer's* perspective, not the producer's. |
| 2 | **Value Stream** | Map every step (value-adding + non-value-adding) from raw material to customer. |
| 3 | **Flow** | Make the remaining value-adding steps *flow* continuously without interruptions. |
| 4 | **Pull** | Let the customer *pull* the product — produce only when there is demand. |
| 5 | **Perfection** | Pursue **kaizen** — continuously remove waste to reach an ideal state. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** *"Name the 5 principles of Lean in order."* Students often forget the exact order. **Tip:** remember the acronym **V-V-F-P-P** → **V**alue → **V**alue Stream → **F**low → **P**ull → **P**erfection.

---

## 1.4 The Three Generations of Lean Thinking

> [!NOTE]
> **Did You Know?** Lean is often described as having evolved across **3 generations**:

1. **1st Generation (1950s–1970s):** Toyota Production System — shop-floor tools (JIT, Kanban, 5S, Poka-Yoke).
2. **2nd Generation (1990s):** Womack & Jones popularized *Lean Thinking* as a business-wide philosophy.
3. **3rd Generation (2000s–Today):** *Lean Enterprise* — extends Lean across the entire value stream, including suppliers, customers, and even non-manufacturing sectors (Lean IT, Lean Healthcare, Lean Startups).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 What is "Waste" (Muda)? — The Heart of Lean

The single most important concept in Lean is the identification and elimination of **waste**. Taiichi Ohno originally identified **7 wastes (muda)**, later expanded to **8** (the 8th being *unused talent/creativity*).

### The Classic 7 + 1 Wastes

| # | Waste | Japanese Term | Engineering Example | Plain-English Description |
|---|-------|---------------|---------------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | **Defects** | *Defects* | Rework, scrap, warranty claims | Producing something wrong that needs fixing or throwing away. |
| 2 | **Overproduction** | *Seisan* | Making 1000 units when only 100 are needed | Producing more, sooner, or faster than the customer requires. (The **worst** waste — it *creates* other wastes.) |
| 3 | **Waiting** | *Taiki* | Idle machines, operators waiting for parts | People or machines standing idle because of unbalanced flow. |
| 4 | **Non-utilized talent** | *Jikan* | Skilled engineers doing clerical photocopying | Wasting human skills, creativity, and brainpower on low-value tasks. |
| 5 | **Transportation** | *Unsou* | Moving parts across long distances in a factory | Moving materials/info more than is minimally required (no value added). |
| 6 | **Inventory** | *Zaiko* | Piles of WIP (Work-In-Progress), finished goods | Excess raw material, WIP, or finished goods sitting in storage. |
| 7 | **Motion** | *Ugoki* | Operators walking 10 m to get a tool | Unnecessary movement of *people* (bending, reaching, walking). |
| 8 | **Extra-processing** | *Muri* (often) | Tightening a bolt 8 times when 4 are enough | Doing more work or using higher precision than the customer actually needs. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse **Transportation** with **Motion**.
> - **Transportation** = movement of *materials/products* (Material flow waste).
> - **Motion** = movement of *people* (Operator movement waste).
> Both are non-value-adding, but they are different categories.

### Mnemonic Device for Exam Recall

Use the acronym **D-O-W-N-T-I-M-E** for the 7 traditional wastes:
**D**efects, **O**verproduction, **W**aiting, **N**on-utilized talent, **T**ransportation, **I**nventory, **M**otion, **E**xtra-processing.

---

## 2.2 The Three Ms of Lean (Muda, Mura, Muri)

Toyota emphasized that Lean is not just about waste. It targets three evils:

| Term | Meaning | Engineering Example |
|------|---------|---------------------|
| **Muda** (むだ) | Waste — non-value-adding activity | Overproduction, defects, waiting |
| **Mura** (むら) | Unevenness — irregularity in flow | Sudden demand spikes, batch processing peaks |
| **Muri** (無理) | Overburden — unreasonable strain | Forcing a machine to run 24 hrs/day, or a worker lifting 50 kg |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** *"Differentiate between Muda, Mura, and Muri."*
> - **Muda** is what you *throw away* (waste output).
> - **Mura** is what you *smooth out* (level the flow).
> - **Muri** is what you *lighten* (reduce strain on people/machines).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Metric Table

Lean Manufacturing uses several quantitative metrics. For KTU numerical/analytical questions, memorize these:

| Metric | Formula | Unit | What It Measures |
|--------|---------|------|------------------|
| **Value-Added Ratio (VAR)** | $VAR = \dfrac{\text{Value-Added Time}}{\text{Total Lead Time}} \times 100$ | % | How much of total time is actually value-adding. Lean target: ↑ this. |
| **Takt Time** | $T_{takt} = \dfrac{\text{Available Production Time}}{\text{Customer Demand}}$ | seconds/unit | The pace at which a product must be produced to satisfy demand. |
| **Cycle Time** | $T_c$ = measured at each workstation | seconds/unit | The actual time a single workstation takes to complete one unit. |
| **Lead Time** | $T_{LT}$ = sum of process times + wait times + transport | seconds/unit | Total time from customer order to delivery. |
| **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | % | Availability × Performance × Quality of equipment. World-class: **≥ 85%**. |
| **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $FPY = \dfrac{\text{Good Units (no rework)}}{\text{Total Units Started}} \times 100$ | % | Quality at first attempt. |
| **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | % | Product of each step's yield (n = number of process steps). |
| **Inventory Turns** | $IT = \dfrac{\text{Cost of Goods Sold}}{\text{Average Inventory}}$ | times/year | How many times inventory is sold/replaced per year. Lean target: ↑ this. |
| **Throughput** | $TH = \dfrac{\text{Units Produced}}{\text{Time}}$ | units/hour | Rate of production output. |
| **Work-In-Progress (WIP)** | Little's Law: $WIP = TH \times T_{LT}$ | units | Fundamental relationship in flow systems. |

> [!TIP]
> **Little's Law is a KTU hot-topic.** If the examiner asks a numerical, this is often the equation being tested. $WIP = TH \times T_{LT}$ (i.e., $L = \lambda W$ in queueing theory).

---

## 2.4 Lean vs. Traditional Mass Production — Conceptual Comparison

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Lean is fundamentally different from mass production** — a high-yield comparison for KTU essays (10–14 markers):

| Dimension | Mass Production (Ford Model) | Lean Production (Toyota Model) |
|-----------|------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| **Driver** | Forecast / push | Actual customer demand / pull |
| **Batch Size** | Large (economies of scale) | Small (single-piece flow ideal) |
| **Inventory** | High (buffers every step) | Minimal (just-in-time) |
| **Lead Time** | Long | Short |
| **Variety** | Low (standardized) | High (customizable) |
| **Workforce** | Specialized, task-bound | Multi-skilled, team-based |
| **Improvement** | Periodic, big changes | Continuous (kaizen) |
| **Quality** | Inspect at end | Built-in (jidoka, poka-yoke) |
| **Supplier Relations** | Adversarial, multi-source | Partnership, single-source |
| **Focus** | Cost per unit | Value to customer |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** Toyota, Tesla use Lean to reduce assembly time. Tesla's *alien dreadnought* factory is a Lean masterpiece.
- **Aerospace:** Boeing 737 production uses Lean cell layouts.
- **Healthcare:** Mayo Clinic applied Lean to reduce patient waiting by 40%.
- **Software:** Spotify's "Squad Model" is Lean-inspired.
- **KTU Project Work:** Final-year B.Tech projects in Mechanical/Production/Industrial Engineering often benchmark Lean metrics for SME factories in Kerala (e.g., coir, rubber, cashew industries).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical: Takt Time & Line Balancing

> [!NOTE]
> This is the most frequently asked numerical in KTU Lean Manufacturing modules. Mastering this guarantees full marks on the calculation sub-part.

### Problem Statement
> A manufacturing unit operates **2 shifts of 8 hours each, 5 days a week**.
> The daily customer demand is **480 units**.
> The factory has **3 sequential workstations**: A → B → C.
> Task times (in seconds) at each workstation are: $t_A = 30$ s, $t_B = 45$ s, $t_C = 25$ s.
> Determine: **(i) Takt Time, (ii) Bottleneck station, (iii) Lead Time, (iv) WIP using Little's Law, (v) Cycle Time efficiency.**

---

### Step 1: Calculate Available Production Time per Day

Available time = Number of shifts × Shift duration × 3600 (to convert to seconds)

$$
T_{available} = 2 \times 8 \times 3600 = 57600 \text{ seconds/day}
$$

> **Logic:** We use seconds because task times are in seconds, ensuring unit consistency.

---

### Step 2: Calculate Takt Time

$$
T_{takt} = \dfrac{\text{Available Production Time}}{\text{Customer Demand}}
$$

$$
T_{takt} = \dfrac{57600 \text{ s}}{480 \text{ units}} = 120 \text{ seconds/unit}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A new unit must be completed every **120 seconds** to keep up with demand.

---

### Step 3: Identify the Bottleneck Station

The bottleneck is the workstation with the **longest task time** (since it determines the maximum cycle time the line can sustain).

$$
t_A = 30 \text{ s}, \quad t_B = 45 \text{ s}, \quad t_C = 25 \text{ s}
$$

$$
t_{max} = t_B = 45 \text{ seconds}
$$

> **Conclusion:** **Station B is the bottleneck.** It governs the maximum output rate of the entire line.

---

### Step 4: Calculate Actual Throughput (TH) of the Line

Because the bottleneck limits production, the actual throughput is dictated by Station B:

$$
TH = \dfrac{\text{Available Time}}{t_{bottleneck}} = \dfrac{57600}{45} = 1280 \text{ units/day}
$$

> **Logic:** Even though takt time suggests 480 units/day, the line can *physically* produce 1280 units/day at Station B's pace. The *true* rate is the lesser of (demand) and (bottleneck capacity).

---

### Step 5: Calculate Lead Time (T_LT)

Lead Time = Sum of all process times + Wait times + Transport time
Assuming negligible transport and that the line runs sequentially (1 unit at a time):

$$
T_{LT} = t_A + t_B + t_C = 30 + 45 + 25 = 100 \text{ seconds/unit}
$$

> **Note:** Lead time and Takt time are different. Lead time is the time to make one unit; takt time is the *required rate*.

---

### Step 6: Calculate WIP using Little's Law

$$
WIP = TH \times T_{LT}
$$

$$
WIP = 1280 \text{ units/day} \times 100 \text{ s/unit}
$$

We must convert 1280 units/day to units/second:

$$
1280 \text{ units/day} = \dfrac{1280}{57600} \text{ units/second} = 0.02222 \text{ units/second}
$$

$$
WIP = 0.02222 \text{ units/s} \times 100 \text{ s} = 2.22 \text{ units}
$$

> **Engineering insight:** At any given moment, about **2–3 units** are inside the system. In a non-Lean (batch) system, WIP could be 50+ units. Less WIP = less tied-up capital = better flow.

---

### Step 7: Calculate Line Cycle Time Efficiency

$$
\eta_{cycle} = \dfrac{t_A + t_B + t_C}{n \times t_{bottleneck}} \times 100
$$

where $n$ = number of stations = 3.

$$
\eta_{cycle} = \dfrac{30 + 45 + 25}{3 \times 45} \times 100 = \dfrac{100}{135} \times 100 = 74.07 \%
$$

> **KTU Insight:** If the line were *perfectly balanced* (all $t_i = 33.33$ s), $\eta_{cycle} = 100\%$. The closer to 100%, the better the line. **74% is decent but improvable via Lean line-balancing techniques.**

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical: OEE Calculation

### Problem Statement
> A CNC machine in a Lean cell has the following data for one shift (8 hours = 480 minutes):
> - Planned production time: 480 min
> - Downtime (breakdowns, setup): 60 min
> - Ideal cycle time: 1.5 min/unit
> - Actual units produced: 250 units
> - Defective units: 25 units
>
> **Calculate OEE and identify the main loss component.**

---

### Step 1: Availability (A)

$$
A = \dfrac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}} = \dfrac{480 - 60}{480} = \dfrac{420}{480} = 0.875 = 87.5\%
$$

---

### Step 2: Performance (P)

$$
P = \dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Units Produced}}{\text{Operating Time}}
$$

$$
P = \dfrac{1.5 \times 250}{420} = \dfrac{375}{420} = 0.8929 = 89.29\%
$$

---

### Step 3: Quality (Q)

$$
Q = \dfrac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units Produced}} = \dfrac{250 - 25}{250} = \dfrac{225}{250} = 0.90 = 90.00\%
$$

---

### Step 4: OEE

$$
OEE = A \times P \times Q = 0.875 \times 0.8929 \times 0.90 = 0.7031 = 70.31\%
$$

> **KTU Insight:** World-class OEE is **≥ 85%** (TPM benchmark). 70.31% indicates room for improvement. The lowest component (**Quality at 90%** here, assuming this is the smallest) should be the first target — apply **Poka-Yoke** (mistake-proofing).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Lean Tools vs. Targeted Waste

This matrix helps KTU students remember **which Lean tool eliminates which waste**:

| Lean Tool | Primary Waste Targeted | Brief Description |
|-----------|------------------------|--------------------|
| **5S** (Sort, Set, Shine, Standardize, Sustain) | Motion, Waiting | Workplace organization |
| **Kanban** | Overproduction, Inventory | Visual pull signal |
| **Poka-Yoke** | Defects | Mistake-proofing devices |
| **Jidoka (Autonomation)** | Defects, Overproduction | Stop-the-line authority |
| **SMED** (Single-Minute Exchange of Dies) | Waiting | Reduce setup time |
| **Heijunka** | Mura (unevenness) | Production leveling |
| **Value Stream Mapping (VSM)** | All wastes | Visual analysis of material/info flow |
| **Kaizen** | Continuous | Incremental improvement |
| **One-piece flow / Cellular Mfg.** | Inventory, Transport, Motion | Layout based on product family |
| **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** | Machine breakdowns | Operator-driven maintenance |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Five Principles of Lean — Logical Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["1. VALUE — Define what the customer truly wants"] --> B["2. VALUE STREAM — Map every step from raw material to customer"]
    B --> C["3. FLOW — Make value-adding steps flow without interruption"]
    C --> D["4. PULL — Produce only when customer pulls the signal"]
    D --> E["5. PERFECTION — Continuous kaizen to eliminate remaining waste"]
    E --> A

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The arrow from E back to A shows that Perfection feeds back into redefining Value — Lean is a *closed-loop* philosophy, not a one-time project.

---

## 4.2 The 7 Wastes (TIMWOOD) — Visual Classification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WASTE["Muda — Any activity that consumes resources but creates no value"]

    WASTE --> CAT1["Category 1: PURE WASTE"]
    WASTE --> CAT2["Category 2: NECESSARY WASTE"]
    WASTE --> CAT3["Category 3: WASTE that creates other waste"]

    CAT1 --> W1["D — DEFECTS (Scrap, Rework)"]
    CAT1 --> W2["O — OVERPRODUCTION (Producing too much)"]
    CAT1 --> W3["W — WAITING (Idle time)"]
    CAT1 --> W4["N — NON-UTILIZED TALENT"]
    CAT1 --> W5["T — TRANSPORTATION (Moving materials)"]
    CAT1 --> W6["I — INVENTORY (Excess stock)"]
    CAT1 --> W7["M — MOTION (Unnecessary movement of people)"]
    CAT1 --> W8["E — EXTRA-PROCESSING (Over-engineering)"]

    CAT2 --> N1["e.g., Inspection, Transport in some cases"]
    CAT3 --> N2["Overproduction creates inventory, defects, transport"]

    style WASTE fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    style CAT1 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333
    style CAT2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333
    style CAT3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
```

---

## 4.3 Lean Implementation Roadmap — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    STAGE1["STAGE 1: Foundation — Top Management Commitment, 5S, Visual Workplace"]
    STAGE2["STAGE 2: Stability — Standard Work, Jidoka, Quality at Source"]
    STAGE3["STAGE 3: Flow — Cellular Layout, One-piece flow, SMED"]
    STAGE4["STAGE 4: Pull — Kanban, Heijunka, Supplier Integration"]
    STAGE5["STAGE 5: Perfection — Kaizen Culture, TPM, Continuous VSM"]

    STAGE1 --> STAGE2
    STAGE2 --> STAGE3
    STAGE3 --> STAGE4
    STAGE4 --> STAGE5
    STAGE5 -.-> STAGE1

    style STAGE1 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333
    style STAGE2 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333
    style STAGE3 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333
    style STAGE4 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333
    style STAGE5 fill:#FFC0CB,stroke:#333
```

> **Note:** This is the standard Toyota *House* model, drawn as a sequential roadmap for KTU exam clarity.

---

## 4.4 Push vs. Pull Production — Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PUSH["PUSH SYSTEM (Traditional Mass Production)"]
        direction LR
        P1["Forecast"] --> P2["Schedule Production"] --> P3["Build to Stock"] --> P4["Push to Next Stage"] --> P5["Warehouse"] --> P6["Customer (Hopefully)"]
    end

    subgraph PULL["PULL SYSTEM (Lean / JIT)"]
        direction LR
        Q1["Customer Order"] --> Q2["Kanban Signal to Upstream"] --> Q3["Produce Only What's Needed"] --> Q4["Deliver Directly"] --> Q5["Customer (Satisfied)"]
    end

    style PUSH fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#900
    style PULL fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#060
```

> **Engineering takeaway:** Notice that in the Pull system, **no inventory is built** between stages. Material only moves when the downstream station *consumes* a unit and sends a signal back.

---

## 4.5 The Lean House (Toyota Production System House)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOF["GOAL: Highest Quality, Lowest Cost, Shortest Lead Time, Best Safety, High Morale"]

    PILLAR1["Just-in-Time — Right item, Right quantity, Right time"]
    PILLAR2["Jidoka — Built-in Quality, Stop the Line"]

    FOUNDATION["Foundation: Standardized Work, Heijunka, Kaizen"]
    BASE["Base: 5S, Visual Management, TPM"]

    ROOF --> PILLAR1
    ROOF --> PILLAR2
    PILLAR1 --- FOUNDATION
    PILLAR2 --- FOUNDATION
    FOUNDATION --> BASE

    style ROOF fill:#FF4500,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:3px
    style PILLAR1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style PILLAR2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style FOUNDATION fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style BASE fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000
```

> **KTU Exam Tip:** Drawing this house in your answer sheet immediately boosts examiner perception of conceptual clarity. Always label the **roof (goal)**, **two pillars (JIT + Jidoka)**, and **foundation (kaizen)**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**"Define Lean Manufacturing. Mention any four of its objectives."**

**Model Answer:**
Lean Manufacturing is a systematic methodology aimed at the elimination of waste (*muda*), unevenness (*mura*), and overburden (*muri*) across an organization's value stream, to deliver maximum value to the customer using minimum resources.

The four key objectives are:
1. **Reduce lead time** of production and delivery.
2. **Improve quality** by building it in (Jidoka) rather than inspecting it in.
3. **Lower inventory levels** through Just-in-Time (JIT) pull systems.
4. **Maximize customer value** by focusing on value-adding activities only.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**"List the seven wastes (muda) of Lean Manufacturing with one example each."**

**Model Answer:**
The seven wastes, remembered by the acronym **TIMWOOD**, are:

1. **T**ransportation — moving materials across long distances.
2. **I**nventory — excess raw material, WIP, or finished goods.
3. **M**otion — operator walking to fetch tools.
4. **W**aiting — machines idle due to part shortages.
5. **O**verproduction — producing more than the customer has ordered.
6. **O**ver-processing — tightening a bolt more times than required.
7. **D**efects — products that fail inspection and require rework.

*(Note: Many texts add an 8th: Non-utilized talent.)*

---

## PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **origin and evolution of the Toyota Production System (TPS)** as the foundation of Lean Manufacturing. Highlight the contributions of Taiichi Ohno, Sakichi Toyoda, and the impact of the MIT IMVP study. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **Five Principles of Lean** as given by Womack and Jones. How do these principles differ from the traditional mass-production paradigm? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**1. Pre-War Japanese Industrial Context (1930s–1945):**
- Sakichi Toyoda founded Toyoda Automatic Loom Works in 1926. His invention of the **automatic loom** (with a mechanism to detect broken threads and stop the machine) sowed the seed of **Jidoka (autonomation)**.
- Kiichiro Toyoda (son) entered the automotive business in 1933 and visited Ford's Rouge Plant in the U.S. in 1929. He was inspired by mass production but realized Japan's *low-volume, high-variety* market could not adopt Ford's method directly.

**2. Birth of TPS (1945–1970):**
- Post-WWII, Japan faced capital scarcity, small domestic market, and a need for **multi-skilled workers**.
- **Eiji Toyoda** and **Taiichi Ohno** developed TPS over 20 years at Toyota.
- Core innovations:
  - **Just-in-Time (JIT):** Produce only what is needed, when needed, in the amount needed.
  - **Jidoka:** "Automation with a human touch" — machines detect defects and auto-stop.
  - **Kanban:** Visual cards to control production and material flow.
  - **Kaizen:** Continuous improvement involving every employee.

**3. The Ohno Revolution:**
- Ohno spent years on the shop floor at Toyota City, observing, experimenting, and eliminating waste. He famously said: *"The only opponent is muda."*
- The **Toyota Publica** (1961) and **Corolla** (1966) embodied TPS principles.

**4. Global Recognition via MIT IMVP (1985–1990):**
- The **International Motor Vehicle Program (IMVP)** at MIT, led by Professors James Womack, Daniel Roos, and Daniel Jones, compared production systems worldwide.
- The 1990 book **"The Machine That Changed the World"** coined the term **"Lean Production"** and declared TPS superior to both mass production (Ford) and craft production.
- This led to global adoption in the 1990s and beyond.

*(Conclusion: TPS is not a toolset but a management philosophy born out of necessity, refined by decades of practice.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**The Five Principles of Lean (Womack & Jones, 1996):**

**1. Value:**
- Value is defined solely from the **customer's perspective**.
- A product has value if it solves a customer's problem and is worth paying for.
- The producer must understand the customer's needs (quality, price, delivery, features).

**2. Value Stream:**
- The **value stream** is the entire set of activities — value-adding and non-value-adding — required to bring a product from raw material to the customer's hands.
- **Value Stream Mapping (VSM)** is the tool used to visualize and analyze this stream.
- Three categories: (i) value-adding, (ii) necessary non-value-adding (NVA but required), (iii) pure waste.

**3. Flow:**
- Once waste is identified, the value-adding steps must be made to **flow continuously** without stops, queues, or backflows.
- Achieved via **cellular layout, line balancing, SMED, and one-piece flow**.
- Flow contrasts with the batch-and-queue approach of mass production.

**4. Pull:**
- Production is **triggered by actual customer demand**, not by forecast.
- **Kanban** signals enable the upstream process to *pull* materials only when needed.
- Pull eliminates overproduction, the worst waste.

**5. Perfection (Kaizen):**
- Perfection is the **continuous pursuit** of the previous four principles.
- It is not a destination but a journey — the assumption is that waste can always be further reduced.

**Comparison with Mass Production (refer to the comparison table in Section 2.4 of these notes).**

*Key differences summarized:*
- Mass production is **forecast-driven (push)**; Lean is **demand-driven (pull)**.
- Mass production relies on **large batches and inventory buffers**; Lean relies on **small batches and minimal WIP**.
- Mass production assigns **specialized labor**; Lean uses **multi-skilled teams**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - **Don't mix up** Lean tools (5S, Kanban, SMED) with Lean *principles* (the 5 Womack & Jones principles). The examiner deducts marks if you list "5S and Kanban" as principles.
> - Always present a **side-by-side comparison** in the mass-production vs. Lean question — examiners reward visual clarity in answer sheets.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Define **Muda, Mura, and Muri**. Explain the seven traditional wastes with their Japanese terminology and provide one industrial example for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing plant works **1 shift of 8 hours, 6 days a week**. The daily demand is **360 units**. The plant has 3 workstations with task times of **40 s, 55 s, and 35 s** respectively. Calculate: **(i) Takt Time, (ii) Bottleneck, (iii) Actual Throughput per day, (iv) WIP using Little's Law, (v) Cycle Time efficiency. Comment on the result.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**The Three Ms of Lean:**

| Term | Meaning | Impact |
|------|---------|--------|
| **Muda** (むだ) | Waste — non-value-adding activity | Consumes resources without adding value |
| **Mura** (むら) | Unevenness — irregular flow or workload | Causes peaks, valleys, and inefficiency |
| **Muri** (無理) | Overburden — unreasonable strain | Damages people, machines, and quality |

> *Muda is what you remove. Mura is what you level. Muri is what you relieve.*

**The 7 Wastes (DOWNTIME Mnemonic):**

1. **D — Defects** (不良, *Furyō*): A faulty weld in a car chassis needing rework.
2. **O — Overproduction** (作りすぎ, *Tsukuri-sugi*): Printing 5000 brochures when only 2000 are needed.
3. **W — Waiting** (待ち, *Machi*): Operator idle while a CNC machine is being repaired.
4. **N — Non-utilized talent** (未活用, *Mika-tsuyō*): PhD engineer spending time filing photocopies.
5. **T — Transportation** (運搬, *Unpan*): Forklift moving parts 200 m across a sprawling factory.
6. **I — Inventory** (在庫, *Zaiko*): Piles of unsold smartphones in a warehouse.
7. **M — Motion** (動き, *Ugoki*): Worker bending repeatedly to pick bolts from a floor bin.
8. **E — Extra-processing** (加工, *Kakō*): Hand-polishing a surface to 0.1 µm when the spec requires only 1.0 µm.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- 1 shift/day × 8 hr × 6 days = wait — problem says "1 shift of 8 hours, 6 days a week" but doesn't specify per-day; we interpret as **per-day operation of 1 shift × 8 hours**.
- Daily demand: 360 units.
- $t_A = 40$ s, $t_B = 55$ s, $t_C = 35$ s.

**(i) Available Production Time per Day:**

$$
T_{available} = 1 \times 8 \times 3600 = 28800 \text{ s/day}
$$

**(ii) Takt Time:**

$$
T_{takt} = \dfrac{28800}{360} = 80 \text{ seconds/unit}
$$

**(iii) Bottleneck:**

$$
t_{max} = 55 \text{ s at Station B}
$$

**Station B is the bottleneck.**

**(iv) Actual Throughput (bottleneck-governed):**

$$
TH = \dfrac{28800}{55} = 523.64 \text{ units/day} \approx 523 \text{ units/day}
$$

**(v) Lead Time (sequential, single piece):**

$$
T_{LT} = 40 + 55 + 35 = 130 \text{ s/unit}
$$

**(vi) WIP using Little's Law:**

Convert throughput to units/second:

$$
TH = \dfrac{523.64}{28800} = 0.01818 \text{ units/s}
$$

$$
WIP = 0.01818 \times 130 = 2.36 \text{ units}
$$

**(vii) Cycle Time Efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{40 + 55 + 35}{3 \times 55} \times 100 = \dfrac{130}{165} \times 100 = 78.79\%
$$

**Comment:**
- The line is **under-utilized** (actual output 523 > demand 360), meaning it has spare capacity.
- However, **efficiency is only 78.79%** — there is a 21% imbalance.
- **Lean recommendation:** Apply *line balancing* — redistribute tasks from Station B (bottleneck) to A and C. For example, shift 10 s of work from B to A and C. This raises efficiency and shortens lead time.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Errors in KTU Numerical Solutions:**
> - **Unit mismatch:** Mixing seconds and minutes without conversion (loses 1–2 marks).
> - **Wrong Little's Law application:** $WIP$ is *not* equal to demand × lead time. It is $WIP = TH \times T_{LT}$, where $TH$ is the *actual* throughput, not demand.
> - **Forgetting the bottleneck:** Always check whether the bottleneck cycle time is **less than** the takt time. If yes, the line cannot meet demand — this is a *key* Lean diagnostic.

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- ✅ **Lean = elimination of waste (Muda) to add maximum value to the customer.**
- ✅ **5 Principles (V-V-F-P-P):** Value → Value Stream → Flow → Pull → Perfection.
- ✅ **7 Wastes acronym:** D-O-W-N-T-I-M-E (Defects, Overproduction, Waiting, Non-utilized talent, Transportation, Inventory, Motion, Extra-processing).
- ✅ **8th Waste** = Non-utilized talent / creativity of employees.
- ✅ **Three Ms:** Muda (waste), Mura (unevenness), Muri (overburden).
- ✅ **Takt Time** = Available time / Customer demand.
- ✅ **Little's Law** = $WIP = TH \times T_{LT}$.
- ✅ **OEE = A × P × Q.** World-class OEE ≥ 85%.
- ✅ **Bottleneck** = station with the longest task time.
- ✅ **TPS House:** Roof = Goal; Pillars = JIT & Jidoka; Foundation = Kaizen & Standardized Work.
- ✅ **Push vs. Pull:** Push = forecast-driven (mass); Pull = demand-driven (Lean).
- ✅ **Origin:** TPS (Toyota) → Lean term coined by Womack & Jones (MIT IMVP, 1990).
- ✅ **Kanban** = visual signal card; **Jidoka** = autonomy + automation; **Heijunka** = level scheduling.
- ✅ **Lean vs. Mass:** Lean = small batch, low inventory, multi-skilled, continuous improvement.
- ✅ Always include a **diagram** (Lean House, Push-Pull, or Waste Tree) in 14-mark answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: History of Lean
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET746 - LEAN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** History of Lean

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Lean Manufacturing** is a systematic, philosophy-driven approach to production that aims to create maximum value for the end customer while minimizing waste (*Muda*), unevenness (*Mura*), and overburden (*Muri*). The historical evolution of Lean traces a continuous line of thought from early craft production to the Toyota Production System (TPS) and finally to its global codification as "Lean" by researchers at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) in the late 1980s.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Lean is not merely a set of tools (5S, Kanban, Poka-Yoke); it is a **management philosophy** whose roots lie in the operational experiments of the Toyota Motor Corporation between 1945 and 1975, preceded by the mass production innovations of Henry Ford and the statistical quality methods of W. Edwards Deming and Joseph Juran.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a street food vendor in Kerala making *appams*. 

- **Traditional Mass Production Approach (Fordism):** You make 1,000 appams in advance, stack them, and hope customers buy them. Many go cold and are wasted. You carry a heavy inventory of batter, oil, and water even if only 10 customers come.
- **Lean Approach (Toyotism):** You make appams **one at a time, only when a customer orders one** (*Just-in-Time*). You arrange your ladle, bowl, and stove in the shortest possible path (*Layout & Flow*). You stop making if a problem occurs (*Jidoka - Autonomation*) and call for help immediately rather than producing a defective product. You keep only one bag of rice flour at a time (*Small Lot Size / Pull System*).

This simple vendor's transformation—from "make and store" to "make on demand with zero defect"—is the **philosophical journey** of Lean across roughly **150 years** of industrial history.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Historical Insight:** Lean is the *English-language, Western-academic packaging* of a Japanese management revolution. The Japanese called it the **Toyota Production System (TPS)**, and the world only adopted the word "Lean" after a 1988 MIT working paper by John Krafcik.

### 1.3 Core Historical Constants and Metrics

| Historical Constant / Metric | Value / Standard |
|---|---|
| Year Henry Ford introduced the Moving Assembly Line | **1913** |
| Year Toyota Motor Corporation was founded | **1937** |
| Year Taiichi Ohno began developing the TPS | **1945** |
| Year Eiji Toyoda's 3-month study of Ford's Rouge Plant | **1950** |
| Year JIT was first documented by Toyota | **1953** (Ohno's *Kanban* system) |
| Year the term "Lean Production" was coined | **1988** (John Krafcik, MIT) |
| Year *The Machine That Changed the World* was published | **1990** |
| Year *Lean Thinking* was published | **1996** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Major Eras of Lean Evolution

The history of Lean is conventionally divided into **four chronological eras**, each contributing a foundational pillar to the modern philosophy.

#### **Era I: The Pre-Industrial & Interchangeable Parts Foundation (1799–1899)**

- **Context:** Craft production was the norm—every product was custom-built by skilled artisans. Production was high quality but slow and expensive.
- **Pivotal Contribution:** In **1799**, American inventor **Eli Whitney** demonstrated the concept of **interchangeable parts** to the U.S. government by building 10 muskets using uniformly manufactured components. This was the first time a *standardized* part from one musket could replace the corresponding part in another.
- **Lean Insight:** Standardization is the silent precondition for any flow-based system. Without interchangeable parts, you cannot run a continuous assembly line.

#### **Era II: Mass Production — The Ford Revolution (1900–1949)**

- **Key Figure:** **Henry Ford** (1863–1947).
- **Innovation:** Ford combined Whitney's interchangeable parts with four radical ideas:
  1. **Flow Production** — components move continuously through the plant rather than being batch-processed.
  2. **Standardization** — the Model T (introduced 1908) came in only *one color (black)* because Ford famously said: *"Any customer can have a car painted any color that he wants so long as it is black."*
  3. **The Moving Assembly Line (1913)** — at Ford's Highland Park Plant, chassis was pulled by a rope, reducing assembly time from **12.5 hours to 93 minutes**.
  4. **High Wages ($5/day, 1914)** — to reduce the brutal attrition of factory workers and to make Ford cars affordable to his own employees.
- **Limitation:** Ford's system was **rigid**—it could not efficiently produce variety. It was optimized for *one* product, *one* color, *one* design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Many students confuse Fordism with Lean. The correct relationship is: **Ford created the mechanical infrastructure (assembly line, interchangeability), and Toyota later added the *intellectual* infrastructure (JIT, Jidoka, pull, Kaizen) on top of it.** Lean = Ford's hardware + Toyota's software.

#### **Era III: The Toyota Production System (1950–1990)**

- **The Toyota Family:**
  - **Sakichi Toyoda** (1867–1930): Founded Toyoda Automatic Loom Works; invented the *Toyoda Automatic Loom Type G* with an automatic stop device—the first ancestor of *Jidoka* (autonomation).
  - **Kiichiro Toyoda** (1894–1952): Sakichi's son; founded **Toyota Motor Corporation in 1937**.
  - **Eiji Toyoda** (1913–2013): Kiichiro's cousin; visited Ford's Rouge Plant in 1950 and famously remarked: *"There is nothing here that we cannot do at Toyota, and do better."*
  - **Taiichi Ohno** (1912–1990): The "father of the Toyota Production System." Began systematic experiments in 1945; developed *Just-in-Time* and the *Kanban* card system.
  - **Shigeo Shingo** (1909–1990): Co-developed the **Poka-Yoke** (mistake-proofing) concept and the **Single-Minute Exchange of Die (SMED)** system.

- **External Influences on Toyota:**
  - **W. Edwards Deming** (American statistician, 1900–1993): Taught statistical quality control to Japanese engineers in 1950 under the U.S. occupation's *Union of Japanese Scientists and Engineers (JUSE)* program. His **14 Points for Management** and the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle** deeply influenced Toyota.
  - **Joseph Juran** (American quality guru, 1904–2008): Followed Deming to Japan in 1954; introduced the **Juran Trilogy** (Quality Planning, Quality Control, Quality Improvement).
  - **Toyota faced a uniquely hostile environment** in post-war Japan: a *tiny domestic market*, *scarce capital*, *limited natural resources*, and *strict export restrictions* by Japan's Ministry of International Trade and Industry (MITI). These constraints *forced* Toyota to invent small-batch, low-inventory production—the opposite of Ford's high-volume philosophy.

- **The TPS House (Toyota's own diagram):**
  - **Roof:** Just-in-Time (produce only what is needed, when needed, in the amount needed).
  - **Foundation:** Heijunka (Level Scheduling / Production Smoothing).
  - **Right Pillar:** Jidoka (Autonomation — built-in quality, automatic stop on defect).
  - **Left Pillar:** Just-in-Time (Pull system driven by Kanban cards).
  - **Center:** Continuous Improvement (Kaizen) and Standardization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Ohno's Core Insight:** Ohno observed that American supermarkets only restocked shelves when items were *taken by a customer*. He adapted this **supermarket pull system** to the factory floor—each downstream workstation "pulls" parts from the upstream station using a **Kanban (signal) card**. This is the intellectual heart of Lean.

#### **Era IV: Global Codification & Lean Beyond Manufacturing (1990–Present)**

- **1988:** **John Krafcik**, an MIT Sloan MBA student working under Prof. James Womack, publishes the working paper *Triumph of the Lean Production System*. He coins the term **"Lean Production"** to describe TPS in language Western managers could understand.
- **1990:** Womack, Roos, and Jones publish ***The Machine That Changed the World*** based on the **International Motor Vehicle Program (IMVP)**—a 5-year, $5 million MIT study comparing mass production and TPS across the global auto industry.
- **1996:** Womack and Jones publish ***Lean Thinking***, expanding Lean beyond the factory floor into services, healthcare, software, and government.
- **2000s:** **Lean Six Sigma** emerges as a hybrid of Lean's flow philosophy and Six Sigma's statistical defect-reduction methods.
- **2010s–2020s:** **Industry 4.0**, **Digital Lean**, and **Lean Startup** (Eric Ries, 2011) extend Lean principles into cyber-physical systems and entrepreneurship.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Since the history of Lean is conceptual rather than numerical, the "formulas" here are **classification frameworks** the KTU board examiner expects you to reproduce.

| # | Framework / "Formula" | Component Elements | Practical Use in KTU Exam |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Deming's 14 Points** | Constancy of purpose, Cease dependence on inspection, Drive out fear, Break down barriers, Institute training, Institute leadership, Eliminate slogans, Remove numerical quotas, Pride of workmanship, Education & self-improvement | Used to compare *American mass production* vs. *Japanese quality revolution* |
| 2 | **Juran Trilogy** | Quality Planning → Quality Control → Quality Improvement | Frequently asked as a 3-mark question: "Name and explain the Juran Trilogy." |
| 3 | **The 3 Ms of Muda** | **Muda** (Waste), **Mura** (Unevenness), **Muri** (Overburden) | Distinguishes the *Japanese original* of Lean from the *Western translation*. |
| 4 | **TPS House Equation (conceptual)** | $\text{TPS} = \text{Jidoka} + \text{JIT} + \text{Heijunka} + \text{Kaizen} + \text{Standardization}$ | Asked as: "Draw and explain the Toyota Production System House." |
| 5 | **Lean = Ford + Toyota** | $\text{Lean} = \text{Ford (Hardware: Assembly Line, Interchangeability)} + \text{Toyota (Software: JIT, Jidoka, Kanban, Kaizen)}$ | The "differentiate" question of KTU. |
| 6 | **Krafcik's Term Origin** | Coined in 1988, published 1990 | Direct 1-mark / 2-mark definition question. |
| 7 | **The Seven Wastes (TIMWOOD)** | Transport, Inventory, Motion, Waiting, Overproduction, Over-processing, Defects | Inherited from Ohno's original 7 wastes. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Trap:** If asked "Who invented Lean?", the correct answer is **Taiichi Ohno** (creator of TPS) or the **Toyota Motor Corporation** as a system. Do **not** answer "John Krafcik"—he only *named* the system in English. This is a classic 1-mark trap.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation: *How did Ford's constraints become Toyota's catalysts?*

This derivation explains the **logical flow of necessity** that forced Toyota to invent Lean. Each step is the cause of the next.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Post-WWII Japan had a chronic shortage of capital, land, and natural resources.} \\
& \therefore \text{Toyota could not afford Ford's high-inventory, high-volume model.} \\
\\
\textbf{Step 2:} \quad & \text{To survive, Toyota had to minimize Work-In-Progress (WIP) inventory.} \\
& \therefore \text{They developed Just-in-Time (JIT) production — parts arrive only when needed.} \\
\\
\textbf{Step 3:} \quad & \text{JIT requires extremely high reliability; a single defect halts the entire line.} \\
& \therefore \text{They developed Jidoka (Autonomation) — machines stop automatically on defect detection.} \\
\\
\textbf{Step 4:} \quad & \text{To feed the assembly line without batching, they needed a pull signal.} \\
& \therefore \text{Ohno adapted the American supermarket restocking model into the Kanban card system.} \\
\\
\textbf{Step 5:} \quad & \text{To handle demand fluctuations without overproduction, they leveled the schedule.} \\
& \therefore \text{They developed Heijunka (Production Leveling) — mix products in small batches.} \\
\\
\textbf{Step 6:} \quad & \text{To sustain all the above, every employee had to continuously solve problems.} \\
& \therefore \text{They institutionalized Kaizen (continuous improvement) and standardization.} \\
\\
\textbf{Step 7:} \quad & \text{The complete integrated system = Toyota Production System (TPS).} \\
& \text{Later renamed "Lean" by Krafcik (1988) and globally popularized by Womack \& Jones (1990).}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation: *The Krafcik 1988 Naming Logic*

Why did Krafcik use the word "Lean"? The derivation follows the **rhetorical reduction** he applied.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1:} \quad & \text{TPS uses } \approx \frac{1}{2} \text{ the human effort, factory space, tooling investment, and engineering hours} \\
& \text{compared to mass production (Ford system) for the SAME output.} \\
\\
\textbf{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Krafcik measured these ratios quantitatively in his IMVP research.} \\
\\
\textbf{Step 3:} \quad & \text{A system that achieves equal output with HALF the input resources is, by definition, "lean."} \\
\\
\textbf{Step 4:} \quad & \text{The English word "Lean" (meaning: thin, minimal, devoid of excess) is intuitive for Western} \\
& \text{managers who would have been alienated by Japanese terminology.} \\
\\
\textbf{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Thus, "Lean Production" became the universal, English-language brand of TPS.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of the Three Founding Systems

| Dimension | **Craft Production** | **Mass Production (Ford)** | **Lean Production (Toyota)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Era of Dominance** | Pre-1900 | 1913–1980 | 1970–Present |
| **Product Variety** | Very High | Very Low (one model) | High |
| **Volume per Variant** | Very Low | Very High | Medium |
| **Lead Time** | Very Long | Short (for one model) | Short |
| **Workforce Skill** | Very High (master craftsman) | Low (unskilled) | High (multi-skilled) |
| **Inventory Level** | Low (work-in-progress) | Very High (buffers) | Very Low (JIT) |
| **Defect Response** | Repair by craftsman | End-of-line inspection | Stop-the-line (Jidoka) |
| **Driving Logic** | Make-to-Order | Make-to-Stock | **Make-to-Order / Pull** |
| **Customer Waiting** | Long | None (stock available) | Minimal (built on demand) |
| **Key Innovation** | Mastery | Assembly Line + Standardization | JIT + Jidoka + Kaizen |
| **Key Person** | Local artisan | Henry Ford | Taiichi Ohno |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** This matrix is the *most frequently asked* Part B question structure: *"Compare Mass Production and Lean Production."* Memorize the rows above. Examiners award 7 marks for a side-by-side comparison, typically expecting at least 6 distinct comparison parameters.

### 3.4 Detailed Step-by-Step Explanation of Ohno's 7 Wastes (Original 1950s Version)

The original Ohno's 7 Wastes (in Japanese: *Nana no Muda*), later expanded to TIMWOOD +1 (Skills underutilized) by Western consultants.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{1. Overproduction (}\text{過剰生産, } \textbf{Kajō Seizan):} \quad & \text{Making more than the customer needs, or earlier than needed.} \\
& \text{Ohno called this the } \textbf{WORST WASTE} \text{ because it causes all other wastes.} \\
\\
\textbf{2. Waiting (}\text{待ち, } \textbf{Machi):} \quad & \text{Operators, machines, or materials idle due to unbalanced flow.} \\
\\
\textbf{3. Transport (}\text{運搬, } \textbf{Unpan):} \quad & \text{Moving materials unnecessarily between processes.} \\
\\
\textbf{4. Over-processing (}\text{加工, } \textbf{Kakō):} \quad & \text{Doing more work than the customer values (e.g., excess precision).} \\
\\
\textbf{5. Inventory (}\text{在庫, } \textbf{Zaiko):} \quad & \text{Excess raw material, WIP, or finished goods.} \\
\\
\textbf{6. Motion (}\text{動作, } \textbf{Dōsa):} \quad & \text{Unnecessary movement of PEOPLE (bending, walking, reaching).} \\
\\
\textbf{7. Defects (}\text{不良, } \textbf{Furyō):} \quad & \text{Production of defective parts requiring rework or scrap.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Point:** **Overproduction is the most dangerous waste** because producing too much, too early, *creates* the need for excess inventory, unnecessary transport, and motion to handle that inventory. It is the **root cause** of the other six wastes.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Figure 1: The Historical Evolution Flow (Mermaid Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["ERA 1: CRAFT PRODUCTION 1799-1899"] --> B["ERA 2: MASS PRODUCTION 1900-1949"]
    B --> C["ERA 3: TOYOTA PRODUCTION SYSTEM 1950-1990"]
    C --> D["ERA 4: GLOBAL LEAN 1990-PRESENT"]

    A1["Eli Whitney: Interchangeable Parts 1799"] -.-> A
    B1["Henry Ford: Moving Assembly Line 1913"] -.-> B
    B2["Ford Model T: Standardization 1908"] -.-> B
    C1["Taiichi Ohno: JIT and Kanban 1953"] -.-> C
    C2["Eiji Toyoda: Rouge Plant Study 1950"] -.-> C
    C3["Deming and Juran: Statistical Quality 1950-1954"] -.-> C
    C4["Shigeo Shingo: Poka-Yoke and SMED 1960s"] -.-> C
    D1["John Krafcik: Coined the word Lean 1988"] -.-> D
    D2["Womack and Jones: The Machine That Changed the World 1990"] -.-> D
    D3["Lean Thinking Book 1996"] -.-> D
    D4["Lean Six Sigma and Industry 4.0 2000s-2020s"] -.-> D

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#ffe1f5,stroke:#cc0066,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006633,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Figure 2: The Toyota Production System (TPS) House

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOF["ROOF: Just-in-Time - Right Item, Right Quantity, Right Time"]
    FOUND["FOUNDATION: Heijunka - Level Production Scheduling"]
    PILLAR1["RIGHT PILLAR: Jidoka - Autonomation - Built-in Quality"]
    PILLAR2["LEFT PILLAR: Just-in-Time - Kanban Pull System"]
    CENTER["CENTER: Standardization and Kaizen Continuous Improvement"]

    ROOF --- PILLAR1
    ROOF --- PILLAR2
    PILLAR1 --- FOUND
    PILLAR2 --- FOUND
    CENTER --- ROOF

    style ROOF fill:#ffcc00,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:3px
    style FOUND fill:#cc9966,stroke:#663300,stroke-width:2px
    style PILLAR1 fill:#66ccff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
    style PILLAR2 fill:#ff99cc,stroke:#660033,stroke-width:2px
    style CENTER fill:#99ff99,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Figure 3: Lean = Ford (Hardware) + Toyota (Software) — Conceptual Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FORD["FORDISM - THE HARDWARE LAYER"]
        F1["Interchangeable Parts"]
        F2["Moving Assembly Line 1913"]
        F3["Standardized Product"]
        F4["High Volume Mass Production"]
    end

    subgraph TOYOTA["TOYOTISM - THE SOFTWARE LAYER"]
        T1["Just-in-Time JIT"]
        T2["Jidoka Autonomation"]
        T3["Kanban Pull System"]
        T4["Kaizen Continuous Improvement"]
    end

    subgraph LEAN["LEAN PRODUCTION - INTEGRATED SYSTEM"]
        L1["The Machine That Changed the World 1990"]
        L2["Lean Thinking 1996"]
        L3["Lean Six Sigma and Beyond"]
    end

    FORD --> LEAN
    TOYOTA --> LEAN

    style FORD fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px
    style TOYOTA fill:#e1e1ff,stroke:#000099,stroke-width:2px
    style LEAN fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:3px
```
