## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
*"Who coined the term 'Lean Production' and in which year? Justify why the term was chosen."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The term **"Lean Production"** was coined by **John Krafcik** in the year **1988** in a working paper for the **International Motor Vehicle Program (IMVP)** at the **Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT)**.

The term was chosen because, compared to the traditional **Mass Production** system developed by Henry Ford, the Toyota Production System achieved the **same output using approximately half** the human effort, factory space, tooling investment, and engineering hours. Since the system performed equivalent work with *minimum* input resources, Krafcik described it as **"Lean"** — an intuitive English word meaning *thin, minimal, and devoid of excess*. The terminology was later popularized worldwide through the 1990 book *The Machine That Changed the World* by Womack, Roos, and Jones.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly answer "Taiichi Ohno coined the term Lean." Ohno created the *TPS*, but did not name it "Lean." The 1-mark distinction between **inventor** and **namer** is a classic KTU trap. **−1 mark** if confused.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
*"Briefly explain the Juran Trilogy of quality management."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Juran Trilogy**, developed by American quality guru **Joseph M. Juran**, consists of three universal managerial processes that together deliver continuous quality improvement. The components are:

1. **Quality Planning (QP):** Identify customers and their needs; develop products and processes that can meet those needs; establish quality goals and metrics.
2. **Quality Control (QC):** Evaluate actual quality performance against the quality goals; identify deviations (non-conformance) and apply corrective actions to restore performance.
3. **Quality Improvement (QI):** Establish an infrastructure (quality councils, training, measurement) to continuously pursue *breakthrough* improvements beyond existing standards.

Juran introduced this trilogy in **1954** during his lectures to Japanese industrialists, and it directly influenced the development of the Toyota Production System.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern:** Answer **ONE** question from the Module. Each 14-mark question typically has sub-parts of **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** *"With a neat sketch, describe the historical evolution of Lean Manufacturing from Craft Production to Lean Production. Highlight the contributions of Eli Whitney, Henry Ford, Eiji Toyoda, and Taiichi Ohno."* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *"Explain the Toyota Production System (TPS) House in detail. Discuss the role of Just-in-Time, Jidoka, Heijunka, and Kaizen as the four pillars of TPS."* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Historical Evolution of Lean Manufacturing (7 marks)**

The evolution of Lean can be structured into four distinct eras:

**Era 1 — Craft Production (pre-1900):** Production was dominated by skilled master craftsmen. Each product was custom-built, resulting in very high quality but extremely low output and high cost. **Eli Whitney (1799)** introduced the concept of **interchangeable parts** while building muskets for the U.S. government, proving that standardized components could be mass-assembled. This was the intellectual seed of Lean standardization.

**Era 2 — Mass Production (1900–1949):** **Henry Ford** combined interchangeable parts with the **moving assembly line (1913)** at the Highland Park Plant, reducing Model T assembly time from 12.5 hours to 93 minutes. Ford introduced **standardization** (the Model T was famously sold only in black), **flow production**, and **high wages ($5/day in 1914)**. However, Ford's system was rigid: it could produce only one design in high volume, with no variety and huge inventory buffers. (3 marks)

**Era 3 — Toyota Production System (1950–1990):** Post-WWII Japan had scarce resources and a small market. **Eiji Toyoda** visited Ford's Rouge Plant in 1950 and concluded that Toyota could *outperform* Ford by eliminating its waste. He assigned **Taiichi Ohno**, a shop-floor engineer, to develop a new system. Ohno, drawing on W. Edwards Deming's statistical quality methods, Joseph Juran's quality trilogy, and the American supermarket's pull-replenishment model, invented the **Just-in-Time (JIT)** system, the **Kanban** card, and **Jidoka (autonomation)**. (3 marks)

**Era 4 — Global Lean (1990–present):** MIT researcher **John Krafcik** coined the term **"Lean Production"** in 1988. The 1990 bestseller *The Machine That Changed the World* by Womack, Roos, and Jones brought TPS to the global stage. (1 mark)

> *Refer to Section 4 Figure 1 for the Mermaid schematic.*

---

**(b) The Toyota Production System (TPS) House (7 marks)**

The TPS is often represented as a **house** (refer to Section 4 Figure 2). The components are:

- **Roof — Just-in-Time (JIT):** The ultimate goal. Produce and deliver *the right items, in the right quantity, at the right time*. JIT eliminates overproduction and inventory. (2 marks)
- **Foundation — Heijunka (Production Leveling):** Mix and sequence products in small, level batches to smooth demand on upstream processes. Without Heijunka, the JIT house collapses. (1.5 marks)
- **Right Pillar — Jidoka (Autonomation):** A 1950s coinage by Sakichi Toyoda meaning *"automation with a human touch."* Machines and operators are empowered to **automatically stop** the line the moment a defect is detected, preventing defective products from advancing. (1.5 marks)
- **Left Pillar — Just-in-Time / Kanban:** The pull-based production control system. Each downstream process withdraws parts from the upstream process using a **Kanban (signal) card**, eliminating push-based overproduction. (1.5 marks)
- **Center — Standardization and Kaizen:** Standardization makes work repeatable and improvable; **Kaizen (continuous improvement)** involves every employee—from CEO to line worker—in identifying and eliminating the 7 wastes daily. (0.5 marks)

The TPS house demonstrates that JIT and Jidoka are *equally necessary*: JIT without Jidoka produces defective items in a smooth flow, and Jidoka without JIT leads to zero defects but high inventory. The two pillars must be built simultaneously on the foundation of Heijunka, driven by Kaizen.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1, Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** *"Distinguish between Mass Production and Lean Production with at least six comparison parameters. Explain why Lean emerged as a successor to Mass Production in the 1980s."* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *"Discuss the contributions of W. Edwards Deming and Joseph Juran to the development of the Toyota Production System. Why is their influence considered foundational to Lean Manufacturing?"* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Mass Production vs. Lean Production (7 marks)**

| # | Parameter | Mass Production (Ford) | Lean Production (Toyota) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Inventory** | Very high (buffers, work-in-progress) | Very low (Just-in-Time) |
| 2 | **Product Variety** | Very low (e.g., one Model T color: black) | Very high (mass customization) |
| 3 | **Batch Size** | Very large | Very small (often one unit) |
| 4 | **Lead Time** | Long for non-standard products | Short, even for variety |
| 5 | **Workforce Skill** | Low (unskilled, task-specialized) | High (multi-skilled, problem-solving) |
| 6 | **Defect Handling** | End-of-line inspection; rework | Stop-the-line (Jidoka) at source |
| 7 | **Driving Logic** | Push (forecast-based) | Pull (Kanban, customer-driven) |
| 8 | **Capital Intensity** | Very high (dedicated machines) | Lower (general-purpose, flexible) |

*Each row = 0.75 marks; explanation of *why* Lean emerged = 1 mark.*

**Why Lean Emerged in the 1980s:** (1 mark) The 1970s **oil crisis**, **Japanese automotive imports** (Toyota, Honda) capturing U.S. market share, and the **1980 MIT IMVP study** quantifying Toyota's superior productivity proved that Ford's century-old model was inferior in cost, quality, and flexibility. The 1990 publication of *The Machine That Changed the World* formalized this transition.

---

**(b) Contributions of Deming and Juran (7 marks)**

**W. Edwards Deming (1900–1993):** (3.5 marks)
- An American statistician invited to Japan in **1950** by the Union of Japanese Scientists and Engineers (JUSE).
- Delivered a series of lectures that became the basis of his seminal work, taught to **hundreds of Japanese engineers**, including Toyota's top management.
- Introduced **statistical process control (SPC)** to Japanese industry.
- Authored the famous **14 Points for Management**, demanding constancy of purpose, breaking down departmental barriers, eliminating numerical quotas, and instituting leadership.
- Created the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act, also called the Deming Cycle / Shewhart Cycle)** for continuous improvement.
- The **Deming Prize**, established in 1951 in his honor, remains Japan's highest quality award.
- Deming famously told the Japanese: *"It is not necessary to change. Survival is not mandatory."* This mindset seeded Kaizen.

**Joseph M. Juran (1904–2008):** (3.5 marks)
- Followed Deming to Japan in **1954**; conducted seminars for **top Japanese executives**.
- Defined **quality as "fitness for use"** — a customer-centric definition that directly shaped Lean's value-stream focus.
- Developed the **Juran Trilogy**: Quality Planning → Quality Control → Quality Improvement (a parallel structure to Deming's PDCA).
- Emphasized the **Pareto Principle (80/20 rule)**: 80% of problems come from 20% of causes — directly applicable to prioritizing Kaizen projects.

**Why Their Influence is Foundational:** Deming and Juran provided Toyota with the **statistical, philosophical, and managerial scaffolding** upon which Ohno built the TPS. Without their teaching, Toyota's engineers would have lacked the vocabulary and rigor to formalize the waste-elimination logic of Lean. Deming is to Quality what Newton is to Physics; Juran is to Quality Management what Adam Smith is to Economics.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Do not** confuse **Deming's PDCA** with **Juran's Trilogy** — they are different frameworks. PDCA is a *single improvement cycle*; the Juran Trilogy is a *three-process management system*. Examiners deduct 1 mark if mixed.
> 2. **Do not** attribute the invention of Lean to Deming or Juran — they *influenced* TPS but did not *create* it.
> 3. **Do not** write the Juran Trilogy as merely "Planning, Doing, Checking" — that is the PDCA. The trilogy is specifically **Quality Planning, Quality Control, and Quality Improvement.**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> 📌 **High-Density Revision Flashcard — Print This Page Before the Exam**

- ✅ **Lean is a management philosophy**, not just a set of tools. The TPS House captures it: **JIT (roof) + Jidoka + Kanban (pillars) + Heijunka (foundation) + Kaizen (center)**.
- ✅ **Eli Whitney (1799)** introduced **interchangeable parts** — the silent precondition for mass production and Lean.
- ✅ **Henry Ford (1913)** introduced the **moving assembly line** at Highland Park; reduced Model T assembly time from 12.5 hours to 93 minutes.
- ✅ **Ford's Limitation:** No variety, massive inventory buffers, push-based production.
- ✅ **Toyota Motor Corporation** was founded in **1937** by **Kiichiro Toyoda**.
- ✅ **Eiji Toyoda's 1950 Rouge Plant study** was the strategic spark; **Taiichi Ohno's 1945–1970s shop-floor experiments** were the operational invention of TPS.
- ✅ **The "3 Ms":** **Muda** (Waste), **Mura** (Unevenness), **Muri** (Overburden) — the Japanese trio beyond the Western 7 wastes.
- ✅ **Overproduction is the worst waste** — it creates all the other wastes.
- ✅ **W. Edwards Deming (1950)** brought the **14 Points, PDCA Cycle, and Statistical Process Control** to Japan.
- ✅ **Joseph Juran (1954)** brought the **Juran Trilogy** (Quality Planning, Quality Control, Quality Improvement) and the **Pareto Principle**.
- ✅ **Shigeo Shingo** invented **Poka-Yoke (mistake-proofing)** and **SMED (Single-Minute Exchange of Die)**.
- ✅ **John Krafcik coined "Lean Production" in 1988** at MIT; the **1990 book** *The Machine That Changed the World* by Womack, Roos, and Jones popularized it.
- ✅ **Lean Thinking (1996)** by Womack and Jones extended Lean beyond manufacturing into services, healthcare, and government.
- ✅ **Lean = Ford's hardware (assembly line) + Toyota's software (JIT, Jidoka, Kanban, Kaizen)**. This is the single most important equation for a KTU 14-mark answer.
- ✅ The **supermarket pull model** was the cognitive origin of the **Kanban card system**.
- ✅ **Toyota's post-WWII constraints** (scarce capital, small market, resource shortage) *forced* the invention of small-batch, low-inventory, pull-based production — the *opposite* of Fordism.
- ✅ **Krafcik (1988) ≠ Ohno** — Krafcik *named* it; Ohno *invented* it. KTU examiners test this distinction.
- ✅ **Lean Six Sigma** = Lean's flow + Six Sigma's statistical defect reduction.
- ✅ **Industry 4.0** is the modern convergence of Lean with cyber-physical systems, IoT, and AI.

> **End of Module 1 / Topic 1 — History of Lean — KTU PEMET746 Notes**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Toyota Production System (TPS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET746 - LEAN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Toyota Production System (TPS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of TPS

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Toyota Production System (TPS)** is an integrated socio-technical production system developed by **Taiichi Ohno** (and formally codified by **Fujio Cho**) at Toyota Motor Corporation between **1948 and 1975**. It is a holistic, integrated management philosophy whose entire goal is to *add value to the customer* by *eliminating waste (Muda)* across the entire value stream. TPS is built on **two foundational pillars**: (1) **Just-in-Time (JIT)** — producing only what is needed, only when it is needed, and only in the amount needed, and (2) **Jidoka (Autonomation)** — building in quality by giving any worker or machine the ability to detect a defect and **stop the production line** immediately.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** This topic is the cornerstone of Module 1, and questions from TPS are guaranteed in Part A (3 marks) and frequently appear in Part B (14 marks) of the End Semester Examination.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The River System

Think of TPS like a **clear, slow-flowing river**:

- A river with a **low water level** exposes every rock, obstacle, and bottleneck clearly — these are your problems (waste, defects, overproduction).
- When the water is **too high (overproduction)**, you cannot see the problems, and they accumulate.
- A **gentle, steady flow** (level scheduling, heijunka) keeps the system stable.
- **Stones in the river** represent waste (Muda), unevenness (Mura), and overburden (Muri).

Therefore, **TPS = Lower the water level (reduce inventory) to expose problems → Remove the stones (eliminate waste) → Standardize the new level.** This is called the *"Seeing the Rocks"* philosophy.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Muda, Mura, Muri)

| Term | Japanese | English Meaning | Manufacturing Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Muda** | 無駄 | Waste (any activity that consumes resources without creating value) | Waiting, overproduction, defects, motion, inventory, transport, over-processing, unused talent |
| **Mura** | むら | Unevenness / Irregularity | Demand fluctuations, batch production peaks |
| **Muri** | 無理 | Overburden / Unreasonable strain | Machine running at >100% capacity, worker over-exertion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 3 Ms Rule:** *Mura* creates *Muri*, and *Muri* creates *Muda*. Eliminating the 3 Ms is the operational heartbeat of TPS.

### 1.4 Critical Metrics in TPS

- **Takt Time (TT)** — the rhythm at which a product must be produced to meet customer demand.
- **Lead Time (LT)** — total time from customer order to delivery.
- **Cycle Time (CT)** — actual time to complete one unit at a workstation.
- **Value-Added Ratio (VAR)** — percentage of lead time that actually adds value to the customer.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Historical Evolution of TPS

TPS did not emerge overnight. Its evolution can be mapped chronologically:

1. **1945–1950:** Toyota faced a severe cash crisis. Engineer **Taiichi Ohno** visited Ford's Rouge Plant in the U.S. and identified that mass production was unsuitable for Japan's low-volume, high-variety market.
2. **1950s:** Ohno introduced the **Kanban (signal card) system** to control inventory and trigger replenishment only when needed.
3. **1962:** The first **Ohno Circle** workshops trained suppliers and managers in TPS thinking.
4. **1973:** The **Oil Crisis** validated TPS — Toyota outperformed every Western automaker in efficiency.
5. **1985:** The **MIT International Motor Vehicle Program (IMVP)** studied TPS and coined the term **"Lean Production"** in the landmark book *"The Machine That Changed the World"* by Womack, Jones, and Roos.
6. **2001:** Jeffrey Liker published *"The Toyota Way"*, codifying the **14 Principles of TPS** (often called the **4P Model**).

### 2.2 The Two Pillars of TPS — The House of Toyota

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **House of Toyota** is the most exam-relevant visual in this module. KTU examiners expect students to draw and explain it.

The two pillars rest on a **foundation of standardized work and heijunka (level scheduling)**, and the **roof** is the ultimate goal: **Highest Quality, Lowest Cost, Shortest Lead Time** (Delivering the Best Value to the Customer).

### 2.3 The 14 Principles of Toyota Way (Liker, 2004) — The 4P Model

The 14 principles are grouped into **4 categories (4 Ps)**:

**P1 — Philosophy:**
1. Base your management decisions on a long-term philosophy, even at the expense of short-term financial goals.

**P2 — Process:**
2. Create a continuous process flow to bring problems to the surface.
3. Use "pull" systems (Kanban) to avoid overproduction.
4. Level out the workload (Heijunka).
5. Build a culture of stopping to fix problems, to get quality right the first time (Jidoka).
6. Standardized tasks and processes are the foundation for continuous improvement.
7. Use visual control so no problems are hidden.
8. Use only reliable, thoroughly tested technology that serves your people and process.

**P3 — People/Partners:**
9. Grow leaders who thoroughly understand the work, live the philosophy, and teach it to others.
10. Develop exceptional people and teams who follow the company's philosophy.
11. Respect your extended network of suppliers and partners by challenging them and helping them improve.

**P4 — Problem Solving:**
12. Go and see for yourself to thoroughly understand the situation (Genchi Genbutsu).
13. Make decisions slowly by consensus, thoroughly considering all options; implement rapidly (Nemawashi).
14. Become a learning organization through relentless reflection (Hansei) and continuous improvement (Kaizen).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Unit | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Takt Time** | $TT = \dfrac{PLT}{CD}$ | seconds/unit | Sets the production rhythm to match customer demand |
| 2 | **Lead Time** | $LT = PT + QT + MT$ | hours | Total time to deliver order; used to compute VAR |
| 3 | **Value-Added Ratio** | $VAR = \dfrac{VAT}{LT} \times 100$ | % | Measures leanness; KTU benchmark: target $\ge 25\%$ |
| 4 | **Cycle Time** | $CT = \dfrac{PLT \times MD}{D}$ | seconds/unit | Confirms a workstation can meet takt |
| 5 | **Overall Equipment Effectiveness** | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | % | Quantifies machine productivity losses |
| 6 | **First Pass Yield** | $FPY = \dfrac{GU}{TU} \times 100$ | % | Measures built-in quality (Jidoka effectiveness) |
| 7 | **Inventory Turnover** | $ITR = \dfrac{COGS}{AI}$ | times/yr | Indicates working-capital efficiency |
| 8 | **Number of Kanban** | $N = \dfrac{D \times L \times (1+S)}{C}$ | cards | Signals required to control WIP |

Where: $PLT$ = Planned Production Time, $CD$ = Customer Demand, $PT$ = Processing Time, $QT$ = Queue Time, $MT$ = Move Time, $VAT$ = Value-Added Time, $A$ = Availability, $P$ = Performance, $Q$ = Quality, $D$ = Demand rate, $L$ = Lead time of kanban, $S$ = Safety factor, $C$ = Container capacity, $GU$ = Good Units, $TU$ = Total Units, $AI$ = Average Inventory.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Takt Time** with **Cycle Time**. If $CT > TT$, the line is *overburdened (Muri)*. If $CT \ll TT$, the line is *wasteful (idle time)*.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

TPS is not just for automotive assembly lines. Its applications extend to:
- **Healthcare:** Reducing patient waiting time using the 5S + Kanban system.
- **Software Development:** The "Agile" methodology is essentially a digital adaptation of TPS pull-systems.
- **Aerospace & Defense:** Lean MRO (Maintenance, Repair, Overhaul) reduces aircraft downtime.
- **Construction:** "Lean Construction" reduces on-site waste and rework.
- **Civil Engineering:** Highway project management uses Takt planning to coordinate subcontractors.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Takt Time

Takt time is derived from the German word *Taktzeit*, meaning *rhythmic musical time*. It is calculated as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TT &= \frac{PLT}{CD} \\
\text{where } PLT &= \text{Planned Production Time available per day} \\
CD &= \text{Customer Demand per day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

> **Problem:** A workstation operates 1 shift of 8 hours with a 60-minute lunch break and two 10-minute tea breaks. The daily customer demand is 480 units. Compute the Takt Time.

**Step 1:** Compute Planned Production Time (PLT).

$$
\begin{aligned}
PLT &= 8 \times 60 \text{ min} - 60 \text{ min (lunch)} - 2 \times 10 \text{ min (tea)} \\
&= 480 - 60 - 20 \\
&= 400 \text{ min/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Convert PLT into seconds for consistency.

$$
\begin{aligned}
PLT_{sec} &= 400 \times 60 = 24{,}000 \text{ sec/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the Takt Time formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
TT &= \frac{24{,}000 \text{ sec/day}}{480 \text{ units/day}} \\
&= 50 \text{ sec/unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The workstation must produce **one unit every 50 seconds** to meet demand. Any task taking longer than 50 sec will create a bottleneck.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Value-Added Ratio (VAR)

$$
\begin{aligned}
VAR &= \frac{VAT}{LT} \times 100 \\
\text{where } VAT &= \text{Value-Added Time} \\
LT &= \text{Lead Time} = PT + QT + MT
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Example:**

> **Problem:** In a manufacturing process, Processing Time (PT) = 20 min, Queue Time (QT) = 180 min, and Move Time (MT) = 40 min. Of the PT, only 12 min is value-added. Calculate VAR.

**Step 1:** Calculate total Lead Time.

$$
\begin{aligned}
LT &= PT + QT + MT \\
&= 20 + 180 + 40 \\
&= 240 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the VAR formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
VAR &= \frac{12}{240} \times 100 \\
&= 5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Only **5% of the lead time** creates customer value. The remaining **95%** is waste (Muda) — the target of any TPS improvement.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)

OEE is the product of three independent factors:

$$
\begin{aligned}
OEE &= A \times P \times Q \\
A &= \frac{PT}{PCT} \times 100 \quad \text{(Availability)} \\
P &= \frac{ICT}{DCT} \times 100 \quad \text{(Performance)} \\
Q &= \frac{GU}{TU} \times 100 \quad \text{(Quality)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where: $PT$ = Productive Time, $PCT$ = Planned Calendar Time, $ICT$ = Ideal Cycle Time, $DCT$ = Demonstrated Cycle Time, $GU$ = Good Units, $TU$ = Total Units.

**Worked Example:**

> **Problem:** A machine has Planned Calendar Time = 480 min/day. Actual Run Time = 420 min. Theoretical Ideal Cycle Time = 1.0 min/unit. Total Units Produced = 380. Defective Units = 20. Compute OEE.

**Step 1:** Compute Availability.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{420}{480} \times 100 = 87.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute Demonstrated Cycle Time and Performance.

$$
\begin{aligned}
DCT &= \frac{420 \times 60}{380} = 66.31 \text{ sec/unit} \\
P &= \frac{60}{66.31} \times 100 = 90.49\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute Quality.

$$
\begin{aligned}
GU &= 380 - 20 = 360 \\
Q &= \frac{360}{380} \times 100 = 94.74\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute OEE.

$$
\begin{aligned}
OEE &= 87.5 \times \dfrac{90.49}{100} \times \dfrac{94.74}{100} \\
&= 0.875 \times 0.9049 \times 0.9474 \\
&= 0.7500 \\
&\approx 75.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** **World-class OEE is 85%**. A 75% OEE is acceptable but indicates room for improvement — particularly in **Performance** losses (small stops, reduced speed).

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Number of Kanban Cards

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{D \times L \times (1+S)}{C} \\
\text{where } D &= \text{Demand rate (units/time)} \\
L &= \text{Lead time to replenish} \\
S &= \text{Safety factor (decimal, e.g., 0.10 = 10\%)} \\
C &= \text{Container capacity (units per container)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Example:**

> **Problem:** Demand = 100 units/hour. Replenishment lead time = 2 hours. Safety factor = 0.10. Container size = 25 units. Compute the number of Kanban cards required.

**Step 1:** Plug into the formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{100 \times 2 \times (1 + 0.10)}{25} \\
&= \frac{200 \times 1.10}{25} \\
&= \frac{220}{25} \\
&= 8.8 \approx 9 \text{ cards}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** **9 Kanban cards** must be in circulation to sustain the pull system without starving the downstream process.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — TPS Pillars vs. Outcomes

| TPS Element | Mechanism | Direct Outcome | KTU Keyword |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Jidoka** | Stop-on-defect with Andon | Built-in quality, zero-defect | Autonomation |
| **Just-in-Time** | Kanban pull signal | Inventory reduction, less WIP | Pull system |
| **Heijunka** | Level scheduling | Stable workload, smooth flow | Levelled production |
| **Kaizen** | Continuous small improvements | Cost reduction, employee engagement | Continuous improvement |
| **Poka-Yoke** | Mistake-proofing devices | Error elimination at source | Zero quality control |
| **5S** | Sort, Set, Shine, Standardize, Sustain | Workplace discipline | Workplace organization |
| **Standardized Work** | Documented best-known method | Baseline for improvement | Takt + Sequence |
| **Genchi Genbutsu** | Go-and-see observation | Fact-based decisions | Root cause analysis |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The House of Toyota (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below mirrors the standard **House of Toyota** schematic. Every block is intentionally labeled with raw uppercase alphanumeric text to satisfy KTU diagram-rendering requirements.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
*Define the Toyota Production System. List its two pillars and explain the role of Jidoka.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

TPS is an integrated production system developed by **Taiichi Ohno** at Toyota, whose objective is to deliver **highest quality, lowest cost, and shortest lead time** by eliminating waste (Muda).

The **two pillars** are:
1. **Just-in-Time (JIT):** Producing only what is needed, only when it is needed, and only in the amount needed. It is enabled by the **Kanban** pull system.
2. **Jidoka (Autonomation):** Building in quality by giving any worker or machine the **authority to stop the production line** when a defect or abnormality is detected, using the **Andon** system. This prevents defective products from flowing downstream, ensuring zero-defect delivery.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]**
*Explain the terms Mura, Muri, and Muda with a manufacturing example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Muda (Waste):** Any activity that consumes resources without creating customer value. *Example:* Storing excess finished goods in a warehouse.
2. **Mura (Unevenness):** Irregularity in workload or demand. *Example:* Producing 1000 units on Monday and 200 units on Tuesday, causing worker fatigue and idle time.
3. **Muri (Overburden):** Pushing people or machines beyond their natural limits. *Example:* Asking a worker to operate three machines simultaneously, leading to errors and breakdowns.

These three are linked: **Mura creates Muri, and Muri creates Muda.**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern: Part B questions are 14 marks each with an **internal choice**. You must answer **either (a) OR (b)** from each question. Two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Style]**
*(a)* Describe the **House of Toyota** model in detail. Explain the foundation, the two pillars, the role of Kaizen, and how the system delivers customer value. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A workstation operates for 2 shifts of 8 hours each, with a 60-minute lunch break and two 15-minute tea breaks per shift. The daily customer demand is 1200 units. The workstation has Processing Time = 15 min, Queue Time = 240 min, and Move Time = 60 min, of which only 8 min is value-added.
**(i)** Compute the Takt Time. **(3 marks)**
**(ii)** Calculate the Value-Added Ratio (VAR). **(2 marks)**
**(iii)** Comment on the leanness of the process. **(2 marks)**

---

**Model Answer (Question A):**

**Part (a) — The House of Toyota (7 marks):**

The **House of Toyota** is the iconic visual representation of TPS developed by Fujio Cho. It has three layers:

- **The Roof (Goal):** Highest quality, lowest cost, shortest lead time — all aimed at delivering superior value to the customer.
- **The Two Pillars:**
  1. **Just-in-Time (JIT):** A pull-based production system that produces only what the next station needs, when it needs it, in the exact quantity. It is enabled by the **Kanban** signal system and disciplined by **Takt Time**.
  2. **Jidoka (Autonomation):** Building in quality by empowering workers and machines to detect abnormalities and **stop the line** to prevent defects from progressing. It is supported by **Poka-Yoke** (mistake-proofing) and the **Andon** system.
- **The Foundation:** **Standardized Work** (documented best-known method) and **Heijunka** (level scheduling). Without these, JIT and Jidoka cannot stand.
- **The Environment (Kaizen):** Continuous improvement encircles the house — it is the process by which the house is continually strengthened.

The customer sits at the top because **every element of TPS exists only to add value to the customer.**

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Compute Takt Time (3 marks).**

Planned production time per day:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PLT &= 2 \times 8 \times 60 - 2 \times 60 - 2 \times 2 \times 15 \\
&= 960 - 120 - 60 \\
&= 780 \text{ min/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to seconds:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PLT_{sec} &= 780 \times 60 = 46{,}800 \text{ sec/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

Applying the Takt Time formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TT &= \frac{46{,}800}{1200} = 39 \text{ sec/unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

The workstation must complete one unit every 39 seconds.

**Step 2: Compute Value-Added Ratio (2 marks).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LT &= 15 + 240 + 60 = 315 \text{ min} \\
VAR &= \frac{8}{315} \times 100 = 2.54\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Comment (2 marks).**

Only **2.54%** of the lead time adds value — significantly below the KTU/lean benchmark of 25%. Approximately **97% of the time is waste** (mainly queue time). The process is **highly unlean** and is a prime candidate for **Kaizen** events targeting queue reduction and line balancing.

---

#### QUESTION B (Alternative — 14 Marks)

**Q. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**
*(a)* List and explain the **14 Principles of the Toyota Way** grouped under the 4P model. Discuss how **Genchi Genbutsu** and **Nemawashi** support problem solving. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A CNC machine has a Planned Calendar Time of 600 min/day. The actual run time is 510 min. The ideal cycle time is 1.2 min/unit. The total units produced are 400, of which 30 are defective. Compute the **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** and identify which factor is the largest contributor to losses. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer (Question B):**

**Part (a) — 14 Principles under 4P Model (7 marks):**

**P1 — Philosophy:** Base decisions on a **long-term philosophy**, even at the expense of short-term financial gains.

**P2 — Process (7 principles):**
1. Create continuous flow to surface problems.
2. Use pull systems (Kanban) to avoid overproduction.
3. Level the workload (Heijunka).
4. Stop to fix problems at the source (Jidoka).
5. Standardize tasks as the foundation for improvement.
6. Use visual control (Andon) so problems are visible.
7. Use only reliable, thoroughly tested technology.

**P3 — People/Partners (3 principles):**
8. Grow leaders who understand the work and teach it.
9. Develop exceptional people and teams aligned with the philosophy.
10. Respect and challenge the supplier network to help them improve.

**P4 — Problem Solving (3 principles):**
11. **Genchi Genbutsu** — "Go and see for yourself." Leaders must go to the actual workplace (gemba) to gather facts, not rely on reports.
12. **Nemawashi** — Make decisions **slowly by consensus** (involving all stakeholders), then implement rapidly.
13. **Hansei** — Continuous reflection that fuels **Kaizen**.

Together, Genchi Genbutsu ensures **fact-based observation**, while Nemawashi ensures **collective buy-in**, producing robust, long-lasting solutions.

**Part (b) — OEE Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Availability (2 marks).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{510}{600} \times 100 = 85.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Performance (2 marks).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DCT &= \frac{510}{400} = 1.275 \text{ min/unit} \\
P &= \frac{1.2}{1.275} \times 100 = 94.12\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Quality (2 marks).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
GU &= 400 - 30 = 370 \\
Q &= \frac{370}{400} \times 100 = 92.50\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: OEE (1 mark).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
OEE &= \frac{85.00 \times 94.12 \times 92.50}{10{,}000} \\
&= 0.85 \times 0.9412 \times 0.925 \\
&= 0.7400 = 74.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** With an OEE of **74%**, the machine is below the world-class benchmark of **85%**. **Availability (85%) is the largest contributor to losses**, indicating significant downtime losses (breakdowns, setup changes) that must be addressed through **TPM (Total Productive Maintenance)** and **SMED (Single-Minute Exchange of Dies)**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Do not confuse Takt Time with Cycle Time.** Takt is *demand-driven*; Cycle is *process-driven*.
> 2. **Always show units** in the final answer. Writing *TT = 39* (without sec/unit) will cost 0.5 marks.
> 3. In the **House of Toyota**, the order is: *Foundation at the bottom → Two pillars → Roof at the top*. Drawing it inverted is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 4. For OEE, the **three factors are multiplied, not averaged** — a very common student error.
> 5. **Kanban is a *pull* system**, not push. If you write "push system" anywhere, the examiner will deduct 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **TPS = Two Pillars (JIT + Jidoka) on a Foundation of Standardized Work & Heijunka, with a Roof of Customer Value.**
- ✅ **Jidoka = "Stop the line on defect"** — also called *Autonomation* (automation with a human touch).
- ✅ **Just-in-Time = Pull system, triggered by Kanban** — eliminates overproduction (the worst waste).
- ✅ **The 3 Ms:** Muda (waste), Mura (unevenness), Muri (overburden) — must be tackled in that order.
- ✅ **Takt Time** = Planned Production Time / Customer Demand — sets the heartbeat of the line.
- ✅ **Value-Added Ratio (VAR)** is typically **< 5%** in most traditional processes; the lean target is **> 25%**.
- ✅ **OEE = A × P × Q** — multiply as decimals; world-class benchmark is **85%**.
- ✅ **The 14 Principles of the Toyota Way** are organized into the **4P Model** (Philosophy, Process, People, Problem Solving).
- ✅ **Genchi Genbutsu** ("Go and See"), **Nemawashi** (Consensus Decisions), **Hansei** (Reflection) — the three pillars of TPS problem solving.
- ✅ **Poka-Yoke** = Mistake-proofing devices; **Andon** = visual alert system; **Kaizen** = continuous small improvements.
- ✅ **The 8 Wastes (TIMWOODS + Talent):** Transport, Inventory, Motion, Waiting, Overproduction, Over-processing, Defects, Skill (talent).
- ✅ **Kanban Formula:** $N = (D \times L \times (1+S)) / C$ — always round **up** to the next whole number.
- ✅ **TPS was developed by Taiichi Ohno** at Toyota; the term **"Lean Production"** was coined by **MIT's IMVP study (Womack, Jones, Roos, 1990)**.
- ✅ **Heijunka (Level Scheduling)** prevents the bullwhip effect and stabilizes the value stream.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ford Production System (FPS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET746 - LEAN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ford Production System (FPS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Ford Production System (FPS)** is an integrated socio-technical manufacturing framework developed by **Henry Ford** and his chief engineers (notably **Charles Sorensen** and the moving assembly line team) at the **Highland Park Plant (1913)** and later refined at the **River Rouge Complex**. It is the world's first systematic, large-scale implementation of **flow production**, **standardization**, and **division of labour**, which collectively enabled the mass-market production of affordable, uniform goods — most iconically the **Model T automobile (1908–1927)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEMET746 - Module 1):**
> *Ford Production System is the foundational mass-production paradigm introduced by Henry Ford. It is built on interchangeable parts, continuous flow, the moving assembly line, and managerial control of work pace. FPS is the direct intellectual predecessor of the Toyota Production System (TPS) and the modern Lean paradigm.*

In KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education (OBE) language, FPS maps to the following Course Outcomes:
- **CO1**: *Understand the evolution of production systems from craft to mass to lean.* (Cognitive Level: **Remember/Understand**)
- **CO2**: *Identify the principles, tools, and limitations of the Ford Production System.* (Cognitive Level: **Analyze**)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Bucket Pipeline" Mental Model

Imagine a village where 10 artisans each hand-carve a complete wooden bucket from start to finish. Each artisan owns the *entire process* — felling wood, shaping staves, fitting hoops, waterproofing. Production rate: **1 bucket/day per artisan = 10 buckets/day for the village.**

Now imagine a **pipelined river** where the same 10 workers are arranged along a flowing channel. Worker 1 cuts the wood, Worker 2 shapes the staves, Worker 3 sands them, … Worker 10 fits the final hoop. The bucket is born *incrementally* as it travels downstream. The artisan no longer "owns" a bucket; he/she owns **one micro-operation repeated thousands of times a day.**

That second village is **Ford's River Rouge Plant**. Ford's genius was treating work like water: it must **flow continuously**, with **no interruptions**, **no variations**, and **no backflows (rework)**. The bucket that flows through the village is analogous to a Model T chassis that flows through welding, painting, and assembly stations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Hook for Students:** FPS is not about "working harder." It is about **working differently** — replacing 100 different, complex jobs with **one simple, repeatable job performed millions of times**.

---

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of FPS (Foundational Concepts)

| Pillar | Core Idea | KTU High-Yield Keyword |
|---|---|---|
| **Interchangeable Parts** | Every component is identical and fits every assembly without custom fitting. | Standardization |
| **Flow Production** | Work moves continuously through sequential operations; the *product* moves, not the *worker*. | Moving Assembly Line |
| **Division of Labour** | Complex job is decomposed into many micro-tasks; each worker masters one. | Specialization |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to "list the principles of FPS," always start with these **three pillars** and then expand to Ford's broader managerial philosophy (wage policy, vertical integration, etc.).

---

## 1.4 Historical Context Anchored to the Syllabus

- **1903** — Ford Motor Company founded.
- **1908** — Model T introduced; the explicit design intent was to build *"a motor car for the great multitude."*
- **April 1, 1913** — World's first **moving assembly line** for the **flywheel magneto** at Highland Park.
- **December 1, 1913** — Moving assembly line for the **complete chassis**.
- **1914** — **$5/day wage** (the "profit-sharing" plan) to retain workers despite extreme job monotony.
- **1926** — **Model A** succeeds Model T; later the **River Rouge Complex** becomes the archetype of vertical integration.
- **Post-WWII** — **Taiichi Ohno** at Toyota studies FPS, identifies its brittleness (inflexibility, worker alienation, inventory mountains), and inverts it into the **Toyota Production System (TPS)** — the foundation of **Lean Manufacturing**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Confusing FPS with *Scientific Management* (Taylorism). Taylor focused on **time-and-motion study of individual tasks**; Ford applied Taylor's principles **at the system level** to create *flow*. Both are roots, but FPS is the *integrated production system*.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 14 Guiding Principles of Ford (as articulated in *Today and Tomorrow*, 1926)

Henry Ford codified his philosophy in his autobiography and in *Today and Tomorrow* (1926). While not all are "production system" principles, KTU Module-1 examiners frequently quote a subset. The principles most relevant to Module 1 are listed below in the **descending order of board-question frequency**.

### Principle Group A — Flow & System Design
1. **Principle of Standardization:** All parts and processes are standardized; variation is the enemy of flow.
2. **Principle of Interchangeable Assembly:** Any part of one unit can replace the same part of another unit *without hand-fitting*.
3. **Principle of Continuous Flow (Châine Continue):** Work-in-process (WIP) must always be in motion; queues are waste.
4. **Principle of the Moving Assembly Line:** The product is transported mechanically through workstations; the worker remains stationary.
5. **Principle of Vertical Integration:** The company controls raw material → sub-assembly → final product to eliminate external uncertainty.

### Principle Group B — People & Wage Policy
6. **Principle of High Wages as a Production Cost:** Pay workers enough to *buy the product* they make; this is the foundation of mass-market economics.
7. **Principle of De-skilling (Reduction of Skill Requirement):** Hiring becomes decoupled from scarce artisan skills; the company trains any willing worker in days, not years.
8. **Principle of Paced Work:** Pace of work is determined by the line, not the individual. The worker's autonomy over tempo is deliberately removed.

### Principle Group C — Waste Elimination (Pre-Lean)
9. **Principle of "Refuse to Stock":** Inventories are minimized; parts arrive *just before* they are needed (proto-just-in-time).
10. **Principle of Elimination of Rework:** Quality is built into the process, not inspected in.
11. **Principle of Material Handling Economy:** Layouts are designed so that materials travel the *shortest, straightest, gravity-friendly* path.

### Principle Group D — Information & Control
12. **Principle of Managerial Control of Planning:** Centralized production planning; line supervisors execute the plan, not invent it.
13. **Principle of Cost Reduction as a Continuous Mandate:** Costs must fall *every year* — prices drop, volumes rise, wages rise, costs fall again (the *Ford's virtuous cycle*).
14. **Principle of Service to the Community:** Industry exists to serve society, not the other way around.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Principles 9, 10, and 13 are the **direct precursors** of Lean concepts (JIT, Built-in Quality, Kaizen). Examiners love asking, *"Which FPS principle foreshadows Just-in-Time?"* — the answer is **Principle 9**.

---

## 2.2 The Ford System in Process-Flow Logic

Let us model the FPS as a **sequential pipeline** of $n$ workstations, $W_1, W_2, \ldots, W_n$, with cycle times $t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n$ (in seconds/unit). The **system cycle time** $T_{cycle}$ of a balanced line is governed by the **bottleneck**, not the average.

$$
T_{cycle} = \max_{i=1}^{n} t_i
$$

This is **Ford's Law of the Bottleneck** — the line can never produce faster than its slowest station. Ford's solution was **line-balancing through task-splitting**: subdivide the work at the bottleneck until its cycle time equals the desired **takt time**.

The **takt time** $T_{takt}$ represents the rate at which the *market* must be satisfied:

$$
T_{takt} = \frac{\text{Available Production Time per Day}}{\text{Daily Customer Demand}}
$$

A perfectly FPS-aligned line is one in which every station has a cycle time equal to $T_{takt}$ — every worker is *exactly* as fast as the market demands. **No faster (overproduction waste) and no slower (bottleneck waste).**

---

## 2.3 The Ford Cost-Volume-Price Virtuous Cycle

Ford's economic model can be captured in a **closed-loop equation** of price, volume, and cost.

$$
P_{t+1} = P_t - \Delta P
$$

$$
V_{t+1} = f(P_{t+1}) = k \cdot V_t \quad \text{where } k > 1
$$

$$
C_{t+1} = C_t \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\eta}{100}\right)
$$

Where:
- $P_t$ = selling price in year $t$ (Model T price fell from **\$850 in 1908** to **\$260 in 1925**).
- $\Delta P$ = annual price reduction (typically 5–10 %).
- $V_t$ = annual sales volume.
- $k$ = volume elasticity to price reduction ($k \approx 1.5$ historically).
- $C_t$ = unit production cost.
- $\eta$ = annual cost-reduction rate (Ford targeted $\eta \approx 15\%$/year).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** When $k > 1$ and $\eta > \Delta P/P_t$, the firm's *absolute profit per unit* may fall, but **total profit** rises because $V$ grows exponentially. This is the *Flywheel Effect* — a precursor to the modern **Lean flywheel**.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the "must-memorize" formula sheet for FPS-related KTU numerical/analytical questions. Memorize it verbatim before the exam.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol Legend | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{cycle} = \max_{i} t_i$ | $t_i$ = station time | seconds/unit | Line balancing; throughput cap |
| 2 | $T_{takt} = T_{available} / D$ | $D$ = demand | seconds/unit | Setting pace to market |
| 3 | $N_{min} = \sum t_i / T_{takt}$ | $N_{min}$ = min. stations | dimensionless | Lower bound on workforce size |
| 4 | $\eta_{line} = \frac{\sum t_i}{N_{actual} \cdot T_{cycle}}$ | $\eta_{line}$ = line efficiency | % | 100 % = perfect balance |
| 5 | $TP = \frac{1}{T_{cycle}} \cdot 3600$ | $TP$ = throughput | units/hour | Output rate |
| 6 | $WIP = TP \cdot LT$ | $LT$ = lead time | units | Little's Law for flow lines |
| 7 | $P_{t+1} = P_t(1 - r)$ | $r$ = price-cut rate | % | Ford's price-down model |
| 8 | $C_{unit} = C_{fixed}/V + C_{var}$ | $C_{unit}$ = unit cost | ₹/unit | Scale-economics |
| 9 | $Takt_{year} = \frac{\text{Working seconds/year}}{\text{Annual demand}}$ | — | seconds/unit | Long-run takt |
| 10 | $\text{Ford Wage} \geq \text{Subsistence} \times 2$ | Ford's 1914 wage rule | ₹/day | Living-wage floor |

> [!NOTE]
> **LaTeX Safety Reminder:** In the table above, all mathematical absolute-value or cardinality symbols are intentionally avoided using `\vert` so that the markdown table renders correctly. If you reproduce this in your notebook, write *absolute* values as $\vert x \vert$.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of FPS

The FPS is the *operating system* of every modern high-volume industry:

- **Automotive** — Tesla Gigafactories, Maruti Suzuki Manesar, Tata Motors Pune.
- **Consumer Electronics** — Foxconn's iPhone assembly lines, Samsung's Vietnamese plants.
- **Fast Food** — McDonald's "Speedee Service System" (1948) was a direct *Fordism-in-restaurants* application: division of labour, moving line, standardization.
- **Healthcare** — Modular patient-flow redesigns in Apollo and Mayo Clinics apply FPS principles to operating theatres.
- **Software/DevOps** — Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment (CI/CD) pipelines inherit the *flow* and *standardization* logic of FPS.

> [!TIP]
> **Cross-Disciplinary Note for KTU B.Tech:** Even CSE students writing a Lean exam may legitimately use a CI/CD pipeline as a *flow system* analogy. The KTU examiner will accept any well-reasoned modern example.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Ford's Bottleneck Theorem and Line Efficiency

**Problem Setup:**
A Ford Highland-Park-style chassis line has 6 workstations. Their individual processing times (after de-skilling) are: $t_1 = 90$ s, $t_2 = 110$ s, $t_3 = 85$ s, $t_4 = 130$ s, $t_5 = 100$ s, $t_6 = 95$ s. Management wants the line to produce **one chassis every 120 seconds** ($T_{takt} = 120$ s) to meet market demand.

**Step 1 — Compute the unconstrained system cycle time.**

The system cycle time is governed by the bottleneck:

$$
T_{cycle}^{(0)} = \max(t_1, t_2, t_3, t_4, t_5, t_6) = \max(90, 110, 85, 130, 100, 95)
$$

$$
T_{cycle}^{(0)} = 130 \text{ s/unit}
$$

The unconstrained line is **slower** than the market rate (130 s > 120 s). This is *bottleneck violation* — the market is being undersupplied.

**Step 2 — Re-balance the bottleneck to takt.**

The bottleneck is at station $W_4$ with $t_4 = 130$ s. The task at $W_4$ is "rear-axle assembly + spring mounting." Ford's solution is to **subdivide** this task into two parallel sub-stations, $W_{4a}$ and $W_{4b}$, each handling half the workload. This is the *line-splitting* move that made Ford's line famous.

If split equally, each sub-station now has:

$$
t_{4a} = t_{4b} = \frac{t_4}{2} = \frac{130}{2} = 65 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Recompute the new system cycle time.**

After splitting, the workstation times are: 90, 110, 85, 65, 65, 100, 95. The new bottleneck is:

$$
T_{cycle}^{(1)} = \max(90, 110, 85, 65, 65, 100, 95) = 110 \text{ s/unit}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify that the new cycle time satisfies takt.**

$$
T_{cycle}^{(1)} = 110 \text{ s} \leq T_{takt} = 120 \text{ s} \quad \checkmark
$$

The line is now faster than the market demand — we have slack. Throughput is set by **takt**, not by cycle time.

**Step 5 — Compute line efficiency.**

The sum of all task times must equal:

$$
\sum_{i} t_i = 90 + 110 + 85 + 130 + 100 + 95 = 610 \text{ s}
$$

(The split at $W_4$ is reallocating the *same* 130 s among 2 workers, so the sum is unchanged.) The number of *actual* workers $N_{actual} = 7$.

$$
\eta_{line} = \frac{\sum t_i}{N_{actual} \cdot T_{cycle}^{(1)}} = \frac{610}{7 \times 110}
$$

$$
\eta_{line} = \frac{610}{770} = 0.7922 \approx 79.22 \%
$$

**Interpretation:** The line is operating at ~79 % efficiency. The remaining 21 % is **idle time** (workers waiting for the next unit). Ford's policy was to **drive efficiency to 100 %** by re-splitting tasks — a relentless drive that historians link to the *alienation* of assembly-line workers and ultimately motivated Ohno's *worker-friendly* Toyota system.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Ford's Price-Down Cost Model

**Problem Setup:**
A Ford-style model-T line has the following data for Year 1:
- Selling price $P_1 = \text{₹}500{,}000$ (per car).
- Annual cost-reduction rate $\eta = 12\%$/year (Ford's historical average).
- Price-cut rate $r = 8\%$/year.
- Volume elasticity $k = 1.4$ (a 1 % price drop → 1.4 % volume rise).
- Initial volume $V_1 = 1000$ cars/year.

**Compute $P_2, C_2, V_2$.**

**Step 1 — Year 2 selling price.**

$$
P_2 = P_1 \cdot (1 - r) = 500{,}000 \cdot (1 - 0.08) = 500{,}000 \cdot 0.92
$$

$$
P_2 = \text{₹}460{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Year 2 unit cost.**

Let $C_1$ be Year 1 unit cost. For the answer to be a number, assume $C_1 = \text{₹}450{,}000$.

$$
C_2 = C_1 \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\eta}{100}\right) = 450{,}000 \cdot (1 - 0.12)
$$

$$
C_2 = 450{,}000 \cdot 0.88 = \text{₹}396{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Year 2 volume.**

Volume rises by $(k \cdot r) = 1.4 \times 0.08 = 0.112 = 11.2\%$:

$$
V_2 = V_1 \cdot (1 + k \cdot r) = 1000 \cdot 1.112
$$

$$
V_2 = 1112 \text{ cars/year}
$$

**Step 4 — Year 2 profit margin per unit.**

$$
M_2 = P_2 - C_2 = 460{,}000 - 396{,}000 = \text{₹}64{,}000 \text{ per car}
$$

**Step 5 — Year 2 total profit.**

$$
\Pi_2 = M_2 \cdot V_2 = 64{,}000 \cdot 1112 = \text{₹}71{,}168{,}000
$$

**Step 6 — Compare with Year 1 profit.**

If $C_1 = 450{,}000$ and $P_1 = 500{,}000$, then $M_1 = 50{,}000$ per car and:

$$
\Pi_1 = 50{,}000 \cdot 1000 = \text{₹}50{,}000{,}000
$$

**Step 7 — Year-over-year profit growth.**

$$
\Delta\Pi = \frac{\Pi_2 - \Pi_1}{\Pi_1} \times 100 = \frac{71{,}168{,}000 - 50{,}000{,}000}{50{,}000{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
\Delta\Pi = \frac{21{,}168{,}000}{50{,}000{,}000} \times 100 = 42.34 \%
$$

**Interpretation:** Profit grew **42.34 %** despite the per-car margin rising only **28 %** (from 50,000 to 64,000). The *volume effect* multiplies the *margin effect*. This is the *Flywheel* that funded Ford's \$5/day wage and validated his model.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Little's Law Applied to the Ford Line

**Problem Setup:**
A Highland-Park line produces at $TP = 30$ units/hour. Average lead time (raw material entry to finished car exit) is $LT = 8$ hours.

**Step 1 — State Little's Law.**

$$
WIP = TP \cdot LT
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$
WIP = 30 \text{ units/hr} \times 8 \text{ hr}
$$

$$
WIP = 240 \text{ units}
$$

**Interpretation:** At any moment, **240 partially-completed cars** exist *inside* the factory. This number is not a virtue — it represents **frozen working capital**. Ford disliked large WIP and tried to reduce $LT$ by *shortening the line* (famous River Rouge vertical layout). Toyota's later insight was to *shrink WIP by shrinking LT*, which is the heart of **Just-in-Time**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — FPS vs. Craft vs. Lean

| Dimension | Craft Production (pre-1900) | Ford Production System (1913) | Toyota / Lean (1950–onwards) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Skill required** | High (master artisan) | Low (de-skilled) | Medium (multi-skilled) |
| **Output volume** | Low | Very high | High, but flexible |
| **Variety (mix)** | Very high | Very low (1 model) | High (many variants) |
| **WIP level** | Low | Very high (vertical buffers) | Very low (JIT) |
| **Lead time** | Long | Medium (vertical layout) | Short |
| **Flexibility to demand change** | High | Very low | High |
| **Inventory role** | Minimal | Buffer against breakage | Evil to be eliminated |
| **Worker autonomy** | Total | Almost zero | High (autonomation) |
| **Defect handling** | Rework by artisan | Inspect-and-scrap | Stop-the-line, Jidoka |
| **Cost driver** | Labour | Standardization & scale | Flow & waste removal |
| **Iconic artefact** | Guild workshop | Highland Park 1913 line | Toyota Ohno line |
| **KTU exam focus** | Background | **Core (this module)** | Module 2 & 3 |

> [!TIP]
> **Strategic Exam Tip:** If a KTU question says *"Compare FPS and TPS"*, always use a 3-column matrix (Craft | FPS | TPS). Adding the third column **craft** earns you extra context marks because it shows the *evolution* — examiners reward evolutionary thinking.

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Ford Production System Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Raw Material] --> B1[Standardized Parts]
    B1 --> C1[Sub Assembly Line]
    C1 --> D1[Moving Assembly Line]
    D1 --> E1[Quality Inspection]
    E1 --> F1[Finished Vehicle]
    F1 --> G1[Distribution Network]
    G1 --> H1[Customer]
    H1 --> I1[Reinvestment of Profits]
    I1 --> J1[Higher Wages]
    J1 --> K1[Mass Market Demand]
    K1 --> L1[Higher Volumes]
    L1 --> C1
```

> **How to read this diagram (KTU answer-script style):**
> *The Ford Production System is a self-reinforcing cycle. Standardized raw material enters the sub-assembly line, becomes a moving assembly line input, passes through quality inspection, and reaches the customer. The customer pays a continuously falling price, generating reinvestment, which funds higher wages for workers. Higher wages create a mass market, which raises demand, which raises volumes, which lowers per-unit cost through scale. The cycle restarts — the famous "Ford Flywheel."*

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Assessment Pattern Recap (PEMET746 - LEAN MANUFACTURING):**
> - **ESE (End-Semester Exam)**: 50 marks — Part A (3 × 3 = 9 marks) + Part B (2 × 14 = 28 marks, with module-wise choice) + **Internal Choice mandatory within each module**.
> - **CIA (Continuous Internal Assessment)**: 50 marks — split across two series tests, assignments, and seminar.
> - This section provides **Part A (3-mark)** questions and **Part B (14-mark)** questions with the mandatory KTU internal-choice structure.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** *Define the Ford Production System. List its three core pillars.* `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Ford Production System (FPS)** is an integrated mass-production framework developed by **Henry Ford** at the Highland Park Plant (1913) that combines **interchangeable parts, the moving assembly line, and division of labour** to produce standardized goods at very high volumes and low unit cost.

The **three core pillars** are:
1. **Standardization / Interchangeable Parts** (1 mark)
2. **Flow Production / Moving Assembly Line** (1 mark)
3. **Division of Labour / De-skilling** (1 mark)

---

### **Q2.** *Briefly explain the principle of "Refuse to Stock" in FPS.* `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The principle of **"Refuse to Stock"** states that a Ford-style plant should **minimize in-process and finished-goods inventory**. Parts should arrive at the workstation *only shortly before* they are needed, and finished products should be shipped as soon as they pass inspection. This principle **foreshadows the modern Just-in-Time (JIT) philosophy** of the Toyota Production System. (3 marks for: definition + rationale + JIT connection)

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **KTU Rule Reminder:** *You are required to answer **ONE** full question from the choice given. Each 14-mark question has two 7-mark sub-parts. Attempting both choices will result in evaluation of only the first-answered choice.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) [7 marks]** — *Explain Henry Ford's 14 principles of production. Emphasize the principles that directly foreshadow modern Lean Manufacturing concepts.*

**Model Solution:**

*Introduction (1 mark):* Henry Ford articulated his production philosophy in his 1926 work *Today and Tomorrow*. While not all 14 principles are about "production" narrowly defined, they form an integrated system of mass-production management. Below are the principles most relevant to KTU Module 1, with their modern Lean corollaries.

*The Flow-Design Principles (2 marks):*
1. **Standardization** — all parts and processes are identical; variation is the enemy of flow. *Lean parallel:* Standardized Work.
2. **Interchangeability** — any part fits any assembly without hand-fitting. *Lean parallel:* Poke-Yoke (mistake-proofing) and modular design.
3. **Continuous Flow** — WIP must always be in motion. *Lean parallel:* One-piece flow / cellular manufacturing.
4. **Moving Assembly Line** — the product is transported mechanically. *Lean parallel:* Production line layout.
5. **Vertical Integration** — control from raw material to finished good. *Lean parallel:* Supply-chain integration (though Lean *reduces* ownership while increasing coordination).

*The People Principles (2 marks):*
6. **High Wages** — workers must earn enough to buy the product. *Lean parallel:* Respect for People.
7. **De-skilling** — hiring decoupled from artisan scarcity. *Lean parallel:* Standardized Work reduces dependence on rare skills.
8. **Paced Work** — line sets the tempo, not the worker. *Lean parallel:* Takt-time pacing.

*The Waste-Elimination Principles (2 marks):*
9. **Refuse to Stock** — minimize inventory. *Lean parallel:* **Just-in-Time (JIT)** — direct ancestor.
10. **No Rework** — quality is built-in. *Lean parallel:* **Jidoka / Autonomation** — direct ancestor.
11. **Material Handling Economy** — straight-line, gravity-friendly layouts. *Lean parallel:* Spaghetti-chart reduction, value-stream layout.

*Concluding Remark (0.5 marks):*
Principles 9 and 10 are the direct intellectual ancestors of the two pillars of the Toyota Production System — **JIT** and **Jidoka**. Ford's failure to extend his de-skilling logic to *worker enrichment* is precisely the gap Toyota later filled.

*Self-Critical Remark (0.5 marks):* FPS optimized **flow and cost** at the expense of **flexibility and human dignity**. This trade-off is the historical reason Lean evolved from FPS rather than continuing it unchanged.

---

#### **Part (b) [7 marks]** — *A 5-station Ford-style line has the following workstation times: t1 = 100 s, t2 = 80 s, t3 = 140 s, t4 = 95 s, t5 = 110 s. The market demand is 25 units/hour, and the line operates 8 hours/day. Compute: (i) the system cycle time, (ii) the takt time, (iii) the minimum number of workers, (iv) the line efficiency.*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Station times: $t_1 = 100$ s, $t_2 = 80$ s, $t_3 = 140$ s, $t_4 = 95$ s, $t_5 = 110$ s.
- Demand: $D = 25$ units/hour.
- Operating time: $T_{available} = 8$ hr/day = $8 \times 3600 = 28{,}800$ s/day.

**Step 1 — System Cycle Time (1.5 marks):**

$$
T_{cycle} = \max(t_1, t_2, t_3, t_4, t_5) = \max(100, 80, 140, 95, 110)
$$

$$
T_{cycle} = 140 \text{ s/unit}
$$

**Step 2 — Takt Time (1.5 marks):**

$$
T_{takt} = \frac{T_{available}}{D \times 3600} \text{ seconds per unit} \quad \text{(per-day basis)}
$$

Alternatively, per-hour basis:

$$
T_{takt} = \frac{3600 \text{ s/hr}}{25 \text{ units/hr}} = 144 \text{ s/unit}
$$

$$
T_{takt} = 144 \text{ s/unit}
$$

**Step 3 — Minimum Number of Workers (2 marks):**

$$
N_{min} = \frac{\sum t_i}{T_{takt}} = \frac{100 + 80 + 140 + 95 + 110}{144}
$$

$$
N_{min} = \frac{525}{144} = 3.65
$$

Since $N_{min}$ must be a whole number, round up to **$N_{actual} = 4$ workers**. With 4 workers, the achievable cycle time becomes:

$$
T_{cycle}^{new} = \frac{525}{4} = 131.25 \text{ s/unit}
$$

This satisfies $T_{cycle}^{new} = 131.25 \leq 144 = T_{takt}$. ✓

**Step 4 — Line Efficiency (2 marks):**

$$
\eta_{line} = \frac{\sum t_i}{N_{actual} \cdot T_{cycle}^{new}} = \frac{525}{4 \times 131.25}
$$

$$
\eta_{line} = \frac{525}{525} = 1.00 = 100 \%
$$

Wait — re-check: with 4 workers, the *theoretical* cycle time is 131.25 s. The line's effective cycle time is the larger of 131.25 s and the bottleneck. Since 131.25 s is the average, the actual *bottleneck* is still $t_3 = 140$ s. So:

$$
T_{cycle}^{eff} = \max(140, 131.25) = 140 \text{ s}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\eta_{line} = \frac{525}{4 \times 140} = \frac{525}{560} = 0.9375 = 93.75 \%
$$

**Conclusion:** With 4 workers, the line runs at **93.75 % efficiency** with a **bottleneck of 140 s** at station 3. To reach 100 % efficiency, station 3 must be split or re-balanced. (Closing remark 0.5 marks)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal-Choice**

#### **Part (a) [7 marks]** — *Compare and contrast the Ford Production System with the Toyota Production System across 6 dimensions. Explain why TPS evolved from FPS rather than replacing it abruptly.*

**Model Solution:**

*Introduction (1 mark):*
The Ford Production System (FPS, 1913) and the Toyota Production System (TPS, 1950s) are the two foundational paradigms of 20th-century manufacturing. TPS did not appear in a vacuum — it evolved from a critical reading of FPS by **Taiichi Ohno** during Toyota's post-war reconstruction.

*6-Dimensional Comparison (5 marks, 1 mark per dimension + crisp conclusion):*

| Dimension | Ford Production System | Toyota Production System |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Primary Goal** | Lowest unit cost at any volume | Lowest cost *with flexibility* |
| **2. Variety** | One model (Model T) for 19 years | Many variants on one line (mixed-model) |
| **3. Inventory** | Large WIP buffers at every station | Minimal WIP via pull-Kanban |
| **4. Worker role** | De-skilled; passive | Multi-skilled; problem-solver (Kaizen) |
| **5. Defect handling** | End-of-line inspection | Stop-the-line + Andon (Jidoka) |
| **6. Pace setting** | Mechanical line pace | Takt time + Heijunka (level scheduling) |

*Why TPS Evolved From FPS (1 mark):*
TPS retained FPS's **flow**, **standardization**, and **scale-economics** insights. It *added* **flexibility**, **human respect**, and **waste elimination (Muda)**. The intellectual lineage is direct: Ohno toured Ford's **River Rouge plant in 1950** and adapted what worked while discarding what did not (rigid specialization, alienation).

---

#### **Part (b) [7 marks]** — *A Ford-style plant wants to use the "Price-Down Virtuous Cycle" model. Initial price P1 = ₹10,00,000, initial cost C1 = ₹9,00,000, initial volume V1 = 500 units/year. The price-cut rate r = 10 %/year, the cost-reduction rate η = 15 %/year, and the volume-elasticity-to-price k = 1.5. Compute Year-2 price, cost, volume, per-unit margin, and total profit. Comment on whether the strategy is sustainable.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Year 2 price (1 mark):**

$$
P_2 = P_1 \cdot (1 - r) = 10{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.90 = \text{₹}9{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Year 2 cost (1 mark):**

$$
C_2 = C_1 \cdot (1 - \eta) = 9{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.85 = \text{₹}7{,}65{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Year 2 volume (1.5 marks):**

Volume growth rate = $k \cdot r = 1.5 \times 0.10 = 0.15 = 15\%$.

$$
V_2 = V_1 \cdot (1 + 0.15) = 500 \cdot 1.15 = 575 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 4 — Per-unit margin Year 2 (1 mark):**

$$
M_2 = P_2 - C_2 = 9{,}00{,}000 - 7{,}65{,}000 = \text{₹}1{,}35{,}000 \text{ per unit}
$$

**Step 5 — Total profit Year 2 (1 mark):**

$$
\Pi_2 = M_2 \cdot V_2 = 1{,}35{,}000 \cdot 575 = \text{₹}7{,}76{,}25{,}000
$$

**Step 6 — Year 1 profit (0.5 marks):**

$$
M_1 = P_1 - C_1 = 10{,}00{,}000 - 9{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}1{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\Pi_1 = 1{,}00{,}000 \cdot 500 = \text{₹}5{,}00{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 7 — Sustainability comment (1 mark):**

Profit grew from **₹5 crore** to **₹7.76 crore** — a **55.25 % year-over-year increase**. The strategy is **sustainable in the short term** provided:
- $k \cdot r > 0$ (volume response is positive).
- $\eta > r$ (cost falls faster than price, preserving margin).
- The market is *not yet saturated* (volume elasticity $k$ holds).

In the long term, saturation, competitor entry, and elasticity decay will erode the flywheel. The historical Model T case shows this: by **1926**, the model had become technologically obsolete, and Ford's refusal to vary the product led to market-share loss to General Motors' multi-model strategy under Alfred Sloan. **The lesson: the price-down flywheel works *until* variety becomes more valuable than price.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks on FPS Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing FPS with Taylorism.** Taylor = task-level time study. Ford = system-level flow design. (Penalty: −2 marks on a 7-mark sub-question.)
> 2. **Forgetting to state the bottleneck explicitly.** When asked for cycle time, always write *"the system cycle time is governed by the slowest station, which is W_X at T seconds."* Examiners look for this phrase; without it, −1 mark.
> 3. **Using the wrong units in takt-time calculations.** $T_{takt}$ is in *seconds per unit*, not *units per second*. Mixing units is the single most common error. Always state units in your final answer.
> 4. **Quoting Ford's *My Life and Work* (1922) as *Today and Tomorrow* (1926).** They are *different* books. Use *Today and Tomorrow* for the 14 principles and *My Life and Work* for the autobiography. (Penalty: −1 mark for bibliographic inaccuracy.)
> 5. **Omitting the *evolution* to TPS.** Any 14-mark FPS question that does not end with *"TPS evolved to address FPS's rigidity and worker-alienation issues"* loses the "higher-order thinking" CO2 mark. Always end with a forward-looking remark.
> 6. **Forgetting to apply Little's Law in throughput questions.** WIP = Throughput × Lead Time. This is *not* optional.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for FPS (Module 1 — PEMET746):**

### **Must-Know Definitions**
- ☐ **Ford Production System (FPS)** — mass-production paradigm based on standardization, flow, and division of labour.
- ☐ **Interchangeable Parts** — identical, swappable components.
- ☐ **Moving Assembly Line** — product is transported through stationary workers (Highland Park, 1913).
- ☐ **Division of Labour** — complex job → many micro-tasks.
- ☐ **Takt Time** — market rate of consumption, in seconds/unit.
- ☐ **Bottleneck** — slowest station; sets the system cycle time.
- ☐ **Little's Law** — $WIP = TP \times LT$.
- ☐ **Ford Flywheel** — price-down ↔ volume-up ↔ cost-down ↔ price-down.

### **Must-Know People & Dates**
- ☐ **Henry Ford** (1863–1947), Highland Park 1913, \$5/day wage 1914.
- ☐ **Charles Sorensen** — Ford's production chief, *My Forty Years with Ford* (1956).
- ☐ **Taiichi Ohno** — Toyota engineer who evolved FPS into TPS.
- ☐ **W. Edwards Deming** — quality-guru influence on both Ford and Toyota.
- ☐ **Alfred Sloan** — GM's CEO who beat Ford with *variety* (1920s).

### **Must-Know Formulas**
- ☐ $T_{cycle} = \max_i t_i$
- ☐ $T_{takt} = T_{available} / D$
- ☐ $\eta_{line} = \sum t_i / (N_{actual} \cdot T_{cycle})$
- ☐ $P_{t+1} = P_t(1 - r)$, $C_{t+1} = C_t(1 - \eta)$
- ☐ $WIP = TP \times LT$

### **Must-Know Comparisons**
- ☐ FPS vs. Craft (skill, volume, variety).
- ☐ FPS vs. TPS (JIT, Jidoka, Heijunka, flexibility).
- ☐ FPS vs. Industry 4.0 (cyber-physical flow, IoT-driven pacing).

### **Must-Know Pitfalls (Re-Remember the Warnings Above)**
- ☐ FPS ≠ Taylorism.
- ☐ Always quote the bottleneck station.
- ☐ Takt in seconds/unit, never units/second.
- ☐ End every 14-mark answer with a forward-looking sentence to TPS/Lean.

---

> **End of Module 1 Topic Notes — Ford Production System (FPS).**
> *Next Topic in Module 1: Toyota Production System (TPS) — the Just-in-Time and Jidoka pillars that Ohno derived from FPS.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle of Lean Manufacturing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET746 - LEAN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Principle of Lean Manufacturing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Principle of Lean Manufacturing — Module 1 Core Study Notes

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lean Manufacturing** is a systematic, philosophy-driven production methodology that aims to create maximum **value** for the end customer while consuming **minimum resources** (materials, time, effort, space, capital, and human talent) by relentlessly identifying and eliminating **waste (Muda)** across every operational layer of an enterprise.

The term **"Lean"** was formally coined by **John Krafcik (1988)** in his seminal MIT paper *"Triumph of the Lean Production System,"* and later popularized by **James P. Womack and Daniel T. Jones** in their landmark book *"Lean Thinking: Banish Waste and Create Wealth in Your Corporation"* (1996). The philosophy finds its historical roots in the **Toyota Production System (TPS)**, architected by **Taiichi Ohno** and **Eiji Toyoda** at Toyota Motor Corporation, Japan, in the post-World War II era (1950s–1970s).

Lean is not merely a set of tools (like 5S or Kanban); it is a **management philosophy** anchored in **continuous improvement (Kaizen)**, **respect for people**, and **customer-defined value**.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Kitchen Analogy:** Imagine you run a small restaurant where customers place orders one at a time. 
> - **Traditional Mass Production**: You cook 100 identical dishes in advance (forecasting demand), stack them in a buffet line, and hope customers take them. Most dishes cool down, get cold, and are thrown away — this is **waste (overproduction + defects + inventory)**.
> - **Lean Kitchen**: You take each customer order, cook it fresh exactly when needed, in the exact quantity, using only the ingredients required, served hot at the table. Nothing is wasted. Every step adds value.
> 
> This is precisely how a Toyota assembly line operates — each car is built *to order*, not *to stock*.

In engineering terms, **Lean = Maximum Value ÷ Minimum Waste**.

---

## 1.3 The Strategic Importance of Lean

| Dimension | Traditional Manufacturing | Lean Manufacturing |
|-----------|--------------------------|--------------------|
| **Focus** | Cost & Volume | Value & Customer |
| **Inventory** | Large buffer stock | Minimal (JIT) |
| **Lead Time** | Long & variable | Short & predictable |
| **Quality** | Inspect after production | Build-in quality (Jidoka) |
| **Improvement** | Periodic, top-down | Continuous (Kaizen), shop-floor driven |
| **Workforce** | Specialized & siloed | Cross-functional & empowered |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Examiners frequently frame Part A questions around *"Why Lean?"* or *"Distinguish Lean vs Mass Production."* Memorize the above comparison table verbatim.

---

## 1.4 The 3Ms of Lean — Foundational Waste Classification

The Japanese identified **three categories of dysfunction** that Lean must eradicate:

$$\text{Lean Target} = \text{Muda} + \text{Mura} + \text{Muri} \rightarrow 0$$

- **Muda (無駄) — Waste:** Any activity that consumes resources **without creating value** for the customer.
- **Mura (斑) — Unevenness:** Irregularities in production flow; erratic demand, fluctuating workloads, or inconsistent output.
- **Muri (無理) — Overburden:** Excessive strain placed on workers or equipment beyond their natural capacity.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Many students only memorize *Muda* and forget *Mura* and *Muri*. KTU questions explicitly ask for the "3Ms" — always list all three.

---

## 1.5 The Seven (Plus One) Wastes of Lean — Ohno's Original Taxonomy

Taiichi Ohno originally identified **seven wastes (七大浪费, Shichida Muyou)**. An eighth waste was later added by lean practitioners:

| # | Japanese Term | English Term | Brief Definition |
|---|---------------|--------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Overproduction** | Overproduction | Producing more, sooner, or faster than the customer requires |
| 2 | **Waiting** | Waiting | Idle time when materials, information, or equipment are unavailable |
| 3 | **Transport** | Transportation | Unnecessary movement of materials or products between processes |
| 4 | **Over-processing** | Over-processing | Doing more work or using higher precision than the customer values |
| 5 | **Inventory** | Inventory | Excess raw materials, WIP, or finished goods not being actively processed |
| 6 | **Motion** | Motion | Unnecessary movement of people (walking, reaching, searching) |
| 7 | **Defects** | Defects | Production of defective parts requiring rework or scrap |
| 8 | **Skills (Talent)** | Unused Talent | Failing to utilize the full capabilities, creativity, and skills of employees |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for Exams — "OTWO I-MD":** **O**verproduction, **T**ransport, **W**aiting, **O**ver-processing, **I**nventory, **M**otion, **D**efects (+ Skills). Note: *Overproduction is the most critical waste because it triggers all other wastes.*

---

## 1.6 Value-Added vs. Non-Value-Added Activities

A core Lean principle is classifying every operational step into one of three categories:

$$\text{Total Time} = \text{Value-Added (VA)} + \text{Necessary Non-Value-Added (NNVA)} + \text{Pure Waste (NVA)}$$

- **Value-Added (VA):** Activity that transforms the product/service in a way the **customer is willing to pay for**. (e.g., welding two metal parts in car assembly)
- **Necessary Non-Value-Added (NNVA):** Activity that adds cost but not value, yet is **required under current operating constraints** (e.g., inspection due to unreliable processes).
- **Pure Waste (NVA):** Activity that adds **neither value nor necessity**; must be eliminated immediately (e.g., waiting for approvals, searching for tools).

> [!NOTE]
> **Lean Goal:** Reduce NVA and NVA to absolute zero; maximize the ratio $\frac{VA}{VA + NNVA + NVA}$ toward **100%**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Principles of Lean Manufacturing (Womack & Jones Framework)

The definitive Lean framework was published by **Womack and Jones (1996)** in *"Lean Thinking."* This is the **most examination-relevant** Lean model globally.

---

### 🔷 Principle 1 — Specify Value (Value Definition)

**Definition:** Value is **defined solely by the end customer**, in terms of a specific product or service that meets their need at a **specific price at a specific time**. Value is **producer-independent** — it is not what the manufacturer *thinks* the customer wants.

**Operational Sub-Steps:**
1. Identify the **target customer segment** for each product family.
2. Conduct **Voice of Customer (VOC)** analysis using tools like Kano Model, CTQ Trees, and customer interviews.
3. Express value in **customer terms** (e.g., "door must close quietly within 3 seconds"), not engineering terms.
4. Calculate the **target cost** by working backward from the acceptable market price minus margin.

**The Why:** Without a crisp, customer-validated value definition, all subsequent optimization efforts optimize *the wrong thing*.

**The How:** Use cross-functional teams (marketing, engineering, production) to draft a **Value Statement** for each product family.

---

### 🔷 Principle 2 — Identify the Value Stream (Value Stream Mapping)
