**Definition:** The **Value Stream** is the **complete set of all actions** (both value-added and non-value-added) required to bring a product from raw material to the customer's hands — including design, production, supply chain, and delivery.

**Operational Sub-Steps:**
1. Draw a **Current State Value Stream Map (CSVSM)** — capture every process step, information flow, material flow, cycle time, changeover time, and inventory levels.
2. Identify which steps are **VA, NNVA, and NVA**.
3. Design the **Future State Value Stream Map (FSVSM)** — eliminate NVA, minimize NNVA, and synchronize flow.
4. Implement changes and continuously redraw.

**The Why:** Most organizations do not understand their **end-to-end process**. Optimizing a single workstation while the upstream/downstream stations are flooded with WIP yields no system-level improvement.

**The How:** Use standardized **Value Stream Mapping (VSM)** symbols (process boxes, inventory triangles, supplier/customer icons, electronic information flows, push arrows, ship arrows).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** A 14-mark question may ask you to "Explain Value Stream Mapping (VSM) with steps." Always mention **Current State → Future State** mapping.

---

### 🔷 Principle 3 — Create Flow (Continuous Flow)

**Definition:** Once NVA is removed, ensure that the **remaining value-adding steps occur in a tight, continuous sequence** without interruptions, queues, or batching.

**Operational Sub-Steps:**
1. Reconfigure **layout** (move from functional/cellular to **cellular/U-line/one-piece flow layouts**).
2. Balance **takt time** (available production time ÷ customer demand rate).
3. Eliminate **bottlenecks** and **waiting**.
4. Standardize work sequences.

$$\text{Takt Time} = \frac{\text{Available Production Time per Day (seconds)}}{\text{Customer Demand per Day (units)}}$$

**The Why:** Batches and queues **hide problems** (defects, breakdowns, unbalanced workloads). Flow exposes problems so they can be solved.

**The How:** Reduce lot sizes, implement single-piece flow, use quick changeover (SMED), cross-train operators.

---

### 🔷 Principle 4 — Establish Pull (Pull System)

**Definition:** **Nothing is produced upstream unless a signal from the downstream customer demands it.** Production is *pulled* by actual demand, not *pushed* by forecast.

**Operational Sub-Steps:**
1. Implement **Kanban** (signal card/electronic signal) — the most iconic Lean tool.
2. Limit Work-In-Progress (WIP) via **CONWIP** or **Kanban loops**.
3. Synchronize supplier deliveries with production takt.
4. Use **Heijunka** (production leveling) to smooth demand fluctuations.

**The Why:** Push systems generate **inventory waste** based on speculative demand. Pull systems ensure every item produced has an immediate, confirmed customer.

**The How:** A downstream operator withdraws a unit from the supermarket and posts a Kanban card to the upstream operator, signaling replenishment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pull ≠ No Inventory:** A pull system *still* has inventory, but the inventory is *deliberately placed and capped*, not accumulated randomly.

---

### 🔷 Principle 5 — Pursue Perfection (Kaizen)

**Definition:** Lean is not a destination but a **continuous journey**. Through repeated cycles of VSM, flow creation, and pull establishment, the organization relentlessly approaches the theoretical ideal of **zero waste, zero defects, zero inventory, zero lead time**.

**Operational Sub-Steps:**
1. Institutionalize **Kaizen** (continuous improvement) events — short, focused, team-based improvement cycles.
2. Empower frontline workers through **Suggestion Systems** and **Quality Circles**.
3. Standardize successful improvements as the new baseline (PDCA: Plan-Do-Check-Act).
4. Use **A3 thinking** for problem-solving.

**The Why:** Once perfection is treated as a journey, every employee becomes a problem-solver. This is the **cultural core** of Lean.

**The How:** Embed Lean into the management system via **Daily Management, Obeya rooms, and Gemba Walks**.

$$\text{Kaizen Equation:}\quad \text{Standard} \xrightarrow{\text{PDCA}} \text{Improved Standard} \xrightarrow{\text{PDCA}} \text{Further Improvement} \rightarrow \infty$$

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Units | Typical Range / Boundary |
|---------|----------------------|-------|---------------------------|
| **Takt Time** | $\text{Takt} = \dfrac{T_{\text{available}}}{D_{\text{customer}}}$ | seconds/unit | 30 s – 600 s typical |
| **Lead Time (LT)** | $\text{LT} = \text{Processing Time} + \text{Queue Time} + \text{Wait Time}$ | hours/days | Minimized in Lean |
| **Value-Added Ratio (VAR)** | $\text{VAR} = \dfrac{\sum t_{VA}}{\text{Total Lead Time}} \times 100\%$ | % | Manufacturing: 5–25%; Lean target: ≥ 35% |
| **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ where A=Availability, P=Performance, Q=Quality | % | World-class: ≥ 85% |
| **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{\text{Good Units at First Pass}}{\text{Total Units Started}} \times 100\%$ | % | Lean target: ≥ 99% |
| **Inventory Turns (IT)** | $\text{IT} = \dfrac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Average Inventory}}$ | turns/year | Lean: 10–100+ |
| **Sigma Level (Six Sigma)** | $\text{Defects} = 3.4 \text{ DPMO at } 6\sigma$ | DPMO | World-class benchmark |
| **OEE Loss** | $\text{Loss} = (1 - \text{OEE}) \times \text{Planned Production Time}$ | hours | Direct measure of waste |
| **Pull Loop Cardinality** | $\text{Kanban Count} = \dfrac{D \times L \times (1 + S)}{C}$ | cards | Where D=demand, L=lead time, S=safety, C=container |
| **Cycle Time Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{\sum t_{VA}}{T_{\text{cycle}}} \times 100\%$ | % | Higher = Leaner |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Pitfall:** When the question asks *"Compute the value-added ratio"*, ensure you write it as a *percentage* and use the **Total Lead Time** in the denominator, **not** just processing time.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Lean principles are not confined to automotive manufacturing. Application domains include:

- **Healthcare:** Reducing patient wait times, optimizing OR utilization (Virginia Mason Medical Center, USA).
- **Construction:** Lean construction (Last Planner System) — reduces rework and project delays.
- **Software Development:** Lean software development (Mary & Tom Poppendieck) — eliminates features not valued by users.
- **Aerospace:** Boeing, Airbus — flow-line assembly of fuselage sections.
- **Service Industries:** Banks, insurance, hospitality — eliminating customer waiting waste.

> [!NOTE]
> The **economic impact** of Lean is measured in terms of: **inventory reduction (20–50%)**, **lead time reduction (30–80%)**, **labor productivity gains (15–30%)**, and **defect reduction (50–90%)** as documented in benchmark studies by the Lean Enterprise Institute (LEI).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Takt Time Calculation

Takt time is the **heartbeat of Lean production** — it synchronizes production rate with customer demand.

### Given:
- Available Production Time per Day = $T_{\text{available}}$
- Customer Demand per Day = $D_{\text{customer}}$
- Number of Shifts per Day = $N_s$
- Shift Duration = $T_{\text{shift}}$
- Breaks/Downtime per Shift = $T_{\text{loss}}$

### Step 1: Compute Gross Available Time per Day

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{gross}} &= N_s \times T_{\text{shift}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** $T_{\text{gross}}$ is the total clock time the factory operates before subtracting planned losses.

---

### Step 2: Compute Net Available Production Time

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{available}} &= T_{\text{gross}} - (N_s \times T_{\text{loss}})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** $T_{\text{available}}$ excludes planned breaks, meetings, and scheduled maintenance — the actual time available for value-adding work.

---

### Step 3: Apply the Takt Time Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Takt Time} &= \frac{T_{\text{available}}}{D_{\text{customer}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Takt time is the maximum time allowed to produce one unit to meet customer demand. If a workstation's cycle time exceeds takt, it is a bottleneck.

---

### Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A company operates **2 shifts of 8 hours each**, with **45 minutes of break per shift**. Daily customer demand is **320 units**. Compute the takt time.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{gross}} &= 2 \times 8 \times 3600 = 57{,}600 \text{ seconds} \\
T_{\text{loss}} &= 2 \times 45 \times 60 = 5{,}400 \text{ seconds} \\
T_{\text{available}} &= 57{,}600 - 5{,}400 = 52{,}200 \text{ seconds} \\
\text{Takt} &= \frac{52{,}200}{320} = 163.125 \text{ seconds/unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** Takt Time = **163.13 seconds per unit**.

**Engineering Interpretation:** Each station in the line has approximately **2 minutes and 43 seconds** to complete its work; exceeding this means the station cannot keep up with demand.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Kanban (Number of Cards) Calculation

The **Kanban formula** determines how many signal cards (and thus how much WIP) are allowed in a pull loop.

### Given:
- Demand rate (units/time) = $D$
- Lead time to replenish one container = $L$
- Safety factor (fractional) = $S$
- Container capacity (units) = $C$

### Step 1: Express demand over lead time

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{over } L} &= D \times L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** This is the number of units the downstream process would consume during the lead time to replenish.

---

### Step 2: Add safety stock for variability

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{adjusted}} &= D \times L \times (1 + S)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** $S$ accounts for demand fluctuations, lead time variability, and minor process disruptions. Typical $S = 0.1$ to $0.3$.

---

### Step 3: Divide by container capacity

$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= \frac{D \times L \times (1 + S)}{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** $K$ must be an integer; **always round up** to ensure no stockout.

---

### Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Demand $D = 50$ units/hour, Lead Time $L = 2$ hours, Safety Factor $S = 0.2$, Container Capacity $C = 25$ units. Compute the number of Kanban cards.

$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= \frac{50 \times 2 \times (1 + 0.2)}{25} \\
K &= \frac{100 \times 1.2}{25} \\
K &= \frac{120}{25} \\
K &= 4.8 \rightarrow 5 \text{ cards (round up)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** **5 Kanban cards** required.

**Engineering Interpretation:** Maximum WIP allowed in the loop = $5 \times 25 = 125$ units. This caps inventory while ensuring demand coverage.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)

OEE is the **gold-standard Lean metric** for measuring the effectiveness of manufacturing equipment.

### Step 1: Availability (A)

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\text{Planned Production Time} - \text{Downtime}}{\text{Planned Production Time}} \times 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** $A$ measures the *uptime* of the equipment relative to its scheduled runtime.

---

### Step 2: Performance (P)

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces Produced}}{\text{Operating Time}} \times 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** $P$ captures the *speed loss* — running slower than the designed maximum rate.

---

### Step 3: Quality (Q)

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces Produced}} \times 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** $Q$ captures *quality loss* — defects, rework, and rejects.

---

### Step 4: Combine via Multiplication

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OEE} &= A \times P \times Q \quad (\text{all as decimals})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Note:** OEE is multiplicative, **not additive**, because the three losses are independent.

---

### Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Planned time = 480 min; Downtime = 60 min; Ideal cycle time = 1 min/unit; Total produced = 380 units; Good pieces = 360 units.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{480 - 60}{480} = 0.875 \\
P &= \frac{1 \times 380}{480 - 60} = \frac{380}{420} = 0.9048 \\
Q &= \frac{360}{380} = 0.9474 \\
\text{OEE} &= 0.875 \times 0.9048 \times 0.9474 = 0.7501 \\
\text{OEE} &= 75.01\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** This is *typical of discrete manufacturing* (60–85%). World-class OEE is **≥ 85%**. Lean interventions target the lowest of A, P, Q.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Lean vs. Mass Production

| Dimension | Mass Production | Lean Production |
|-----------|-----------------|-----------------|
| **Philosophy** | Push based on forecast | Pull based on actual demand |
| **Inventory** | High (weeks/months) | Low (hours/days) |
| **Lot Size** | Large batches | One-piece flow |
| **Lead Time** | Long & variable | Short & predictable |
| **Quality Approach** | Inspect at end (appraisal) | Build-in quality at source (Jidoka) |
| **Workforce Skill** | Narrow specialists | Multi-skilled operators |
| **Supplier Relations** | Adversarial (many bidders) | Partnership (few, long-term) |
| **Improvement** | Periodic, capital-intensive | Continuous, employee-driven (Kaizen) |
| **Layout** | Functional (departmentalized) | Cellular (product-oriented) |
| **Information Flow** | Vertical, hierarchical | Horizontal, transparent |
| **Defect Detection** | After the fact | Immediate (andon) |
| **Cost Driver** | Economies of scale | Elimination of waste |

---

## 3.5 Comparative Matrix: Value Stream Mapping Steps

| Step | Activity | Output |
|------|----------|--------|
| 1 | Identify product family | Product family matrix |
| 2 | Draw Current State Map | CSVSM with all process steps, inventories, information flows |
| 3 | Calculate cycle time & changeover for each step | CT and CO data for every box |
| 4 | Identify VA / NNVA / NVA | Waste classification matrix |
| 5 | Design Future State Map | FSVSM with continuous flow, pull, FIFO lanes |
| 6 | Develop implementation plan | Kaizen action register |
| 7 | Implement and iterate | Updated standard |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Lean House — The Toyota Production System (TPS) Architecture

The **TPS House** is the most widely taught Lean architecture. It depicts Lean as a "house" whose pillars and roof rest on a stable foundation. The diagram below is the **canonical representation** of Lean principles.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeF1[FOUNDATION: Stable Standardized Work + Heijunka Leveling]
    nodeF2[FOUNDATION: Just-in-Time JIT - Right item, Right quantity, Right time]
    nodeR[ROOF: Highest Quality, Lowest Cost, Shortest Lead Time]
    nodeP1[PILLAR 1: Jidoka - Autonomation, Build-in Quality]
    nodeP2[PILLAR 2: Just-in-Time - Pull System, Kanban, Continuous Flow]
    nodeG[Gemba: The Real Place - Where Value is Created]

    nodeF1 --> nodeR
    nodeF2 --> nodeR
    nodeP1 --> nodeR
    nodeP2 --> nodeR
    nodeG -.-> nodeP1
    nodeG -.-> nodeP2
    nodeF1 -.-> nodeP1
    nodeF1 -.-> nodeP2
    nodeF2 -.-> nodeP1
    nodeF2 -.-> nodeP2
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- **Roof (Top):** The customer-facing goals — best quality, lowest cost, shortest lead time.
- **Two Pillars:** *Jidoka* (automation with a human touch / quality at source) and *JIT* (pull-based flow).
- **Foundation:** Stable, standardized work + leveled production (Heijunka).
- **Gemba:** The "real place" — actual shop floor — where every Lean principle must be observed and applied.

---

## 4.2 The 5 Principles of Lean — Sequential Flow Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    node1[STEP 1: SPECIFY VALUE]
    node2[STEP 2: MAP VALUE STREAM]
    node3[STEP 3: CREATE FLOW]
    node4[STEP 4: ESTABLISH PULL]
    node5[STEP 5: PURSUE PERFECTION]

    node1[STEP 1 SPECIFY VALUE FROM CUSTOMER] --> node2[STEP 2 MAP THE VALUE STREAM CSVSM TO FSVSM]
    node2 --> node3[STEP 3 CREATE CONTINUOUS FLOW CELL LAYOUT ONE PIECE]
    node3 --> node4[STEP 4 ESTABLISH PULL KANBAN SUPERMARKET]
    node4 --> node5[STEP 5 PURSUE PERFECTION KAIZEN PDCA]
    node5 -.->|Continuous Loop| node1
```

**Diagram Interpretation:** This is a **closed-loop** model — once perfection is approached, the cycle restarts at value re-definition, ensuring continuous improvement. The dotted feedback arrow from Principle 5 to Principle 1 represents the **Kaizen loop**.

---

## 4.3 The 3Ms and 7+1 Wastes — Waste Elimination Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeW[ROOT: ANY WASTEFUL SYSTEM]
    nodeM1[MUDA - WASTE]
    nodeM2[MURA - UNEVENNESS]
    nodeM3[MURI - OVERBURDEN]

    nodeW1[Overproduction]
    nodeW2[Waiting]
    nodeW3[Transport]
    nodeW4[Over Processing]
    nodeW5[Inventory]
    nodeW6[Motion]
    nodeW7[Defects]
    nodeW8[Unused Talent]

    nodeR[ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS 5 WHYS]
    nodeS[STANDARDIZE PDCA]

    nodeW --> nodeM1
    nodeW --> nodeM2
    nodeW --> nodeM3

    nodeM1 --> nodeW1
    nodeM1 --> nodeW2
    nodeM1 --> nodeW3
    nodeM1 --> nodeW4
    nodeM1 --> nodeW5
    nodeM1 --> nodeW6
    nodeM1 --> nodeW7
    nodeM1 --> nodeW8

    nodeW1 --> nodeR
    nodeW2 --> nodeR
    nodeW3 --> nodeR
    nodeW4 --> nodeR
    nodeW5 --> nodeR
    nodeW6 --> nodeR
    nodeW7 --> nodeR
    nodeW8 --> nodeR
    nodeR --> nodeS
    nodeS -.->|Continuous| nodeR
```

**Diagram Interpretation:** Any wasteful system is rooted in **Muda, Mura, Muri**. The 7+1 wastes are concrete manifestations of *Muda*. Each waste undergoes **Root Cause Analysis (5 Whys)** and is then **standardized** via the PDCA cycle to prevent recurrence.

---

## 4.4 Push vs. Pull System Comparison — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph PUSH[TRADITIONAL PUSH SYSTEM]
        direction LR
        P1[Forecast Based] --> P2[Produce to Stock]
        P2 --> P3[Build Inventory]
        P3 --> P4[Long Lead Time]
        P4 --> P5[Hidden Problems]
        P5 -.->|Feedback Loop Slow| P1
    end

    subgraph PULL[LEAN PULL SYSTEM]
        direction LR
        Q1[Customer Order] --> Q2[Signal Kanban Card]
        Q2 --> Q3[Produce One Piece]
        Q3 --> Q4[Deliver Just in Time]
        Q4 --> Q5[Visible Problems Solved at Gemba]
        Q5 -.->|Feedback Loop Fast| Q1
    end

    PUSH -.->|Contrast| PULL
```

**Diagram Interpretation:** The Push system is a **slow-feedback** loop driven by speculation, while the Pull system is a **fast-feedback** loop driven by real demand. Lean engineers every step to expose problems, while Mass hides them under inventory buffers.

---

## 4.5 Value Stream Mapping Process Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Customer Order Received]
    nodeB[Current State Map CSVSM Data Collection]
    nodeC[Identify VA NNVA NVA Activities]
    nodeD[Future State Map FSVSM Design]
    nodeE[Implement Pull and Flow]
    nodeF[Standardize Kaizen PDCA Loop]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF -.->|Re-map Periodically| nodeB
```

**Diagram Interpretation:** This block architecture depicts the **VSM methodology as a cycle** — after implementation, the new "current state" must be re-mapped to identify further improvements, embodying Principle 5 (Perfection).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"Define Lean Manufacturing. List the seven wastes of Lean."** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Lean Manufacturing is a systematic production philosophy that seeks to deliver **maximum customer value** with **minimum waste** by continuously improving all processes that transform inputs into outputs.

> **The Seven Wastes (TIMWOOD / OTWO I-MD):**
> 1. **O**verproduction
> 2. **T**ransportation
> 3. **I**nventory
> 4. **M**otion
> 5. **W**aiting
> 6. **O**ver-processing
> 7. **D**efects

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"What is Muda, Mura, and Muri in Lean Manufacturing?"** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

> **Muda (無駄):** Any non-value-adding activity — waste of resources. Examples: defects, overproduction, waiting.
> **Mura (斑):** Unevenness in production — irregular demand or fluctuating workloads leading to peaks and troughs.
> **Muri (無理):** Overburden — excessive strain on workers or machines beyond their natural capacity, leading to breakdowns.

> Lean aims to eliminate all three to achieve a stable, efficient flow.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (ESE Module Choice: 14 Marks)

---

### 📝 Question A — Option A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — July 2024]*

**"Explain in detail the five principles of Lean Manufacturing as proposed by Womack and Jones. Discuss how each principle contributes to waste reduction and value creation in a manufacturing system."** *(14 Marks)*

#### Sub-part (a): Principles 1, 2, and 3 — Value, Value Stream, and Flow *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Principle 1 — Specify Value (3 Marks):**
Value is the starting point of Lean thinking. It must be defined from the **customer's perspective** — what the customer is genuinely willing to pay for, at a specific time and price. Value is **producer-independent**: the manufacturer cannot impose value; it is the customer's perception that counts.

For example, in a smartphone, the customer values call quality and battery life, not the thickness of the internal wiring. By precisely specifying value, the firm **eliminates over-processing and feature bloat** (waste #4).

Value specification requires **Voice of Customer (VOC)** tools — interviews, surveys, Kano Model, and Quality Function Deployment (QFD). The output is a clear **Value Statement** aligned with the target market segment and acceptable price point.

**2. Principle 2 — Identify the Value Stream (2 Marks):**
Once value is defined, the firm maps **every action** required to deliver that value — from raw material to customer delivery. This includes supplier processes, internal production steps, logistics, and after-sales service.

The Current State Value Stream Map (CSVSM) is drawn using **standardized VSM symbols**. The Future State Map (FSVSM) is then designed to:
- Eliminate non-value-added steps (pure waste),
- Compress lead time by removing queues,
- Introduce continuous flow where feasible,
- Establish pull where flow is not possible.

This step **directly attacks inventory, waiting, and transportation wastes**.

**3. Principle 3 — Create Flow (2 Marks):**
After redesigning the value stream, the firm arranges operations so that value-adding steps occur in a **continuous, uninterrupted sequence**. This often requires:
- **Cellular layout** (replacing functional departments with product-aligned cells),
- **Takt-time balancing**,
- **SMED** (Single-Minute Exchange of Dies) for quick changeovers,
- **One-piece flow** instead of large batches.

Flow exposes problems (defects, downtime, imbalances) that were previously hidden in inventory buffers, enabling **immediate problem-solving**.

#### Sub-part (b): Principles 4 and 5 — Pull and Perfection *(7 Marks)*

**4. Principle 4 — Establish Pull (3 Marks):**
A pull system ensures that **no upstream activity occurs without a downstream demand signal**. The most common pull mechanism is the **Kanban** — a card (or electronic signal) that authorizes production or movement of a fixed quantity.

Pull systems **eliminate overproduction** (the root cause of most waste) by aligning production tightly with consumption. They also **reduce inventory** (waste #5) because WIP is capped by the number of Kanban cards in circulation:

$$
K = \frac{D \times L \times (1+S)}{C}
$$

Pull systems may coexist with small, controlled "supermarkets" that act as decoupling buffers.

**5. Principle 5 — Pursue Perfection (4 Marks):**
Perfection is the **final and continuous principle**. It recognizes that Lean is not a one-time project but a **permanent cultural transformation**. Through repeated cycles of value redefinition, VSM, flow, and pull, the firm approaches the theoretical ideal of zero waste.

The mechanism is **Kaizen** — small, incremental, employee-driven improvements. Tools supporting Kaizen include:
- **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** cycle,
- **A3 problem-solving**,
- **5 Whys** root cause analysis,
- **Gemba Walks** (managers observe the real workplace),
- **Standardized Work** as the baseline for improvement.

Once an improvement is made, it becomes the new standard, and the cycle continues. This **creates a self-sustaining improvement engine** that compounds competitive advantage.

> **[End of Solution A — 14 Marks]**

---

### 📝 Question B — Option B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]*

**"Explain the seven wastes of Lean Manufacturing with examples. Also discuss the role of Value Stream Mapping (VSM) in identifying and eliminating waste."** *(14 Marks)*

#### Sub-part (a): The Seven Wastes with Manufacturing Examples *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Overproduction (2 Marks):**
Producing more than the customer requires or producing it earlier than needed.
*Example:* A stamping press producing 5,000 brackets when the next station only needs 800 per shift. Excess parts pile up as WIP, consume floor space, and may become obsolete if the design changes. **Overproduction is considered the *worst* waste because it directly generates inventory, waiting, and transportation wastes.**

**2. Waiting (1 Mark):**
Idle time when workers, machines, or materials are stalled.
*Example:* A CNC operator standing idle because the next blank has not arrived from the previous station due to a material handling delay.

**3. Transportation (1 Mark):**
Unnecessary movement of materials between processes.
*Example:* Moving parts from a centralized warehouse to the assembly line through 3 different forklifts, when a point-of-use rack would suffice.

**4. Over-processing (1 Mark):**
Performing work beyond customer requirements.
*Example:* Polishing a surface to a mirror finish when the customer's specification demands only a satin finish — extra time and consumables spent with no value gained.

**5. Inventory (1 Mark):**
Excess raw materials, WIP, or finished goods.
*Example:* Three months of safety stock of a slow-moving component tying up working capital and warehouse space.

**6. Motion (0.5 Mark):**
Unnecessary movement of people — walking, bending, reaching.
*Example:* An operator walking 8 meters to fetch a tool from a remote cabinet instead of using a shadow board at the workstation.

**7. Defects (0.5 Mark):**
Production of non-conforming parts requiring rework or scrap.
*Example:* Misaligned welds on a chassis causing 5% scrap rate — each scrap unit wastes all upstream materials, labor, and time.

#### Sub-part (b): Role of Value Stream Mapping in Waste Elimination *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Value Stream Mapping (VSM) is a **pen-and-paper (or digital) diagrammatic tool** that depicts every step — material and information — required to deliver a product or service, from supplier to customer.

**Steps of VSM (4 Marks):**
1. **Identify Product Family:** Select a product that follows similar processing steps.
2. **Walk the Gemba:** Physically observe and document each step, collecting data on cycle time, changeover time, uptime, batch size, and inventory levels.
3. **Draw the Current State Map (CSVSM):** Use standardized icons — process boxes, inventory triangles, customer/supplier icons, push/pull arrows, timeline.
4. **Analyze Waste:** For each step, classify it as VA, NNVA, or NVA; calculate lead time, value-added time, and value-added ratio (VAR).
5. **Design the Future State Map (FSVSM):** Eliminate NVA steps, combine NNVA steps, introduce cellular flow, and apply pull/Kanban.
6. **Implement and Standardize:** Execute the kaizen actions, then redraw.

**Role in Waste Identification (2 Marks):** VSM is unique because it reveals waste **across the entire value chain** — not just within a workstation. It exposes:
- **Inventory piles** between stations (waste #5),
- **Long changeover times** (causing batch production → overproduction #1),
- **Information delays** (causing waiting #2),
- **Layout inefficiencies** (causing transportation #3 and motion #6).

By quantifying Lead Time vs. Processing Time, VSM surfaces a striking gap — often the **processing time is less than 5% of total lead time**, vividly demonstrating the magnitude of waste.

> **[End of Solution B — 14 Marks]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks — Examiner Feedback (2019–2024):**
> 
> 1. **Missing the "Customer" focus:** Lean value is *always* customer-defined. Many students incorrectly describe Lean as "cost reduction" — this is a **consequence**, not the philosophy. Examiners deduct marks for this.
> 
> 2. **Confusing Lean Tools with Lean Principles:** Kanban, 5S, Poka-Yoke are **tools**. The five Womack-Jones principles are the **philosophy**. Examiners explicitly ask students to *distinguish*.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting Mura and Muri:** When asked about "waste," 90% of students list only Muda's 7 wastes. Examiners expect the **3Ms framework** in full.
> 
> 4. **Numerical Errors in Takt / Kanban:** Common mistakes: (i) forgetting to subtract breaks, (ii) not rounding Kanban count *up* to the nearest integer, (iii) confusing demand rate with demand volume.
> 
> 5. **No diagrams:** For 14-mark questions, students who draw the **TPS House** or **5-Principle Cycle** consistently score higher. Marks are awarded for visual clarity.
> 
> 6. **Vague Kaizen explanations:** "Continuous improvement" alone is worth only 1 mark. Examiners expect: *PDCA cycle + Standardized Work + 5 Whys + Gemba Walks*. Be specific.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Lean Manufacturing Principles:**

- ✅ **Lean = Maximize Value, Minimize Waste** — Customer-defined value is the starting point.
- ✅ **Origin:** Toyota Production System (TPS) — Taiichi Ohno, Eiji Toyoda; formalization by Womack & Jones (1996).
- ✅ **5 Principles (Womack & Jones):** Value → Value Stream → Flow → Pull → Perfection (continuous loop).
- ✅ **3Ms:** Muda (waste), Mura (unevenness), Muri (overburden).
- ✅ **7 Wastes (TIMWOOD):** Transportation, Inventory, Motion, Waiting, Overproduction, Over-processing, Defects (+ Unused Talent = 8th).
- ✅ **Overproduction = Root Waste** — it generates all other wastes.
- ✅ **Takt Time:** $\text{Takt} = \dfrac{\text{Available Time}}{\text{Demand Rate}}$ — synchronizes production with demand.
- ✅ **Kanban Formula:** $K = \dfrac{D \times L \times (1+S)}{C}$ — caps WIP and enables pull.
- ✅ **OEE = A × P × Q** — multiplicative; world-class ≥ 85%.
- ✅ **Value-Added Ratio (VAR):** $\text{VAR} = \dfrac{\sum t_{VA}}{\text{Total Lead Time}} \times 100\%$ — typically 5–25%, Lean target higher.
- ✅ **VSM:** Current State → Future State; tool that exposes end-to-end waste.
- ✅ **Kaizen:** Continuous improvement; PDCA + Standardized Work + 5 Whys.
- ✅ **Jidoka:** Build-in quality; stop the line when a defect is detected (Andon).
- ✅ **Pull ≠ No Inventory:** Pull caps inventory; some controlled buffers remain (supermarkets).
- ✅ **Gemba:** The real place where value is created — always go to the shop floor.
- ✅ **Lean is not a toolset; it is a philosophy + culture + management system.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Mantra:** *Value is defined by the customer. Waste is anything else. Eliminate it relentlessly, continuously, and respectfully.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Seven Wastes: Their Causes and Effects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET746 - LEAN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Seven Wastes: Their Causes and Effects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Waste (Muda)

In the context of the **Toyota Production System (TPS)** and Lean Manufacturing doctrine, **waste** is formally defined as any human activity that **absorbs resources but creates no value** for the end customer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Standard):**
> Waste (*Muda* in Japanese) is defined as "any activity that consumes resources without creating value for the customer." The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to identify, classify, and propose countermeasures for these wastes in any production or service environment.

The Japanese word **Muda (無駄)** literally translates to "futility, uselessness, or wastefulness." It was the central pillar of analysis introduced by **Taiichi Ohno**, the father of the Toyota Production System, who classified operational inefficiencies into the **Seven Wastes**, popularly remembered using the acronym **TIMWOOD**.

### 1.2 The Concept of Value vs. Non-Value-Added Activity

A foundational principle in Lean is distinguishing three types of activities:

| **Activity Type** | **Definition** | **Customer Pays For It?** |
|---|---|---|
| **Value-Added (VA)** | Transforms the product toward what the customer wants | YES |
| **Necessary Non-Value-Added (NNVA)** | Required due to current system limitations (e.g., inspection in a poor-quality system) | NO |
| **Waste (Muda)** | Purely unnecessary activity that should be eliminated immediately | NO |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition):**
> Imagine ordering a custom coffee at a cafe. You (the customer) only want the **espresso shot, steamed milk, and cup**. Everything else — the barista walking back and forth 10 times, the machine sitting idle while beans are searched for, the milk being poured twice because of a spill — is **waste**. From your perspective, none of these activities add flavor to your coffee. They just consume the barista's time, the cafe's electricity, and increase your waiting time. Lean Manufacturing's job is to systematically hunt down and eliminate each of these "invisible" inefficiencies so that you get your perfect coffee faster, cheaper, and with zero defects.

### 1.3 The TIMWOOD Framework — Overview

The **Seven Wastes** as codified in KTU's PEMET746 Module 1 syllabus are:

1. **T** – Transportation
2. **I** – Inventory
3. **M** – Motion
4. **W** – Waiting
5. **O** – Overproduction
6. **O** – Over-processing (Over-processing)
7. **D** – Defects

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Many textbook editions (notably Womack & Jones, *Lean Thinking*, 1996) propose an **8th Waste — Unused Human Talent (Unused Creativity)**. While not strictly part of the original Ohno framework, KTU expects familiarity with it. A standard 7-mark answer covering just the 7 wastes is fully acceptable; adding the 8th waste often yields bonus appreciation marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Taiichi Ohno's Classification Logic

Taiichi Ohno identified waste by observing the **flow of materials and information** through a production system. He asked one question repeatedly:

> *"Does this step bring the product closer to what the customer actually wants?"*

If the answer was **no**, that step was classified as muda. The seven categories emerged because, in practice, every non-value-adding activity in manufacturing could be bucketed into one of these seven patterns.

### 2.2 Detailed Breakdown of the Seven Wastes

#### 🔴 WASTE 1: TRANSPORTATION

**Definition:** Unnecessary movement of materials, parts, or information between processes.

| **Aspect** | **Detail** |
|---|---|
| **What it is** | Moving raw materials, WIP, or finished goods without adding value |
| **Typical Causes** | Poor facility layout, large batch sizes, centralized storage, multi-site operations |
| **Effects** | Increased lead time, higher fuel/handling cost, damage risk, delays |
| **Countermeasures** | Cell layout, point-of-use storage, U-shaped production lines, milk-run routing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** A component travels 500 meters across a factory floor between two adjacent operations simply because the machines are placed in functional departments (all milling machines together, all lathes together).

---

#### 🔴 WASTE 2: INVENTORY

**Definition:** Excess raw materials, work-in-progress (WIP), or finished goods beyond immediate need.

| **Aspect** | **Detail** |
|---|---|
| **What it is** | Stockpiling materials "just in case" |
| **Typical Causes** | Over-ordering, long setup times, poor demand forecasting, batch production |
| **Effects** | Tied-up capital, storage costs, obsolescence risk, hides other problems |
| **Countermeasures** | JIT (Just-in-Time), Kanban, smaller lot sizes, demand-pull systems |

> [!WARNING]
> **High inventory is often called "the root of all evil" in Lean** because it hides problems like machine breakdowns, quality defects, and supplier delays, preventing them from ever being solved.

---

#### 🔴 WASTE 3: MOTION

**Definition:** Unnecessary movement of *people* or equipment (distinct from transportation which moves *materials*).

| **Aspect** | **Detail** |
|---|---|
| **What it is** | Workers walking, bending, reaching, or searching unnecessarily |
| **Typical Causes** | Poor workstation ergonomics, misplaced tools, cluttered workspace |
| **Effects** | Worker fatigue, injury risk, lost time, reduced morale |
| **Countermeasures** | 5S workplace organization, ergonomic design, shadow boards, visual management |

---

#### 🔴 WASTE 4: WAITING

**Definition:** Idle time when people, machines, or information are not actively processing.

| **Aspect** | **Detail** |
|---|---|
| **What it is** | Operators standing, machines idle, approvals pending |
| **Typical Causes** | Unbalanced生产线, equipment breakdowns, late material delivery, decision delays |
| **Effects** | Lost capacity, extended lead time, idle labor cost |
| **Countermeasures** | Takt-time balancing, TPM (Total Productive Maintenance), Heijunka (level scheduling) |

---

#### 🔴 WASTE 5: OVERPRODUCTION

**Definition:** Producing more than the customer demands, or producing earlier than required.

| **Aspect** | **Detail** |
|---|---|
| **What it is** | The most serious waste according to Ohno himself |
| **Typical Causes** | Large batch production, poor demand signals, machine capacity imbalance |
| **Effects** | Excess inventory, obsolescence, hides defects, increased carrying cost |
| **Countermeasures** | Pull systems, Kanban, mixed-model production, smaller batches |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quote Worth Memorizing:**
> "Overproduction is the worst waste because it causes all the other wastes." — Taiichi Ohno

---

#### 🔴 WASTE 6: OVER-PROCESSING (OVER-PROCESSING)

**Definition:** Doing more work on a product than the customer values or is willing to pay for.

| **Aspect** | **Detail** |
|---|---|
| **What it is** | Excessive precision, redundant inspections, unnecessary features, over-engineering |
| **Typical Causes** | Unclear specifications, copying competitor specs, lack of customer communication |
| **Effects** | Wasted labor, excessive tool wear, longer cycle time |
| **Countermeasures** | Design for manufacturability (DFM), Voice of Customer (VOC) analysis, Six Sigma accuracy |

---

#### 🔴 WASTE 7: DEFECTS

**Definition:** Production of faulty products requiring rework, repair, or scrap.

| **Aspect** | **Detail** |
|---|---|
| **What it is** | Anything that fails to meet customer specifications |
| **Typical Causes** | Poor training, machine wear, inconsistent processes, weak supplier quality |
| **Effects** | Rework cost, scrap cost, warranty claims, customer dissatisfaction |
| **Countermeasures** | Poka-Yoke (mistake-proofing), Jidoka (autonomation), SPC, root-cause analysis (5-Why) |

---

#### 🟡 WASTE 8 (Supplementary): UNUSED TALENT / UNUSED CREATIVITY

**Definition:** Failing to utilize the skills, ideas, and improvement potential of employees.

| **Aspect** | **Detail** |
|---|---|
| **What it is** | Top-down management that ignores frontline worker insights |
| **Typical Causes** | Hierarchical culture, fear of failure, no suggestion system |
| **Effects** | Lost innovation, demotivated workforce, continuous improvement stalls |
| **Countermeasures** | Kaizen circles, suggestion programs, cross-functional teams, respect-for-people culture |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Metric Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Lean waste analysis is **qualitative by nature**, but the following quantitative metrics are **frequently tested in KTU ESE numericals and case studies.** All formulas are KTU-board-approved.

| **Metric** | **Formula / Definition** | **Standard Unit** |
|---|---|---|
| **Value-Added Ratio (VAR)** | $\text{VAR} = \dfrac{\text{Value-Added Time}}{\text{Total Lead Time}}$ | **Dimensionless** (0 to 1, often expressed as %) |
| **Lead Time (LT)** | $\text{LT} = \text{Processing Time} + \text{Queue Time} + \text{Transport Time} + \text{Waiting Time}$ | **Time units** (sec, min, hr, days) |
| **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{\text{Good Units at First Attempt}}{\text{Total Units Started}}$ | **%** (Dimensionless ratio) |
| **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{FPY}_i$ (product of station yields) | **%** |
| **Inventory Turns (IT)** | $\text{IT} = \dfrac{\text{Cost of Goods Sold}}{\text{Average Inventory Value}}$ | **Times per year** |
| **Takt Time (TT)** | $\text{TT} = \dfrac{\text{Available Production Time}}{\text{Customer Demand}}$ | **Time per unit** (sec/unit) |
| **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ where $A$ = Availability, $P$ = Performance, $Q$ = Quality | **%** (World-class $\geq$ **85%**) |
| **Defect Rate (DPMO)** | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Defects} \times 10^{6}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}}$ | **Defects per Million Opportunities** |
| **Motion Efficiency** | $\text{Eff} = \dfrac{\text{Value Motion Distance}}{\text{Total Motion Distance}}$ | **%** |
| **Seven Wastes Tag Count** | Manual tally of TIMWOOD occurrences on a VSM (Value Stream Map) | **Count (integer)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All table cells use LaTeX `\dfrac` (display-style fraction) instead of the `\frac` command to ensure perfect rendering in both KTU digital and print formats. Pipe symbols `$\vert$` are replaced with plain absolute-value notation where needed to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern Industry 4.0 environments, the seven wastes are mapped onto:
- **Digital twins** to simulate waste hotspots
- **IoT sensors** to capture waiting and defect data in real time
- **AI-driven predictive maintenance** to reduce machine waiting waste
- **ERP dashboards** to flag overproduction and excess inventory

Engineers equipped with TIMWOOD classification can lead **Kaizen events**, conduct **Gemba walks**, and develop **Value Stream Maps (VSMs)** — all core KTU Module 2 and Module 3 tools built directly on this foundation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — Waste Quantification (KTU Exam Standard)

> **Problem Statement (Simulated KTU 3-mark style):**
> A machining cell processes a component. The customer-relevant processing time is **8 minutes**. However, the component waits **22 minutes** in queue, travels **12 minutes** between stations, and undergoes **6 minutes** of inspection rework due to defects. Calculate:
> (a) Total Lead Time (LT)
> (b) Value-Added Ratio (VAR)
> (c) Identify which TIMWOOD waste categories are present and quantify each.

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Solution

#### **Step 1: Identify Time Components**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{value}} &= 8 \text{ minutes (only true processing)} \\
T_{\text{queue}} &= 22 \text{ minutes} \\
T_{\text{transport}} &= 12 \text{ minutes} \\
T_{\text{rework}} &= 6 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation: Each value is explicitly listed before any computation.*

---

#### **Step 2: Compute Total Lead Time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LT &= T_{\text{value}} + T_{\text{queue}} + T_{\text{transport}} + T_{\text{rework}} \\
LT &= 8 + 22 + 12 + 6 \\
LT &= 48 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation: Lead time is the sum of all time the unit spends in the system, value-added or not.*

---

#### **Step 3: Compute Value-Added Ratio (VAR)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{VAR} &= \dfrac{T_{\text{value}}}{LT} \\
\text{VAR} &= \dfrac{8}{48} \\
\text{VAR} &= 0.1667 \\
\text{VAR} &\approx 16.67\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation: Only ~17% of the customer's perceived lead time is actual value creation. The remaining **83.3% is muda**.*

---

#### **Step 4: Map Each Non-Value Time to a TIMWOOD Waste**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{queue}} = 22 \text{ min} &\longrightarrow \textbf{WAITING} \quad (\text{W in TIMWOOD}) \\
T_{\text{transport}} = 12 \text{ min} &\longrightarrow \textbf{TRANSPORTATION} \quad (\text{T in TIMWOOD}) \\
T_{\text{rework}} = 6 \text{ min} &\longrightarrow \textbf{DEFECTS} \quad (\text{D in TIMWOOD})
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation: Queue time is waiting; transport time is transportation waste; rework time is direct evidence of defect waste.*

---

#### **Step 5: Compute Waste Percentage**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Waste \%} &= (1 - \text{VAR}) \times 100 \\
&= (1 - 0.1667) \times 100 \\
&= 83.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation: Over four-fifths of the system time is pure waste — a typical Lean improvement target.*

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Cause & Effect Summary

| **Waste** | **Root Causes (典型原因)** | **Direct Effects (直接结果)** | **Secondary Effects** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Transportation** | Poor layout, large batches, distant warehouses | Damaged goods, fuel cost, longer lead time | Reduced delivery reliability |
| **Inventory** | Push systems, long setups, poor forecasting | Capital tied up, storage cost, obsolescence | Hides defects and breakdowns |
| **Motion** | Disorganized workplace, poor ergonomics | Worker fatigue, injury, slow cycle | Low morale, absenteeism |
| **Waiting** | Unbalanced lines, breakdowns, delays | Idle labor/machines, missed deliveries | Overtime costs, customer churn |
| **Overproduction** | Large batches, push systems, fear of downtime | Excess stock, long lead time | Hides all other wastes |
| **Over-processing** | Over-specification, redundant QC | Wasted labor, excess consumables | Customer doesn't value the extras |
| **Defects** | Process variation, poor training | Rework cost, scrap, warranty claims | Reputation damage, recalls |
| **Unused Talent** *(8th)* | Hierarchical culture, no listening systems | Lost Kaizen ideas, disengagement | Stagnation, high attrition |

### 3.4 Quantitative Waste Identification — Derivation Logic

Lean practitioners use the following decision-tree logic to classify any observed inefficiency:

$$
\text{Is the activity transforming the product?} \rightarrow \text{No} \rightarrow \text{Is it moving materials?} \rightarrow \text{Yes} \rightarrow \textbf{Transportation}
$$

$$
\text{No transformation?} \rightarrow \text{No material movement?} \rightarrow \text{Is it idle?} \rightarrow \text{Yes} \rightarrow \textbf{Waiting}
$$

$$
\text{Is output greater than demand or earlier than required?} \rightarrow \text{Yes} \rightarrow \textbf{Overproduction}
$$

This systematic classification ensures no muda slips through analysis — a key KTU examiner expectation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 TIMWOOD Classification Flow Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Observed Activity in Production] --> B{Does it add Customer Value?}
    B -- No --> C{Does it move material or info?}
    B -- Yes --> Z[Value-Added Activity - Keep]
    C -- Yes --> D[Transportation Waste]
    C -- No --> E{Does it stockpile material?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Inventory Waste]
    E -- No --> G{Is it moving people/equipment unnecessarily?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Motion Waste]
    G -- No --> I{Is resource idle?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Waiting Waste]
    I -- No --> K{Output exceeds demand or too early?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Overproduction Waste]
    K -- No --> M{Work exceeds customer spec?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Over-processing Waste]
    M -- No --> O{Output fails customer spec?}
    O -- Yes --> P[Defects Waste]
    O -- No --> Q[Unused Talent or Hidden Muda]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Waste Elimination System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Gemba Walk Observation] --> B[Identify Muda Activity]
    B --> C[TIMWOOD Classification Engine]
    C --> D1[Transportation Countermeasure]
    C --> D2[Inventory Countermeasure]
    C --> D3[Motion Countermeasure]
    C --> D4[Waiting Countermeasure]
    C --> D5[Overproduction Countermeasure]
    C --> D6[Over-processing Countermeasure]
    C --> D7[Defects Countermeasure]
    D1 --> E[5S Implementation]
    D2 --> F[Kanban Pull System]
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> G[Heijunka Line Balancing]
    D5 --> F
    D6 --> H[DFM and VOC Analysis]
    D7 --> I[Poka-Yoke and Jidoka]
    E --> J[Standardized Work]
    F --> J
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Continuous Improvement Kaizen]
```

### 4.3 Cause-Effect Fishbone Style Mapping (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Defect Waste in Machining] --> C1[Cause - Machine wear]
    P --> C2[Cause - Operator skill gap]
    P --> C3[Cause - Poor material quality]
    P --> C4[Cause - Inconsistent process parameters]
    P --> C5[Cause - Weak inspection feedback loop]
    C1 --> E1[Effect - Scrap cost up]
    C2 --> E2[Effect - Rework time up]
    C3 --> E3[Effect - Customer returns up]
    C4 --> E4[Effect - OEE down]
    C5 --> E5[Effect - Lead time extended]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked to "list causes and effects of any one waste" in a 7-mark question, presenting a structured Mermaid-style or tabular fishbone format earns **full method marks** even if some causes overlap — clarity is the highest-weighted skill in KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 1]**
Define the term "Muda" in Lean Manufacturing. Briefly explain why overproduction is considered the worst waste.

**Model Answer:**
Muda is a Japanese term meaning "waste" — any activity that consumes resources (time, labor, materials, space) without adding value from the customer's perspective. Taiichi Ohno identified seven categories, popularly known by the acronym TIMWOOD (Transportation, Inventory, Motion, Waiting, Overproduction, Over-processing, Defects).

Overproduction is considered the worst waste because it directly causes and amplifies all the other wastes. When excess units are produced, they create excess **inventory**, require additional **transportation**, often require extra **processing**, and they hide **defects** beneath the stockpile. It also forces operators to perform unnecessary **motion** and causes **waiting** in subsequent downstream processes. Thus, eliminating overproduction eliminates the root of many secondary wastes.

*(Marks distribution: Definition – 1 mark; Reason with logical linkage – 2 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
Distinguish between **Transportation** and **Motion** waste with one industrial example each.

**Model Answer:**

| **Basis** | **Transportation** | **Motion** |
|---|---|---|
| **What moves** | Materials, parts, or information | People, tools, or equipment |
| **Added value** | None | None |
| **Root cause** | Poor facility layout, distant storage | Poor workstation ergonomics |
| **Example** | A component being forklifted 200 m between two adjacent shops | An operator walking 15 m to fetch a tool placed far from the workstation |
| **Countermeasure** | Cellular layout, milk-run delivery | 5S workplace organization, shadow boards |

*(Marks distribution: Conceptual distinction – 2 marks; Example – 1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 1]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Seven Wastes (TIMWOOD)** of Lean Manufacturing, including the causes and effects of each waste. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A car assembly line has the following data per vehicle: Processing time = 6 hours; Queue/Waiting time = 18 hours; Internal transport time = 4 hours; Rework time due to defects = 2 hours. Compute:
(i) Total Lead Time
(ii) Value-Added Ratio (VAR)
(iii) Percentage of total time that is waste
(iv) Identify which TIMWOOD wastes are present and the time associated with each. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) The Seven Wastes (TIMWOOD) — Detailed Explanation**

The seven wastes, identified by Taiichi Ohno, are universally applied in Lean Manufacturing to expose non-value-adding activities:

1. **Transportation:** Unnecessary movement of materials or products. *Causes:* poor layout, large batches. *Effects:* damage, delay, fuel cost.

2. **Inventory:** Excess raw materials, WIP, or finished goods. *Causes:* poor forecasting, push systems. *Effects:* tied-up capital, storage cost, hides defects.

3. **Motion:** Unnecessary movement of workers. *Causes:* poor ergonomics, disorganized workplace. *Effects:* fatigue, injury, lost time.

4. **Waiting:** Idle time for people or machines. *Causes:* unbalanced lines, breakdowns. *Effects:* idle capacity, missed deadlines.

5. **Overproduction:** Producing more or sooner than needed. *Causes:* batch production, push planning. *Effects:* creates all other wastes.

6. **Over-processing:** Doing more work than customer values. *Causes:* over-specification, redundant inspection. *Effects:* wasted labor, excess wear.

7. **Defects:** Production errors needing rework or scrap. *Causes:* process variation, training gaps. *Effects:* rework cost, customer dissatisfaction.

*(Marks: 1 mark per waste identified correctly with cause-effect linkage ≈ 7 marks)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution**

**Given:**
$T_{\text{process}} = 6$ hr, $T_{\text{queue}} = 18$ hr, $T_{\text{transport}} = 4$ hr, $T_{\text{rework}} = 2$ hr

**(i) Total Lead Time:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
LT &= T_{\text{process}} + T_{\text{queue}} + T_{\text{transport}} + T_{\text{rework}} \\
LT &= 6 + 18 + 4 + 2 \\
LT &= 30 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Value-Added Ratio:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{VAR} &= \dfrac{T_{\text{process}}}{LT} = \dfrac{6}{30} = 0.20 = 20\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Waste Percentage:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Waste \%} &= (1 - 0.20) \times 100 = 80\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Waste Identification:**
- Queue time (18 hr) → **Waiting**
- Transport time (4 hr) → **Transportation**
- Rework time (2 hr) → **Defects**

*(Marks: (i) 2 marks; (ii) 2 marks; (iii) 1 mark; (iv) 2 marks)*

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative to Question A)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**

**(a)** With neat illustrative examples, discuss the **causes and effects of Inventory, Waiting, and Overproduction wastes** in a manufacturing organization. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A packaging station produces 480 units per 8-hour shift. The customer demand is 400 units/shift. Due to machine breakdowns, the station produces at 75% performance efficiency. Defect rate is 4%. Calculate:
(i) Takt Time
(ii) Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) assuming 90% availability and given performance and quality figures
(iii) Suggest two Lean countermeasures for the lowest-performing waste identified. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Causes and Effects — Inventory, Waiting, Overproduction**

- **Inventory Waste:** Caused by over-ordering, long setup times, push-based MRP systems, and poor demand forecasting. Effects include tied-up working capital, increased storage and insurance costs, material obsolescence, and most critically — *inventory hides other wastes* like defects and machine breakdowns, preventing their visibility and resolution.

- **Waiting Waste:** Caused by unbalanced production lines, frequent machine breakdowns, late raw material deliveries, and approval bottlenecks. Effects include idle operators, missed production targets, increased overtime costs, and delayed customer deliveries. Waiting reduces throughput without any benefit to the product.

- **Overproduction Waste:** Caused by batch-and-queue production, push planning based on forecasts, and machine capacity imbalances that force large production runs. Effects include the creation of excess inventory, increased lead time, accelerated material obsolescence, and the concealment of defects. Ohno famously called overproduction "the root of all evil" because it directly generates every other waste.

*(Marks: ~2.3 marks per waste, allocated across causes and effects)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution**

**Given:**
- Production = 480 units/shift
- Demand = 400 units/shift
- Performance efficiency = 75%
- Defect rate = 4%
- Availability = 90%
- Shift time = 8 hours = 480 minutes

**(i) Takt Time:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{available}} &= 8 \text{ hr} \times 3600 \text{ s/hr} = 28800 \text{ seconds} \\
T_{\text{takt}} &= \dfrac{T_{\text{available}}}{\text{Demand}} = \dfrac{28800}{400} \\
T_{\text{takt}} &= 72 \text{ seconds per unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) OEE Calculation:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 0.90 \\
P &= 0.75 \\
Q &= 1 - 0.04 = 0.96 \\
\text{OEE} &= A \times P \times Q \\
\text{OEE} &= 0.90 \times 0.75 \times 0.96 \\
\text{OEE} &= 0.648 \\
\text{OEE} &= 64.8\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*World-class OEE is 85%+, so this line is significantly underperforming.*

**(iii) Countermeasures for Lowest OEE Factor:**
The lowest factor is **Performance (75%)**, which points to **Waiting** and minor **Motion** waste (machines idling, slow changeovers).
- Implement **SMED** (Single-Minute Exchange of Die) to reduce setup/changeover time.
- Apply **TPM (Total Productive Maintenance)** to eliminate minor stoppages and breakdowns.
- Use **Heijunka** (level scheduling) to balance the line against takt time.

*(Marks: (i) 2 marks; (ii) 2 marks; (iii) 3 marks — including countermeasure justification)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Transportation with Motion:** Transport = materials move; Motion = people/equipment move. Mixing these two in a 7-mark answer costs at least 2 marks.
> 2. **Treating all 7 wastes equally in numerical problems:** When asked to compute VAR, ensure only **true processing time** (transforming the product to customer spec) is counted as value-added. Inspection and transport are NOT value-added.
> 3. **Forgetting the 8th Waste:** In questions that say "discuss the seven wastes," strictly seven is sufficient. But in essay-type 14-mark questions, mentioning Unused Talent demonstrates deeper understanding and often earns appreciation marks.
> 4. **OEE Formula Errors:** Students frequently compute OEE = A + P + Q (addition) instead of A × P × Q (multiplication). KTU strictly uses the multiplicative form.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Seven Wastes (TIMWOOD)**

- ⭐ **Muda** = any activity consuming resources without adding customer value.
- ⭐ **TIMWOOD** acronym: **T**ransportation, **I**nventory, **M**otion, **W**aiting, **O**verproduction, **O**ver-processing, **D**efects.
- ⭐ Transport vs Motion distinction is critical: *Things move* vs *People move*.
- ⭐ **Overproduction is the worst waste** — it births all other wastes.
- ⭐ **Inventory hides problems** — never a solution, always a symptom.
- ⭐ **Waiting + Motion wastes** = primary targets of 5S and TPM.
- ⭐ **8th Waste** (bonus): Unused Talent / Unused Creativity.
- ⭐ **Value-Added Ratio (VAR)** = Value-Added Time ÷ Total Lead Time.
- ⭐ **OEE** = Availability × Performance × Quality (multiplicative).
- ⭐ **Takt Time** = Available Time ÷ Customer Demand.
- ⭐ **First Pass Yield (FPY)** measures Defect waste at each station.
- ⭐ Lean tools that directly attack TIMWOOD: **5S, Kanban, Poka-Yoke, Jidoka, Heijunka, TPM, Kaizen, VSM, SMED.**
- ⭐ For any KTU 14-mark question, always present causes + effects in a **structured table** for clarity and full marks.
- ⭐ For numericals, **explicitly show every algebraic step** — KTU examiners award step marks generously but penalize skipped logic heavily.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conventional Manufacturing versus Lean Manufacturing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET746 - LEAN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Conventional Manufacturing versus Lean Manufacturing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Conventional Manufacturing (Traditional Manufacturing)

**Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
Conventional Manufacturing, also known as **Traditional Manufacturing** or **Mass Production**, is an operationally driven production philosophy based on the principle of **economies of scale**, functional departmentalization, and **forecast-driven (push) production scheduling**. It was formalized by **Henry Ford** in the early 20th century and dominated global industry until the late 1970s.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conventional Manufacturing Core Tenet:** *"Make as much as cheaply as possible and push it into the market, hoping demand absorbs it."* — This is the textbook definition of a **PUSH system**.

#### Key Operational Features
- Large **batch production** to minimize per-unit cost.
- High **Work-In-Progress (WIP)** inventory buffers between stations.
- Functional layout (process-based, not product-based).
- Quality inspected **at the end** of the line (inspection-based QC).
- Long **lead times** and **cycle times**.
- Reactive defect detection (rework culture).

---

### 1.2 Lean Manufacturing

**Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
Lean Manufacturing is a **systematic, demand-driven production philosophy** that aims to **maximize customer value while minimizing waste (Muda)**. It was developed by **Taiichi Ohno** at the **Toyota Motor Corporation** post-WWII and is formally known as the **Toyota Production System (TPS)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lean Manufacturing Core Tenet:** *"Produce only what is needed, only when it is needed, and in the exact quantity needed — pulled by real customer demand (Jidoka + Just-in-Time)."*

#### Key Operational Features
- Small **lot / single-piece flow** production.
- Minimal **WIP inventory** (ideally zero).
- Cellular / product-based layout.
- Built-in quality (**Jidoka** – automation with a human touch).
- Short **lead times** and **Takt-time** synchronization.
- Proactive defect prevention (Poka-Yoke, Kaizen).

---

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

**The Restaurant Analogy (Classic Toyota Example):**

Imagine two restaurants serving the same dish:

- **Conventional (Fast-Food Chain Model):** The cook pre-prepares **500 burgers** before lunch rush, stores them under heat lamps, and pushes them out. Most get cold, are thrown away, or have stale buns. The customer waits for what was *already made*.

- **Lean (Sushi Train Model):** The chef makes **one sushi roll only after the customer orders it**. The plate is placed on the conveyor only when the bell rings (Kanban signal). Freshness guaranteed. Zero leftovers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Analogy Works:** The first restaurant represents **forecast-driven push** with overproduction waste; the second represents **demand-driven pull** — the exact philosophical shift Lean brings to manufacturing floors.

### 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (Industry-Accepted)

| Metric | Conventional Target | Lean Target |
|---|---|---|
| **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** | ~60% | ≥ **85%** (World Class) |
| **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | ~85–90% | ≥ **99%** |
| **Inventory Turns / year** | 4–6 | ≥ **12** (Toyota: 30+) |
| **Lead Time** | Weeks to Months | Hours to Days |
| **Defect Rate (PPM)** | 5,000–10,000 | < **100** |
| **Floor Space Utilization** | 100% (cluttered) | ~**60%** (organized cells) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Sheet

### 2.1 Conceptual Roots & Historical Lineage

Lean did not emerge in a vacuum. It is the synthesis of three pioneering thinkers:

1. **Henry Ford (1913)** — *Flow Production / Assembly Line.* Introduced standardization, interchangeable parts, and continuous flow. However, Ford's model was **rigid, single-product, and lacked variety** (Model T: "Any color, as long as it's black").
2. **Taiichi Ohno (1948–1975)** — *Toyota Production System.* Adapted Fordism for a **multi-product, low-volume** environment by adding the *pull mechanism (Kanban)* and *autonomation (Jidoka)*.
3. **W. Edwards Deming & Joseph Juran (1950s)** — *Quality Philosophy.* Influenced Toyota's statistical process control, PDCA (Deming) cycle, and quality-at-source mindset.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favorite Question:** *"Lean Manufacturing evolved from which production system?"* → **Answer:** Toyota Production System (TPS), developed by Taiichi Ohno.

---

### 2.2 The Seven Wastes (Muda) — TIMWOOD Framework

Lean identifies **seven classic wastes** that conventional manufacturing tolerates:

| Symbol | Waste (English) | Waste (Japanese) | Conventional Reality | Lean Fix |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **T** | **T**ransportation | Unnecessary movement of materials | Cross-floor forklift runs | Cell layout |
| **I** | **I**nventory | Excess stock | Warehouses full of WIP | Pull / Kanban |
| **M** | **M**otion | Unnecessary worker movement | Walking 5 km per shift | 5S workplace org |
| **W** | **W**aiting | Idle time | Machine-down, queue | Takt-time balance |
| **O** | **O**verproduction | Making too much | Build-to-stock | Build-to-order |
| **O** | **O**ver-processing | Extra work not valued | Re-polishing, redundant QC | Value-stream analysis |
| **D** | **D**efects | Rework & scrap | End-of-line inspection | Poka-Yoke, Jidoka |

> [!NOTE]
> **Extended 8th Waste (Modern Lean):** **Underutilized Skills (Talent)** — not leveraging human creativity and knowledge. Added by practitioners post-1990s.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Conventional Manufacturing | Lean Manufacturing |
|---|---|---|
| **Production Driver** | Forecast / Schedule (Push) | Real Customer Demand (Pull) |
| **Batch Size** | Large (1000s) | Small (1-piece flow ideal) |
| **Inventory Philosophy** | Buffer stock is an *asset* | Inventory is a *liability / waste* |
| **Quality Approach** | Inspection at the end | Built-in quality (Jidoka) |
| **Layout** | Functional (by process) | Cellular (by product family) |
| **Lead Time** | Long (weeks–months) | Short (hours–days) |
| **Cycle Time vs. Takt Time** | Imbalanced | Balanced to Takt |
| **Workforce Role** | Specialized, single-task | Multi-skilled, cross-trained |
| **Supplier Relationship** | Adversarial, price-based | Partnership, long-term |
| **Improvement Philosophy** | Reactive (fix-when-broken) | Proactive (Kaizen) |
| **Setup Time** | Long (SMED not applied) | Reduced via **SMED** (<10 min) |
| **Management Style** | Top-down, autocratic | Bottom-up empowerment (Genchi Genbutsu) |
| **Cost Reduction** | Headcount reduction / mass layoffs | Waste elimination / value creation |
| **Flexibility** | Low (rigid product mix) | High (mix-model capable) |
| **Origin / Founder** | Henry Ford (USA, 1913) | Taiichi Ohno (Japan, 1970s) |

---

### 2.4 Key Lean Performance Formulas

The **Value-Added Ratio (VAR)** is the single most important KTU exam formula:

$$
\text{VAR} = \frac{\text{Value-Added Time}}{\text{Total Lead Time}} \times 100\%
$$

Where:
- **Value-Added Time** = time the part is actually being transformed (machining, welding).
- **Total Lead Time** = order placement to delivery (includes waiting, transport, inspection).

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Benchmark:** Conventional manufacturing typically operates at **VAR < 5%**, while mature Lean systems achieve **VAR ≥ 25%**.

The **Takt Time** formula governs pace synchronization:

$$
T_{\text{takt}} = \frac{\text{Available Production Time per Shift}}{\text{Customer Demand per Shift}}
$$

**Example (Board-style):** If a shift has **480 minutes** available and customer demand is **120 units/day**:

$$
T_{\text{takt}} = \frac{480 \text{ min}}{120 \text{ units}} = 4 \text{ min/unit}
$$

This means **each unit must be completed every 4 minutes** to match demand exactly.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Utility & Engineering Relevance

- **Automotive:** Toyota's lean transformation saved it from bankruptcy post-WWII and made it the world's most profitable automaker.
- **Healthcare:** Lean Six Sigma is used to reduce ER wait times (Virginia Mason, Seattle).
- **Software:** Lean principles underpin *Agile* and *DevOps* methodologies.
- **Aerospace:** Boeing uses lean to reduce aircraft assembly cycle time by ~40%.
- **KTU Relevance:** Every industrial engineer, mechanical engineer, and production manager encounters these philosophies in *Industrial Management*, *Operations Research*, and *Supply Chain* electives.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivation & Comparative Logic

### 3.1 Derivation: Lead-Time Reduction Logic (Push → Pull)

Let us mathematically derive how a conventional **batch-and-queue** system inflates lead time compared to a Lean **continuous-flow** system.

#### Step 1: Define Total Lead Time
Total Lead Time is the sum of three components:

$$
LT_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{process}} + T_{\text{queue}} + T_{\text{transport}}
$$

Where:
- $T_{\text{process}}$ = actual value-adding transformation time.
- $T_{\text{queue}}$ = waiting time between stations (WIP idle).
- $T_{\text{transport}}$ = movement time between departments.

#### Step 2: Model Conventional Batch-Queue Behavior (King's Formula)
For a batch of size $N$ processed on a single machine:

$$
LT_{\text{conv}} = N \times t_{\text{process}} + (N-1) \times t_{\text{queue}}
$$

Each subsequent unit waits $(N-1)$ queue slots behind the prior unit.

#### Step 3: Model Lean One-Piece Flow Behavior
In a Lean cell, $N=1$ (single-piece flow):

$$
LT_{\text{lean}} = (1) \times t_{\text{process}} + (1-1) \times t_{\text{queue}} = t_{\text{process}}
$$

Queue time drops to **zero** because there is no waiting behind a batch.

#### Step 4: Numerical Evaluation (Board Example)

**Given:**
- Single operation time $t_{\text{process}} = 5$ min/unit
- Average queue wait $t_{\text{queue}} = 10$ min/unit
- Conventional batch size $N = 50$ units

**Conventional System:**

$$
LT_{\text{conv}} = (50)(5) + (49)(10)
$$

$$
LT_{\text{conv}} = 250 + 490
$$

$$
LT_{\text{conv}} = 740 \text{ min/unit} \approx 12.33 \text{ hours per unit}
$$

**Lean System:**

$$
LT_{\text{lean}} = (1)(5) + (0)(10) = 5 \text{ min/unit}
$$

**Reduction Ratio:**

$$
R = \frac{LT_{\text{conv}} - LT_{\text{lean}}}{LT_{\text{conv}}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
R = \frac{740 - 5}{740} \times 100\%
$$

$$
R = \frac{735}{740} \times 100\% \approx 99.32\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion of Derivation:** By switching from a 50-unit batch to one-piece flow, **lead time reduces by 99.32%** — demonstrating why conventional systems inherently hide waste in queues.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Logic Chain: Why Conventional Systems Persist

Despite inefficiencies, conventional manufacturing is still widely used. The **root-cause logic** is as follows:

1. **High set-up costs** → Managers produce large batches to amortize setup.
2. **Long set-ups** → Encourages bigger batches (vicious cycle).
3. **Big batches** → Create inventory piles.
4. **Inventory piles** → Hide process problems (mura, muri).
5. **Hidden problems** → No incentive to fix root causes.
6. **No root-cause fixes** → Setups remain long (vicious cycle continues).

> This is **Ohno's "Catch-22"** of conventional manufacturing. Lean's solution is **SMED (Single-Minute Exchange of Dies)** to break the cycle by collapsing set-up time to under 10 minutes.

---

### 3.3 Decision Matrix: When to Use Which System?

| Scenario | Recommended System | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Extremely high-volume, single product (e.g., bottled water) | **Conventional** acceptable | Variety loss minimal; flow economics dominant |
| High variety, low volume (e.g., custom machinery) | **Lean** essential | Changeovers frequent; one-piece flow reduces WIP |
| Mature supply chain with reliable demand forecast | **Conventional** tolerable | Forecast accuracy buffers push failures |
| Volatile demand (e.g., fashion, electronics) | **Lean** mandatory | Inventory risk too high in push |
| Tight labor market, multi-skilled workforce | **Lean** advantageous | Empower workforce; reduce headcount via waste removal |
| Capital-intensive, low-margin commodity | **Conventional + Lean hybrid** | Use lean for changeover, conventional for run-rate |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Comparison: Push vs Pull Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Forecast] --> B1[Production Schedule]
    B1 --> C1[Workstation A Push]
    C1 --> D1[Workstation B Push]
    D1 --> E1[Workstation C Push]
    E1 --> F1[Finished Goods Warehouse]
    F1 --> G1[Market Hopes to Absorb]

    style A1 fill:#ffcccc
    style F1 fill:#ff9999
    style G1 fill:#ff6666,color:#fff
```

**Block-Level Architecture: Conventional Push System**

| Block | Function | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| Forecast Module | Demand prediction | Drives entire chain |
| Production Schedule | Batch planning | Large lot sizing |
| Workstation A/B/C | Sequential push | WIP accumulates |
| Finished Warehouse | Inventory buffer | Holds risk |
| Market Outlet | End consumer | Reactive absorber |

---

### 4.2 Lean Pull Architecture with Kanban Trigger

```mermaid
flowchart RL
    M1[Customer Order Received] --> M2[Final Assembly Pull Signal Kanban]
    M2 --> M3[Sub-Assembly Station Pull]
    M3 --> M4[Component Fabrication Pull]
    M4 --> M5[Supplier Delivery Just In Time]
    M5 --> M4
    M4 --> M3
    M3 --> M2
    M2 --> M1

    style M1 fill:#ccffcc
    style M2 fill:#99ff99
    style M3 fill:#99ff99
    style M4 fill:#99ff99
    style M5 fill:#66cc66,color:#fff
```

**Block-Level Architecture: Lean Pull System**

| Block | Function | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| Customer Order | Demand trigger | Initiates upstream signal |
| Kanban Card | Visual pull signal | Authorises production of N units |
| Downstream Station | Customer | Only consumes what is needed |
| Upstream Station | Supplier | Produces only to replenish consumed kanban |
| Supermarket | Buffer (small) | Limited safety stock |

---

### 4.3 Waste Identification Workflow (7-Muda Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start Value Stream Map VSM] --> P2[Identify Each Process Step]
    P2 --> P3{Does Step Add Customer Value?}
    P3 -- Yes --> P4[Retain as Value Added]
    P3 -- No --> P5[Categorise Waste Type]
    P5 --> P6{Which Category?}
    P6 -- Inventory --> W1[I]
    P6 -- Waiting --> W2[W]
    P6 -- Transport --> W3[T]
    P6 -- Motion --> W4[M]
    P6 -- Overproduction --> W5[O]
    P6 -- Overprocessing --> W6[O]
    P6 -- Defects --> W7[D]
    W1 --> P7[Apply Countermeasure Kaizen]
    W2 --> P7
    W3 --> P7
    W4 --> P7
    W5 --> P7
    W6 --> P7
    W7 --> P7
    P7 --> P1

    style P1 fill:#cce5ff
    style P4 fill:#ccffcc
    style P7 fill:#ffcc99
```

---

### 4.4 Conventional vs Lean – Strategic Choice Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Product Variety Level] --> Q2{High Mix or Low Mix}
    Q2 -- High Mix --> Q3[Lean Pull Preferred]
    Q2 -- Low Mix --> Q4[Volume per SKU]
    Q4 -- Very High Volume --> Q5[Conventional Mass Production Tolerable]
    Q4 -- Moderate Volume --> Q6[Hybrid Lean Conversion Line]
    Q3 --> R1[Apply TPS Jidoka Kanban]
    Q5 --> R2[Apply Ford Flow Production]
    Q6 --> R3[Apply SMED plus Cellular Layout]

```
