<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Lean Manufacturing. List any **three** major wastes it aims to eliminate.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (1 Mark):** Lean Manufacturing is a systematic production philosophy, derived from the Toyota Production System (TPS), that aims to **maximize customer value** while **minimizing waste (Muda)** across the entire value stream. It is built on the twin pillars of **Just-in-Time (JIT)** and **Jidoka (Autonomation)**.

**Three Major Wastes (1 Mark each):**

| # | Waste | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Overproduction** | Producing more, sooner, or faster than the next process or customer requires — the root waste that creates all others. |
| 2 | **Waiting** | Idle time of workers, machines, or materials due to imbalanced flow, breakdowns, or upstream delays. |
| 3 | **Inventory** | Excess raw material, WIP, or finished goods that tie up capital, space, and hide systemic problems. |

*(Acceptable alternatives: Defects, Motion, Transportation, Over-processing, Underutilized skills — any 3.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Compare Conventional and Lean Manufacturing on the basis of **(i)** Production driver, **(ii)** Batch size, and **(iii)** Quality approach.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 Mark per parameter):**

| Parameter | Conventional | Lean |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Production Driver** | Forecast-based **PUSH** system — production starts even before a confirmed order. | Real customer demand-based **PULL** system — triggered by Kanban from downstream. |
| **(ii) Batch Size** | Large batches (hundreds to thousands) to amortize long set-ups. | Small lots, ideally **one-piece flow**, enabled by quick changeovers (SMED). |
| **(iii) Quality Approach** | **Inspection-based** — defects detected at end-of-line; rework culture prevalent. | **Built-in quality (Jidoka)** — defects detected and contained at the source via Poka-Yoke. |

---

### PART B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the **historical evolution of Lean Manufacturing** from Henry Ford's mass production to the Toyota Production System. Discuss the contributions of Taiichi Ohno and W. Edwards Deming. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**1. Henry Ford's Mass Production (1913) — 2 Marks**
- Ford introduced the **moving assembly line** at Highland Park in 1913.
- Key innovations: **interchangeable parts**, **standardization**, **single-piece flow within rigid product**.
- Limitation: Ford's system was **single-product** (Model T) — could not handle variety. The famous quote "Any customer can have a car painted any colour that he wants, so long as it is black" reflects this rigidity.
- Ford's flow principles became the **first foundational pillar** of Lean.

**2. Toyota Production System & Taiichi Ohno (1948–1975) — 3 Marks**
- Post-WWII, Toyota (Japan) faced the "3 Ms" problem: **Muda** (waste), **Mura** (unevenness), **Muri** (overburden).
- **Taiichi Ohno**, Toyota's Chief Engineer, developed TPS to compete with Western mass producers in a **low-volume, high-variety** context.
- Core TPS pillars:
  - **Just-in-Time (JIT):** Every part produced only when needed by the next process.
  - **Jidoka (Autonomation):** Automation with a human touch — machines auto-stop on detecting abnormality.
  - **Heijunka:** Level production scheduling to smooth demand.
  - **Kanban:** Visual signalling system to pull production.
- Outcome: Toyota became the global benchmark for quality, cost, and delivery.

**3. W. Edwards Deming's Influence — 2 Marks**
- Deming's **14 Points for Management** and **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle** deeply influenced Toyota's quality culture.
- Concepts of **statistical process control (SPC)**, **continuous improvement (Kaizen)**, and **system thinking** were adopted by Toyota in the 1950s.
- Deming famously said: *"Quality is not an act, it is a habit."* This philosophy underpins Lean.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often attribute Lean *entirely* to Ohno. Marks are **deducted** if Deming/Ford contributions are omitted. Always cite **all three** pioneers.

---

**Q. (b)** With a **suitable numerical example**, derive the lead-time reduction achieved by converting a **conventional batch-and-queue** system into a **Lean one-piece flow** system. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 Mark): Define Lead Time**

$$
LT_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{process}} + T_{\text{queue}} + T_{\text{transport}}
$$

**Step 2 (2 Marks): Conventional Batch-Queue Formula**

For a batch of $N$ units:

$$
LT_{\text{conv}} = N \cdot t_{\text{process}} + (N-1) \cdot t_{\text{queue}}
$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark): Lean Formula**

For $N=1$:

$$
LT_{\text{lean}} = 1 \cdot t_{\text{process}} + 0 = t_{\text{process}}
$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks): Numerical Example**

Given: $t_{\text{process}} = 4$ min, $t_{\text{queue}} = 8$ min, $N = 40$.

Conventional:

$$
LT_{\text{conv}} = (40)(4) + (39)(8) = 160 + 312 = 472 \text{ min/unit}
$$

Lean:

$$
LT_{\text{lean}} = (1)(4) = 4 \text{ min/unit}
$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark): Percentage Reduction**

$$
R = \frac{472 - 4}{472} \times 100\% \approx 99.15\%
$$

**Conclusion (1 Mark embedded):** Lead time drops by **~99%**, freeing working capital, reducing storage, and exposing hidden problems — the foundation of Lean economics.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**Q. (a)** Explain the **Seven Wastes (TIMWOOD)** of Lean Manufacturing. For each waste, provide **one real-time shop-floor example** and the corresponding **Lean countermeasure**. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer (7 Marks — 1 Mark per waste)**

| # | Waste (TIMWOOD) | Shop-Floor Example | Lean Countermeasure |
|---|---|---|---|
| **T** | **Transportation** | Forklift moving raw material 200 m across factory floor. | Design **U-shaped cell**; colocate processes. |
| **I** | **Inventory** | 6 months of WIP stacked between machining and assembly. | Implement **Kanban pull**; reduce lot size. |
| **M** | **Motion** | Operator walking 3 km per shift searching for tools. | Apply **5S workplace organization**; shadow boards. |
| **W** | **Waiting** | CNC machine idle for 30 min waiting for tool change. | Balance line to **Takt time**; use SMED. |
| **O** | **Overproduction** | Producing 500 units/day when demand is 300. | Adopt **build-to-order pull system**. |
| **O** | **Over-processing** | Hand-polishing parts to mirror finish when functional roughness suffices. | **Value-stream analysis**; remove non-value steps. |
| **D** | **Defects** | 8% rejection rate at final inspection; rework queue 2 days long. | **Poka-Yoke** error-proofing; **Jidoka** line-stop. |

*(Optional 8th waste: **Talent underutilization** — employees not involved in improvement decisions; fix via Kaizen circles.)*

---

**Q. (b)** *"Lean Manufacturing is fundamentally a strategic shift from a Push system to a Pull system."* Critically evaluate this statement with reference to **Kanban, Takt Time, and Heijunka**. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**1. Push vs Pull – Conceptual Contrast (2 Marks)**
- **Push (Conventional):** Production scheduled based on forecast; finished goods accumulate whether sold or not.
- **Pull (Lean):** Each process produces only what the downstream process has consumed; Kanban card triggers replenishment.

**2. Role of Kanban (2 Marks)**
- Kanban (Japanese for "signal card") is the **information carrier** of the pull system.
- Two main types: **Production Kanban** (authorizes upstream to make) and **Withdrawal Kanban** (authorizes downstream to pull).
- Limits WIP via the **CONWIP** principle, making overproduction physically impossible.

**3. Role of Takt Time (1.5 Marks)**

$$
T_{\text{takt}} = \frac{\text{Available Time}}{\text{Customer Demand}}
$$

- Sets the **pulse rate** of the entire production system.
- Example: 480 min/day ÷ 240 units = 2 min/unit → every workstation must cycle in ≤2 min.
- Eliminates the **overburden (Muri)** of workers and machines.

**4. Role of Heijunka (1 Mark)**
- Heijunka = **production leveling** to smooth demand.
- Prevents bullwhip effect and chaotic schedule changes.
- Example: Rather than 100 units Monday + 0 units Tuesday, produce 50 each day.

**5. Critical Conclusion (0.5 Mark)**
The statement is **correct but partial**. Lean is *not only* push-to-pull — it equally requires **Jidoka** (quality pillar) and **respect for people** (cultural pillar). A pure pull system without Jidoka produces defective items at the same speed.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Confusing **Heijunka with Kanban**. Heijunka = *leveling* (time axis). Kanban = *signalling* (information axis). Both essential but distinct — losing marks if interchanged.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- [x] **Conventional Manufacturing** = Ford-era mass production, **PUSH**, large batches, end-of-line inspection, functional layout.
- [x] **Lean Manufacturing** = Toyota Production System (TPS), **PULL**, small lots, built-in quality (Jidoka), cellular layout.
- [x] **Origin:** Taiichi Ohno (Toyota, 1970s); influenced by Ford + Deming + Juran.
- [x] **Push System:** Production based on **forecast**, regardless of actual demand.
- [x] **Pull System:** Production triggered by **downstream consumption signal (Kanban)**.
- [x] **Seven Wastes (TIMWOOD):** Transportation, Inventory, Motion, Waiting, Overproduction, Over-processing, Defects.
- [x] **8th Waste:** Underutilized Talent (modern extension).
- [x] **Takt Time formula:** Available Time ÷ Demand — sets the heartbeat of Lean.
- [x] **Value-Added Ratio (VAR):** Value-Added Time ÷ Total Lead Time. Conventional <5%, Lean ≥25%.
- [x] **Lead-time reduction** in batch-to-flow conversion can exceed **99%** mathematically.
- [x] **Three Pillars of TPS:** JIT (pull), Jidoka (quality), Respect for People (culture).
- [x] **SMED:** Single-Minute Exchange of Dies — technique to reduce setup time below 10 minutes, enabling small batches.
- [x] **5S Foundation:** Sort, Set in Order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain — prerequisite workplace discipline for Lean.
- [x] **Poka-Yoke:** Mistake-proofing devices that prevent defects at source.
- [x] **Kaizen:** Continuous, incremental improvement involving all employees.
- [x] **VSM (Value Stream Mapping):** Pencil-and-paper tool used to visualize and eliminate non-value-added steps.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tools of Lean Manufacturing: 5-S
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET746 - LEAN MANUFACTURING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Tools of Lean Manufacturing: 5-S # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus) > [!IMPORTANT] > **5-S** is a structured, five-step Japanese workplace organization methodology that forms the foundational pillar of the **Toyota Production System (TPS)** and modern Lean Manufacturing. It is a systematic approach to establishing and maintaining a disciplined, organized, clean, and standardized work environment that eliminates waste, reduces variation, and builds a culture of continuous improvement. The acronym **5-S** represents five sequential Japanese words beginning with the letter "S": **Seiri, Seiton, Seiso, Seiketsu, and Shitsuke**. The methodology was first formalized at **Hino Motors** in the 1960s and later propagated globally by **Toyota** as the bedrock of operational excellence. The five pillars in their original Japanese (Romanized) form with their English equivalents are: | Romanized Term | English Translation | Core Intent | |---|---|---| | **Seiri** | Sort | Eliminate the unnecessary | | **Seiton** | Set in Order | Arrange items for easy access | | **Seiso** | Shine | Clean the workplace thoroughly | | **Seiketsu** | Standardize | Establish norms and visual controls | | **Shitsuke** | Sustain | Maintain discipline and self-audit | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Think of 5-S like organizing your personal study desk before an examination.** Imagine a student preparing for exams in a completely cluttered room — old notes piled up, broken pens, half-eaten snacks, three different calculators scattered around. When the exam arrives, **finding the right formula takes 20 minutes**, the **pen runs out of ink**, and stress levels are high. Now apply 5-S to that desk: - **Sort (Seiri):** Throw away old papers, broken pens, and useless notes. Keep only what is *needed* for current subjects. - **Set in Order (Seiton):** Place the calculator in a specific corner, the geometry box in a drawer, and active notes in a labeled stack — *"a place for everything, and everything in its place."* - **Shine (Seiso):** Wipe the desk daily. A clean surface makes dust, leaks, and missing items immediately visible. - **Standardize (Seiketsu):** Use color-coded folders, fixed photo of "what the desk *should* look like," and a 5-minute end-of-day routine. - **Sustain (Shitsuke):** Make this routine a *habit* — discipline yourself to follow it every single day, not just before exams. The result: **search time drops, mistakes reduce, stress disappears, and the desk becomes a "silent teacher."** This is exactly what 5-S does to a factory floor, machine shop, hospital ward, or office. ## 1.3 Why 5-S is Mandatory Before Any Lean Implementation > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Most students wrongly treat 5-S as a "housekeeping activity." It is actually a **pre-requisite** for every other Lean tool — *Kanban, SMED, Poka-Yoke, TPM, Jidoka* — because none of these tools can survive in a chaotic, dirty, disorganized workplace. KTU questions often test this foundational relationship. - **Foundation for Visual Management:** Without Seiton and Seiketsu, visual signals become invisible. - **Pre-requisite for TPM:** Autonomous Maintenance (Jishu Hozen) requires clean machines (Seiso). - **Pre-requisite for Standard Work:** Standardization (Seiketsu) creates the baseline for takt time and work combination tables. - **Cultural Bedrock:** Shitsuke builds the *Kaizen mindset* needed for all future improvements. ## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in 5-S The following measurable indicators are widely used in industry and may appear in KTU numerical questions: - **5-S Audit Score (%)** — measured via standardized checklists - **Number of Red Tags generated** per Sort campaign - **% of items with designated locations** after Set in Order - **Mean Time To Find (MTTF)** any tool/document, target: **< 30 seconds** - **Number of Safety Incidents per month** — expected to fall by **30–50%** after full 5-S rollout - **Walking Distance (m/day)** saved by improved layout # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ## 2.1 Pillar-by-Pillar Deep Analysis ### 2.1.1 Seiri (Sort) — "The Great Decluttering" **Objective:** Separate *needed* from *unneeded* items in the workspace and **remove the unneeded permanently**. **Why this comes first:** You cannot organize what you have not yet decided to keep. Sorting is the *pruning* step that creates physical and mental space. **Standard Decision Rule (the "Red Tag" System):** - A red tag is physically attached to any item suspected of being unnecessary. - The item is moved to a **"Red Tag Area"** (quarantine zone) for a holding period — usually **30 days**. - If the item is *not* claimed during this period, it is disposed of, recycled, or relocated. - Typical disposal categories: **scrap, surplus, repair, return to vendor, archive.** **Engineering Heuristic — Frequency of Use Test:** - Used **every hour** → keep at workstation - Used **once per shift** → keep in nearby team area - Used **once per week** → keep in central storage - Used **once per month** → keep in warehouse/archive - **Never used** → dispose > [!TIP] > **Quantified Target:** Industry benchmarks suggest that the first Sort campaign typically removes **20–40%** of total floor area and **15–30%** of inventory items. ### 2.1.2 Seiton (Set in Order) — "A Place for Everything" **Objective:** Arrange the *kept* items in a logical, ergonomic, and visually self-explanatory layout so that they can be **found, used, and returned in less than 30 seconds** by anyone. **Key Tools and Techniques:** - **Floor Marking:** Painted lanes, walkways, work zones (commonly yellow for walkways, white for work areas, red for safety/no-go). - **Shadow Boards:** Tool boards with painted silhouettes of each tool — missing tool is instantly visible. - **Address Labels (Visual Codes):** Every storage location has a unique alphanumeric code. - **FIFO lanes (First-In-First-Out):** Used heavily with parts bins. - **Height & Frequency Rules:** Most-used items at **waist height**; heaviest items on **lower shelves**; lightest/rare items higher up. > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Seiton is also called *"Seiri at the visual level."* It is the **arrangement phase** that turns Sort outcomes into a navigable, intuitive space. ### 2.1.3 Seiso (Shine) — "Cleaning is Inspection" **Objective:** Clean the workplace *completely*, so that **dirt, leaks, misalignments, and abnormalities become immediately visible** — turning the act of cleaning into a daily inspection. **Operational Principles:** - Cleaning is **not the job of a janitor**; it is the operator's responsibility. - Cleaning must identify the **source of contamination** (oil leak, dust, chip buildup) and eliminate it, not just wipe it. - Shine activities create the conditions for **Autonomous Maintenance** in TPM. **Outputs of Effective Shine:** - Zero oil pools on floor - Clean machine surfaces showing paint and labels clearly - Glass, mirrors, gauges — all transparent - Floor markings visible at all times ### 2.1.4 Seiketsu (Standardize) — "Make the First Three S's a Norm" **Objective:** Convert the gains of Seiri, Seiton, and Seiso into **repeatable, organization-wide standards** so that the achieved state cannot drift back into disorder. **Standardization Tools:** - **Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)** with photographs - **5-Minute 5-S Checklists** posted at every workstation - **Visual Management Boards** showing "before" and "after" photos - **Color Coding Systems** (e.g., red = defect, green = OK, blue = information) - **Photographic Standards** — pictures of the *correct* state pinned at every work cell **Critical Concept:** Seiketsu answers *"What is the standard look of this area?"* If a new employee can walk into the cell and match the standard in 5 minutes, Seiketsu is achieved. ### 2.1.5 Shitsuke (Sustain / Self-Discipline) — "Make it a Culture" **Objective:** Transform the four previous S's from *activities* into *habits* through training, audits, and cultural reinforcement. **Sustaining Mechanisms:** - **5-S Audits** (typically monthly, scored on a 100-point scale) - **Management Walks (Gemba Walks)** — leaders physically visit the floor - **Suggestion Systems** — workers propose improvements - **Training and Induction** — every new employee receives 5-S orientation - **Recognition and Reward** — best 5-S cell of the month awards > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Trap:** Students often believe 5-S ends at Seiketsu. **Shitsuke is the hardest and most important pillar** — without it, all previous gains decay within 60–90 days (this is called the "5-S decay curve"). ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Reference Table > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table consolidates the high-yield facts examiners frequently test. Memorize the Japanese terms, English meanings, and the corresponding action verbs. | Pillar | Japanese | English | Key Verb | Visual Tool Used | Common Mistake to Avoid | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Seiri** | Sort | Eliminate | Red Tag System | Confusing it with general cleaning | | 2 | **Seiton** | Set in Order | Arrange | Shadow Boards, Floor Marking | Leaving it as "organized chaos" | | 3 | **Seiso** | Shine | Clean & Inspect | Cleaning schedules | Delegating to housekeeping only | | 4 | **Seiketsu** | Standardize | Normalize | SOPs, Checklists, Visual Standards | Writing manuals no one reads | | 5 | **Shitsuke** | Sustain | Habituate | 5-S Audits, Gemba Walks | Treating 5-S as a one-time event | ## 2.3 Real-World Industrial Utility 5-S is not confined to manufacturing. It is deployed across: - **Automotive:** Toyota, Maruti Suzuki (India) — every workstation follows 5-S - **Healthcare:** Apollo Hospitals, Mayo Clinic — operating theatre 5-S reduces surgical errors - **Aviation MRO:** Aircraft hangars use 5-S for tool control (FOD prevention) - **IT/Software:** Agile teams use 5-S for digital workspace hygiene (shared drives, repos) - **Offices:** Government and banking back-offices for document retrieval efficiency # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Implementation Roadmap & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 The Standard 5-S Implementation Roadmap The following eight-step sequence is the industry-standard implementation flow tested in KTU 14-mark questions. Each step is explained with its *operational logic* (the "why") and *concrete activities* (the "how"). ### Step 1 — Top Management Commitment & Awareness **Logic:** Without leadership commitment, 5-S dies the moment the first audit fails. Culture flows from the top. **Activities:** - CEO/MD announces 5-S as a strategic priority - Budget allocated for 5-S tools, training, signage - A **5-S Steering Committee** is formed (cross-functional) ### Step 2 — 5-S Training & Awareness Campaign **Logic:** Workers cannot apply a tool they do not understand. **Activities:** - 2-hour classroom module on the five S's with photos and videos - Demonstrations of *before* and *after* states - Distribution of pocket-sized 5-S cards in regional languages ### Step 3 — Pilot Area Selection **Logic:** A full-plant rollout is risky; a pilot builds proof and confidence. **Activities:** - Choose one model cell (e.g., a CNC machining cell or assembly line) - Define the **pilot boundary** clearly with floor markings ### Step 4 — Sort (Seiri) Campaign **Logic:** Identify and remove the *unnecessary* first. **Activities:** - Conduct a "Big Clean Day" — entire team cleans the cell together - Every questionable item receives a **Red Tag** with date, owner, reason - Items move to a designated **Red Tag Holding Area** for 30 days - After 30 days, unclaimed items are **disposed/recycled** ### Step 5 — Set in Order (Seiton) Layout Design **Logic:** With clutter removed, design the *optimal* layout for the remaining items. **Activities:** - Apply **floor marking** for machines, walkways, storage zones - Install **shadow boards** for tools - Create **address labels** for all storage locations - Validate with the **30-second retrieval test** ### Step 6 — Shine (Seiso) Schedule Activation **Logic:** Cleaning is inspection; establish a daily rhythm. **Activities:** - Prepare a **machine-by-machine cleaning checklist** (15 minutes at end of shift) - Identify root causes of dirt (e.g., hydraulic leak → install drip tray) - Assign cleaning zones to specific operators ### Step 7 — Standardize (Seiketsu) Documentation **Logic:** Make the new state the *only* accepted state. **Activities:** - Take **photographs** of the standardized cell — pin them at the cell entrance - Create **5-S check sheets** (daily, weekly, monthly) for operators - Implement **visual management boards** (Andon, KPI displays) ### Step 8 — Sustain (Shitsuke) Audits & Discipline **Logic:** Standards drift without measurement and accountability. **Activities:** - Conduct **monthly 5-S audits** using a standardized 100-point scorecard - Publish audit results on the plant communication board - **Management Gemba Walks** every fortnight - Annual **5-S Kaizen Competition** with rewards ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — The 5-S Audit Scoring Methodology KTU questions frequently ask students to **compute a 5-S audit score** for a given workplace. The standard industry formula is reproduced below. ### The 5-S Audit Score Formula The 5-S audit score $S_{5S}$ is computed as the **weighted percentage** of points earned across the five pillars. $$ S_{5S} \;=\; \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i \cdot P_i}{S_{max}} $$ Where: - $P_i$ = Points earned in pillar $i$ (out of a pillar-wise maximum) - $w_i$ = Weight assigned to pillar $i$ (sum of weights = 1) - $S_{max}$ = Maximum possible score for the entire audit > [!NOTE] > **Weight Convention (Industry Standard):** > Sort and Set in Order typically receive the highest weights because they produce the largest visible waste reduction. Sustain typically receives the lowest weight in early audits and is *increased* as the program matures. | Pillar | Typical Weight $w_i$ | |---|---| | Seiri (Sort) | 0.25 | | Seiton (Set in Order) | 0.25 | | Seiso (Shine) | 0.20 | | Seiketsu (Standardize) | 0.15 | | Shitsuke (Sustain) | 0.15 | | **Total** | **1.00** | ### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > [!IMPORTANT] > A KTU numerical problem on 5-S typically provides pillar-wise scores out of 20 and asks for the overall audit percentage using the weight table above. **Problem Statement:** A manufacturing cell is audited on a 20-point scale per pillar. The auditor records the following raw scores: - Seiri = 16, Seiton = 14, Seiso = 18, Seiketsu = 12, Shitsuke = 10. - Using the standard industry weights (0.25, 0.25, 0.20, 0.15, 0.15), calculate the overall 5-S audit score. **Step-by-Step Solution:** $$ S_{5S} \;=\; \frac{(0.25)(16) + (0.25)(14) + (0.20)(18) + (0.15)(12) + (0.15)(10)}{20} $$ Compute each weighted contribution: $$ (0.25)(16) \;=\; 4.00 $$ $$ (0.25)(14) \;=\; 3.50 $$ $$ (0.20)(18) \;=\; 3.60 $$ $$ (0.15)(12) \;=\; 1.80 $$ $$ (0.15)(10) \;=\; 1.50 $$ Sum the weighted points: $$ \sum w_i P_i \;=\; 4.00 + 3.50 + 3.60 + 1.80 + 1.50 \;=\; 14.40 $$ Divide by the maximum possible score (20): $$ S_{5S} \;=\; \frac{14.40}{20} \times 100\% \;=\; 72.00\% $$ **Interpretation Bands (Industry Standard):** | Score Range | Rating | Action | |---|---|---| | **90–100%** | World Class | Maintain, share best practices | | **75–89%** | Good | Targeted improvement on weak pillars | | **60–74%** | Average | Major Seiketsu and Shitsuke work needed | | **< 60%** | Poor | Re-launch 5-S with management intervention | **Verdict for the cell above:** At **72%**, the cell is in the *Average* band, requiring focused effort on **Standardize (12/20)** and **Sustain (10/20)** pillars. ## 3.3 Expansion: From 5-S to 6-S and 7-S KTU 2024 syllabus sometimes tests whether 5-S can be extended. Memorize the two common expansions: $$ 5\text{-}S \;+\; \text{Safety} \;=\; 6\text{-}S $$ $$ 5\text{-}S \;+\; \text{Safety} \;+\; \text{Security/Society} \;=\; 7\text{-}S $$ **Safety (the 6th S):** Adds OSHA-style hazard identification, PPE compliance, and lock-out/tag-out discipline. Many modern plants now mandate 6-S instead of 5-S. **Security/Society (the 7th S):** Adds environmental compliance, information security, and corporate social responsibility — aligning 5-S with **ESG frameworks**. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Five Pillars of 5-S (Hierarchical Mermaid Mind Map) The following Mermaid graph maps the Japanese terms, English meanings, and the dominant visual tool associated with each pillar of 5-S. ```mermaid graph TD node5S["5-S FOUNDATION OF LEAN"] p1["SEIRI
SORT"] p2["SEITON
SET IN ORDER"] p3["SEISO
SHINE"] p4["SEIKETSU
STANDARDIZE"] p5["SHITSUKE
SUSTAIN"] t1["Red Tag System
Eliminate Unnecessary"] t2["Shadow Boards
Floor Marking
Address Labels"] t3["Daily Cleaning
Inspection
Autonomous Maintenance"] t4["SOPs
5-S Checklists
Visual Standards"] t5["5-S Audits
Gemba Walks
Training and Recognition"] node5S --> p1 node5S --> p2 node5S --> p3 node5S --> p4 node5S --> p5 p1 --> t1 p2 --> t2 p3 --> t3 p4 --> t4 p5 --> t5 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The central node (5-S) branches sequentially into the five pillars. Each pillar then expands into the *primary tools* used to implement that pillar. This is the visual answer KTU examiners expect when a question asks *"Sketch the 5-S framework with examples."* ## 4.2 Diagram 2 — 5-S Implementation Flow (Sequential Pipeline) The following Mermaid flowchart represents the **8-step implementation roadmap** discussed in SECTION 3, shown as a sequential pipeline that loops back through the audit cycle. ```mermaid graph LR s1["STEP 1
Top Management
Commitment"] s2["STEP 2
Training and
Awareness"] s3["STEP 3
Pilot Area
Selection"] s4["STEP 4
SORT Campaign
Red Tag System"] s5["STEP 5
SET IN ORDER
Layout Design"] s6["STEP 6
SHINE
Cleaning Schedules"] s7["STEP 7
STANDARDIZE
SOPs and Visuals"] s8["STEP 8
SUSTAIN
Audits and Gemba"] s1 --> s2 s2 --> s3 s3 --> s4 s4 --> s5 s5 --> s6 s6 --> s7 s7 --> s8 s8 -->|"Audit Findings
Kaizen"| s4 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **The feedback loop** from Step 8 back to Step 4 is critical. It represents the *continuous improvement* (Kaizen) principle — even after the initial rollout, each monthly audit generates new Red Tag campaigns. Without this loop, 5-S decays. ## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Functional Block Architecture: 5-S as a Lean Enabler This block diagram shows how 5-S acts as the *foundation layer* that enables higher-order Lean tools. This is a high-yield KTU diagram for the 14-mark question *"Discuss 5-S as a prerequisite for other Lean tools."* ```mermaid graph TD fdn["5-S FOUNDATION
Workplace Discipline"] l1["Visual Management
Andon Signals"] l2["TPM
Autonomous Maintenance"] l3["Standard Work
Takt Time Design"] l4["Kanban
Pull System"] l5["Poka Yoke
Mistake Proofing"] l6["Kaizen
Continuous Improvement"] fdn --> l1 fdn --> l2 fdn --> l3 fdn --> l4 fdn --> l5 fdn --> l6 l3 --> l4 l1 --> l5 l2 --> l6 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Logic of the Block Diagram:** > 5-S sits at the **base layer** because every higher Lean tool requires a *clean, organized, standardized* workspace. Visual Management needs clean walls to display boards. TPM needs clean machines to detect leaks. Standard Work needs visual controls. Kanban needs labeled storage. Poka-Yoke needs visible fixtures. Kaizen needs disciplined workers to propose ideas. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024 Scheme]** **Q: Define the term "5-S" in Lean Manufacturing. List the five Japanese terms with their English meanings.** **Model Answer:** > 5-S is a **systematic methodology** for workplace organization and standardization that originated in Japan as part of the Toyota Production System. It aims to establish a clean, organized, and disciplined work environment that eliminates waste and supports continuous improvement. The five Japanese terms with English meanings are: | Japanese | English Meaning | |---|---| | **Seiri** | Sort — eliminate the unnecessary | | **Seiton** | Set in Order — arrange items for easy retrieval | | **Seiso** | Shine — clean the workplace | | **Seiketsu** | Standardize — establish norms and visual controls | | **Shitsuke** | Sustain — maintain discipline | *(3 marks — full marks awarded for accurate Japanese terms + English meanings + a one-line definition.)* --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023 Style]** **Q: What is the "Red Tag System" in 5-S? Explain its role in the Sort (Seiri) pillar.** **Model Answer:** > The **Red Tag System** is a visual tool used in the **Seiri (Sort)** pillar to identify, isolate, and remove unnecessary items from the workplace. **Procedure:** 1. A **red-coloured tag** is attached to any item whose necessity is doubtful. 2. The tagged item is moved to a designated **Red Tag Holding Area**. 3. The item remains in the holding area for a defined quarantine period — typically **30 days**. 4. If no operator claims the item within 30 days, it is **disposed, recycled, returned to the vendor, or relocated** to a remote warehouse. **Role in Sort (Seiri):** The Red Tag System forces a clear, evidence-based decision on every item in the workspace. It removes the ambiguity of "I might need this someday" and produces a measurable reduction in floor clutter — typically **20–40%** of total floor area in the first campaign. *(3 marks — awarded for procedure + role + quantified outcome.)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per **KTU 2024 ESE regulation**, Module 2 long-answer questions offer a choice between two alternatives. Both alternatives are provided below with complete model solutions. --- ### QUESTION A — Option 1 (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]** **Q: (a)** Explain in detail the **five pillars of 5-S** with their Japanese terms, objectives, and at least one practical activity for each pillar. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the **step-by-step implementation roadmap** for rolling out 5-S in a manufacturing organization, highlighting the role of management commitment and the **5-S audit cycle** in sustaining the gains. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] The five pillars of 5-S are: **1. Seiri (Sort) — [1 mark]** - **Objective:** Distinguish between *needed* and *unneeded* items in the workplace and remove the unneeded permanently. - **Practical Activity:** Conduct a "Big Clean Day" where every questionable item is tagged with a **Red Tag** and quarantined in a holding area for 30 days. Unclaimed items are disposed of. **2. Seiton (Set in Order) — [1 mark]** - **Objective:** Arrange the *kept* items so they can be located and retrieved in **under 30 seconds** by anyone. - **Practical Activity:** Install **shadow boards** with painted tool silhouettes; apply **colour-coded floor markings** (yellow for walkways, white for workstations, red for restricted zones); and label every storage location with a unique alphanumeric code. **3. Seiso (Shine) — [1 mark]** - **Objective:** Clean the workplace completely so that abnormalities (oil leaks, dust, loose bolts) are **immediately visible** — turning cleaning into daily inspection. - **Practical Activity:** Assign each operator a **15-minute end-of-shift cleaning zone** on a specific machine. Investigate and eliminate the *root cause* of dirt (e.g., install a drip tray for an oil leak). **4. Seiketsu (Standardize) — [2 marks]** - **Objective:** Convert the gains of the first three S's into **repeatable, organization-wide standards** so the achieved state cannot drift. - **Practical Activities:** - Take **photographs** of the standardized cell and pin them at the entrance as the "visual standard." - Issue a **5-S daily checklist** for operators to tick off at the end of each shift. - Document **SOPs** for every cleaning and arrangement task. **5. Shitsuke (Sustain) — [2 marks]** - **Objective:** Make the first four S's a **cultural habit** through audits, training, and management engagement. - **Practical Activities:** - Conduct **monthly 5-S audits** using a 100-point scorecard; publish scores on the plant board. - Leaders conduct **Gemba Walks** (floor visits) every fortnight. - Run a **5-S Kaizen Competition** with awards for the best cell each quarter. #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] A standard 5-S implementation roadmap contains **eight sequential steps**: **Step 1 — Top Management Commitment:** [1 mark] - Senior leadership announces 5-S as a strategic priority, allocates budget, and forms a cross-functional **5-S Steering Committee**. Without this commitment, the program collapses the moment the first audit reveals non-compliance. **Step 2 — Training and Awareness:** [1 mark] - A 2-hour classroom module trains all employees on the five S's using visual examples, photographs, and before/after case studies. Pocket-sized 5-S cards are distributed in regional languages. **Step 3 — Pilot Area Selection:** [1 mark] - A single model cell (e.g., a CNC machining cell) is chosen as a pilot. The pilot's success becomes a *proof of concept* for the rest of the plant. **Step 4 — Sort (Seiri) Campaign:** [1 mark] - The pilot cell conducts a "Big Clean Day." Red Tags are attached to all questionable items. A 30-day Red Tag Area is set up. Items unclaimed after 30 days are disposed of. **Step 5 — Set in Order (Seiton) Layout:** [1 mark] - Floor markings, shadow boards, and address labels are installed. The cell is validated using the **30-second retrieval test** for every tool. **Step 6 — Shine (Seiso) Schedule:** [0.5 mark] - A 15-minute end-of-shift cleaning checklist is assigned per operator per machine. Root causes of contamination are eliminated (e.g., leak repair, chip conveyor). **Step 7 — Standardize (Seiketsu):** [0.5 mark] - Photographic standards are pinned at the cell entrance. A 5-S daily check sheet is issued. A visual management board displays the cell's 5-S KPIs. **Step 8 — Sustain (Shitsuke) Audit Cycle:** [1 mark] - **Monthly 5-S audits** are conducted using a weighted 100-point scorecard (weights: Sort 0.25, Set in Order 0.25, Shine 0.20, Standardize 0.15, Sustain 0.15). - Audit findings are published on the plant communication board. - **Management Gemba Walks** occur every fortnight. - An **annual 5-S Kaizen Competition** rewards the best-performing cell. The audit cycle generates continuous improvement: each monthly audit identifies new Red Tag opportunities, which loops back to Step 4. This *closed-loop* mechanism prevents the **5-S decay curve** (where gains erode within 60–90 days if not audited). --- ### QUESTION B — Option 2 (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]** **Q: (a)** Discuss **5-S as a foundational pre-requisite** for other Lean Manufacturing tools. Explain how 5-S enables Visual Management, TPM, and Standard Work with suitable examples. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A manufacturing cell is audited on a 20-point scale per pillar. The auditor records the following raw scores: Seiri = 18, Seiton = 16, Seiso = 14, Seiketsu = 10, Shitsuke = 8. Using the standard industry weights (Sort 0.25, Set in Order 0.25, Shine 0.20, Standardize 0.15, Sustain 0.15), calculate the overall 5-S audit score, identify the rating band, and recommend two specific corrective actions for the weakest pillar. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] 5-S is universally regarded as the **foundational layer** of the Lean system. Without an organized, clean, and standardized workplace, no other Lean tool can be sustained. The relationship between 5-S and three major Lean tools is explained below. **1. 5-S Enables Visual Management — [2.5 marks]** Visual Management is the practice of making the status of work, quality, and safety **immediately visible** to anyone — for example, Andon lights, traffic-light boards, shadow boards. Visual Management is **impossible** in a cluttered workspace where signals are obscured by dirt, disorganization, or misplaced items. - **Seiri** ensures that only relevant signals remain on display. - **Seiton** places the visual displays at the *line of sight* of operators. - **Seiso** keeps the displays clean and readable. - **Seiketsu** standardizes *what* the visual should look like (colour, position, size). - **Shitsuke** ensures workers actually *look* at the displays and respond. *Example:* An Andon light signalling machine breakdown is invisible if the machine surface is covered in oil and chips (failure of Seiso). **2. 5-S Enables Total Productive Maintenance (TPM) — [2.5 marks]** TPM's first pillar — **Autonomous Maintenance (Jishu Hozen)** — requires operators to perform daily cleaning, lubrication, and inspection of their own machines. This is essentially a fusion of **Seiso + Seiketsu**. - **Seiri** removes obstacles blocking access to machine components. - **Seiton** ensures cleaning tools, grease guns, and gauges are within arm's reach. - **Seiso** is the *core activity* of Autonomous Maintenance — cleaning reveals leaks, loose bolts, and abnormal vibrations. - **Seiketsu** converts cleaning schedules into SOPs with time standards. - **Shitsuke** sustains the operator's daily ownership of the machine. *Example:* Toyota's operators spend the first 15 minutes of every shift cleaning their machines — this single act of Seiso prevents **70% of unplanned breakdowns** that would otherwise require maintenance crew intervention. **3. 5-S Enables Standard Work — [2 marks]** Standard Work (a Toyota Production System pillar) requires that the *sequence*, *takt time*, and *standard in-process stock* for every operation be documented, displayed, and followed. This is impossible without 5-S. - **Seiton** ensures that the standard layout (machine positions, parts bins, operator paths) is repeatable. - **Seiketsu** documents the layout in **Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)** and **Work Combination Tables**. - **Shitsuke** ensures operators *follow* the standard and supervisors *audit* the standard. *Example:* A Work Combination Table cannot be drawn accurately if tools are not in fixed locations — only Seiton and Seiketsu provide that stability. #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] **Given Data:** - Seiri = 18, Seiton = 16, Seiso = 14, Seiketsu = 10, Shitsuke = 8 (each out of 20) - Weights: $w_1 = 0.25, w_2 = 0.25, w_3 = 0.20, w_4 = 0.15, w_5 = 0.15$ **Step 1 — Apply the 5-S Audit Formula:** $$ S_{5S} \;=\; \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i \cdot P_i}{S_{max}} $$ **Step 2 — Compute each weighted contribution:** $$ w_1 P_1 \;=\; 0.25 \times 18 \;=\; 4.50 $$ $$ w_2 P_2 \;=\; 0.25 \times 16 \;=\; 4.00 $$ $$ w_3 P_3 \;=\; 0.20 \times 14 \;=\; 2.80 $$ $$ w_4 P_4 \;=\; 0.15 \times 10 \;=\; 1.50 $$ $$ w_5 P_5 \;=\; 0.15 \times 8 \;=\; 1.20 $$ **Step 3 — Sum the weighted points:** $$ \sum w_i P_i \;=\; 4.50 + 4.00 + 2.80 + 1.50 + 1.20 \;=\; 14.00 $$ **Step 4 — Convert to percentage:** $$ S_{5S} \;=\; \frac{14.00}{20} \times 100\% \;=\; 70.00\% $$ **Step 5 — Identify the Rating Band:** A score of **70%** falls in the **60–74% — Average** band. The cell requires major improvement in **Seiketsu** and **Shitsuke**. **Step 6 — Identify the Weakest Pillar and Recommend Corrective Actions:** The lowest raw score is **Shitsuke = 8/20 (40%)**, making it the weakest pillar. Two specific corrective actions: **Corrective Action 1 — Establish a Formal Monthly 5-S Audit Calendar** *(addresses the absence of measurement)* - Appoint a cross-functional audit team of 3 members (one from each shift). - Audit every cell on the **first Saturday of every month** using the standardized 100-point scorecard. - Publish scores on the plant's 5-S noticeboard and email results to all cell leaders within 48 hours. - Cells scoring below 75% must submit a **30-day improvement plan** to the 5-S Steering Committee. **Corrective Action 2 — Implement a Recognition and Reward Program** *(addresses the cultural/discipline gap)* - Introduce a **"Best 5-S Cell of the Month"** award with a small monetary incentive (e.g., INR 5,000) and a public certificate signed by the Plant Head. - Display the winning cell's photograph on the entrance noticeboard. - Conduct **quarterly Gemba Walks** where the Plant Head personally visits the top three cells and acknowledges the team. Both actions directly target the *sustain* (Shitsuke) gap by introducing **structure (audits)** and **motivation (recognition)** — the two pillars of habit formation. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks on 5-S questions — read carefully:** > > 1. **Confusing 5-S with "general housekeeping":** Examiners expect 5-S to be positioned as a *Lean tool with cultural and engineering significance*. Describing it merely as "cleaning the floor" will fetch at most 1 of 7 marks. Always link each S to a *Lean objective* (waste elimination, visual control, standardization, discipline). > > 2. **Skipping the Japanese terms:** The Romanized Japanese words (Seiri, Seiton, Seiso, Seiketsu, Shitsuke) are **mandatory** in KTU answers. A student writing only English terms (Sort, Set in Order, etc.) loses 0.5–1 mark per pillar. > > 3. **Forgetting the 5-S Audit Score formula:** The weighted-score formula in SECTION 3.2 is the *only* numerical content in Module 2 that KTU tests. Memorize the formula, the standard weights, and the rating bands. > > 4. **Treating 5-S as a one-time event:** A 14-mark answer that does not mention **Shitsuke (Sustain)** and the *5-S decay curve* is considered incomplete. Always close your answer with a paragraph on audits, Gemba Walks, and continuous improvement. > > 5. **Ignoring the link to other Lean tools:** A high-scoring 14-mark answer in Part (b) must explicitly connect 5-S to **TPM, Visual Management, Standard Work, Kanban, or Poka-Yoke**. This shows the examiner that the student understands 5-S as a *foundation* rather than an isolated activity. > > 6. **Arithmetic mistakes in audit scores:** Always show all five weighted products *separately* before summing. Many students combine the weights and the scores incorrectly, leading to a 70% answer being marked as 50% due to a single multiplication error. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-density revision checklist — read this section 24 hours before the exam.** - **Origin:** 5-S originated at **Hino Motors (1960s)** in Japan; later propagated by **Toyota Production System**. - **Five Pillars (Japanese → English):** - Seiri → Sort - Seiton → Set in Order - Seiso → Shine - Seiketsu → Standardize - Shitsuke → Sustain / Self-Discipline - **Sequence is strict:** Sort → Set in Order → Shine → Standardize → Sustain. Do not skip or reorder. - **Red Tag System:** Used in Seiri; **30-day quarantine**; unclaimed items are disposed. - **Shadow Boards & Floor Marking:** Core tools of Seiton. - **30-Second Retrieval Test:** Validation criterion for Seiton — any tool should be findable in ≤ 30 seconds. - **"Cleaning is Inspection":** The Seiso philosophy — operators (not janitors) clean to detect abnormalities. - **Seiketsu outputs:** SOPs, photographic standards, 5-S daily checklists, visual boards. - **Shitsuke outputs:** Monthly 5-S audits, Gemba Walks, recognition programs, training. - **Audit Formula:** $S_{5S} = \frac{\sum w_i P_i}{S_{max}}$, with industry weights **0.25, 0.25, 0.20, 0.15, 0.15**. - **Rating Bands:** 90–100% World Class · 75–89% Good · 60–74% Average · < 60% Poor. - **Foundational Status:** 5-S is a *pre-requisite* for TPM, Visual Management, Standard Work, Kanban, and Poka-Yoke. - **5-S Decay Curve:** Gains erode within 60–90 days *without* Shitsuke — a frequent 3-mark KTU question. - **Expansions:** 5-S + Safety = **6-S**; 5-S + Safety + Security/Society = **7-S**. - **Common Industrial Outcomes:** 20–40% floor area freed · 15–30% inventory reduction · 30–50% safety incident reduction · 70% reduction in unplanned breakdowns (when combined with TPM). - **Examiner's Golden Phrases to Use:** *"a place for everything and everything in its place,"* *"cleaning is inspection,"* *"the 5-S decay curve,"* *"the closed-loop audit cycle,"* *"5-S as the bedrock of the Toyota Production System."*

topics.title: Total Productive Maintenance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET746 - LEAN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Total Productive Maintenance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** is a holistic, company-wide, proactive, and preventive maintenance philosophy that aims to **maximize Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** by **eliminating breakdowns, defects, accidents, and all forms of waste (the Six Big Losses)**, thereby establishing a zero-loss, zero-defect, zero-accident, and zero-breakdown production environment across the entire organization.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, TPM is defined as the **ultimate goal of autonomous maintenance by operators** combined with **planned maintenance by the maintenance department**, driven by a **company-wide Small-Group Activity (SGA) / Kaizen** structure, originating from the **JIPM (Japan Institute of Plant Maintenance)** model proposed by **Seiichi Nakajima (1971)**.

The formal Japanese equivalent is **"Nō-son-pi" (能率増進)** — literally *Productive Efficiency Improvement*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body

> [!NOTE]
> Think of a factory machine as a **human body**, and TPM as a lifestyle combining **Yoga + Balanced Diet + Annual Health Checkup + Mindful living**.

| Human Body | Factory Machine (TPM Equivalent) |
|---|---|
| Daily brushing & bathing | Cleaning, Lubrication, Tightening (Autonomous Maintenance) |
| Periodic doctor visits | Planned Preventive Maintenance |
| Sudden fever / collapse | Breakdown / Unplanned Downtime |
| Heart Attack / Stroke | Major equipment failure (chronic loss) |
| BP, Sugar, Cholesterol report | OEE, MTBF, MTTR (Health Metrics) |
| Yoga & diet discipline | Kaizen & 5S workplace discipline |
| Fitness trainer | TPM Small Group Activities (SGA) |

A healthy person rarely falls sick — similarly, a TPM plant rarely breaks down. The body "operates itself" with good habits; likewise, the machine "maintains itself" through **autonomous maintenance by operators**.

## 1.3 Historical Evolution of Maintenance Thinking

The conceptual lineage of TPM is critical for KTU exams. Maintenance thinking evolved in **four distinct generations**:

1. **Breakdown Maintenance (BM)** — Fix only when it fails (Reactive era, pre-1950s).
2. **Preventive Maintenance (PM)** — Schedule-based interventions (1950s, USA).
3. **Productive Maintenance (MP / Prod Maintenance)** — Combines PM + Corrective + Preventative (1960s, USA).
4. **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** — Operates from **1971 (Nakajima)**. Extends to **all employees**, **all equipment**, and the **entire lifecycle** of the asset, integrating 5S, Quality Maintenance, and Kaizen.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TPM ≠ Just Maintenance.** TPM is a **management strategy** that embeds maintenance into the corporate culture, where **operators become the first-line maintainers** and **engineers become loss-prevention specialists**.

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Bold Constants)

The five foundational metrics evaluated throughout this module:

- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** = Reliability measure
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)** = Maintainability measure
- **Availability (A)** = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)
- **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** = Availability × Performance Rate × Quality Rate
- **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure)** = For non-repairable items

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEMET746 M2):** The focus is on understanding TPM as a **pillar of Lean Manufacturing**, its **relationship to Jidoka (Autonomation)**, and its role in eliminating the **Six Big Losses** that erode OEE.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Pillars of TPM (JIPM Model)

The JIPM model organizes TPM into **8 functional pillars**, grouped into two categories:

### Category A — Foundational Pillars (1 to 5)

| Pillar | Japanese Term | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Pillar 1: 5S Foundation** | Seiri, Seiton, Seiso, Seiketsu, Shitsuke | Workplace discipline, visualization, elimination of dirt, disorder & waste |
| **Pillar 2: Autonomous Maintenance (Jishu Hozen)** | 自発保全 | Operators take ownership of basic upkeep: cleaning, lubricating, inspecting, tightening |
| **Pillar 3: Planned Maintenance (Keikaku Hozen)** | 計画保全 | Maintenance department takes over higher-level preventive & predictive tasks |
| **Pillar 4: Quality Maintenance (Hinshitsu Hozen)** | 品質保全 | Zero-defect condition; PM analysis, P-Matrix, FMEA to prevent recurrence |
| **Pillar 5: Education & Training (Kyoiku)** | 教育 | Multi-skill development; "One-Best-Way" operation & maintenance skills |

### Category B — Cross-Functional Pillars (6 to 8)

| Pillar | Japanese Term | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Pillar 6: Safety, Health & Environment (SHE)** | Anzen Eisei | Zero accident, zero pollution; risk assessment & JSA |
| **Pillar 7: Office TPM (Administrative TPM)** | Ofisu TPM | Eliminates administrative & information losses (intangible losses in office) |
| **Pillar 8: TPM in Supply Chain / New Product / Logistics** | — | Extends TPM principles to vendors, design, and distribution |

## 2.2 The Six Big Losses (TPM Loss Classification)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Six Big Losses** is the **single most exam-relevant framework** in this module. These are the **6 categories of loss** that reduce OEE and are the direct target of every TPM activity.

The Six Big Losses are grouped into **3 categories** that correspond exactly to the **3 components of OEE**:

### A. Availability Losses (Downtime)

1. **Equipment Failure / Breakdown Losses** — Unplanned failures, sudden breakdowns.
2. **Setup & Adjustment Losses** — Changeover time, minor stoppages, idling after setup.

### B. Performance Losses (Speed)

3. **Reduced Speed Losses** — Operating below design speed (e.g., 80 RPM instead of 100 RPM).
4. **Idling & Minor Stoppage Losses** — Brief halts, no production, no major breakdown logged.

### C. Quality Losses (Defects)

5. **Process Defect Losses** — Defects produced during steady-state running (scrap & rework).
6. **Startup / Yield Losses** — Defects during warm-up, start-up, shutdown, or ramp-down.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute value expressions in exam answers to maintain table integrity.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Availability | $0 \le A \le 1$ (or 0%–100%) |
| 2 | $MTBF = \dfrac{\text{Total Uptime}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | Mean Time Between Failures | Hours |
| 3 | $MTTR = \dfrac{\text{Total Downtime}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | Mean Time To Repair | Hours |
| 4 | $MTTF = \dfrac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | Mean Time To Failure (non-repairable) | Hours |
| 5 | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | Overall Equipment Effectiveness | $0 \le OEE \le 1$ |
| 6 | $P = \dfrac{\text{Actual Output} \times \text{Ideal Cycle Time}}{\text{Operating Time}}$ | Performance Rate | $0 \le P \le 1$ |
| 7 | $Q = \dfrac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units Produced}}$ | Quality Rate | $0 \le Q \le 1$ |
| 8 | $OEE = \dfrac{\text{Good Units} \times \text{Ideal Cycle Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}$ | Direct OEE (single-shot) | $0 \le OEE \le 1$ |
| 9 | $\text{Losses} = 1 - OEE$ | Total loss fraction | $0 \le \text{Loss} \le 1$ |
| 10 | $\text{MTBF}_{new} = MTBF_{old} \times (1+r)^{n}$ | Reliability growth (Duane model) | r = growth rate per year |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **World-Class Benchmarks (JIPM / SEMI E10):**
> - World-class **OEE = 85%** (or 0.85)
> - World-class **Availability ≥ 90%**
> - World-class **Performance ≥ 95%**
> - World-class **Quality ≥ 99%**
>
> Any KTU numerical problem asking for "losses" or "improvement required to reach world-class" should anchor on these.

## 2.4 Why TPM? The Real-World Engineering Utility

TPM bridges the gap between **Lean philosophy (Muda elimination)** and **operational reliability engineering**. In a Lean system:

- **TPM eliminates MUDA (waste)** by attacking the *Six Big Losses*.
- **TPM enables JIT (Just-In-Time)** because unstable equipment destroys flow.
- **TPM supports Jidoka (Autonomation)** — a machine must be "in control" to detect abnormalities.
- **TPM feeds Kaizen** with quantified loss data, enabling **Pareto-based countermeasure selection**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever the question asks *"How does TPM support Lean?"*, the answer is: **TPM stabilizes equipment, which stabilizes flow, which is the prerequisite for JIT, Jidoka, and continuous flow** — all pillars of the Toyota Production System (TPS).

## 2.5 The 7-Step Autonomous Maintenance Implementation Model (JIPM)

This 7-step protocol is heavily tested in KTU Part B questions:

1. **Cleaning (Seiso)** — Initial deep cleaning to expose hidden defects ("Cleaning is inspection").
2. **Counter-measures for Sources of Contamination & Hard-to-Clean Areas** — Eliminate root causes of dirt, leak, spillage.
3. **Tentative (Draft) Cleaning & Lubrication Standards** — Create Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs).
4. **General Inspection (Jishu Kensa)** — Train operators in basic condition-monitoring skills (sight, sound, touch, temperature, vibration).
5. **Autonomous Inspection Standards** — Operators inspect using calibrated checklists & sensor readings.
6. **Process Confirmation & Standardization** — Quality and engineering verify that the autonomous process achieves target conditions.
7. **Autonomous Management / Step-Up** — Full operator ownership; maintenance department shifts to higher-skill activities.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of OEE from First Principles

We begin with the most fundamental definition of productive time and progressively expand it into the three OEE sub-metrics.

**Step 1: Define the available time horizon**

Let $T_P$ = Planned Production Time (i.e., total time the machine *should* have been running per shift, minus scheduled breaks).

$$T_P = T_{shift} - T_{planned\ stops}$$

**Step 2: Define Loading Time vs Operating Time**

Loading time accounts for **changeovers (Setup/Adjustment losses)**. Operating time is loading time minus **unplanned downtime (breakdown losses)**.

$$T_{L} = T_P - T_{setup} \quad \text{(Loading Time)}$$

$$T_{O} = T_{L} - T_{downtime} \quad \text{(Operating Time)}$$

**Step 3: Form the Availability Ratio**

Availability is the fraction of *Planned Production Time* that the machine was actually running.

$$A = \dfrac{T_{O}}{T_{P}} = \dfrac{T_{P} - T_{downtime} - T_{setup}}{T_{P}}$$

This single ratio combines **both Availability losses** (breakdown + setup) into one number.

**Step 4: Form the Performance Ratio**

Performance Rate compares *actual cycle output* to *ideal cycle output* during the time the machine was running.

$$P = \dfrac{\text{Actual Output} \times T_{ideal}}{\text{Operating Time}}$$

where $T_{ideal}$ is the *ideal cycle time per unit* (e.g., 30 seconds per part).

**Step 5: Form the Quality Ratio**

$$Q = \dfrac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units Produced}}$$

**Step 6: Combine into the OEE identity**

$$\boxed{OEE = A \times P \times Q = \dfrac{T_{O}}{T_{P}} \times \dfrac{\text{Actual Output} \times T_{ideal}}{T_{O}} \times \dfrac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Actual Output}}}$$

The $T_{O}$ and the *Actual Output* terms cancel out (telescoping product), leaving the elegant single-shot identity:

$$\boxed{OEE = \dfrac{\text{Good Units} \times T_{ideal}}{T_{P}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** The *telescoping* derivation above is a frequent Part B (14-mark) question. Memorize the cancellation — it demonstrates that **OEE is independent of internal time measurements** and depends only on **good units produced vs ideal capacity**.

## 3.2 Derivation of Availability from MTBF and MTTR

**Step 1: Define the Total Cycle Time** for a single failure-repair cycle.

$$T_{cycle} = MTBF + MTTR$$

**Step 2: Define the failure rate $\lambda$ and repair rate $\mu$**

$$\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF} \qquad \mu = \dfrac{1}{MTTR}$$

**Step 3: Form the steady-state Availability**

The fraction of time the equipment is *up* equals the ratio of uptime duration to total cycle duration.

$$\boxed{A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}}$$

**Step 4: Limiting behavior analysis**

- If $MTTR \to 0$ (instantaneous repair), then $A \to 1$ (perfect availability).
- If $MTBF \to \infty$ (no failures), then $A \to 1$.
- If $MTBF \to 0$ (constant failures), then $A \to 0$.

This shows that **TPM improves availability by either increasing MTBF (reliability) or decreasing MTTR (maintainability)**.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Standard KTU Pattern)

> **Problem:** A CNC turning cell operates for **480 minutes per shift**. It experiences:
> - 2 breakdowns: 45 min & 30 min
> - 1 setup: 25 min
> - 1 minor stoppage: 10 min
> - 1 speed reduction: produces 80 parts at 90 sec each (ideal cycle = 60 sec)
> - 3 defects out of 80 parts
>
> **Compute Availability, Performance, Quality, and OEE.**

**Solution — Step 1: Planned Production Time**

$$T_P = 480\ \text{minutes}$$

**Solution — Step 2: Compute Downtime (Availability Losses)**

$$T_{down} = 45 + 30 + 25 + 10 = 110\ \text{minutes}$$

**Solution — Step 3: Operating Time**

$$T_O = 480 - 110 = 370\ \text{minutes}$$

**Solution — Step 4: Availability**

$$A = \dfrac{370}{480} = 0.7708 = 77.08\%$$

**Solution — Step 5: Performance Rate**

Using *Actual Output × Ideal Cycle Time ÷ Operating Time*:

$$P = \dfrac{80 \times 60}{370 \times 60} = \dfrac{80}{370} = 0.2162$$

Wait — this gives a non-intuitive value. The issue: the *Actual Output × Ideal Cycle* represents the *ideal time to make 80 parts at the ideal cycle*. We must compare this to *Operating Time*.

$$P = \dfrac{80 \times 1.0\ \text{min}}{370\ \text{min}} = 0.2162 = 21.62\%$$

This low value correctly indicates **major speed losses**. The standard convention in many KTU numerical problems normalizes as:

$$P = \dfrac{\text{Actual Cycle Time}}{\text{Ideal Cycle Time}}\ \text{(or its inverse)}$$

Using the canonical SEMI E10 form:

$$P = \dfrac{\text{Ideal Output Rate}}{\text{Actual Output Rate}} = \dfrac{80 \times (60/60)}{80 \times (90/60)} = \dfrac{80}{120} = 0.6667 = 66.67\%$$

We adopt this canonical form: **P = 66.67%**.

**Solution — Step 6: Quality Rate**

$$Q = \dfrac{80 - 3}{80} = \dfrac{77}{80} = 0.9625 = 96.25\%$$

**Solution — Step 7: OEE**

$$OEE = A \times P \times Q = 0.7708 \times 0.6667 \times 0.9625 = 0.4946 = 49.46\%$$

**Solution — Step 8: Loss Gap to World Class**

$$\text{Loss} = 1 - 0.4946 = 0.5054 = 50.54\%$$

$$\text{Gap to World Class} = 0.85 - 0.4946 = 0.3554 = 35.54\ \text{percentage points}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Six Big Losses → OEE Component Mapping

| # | Loss Category | Sub-Type | OEE Component | Typical Countermeasure (TPM Tool) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Availability | Equipment Failure | A (reduces numerator) | Predictive Maintenance, RCM, RCA |
| 2 | Availability | Setup & Adjustment | A (reduces numerator) | SMED (Single-Minute Exchange of Die) |
| 3 | Performance | Idling & Minor Stops | P (throughput loss) | 5S, Autonomous Maintenance, Poke-Yoke |
| 4 | Performance | Reduced Speed | P (throughput loss) | Design-of-Experiments, Cycle time reduction |
| 5 | Quality | Process Defects | Q (rejects produced) | Poka-Yoke, SPC, Quality Maintenance (Pillar 4) |
| 6 | Quality | Reduced Yield | Q (startup scrap) | TPM Startup Standards, Warm-up procedures |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** A common 14-mark question is: *"Explain the Six Big Losses with examples and map each to the relevant TPM pillar."* The matrix above is your exam-ready answer.
