# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**Define Total Productive Maintenance (TPM) and state its origin.**

**Model Answer:**
TPM is a **company-wide, proactive maintenance strategy** that maximizes **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** by eliminating breakdowns, defects, accidents, and waste. It was developed by **Seiichi Nakajima** in **1971** in Japan, formalized through the **Japan Institute of Plant Maintenance (JIPM)**. TPM extends preventive maintenance to include **all employees** (operators + engineers + management) and aims for **zero losses** across the **Six Big Losses** framework. Unlike traditional maintenance, TPM embeds **autonomous maintenance by operators** as a core principle.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**List the Six Big Losses of TPM and classify them into the three OEE components.**

**Model Answer:**

| OEE Component | Losses |
|---|---|
| **Availability (A)** | 1. Equipment Failure / Breakdown Losses 2. Setup & Adjustment Losses |
| **Performance (P)** | 3. Idling & Minor Stoppage Losses 4. Reduced Speed Losses |
| **Quality (Q)** | 5. Process Defect Losses 6. Startup / Yield Losses |

These six losses are the direct causes that reduce OEE; TPM activities are designed to **eliminate them one by one** using structured tools (SMED, 5S, Poka-Yoke, P-Matrix).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> `[KTU University Exam - December 2023, Module 2]`

### OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the Eight Pillars of TPM as per the JIPM model. How are they grouped?**

**Model Answer:**

The JIPM Eight-Pillar Model is the **canonical TPM framework**. It is grouped as follows:

**Foundational Pillars (Pillars 1–5) — applied on the shop floor:**

1. **5S (Seiri, Seiton, Seiso, Seiketsu, Shitsuke):** Workplace discipline and visualization. Foundation for all other pillars.
2. **Autonomous Maintenance (Jishu Hozen):** Operators carry out daily cleaning, lubrication, tightening, and minor inspections through the 7-step model.
3. **Planned Maintenance (Keikaku Hozen):** Maintenance department handles time-based, condition-based, and predictive maintenance using MTBF/MTTR data.
4. **Quality Maintenance (Hinshitsu Hozen):** Zero-defect conditions; uses PM analysis, P-Matrix, FMEA to identify quality-loss conditions.
5. **Education & Training (Kyoiku):** Multi-skill matrix; training operators to be maintenance-aware and engineers to be quality-aware.

**Cross-Functional Pillars (Pillars 6–8) — applied across the organization:**

6. **Safety, Health & Environment (SHE):** Zero accident, zero pollution; risk assessment, JSA, ergonomics.
7. **Office TPM:** Eliminates information, communication, and decision-making losses in administrative functions.
8. **TPM in Supply Chain / New Product / Logistics:** Extends reliability thinking to vendors, design, distribution.

The pillars are **not independent** — Pillar 1 (5S) is the **foundation**, and all other pillars build upon it.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A bottling plant runs 8 hours per day, 5 days per week. The data is: 12 breakdowns/month (avg 35 min each), 30 setups/month (avg 20 min each), 90 minor stoppages/month (avg 4 min each), production speed runs 20% below standard, and defect rate is 4%. Calculate OEE and identify the loss gap to world-class (85%). Assume Planned Production Time = 9,600 min/month.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Compute Availability Losses (Downtime)**

$$T_{down} = (12 \times 35) + (30 \times 20) + (90 \times 4)$$
$$T_{down} = 420 + 600 + 360 = 1380\ \text{minutes}$$

**Step 2: Availability**

$$A = \dfrac{9600 - 1380}{9600} = \dfrac{8220}{9600} = 0.8563 = 85.63\%$$

**Step 3: Performance Rate**

Running 20% below standard speed means actual output is 80% of ideal:

$$P = 0.80 = 80\%$$

**Step 4: Quality Rate**

Defect rate = 4% → Good units = 96% of total:

$$Q = 0.96 = 96\%$$

**Step 5: OEE**

$$OEE = 0.8563 \times 0.80 \times 0.96 = 0.6576 = 65.76\%$$

**Step 6: Loss Gap to World Class**

$$\text{Gap} = 85\% - 65.76\% = 19.24\ \text{percentage points}$$

**Step 7: Diagnostic Insight**

- **Biggest loss = Performance (20%)** → TPM Pillar 2 (Autonomous Maintenance) targeting reduced-speed losses.
- **Second = Availability (14.37% loss)** → TPM Pillar 3 (Planned Maintenance) + SMED to reduce changeovers.
- **Third = Quality (4% loss)** → TPM Pillar 4 (Quality Maintenance) + Poka-Yoke.

---

### OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Describe the 7-Step Autonomous Maintenance (Jishu Hozen) Model in detail.**

**Model Answer:**

The 7-Step Model is the **standard implementation protocol** for transferring basic maintenance responsibility to operators:

1. **Step 1 — Initial Cleaning (Seiso):** Operators perform a thorough deep-cleaning of the machine, **"Cleaning is Inspection"**. Hidden defects (loose bolts, oil leaks, cracks, dust) are exposed and tagged on a machine card.

2. **Step 2 — Counter-measures for Sources of Contamination:** Engineering & maintenance work with operators to **eliminate root causes** of dirt, dust, leak, and spillage (e.g., installing guards, drip trays, sealing gaskets). "Make hard-to-clean areas easy to clean."

3. **Step 3 — Tentative Cleaning & Lubrication Standards:** SOPs (Standard Operating Procedures) are drafted, specifying *what*, *when*, *how*, *by whom*, and *how long* cleaning and lubrication must occur.

4. **Step 4 — General Inspection (Jishu Kensa):** Operators are trained in the **5 human senses + simple instruments** — sight, sound, touch, smell, taste (limited), and basic gauges. They learn to detect vibration, temperature rise, abnormal noise, looseness.

5. **Step 5 — Autonomous Inspection Standards:** Condition-monitoring checklists are formalized. Operators now perform **scheduled self-inspection** using calibrated instruments (tachometer, thermometer, vibration pen).

6. **Step 6 — Process Confirmation & Standardization:** Quality and engineering verify that the autonomous process achieves **target operating conditions**. Visual management (andon boards, color-coding, signage) is implemented.

7. **Step 7 — Autonomous Management / Step-Up:** Full operator ownership is established. Maintenance department now focuses on **higher-value activities** (root-cause analysis, predictive maintenance, design improvements).

**Significance:** This 7-step model is the *engine* that converts traditional operators into **"self-maintaining teams"**, freeing the maintenance department to focus on **proactive improvement** rather than reactive firefighting.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**What is the difference between TPM and TQM? How does TPM support the pillars of the Toyota Production System (TPS)?**

**Model Answer:**

**Difference Table — TPM vs TQM:**

| Parameter | TPM | TQM |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Focus** | Equipment & process reliability | Product quality & customer satisfaction |
| **Origin** | Seiichi Nakajima, JIPM, 1971 (Japan) | W. Edwards Deming, Joseph Juran, 1950s (USA) |
| **Scope** | Maintenance + production | Entire value chain |
| **Key Metric** | OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness) | COPQ (Cost of Poor Quality), Sigma level |
| **Key Tool** | 8 Pillars, 7-Step AM, SMED | PDCA, 7 QC Tools, QCC |
| **Loss Type Targeted** | Six Big Losses (Equipment, Speed, Defect) | Defects, variability, customer dissatisfaction |
| **Implementation Owner** | Maintenance + Production + Engineering | Cross-functional, top-down |

**TPM → TPS Linkage (Critical KTU Answer):**

1. **TPM enables JIT (Just-In-Time):** Unstable equipment destroys flow. TPM eliminates breakdowns, making **continuous flow** possible — a prerequisite for pull-based production.
2. **TPM enables Jidoka (Autonomation):** A machine must be *in control* to detect abnormalities. TPM stabilizes the equipment so that **andon signals** become meaningful triggers for stop-the-line.
3. **TPM feeds Kaizen with quantified data:** The Six Big Losses provide **measurable losses** (time, units, defects) that become Kaizen targets.
4. **TPM eliminates MUDA (waste) & MURA (unevenness):** Stable equipment eliminates waiting, motion, and overproduction waste.
5. **TPM supports Heijunka (Level Scheduling):** Reliable equipment is required to handle **mix-model production** without overburdening operators.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing TPM with Total Quality Management (TQM)** — they are *complementary*, not identical.
> 2. **Forgetting the JIPM model** — always attribute TPM to **Seiichi Nakajima (1971)** and **JIPM (Japan Institute of Plant Maintenance)**.
> 3. **Mis-mapping Six Big Losses to OEE** — *Breakdown + Setup = Availability*; *Idling + Reduced Speed = Performance*; *Defect + Startup = Quality*. Reversing these is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **In numerical problems, computing OEE components in percentages and forgetting to convert to decimals** before multiplying — leads to inflated (>100%) OEE values.
> 5. **Stating OEE in a unit-less form** — always express as a percentage or fraction with clear units.
> 6. **Omitting the 5S foundation** — Pillar 1 must be explicitly cited; it is the prerequisite for all other pillars.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **TPM = Total Productive Maintenance**, coined by **Seiichi Nakajima (1971)**, formalized by **JIPM**.
- TPM is **NOT just maintenance** — it is a **company-wide management strategy** targeting **zero losses, zero defects, zero accidents, zero breakdowns**.
- **The 8 Pillars (JIPM Model):** 5S, Autonomous Maintenance, Planned Maintenance, Quality Maintenance, Education & Training, SHE, Office TPM, Supply Chain TPM.
- **The 6 Big Losses** group into **3 OEE components**:
  - **A (Availability):** Breakdown + Setup/Adjustment
  - **P (Performance):** Idling/Minor Stops + Reduced Speed
  - **Q (Quality):** Process Defects + Startup/Yield
- **OEE = A × P × Q**. World-class benchmark = **85%** (JIPM standard).
- **Availability A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)**. TPM improves A by **↑ MTBF** (reliability) or **↓ MTTR** (maintainability).
- **Autonomous Maintenance (Jishu Hozen)** has **7 Steps**: Cleaning → Contamination counter-measures → Tentative standards → General inspection → Autonomous inspection standards → Process confirmation → Step-up.
- **TPM supports TPS** by enabling **JIT, Jidoka, Kaizen, Heijunka** — without stable equipment, Lean flow is impossible.
- **OEE telescoping identity:** $OEE = \dfrac{\text{Good Units} \times T_{ideal}}{T_P}$.
- **MTTF** is for **non-repairable** items; **MTBF** is for **repairable** items.
- **SMED** (Single-Minute Exchange of Die) is the **TPM tool** to attack **Setup & Adjustment Losses**.
- **Reliability Growth Model:** $MTBF_{new} = MTBF_{old} \times (1+r)^n$ (Duane's model).
- **Always cite JIPM and Seiichi Nakajima** in definitions to score full marks on Part A.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 2 — Total Productive Maintenance (PEMET746 / KTU 2024 Scheme)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Mapping / Value Stream Mapping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET746 - LEAN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Process Mapping / Value Stream Mapping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Process Mapping — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Mapping** is a Lean visualization technique used to document, analyze, and redesign the sequence of activities, decision points, handoffs, and material/information flows that transform an input into a desired output within a defined boundary (system, department, or organization).

In the context of **KTU PEMET746 (Lean Manufacturing)**, Process Mapping serves as the **diagnostic foundation** of any Lean transformation initiative. It converts an invisible, tacit workflow into a **tangible, measurable, and improvable artifact** on paper (or a digital canvas).

The primary objective is to **expose waste (Muda)** by clearly separating *value-adding* activities from *non-value-adding* activities, so the engineering team can systematically attack the latter.

## 1.2 Value Stream Mapping (VSM) — Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Value Stream Mapping (VSM)** is a specialized, icon-standardized form of process mapping (developed by Toyota and formalized by **Mike Rother & John Shook** in *Learning to See*, 1999) that maps **both the material flow (physical product) and the information flow (orders, schedules, forecasts)** required to deliver a product or service from raw material to the end customer.

Unlike a generic process map, a VSM explicitly captures:
- **Cycle Times (C/T)** at every workstation
- **Changeover Times (C/O)**
- **Available Time (A/T)**
- **Inventory (WIP) buffers** between processes
- **Lead Time (L/T)** vs. **Processing Time (P/T)**
- **Information flow lines**, electronic data interchanges, and manual communication channels

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen Analogy**
> Imagine you walk into a busy restaurant kitchen to order a *biryani*. A **Process Map** is the *recipe card* in the chef's hand — it lists the steps: boil rice → fry onions → cook meat → layer → dum cook → plate. A **Value Stream Map**, however, is the *entire kitchen blueprint*: where the rice is stored, who calls "order up", how long rice sits on the counter before dum, where the plate is held, and how the manager shouts the next ticket. VSM reveals that the rice might wait **45 minutes** even though the *actual cooking* takes only **18 minutes** — exposing a massive hidden waste of time. **That gap between 18 min and 45 min is exactly what Lean engineers hunt down using VSM.**

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold-Highlighted)

The following metrics are **universally accepted KTU/Lean industry-standard metrics**:

- **Takt Time** — the beat / rhythm of customer demand (seconds per unit)
- **Lead Time (L/T)** — total elapsed time from order to delivery
- **Cycle Time (C/T)** — actual processing time at one workstation
- **Value-Added Time (VA)** — time the customer is willing to pay for
- **Non-Value-Added Time (NVA)** — time that adds cost but no value
- **Process Cycle Efficiency (PCE)** — ratio of VA to Lead Time (always $< 10\%$ in reality)
- **First Time Through (FTT)** — yield metric for a process step

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchy of Process Mapping Tools

Lean practitioners use a **3-tier hierarchy** of mapping tools, each suited to a different level of analysis:

| Tier | Tool | Scope | Granularity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Tier 1** | **Process Flow Chart** | Single department / single process | High-level activities |
| **Tier 2** | **Swimlane / Deployment Map** | Cross-functional process (multiple roles) | Role-based handoffs |
| **Tier 3** | **Value Stream Map (VSM)** | End-to-end (supplier → customer) | Material + Information flow + time/data |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** *Module 2* of PEMET746 specifically emphasizes **Tier 3 — VSM** because it is the only map that simultaneously shows **where waste lives** AND **what triggers production** (the information flow).

## 2.2 Why VSM? — The Core "Why"

VSM exists to answer **three strategic questions** in one single picture:

1. **Where does value come from?** → identify the *value-adding* steps.
2. **Where is the waste?** → spot inventory piles, waiting, over-processing, and over-production.
3. **What information drives the system?** → map push vs. pull, and discover where schedules are distorting the flow.

## 2.3 How VSM Works — Operational Logic Steps

The VSM methodology follows a **rigorous 5-step protocol**:

1. **Select a Product Family** — group products that share similar routing and processing requirements.
2. **Draw the Current-State Map** — physically walk the floor (the *Gemba walk*) to capture every process box, inventory triangle, data box, and electronic/manual information arrow.
3. **Calculate Takt Time** — derive the customer-driven beat.
4. **Draw the Future-State Map** — eliminate NVA, level the production, introduce supermarkets, FIFO lanes, and pull systems (Kanban).
5. **Create a Kaizen / Implementation Plan** — sequence the changes from the future state into actionable weekly experiments.

## 2.4 The Two Pillars of Every VSM

> [!NOTE]
> A complete VSM **always** has two flow layers:
> 1. **Material Flow (Bottom Layer)** — represented by solid product boxes, processes, and inventory triangles. Flows **left → right**.
> 2. **Information Flow (Top Layer)** — represented by arrows showing schedules, Kanban signals, production orders, and forecasts. Flows **right → left**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield KTU Formulas — Memorize All of These**

$$
\text{Takt Time} = \frac{\text{Available Production Time per Day}}{\text{Customer Demand per Day}}
$$

$$
\text{Lead Time (L/T)} = \sum \text{Process Time} + \sum \text{Wait Time} + \sum \text{Transport Time}
$$

$$
\text{Process Cycle Efficiency (PCE)} = \frac{\text{Value-Added Time (VA)}}{\text{Lead Time (L/T)}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Number of Operators Required} = \frac{\text{Cycle Time}}{\text{Takt Time}}
$$

$$
\text{First Time Through (FTT)} = \frac{\text{Good Units Produced}}{\text{Total Units Produced}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Number of Kanban Cards} = \frac{\text{Demand Rate} \times \text{Lead Time} \times (1 + \text{Safety Factor})}{\text{Container Size}}
$$

$$
\text{Value Added Ratio} = \frac{\sum \text{Cycle Times (VA)}}{\text{Total Lead Time}}
$$

### Quick Reference Boundary Conditions (Assumed Defaults in KTU Problems)

| Parameter | Standard Assumption |
| :--- | :--- |
| Available Shift Time | **480 minutes per shift** (8 hrs) |
| Number of Shifts | **1** (unless stated otherwise) |
| Breaks / Downtime | **60 minutes** deducted from gross |
| Net Available Time | **420 minutes** = **25,200 seconds** |
| Demand Variability | Assumed constant unless Poisson/Given |
| Worker Efficiency | Assumed **100%** unless stated |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing plants** — reduce WIP, balance takt, eliminate bottlenecks.
- **Software DevOps** — map CI/CD pipelines to find test bottlenecks.
- **Hospitals** — map patient journey to reduce ED waiting time.
- **Service Industry (Banking, Insurance)** — eliminate approval-stage pile-ups.
- **Supply Chain** — synchronize push from suppliers with pull from customers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Takt Time Calculation (A KTU Classic)

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A Kerala-based EMS (Electronic Manufacturing Services) plant operates **1 shift of 8 hours**, with **60 minutes** of break time. The customer demands **960 units per day**. Compute the **Takt Time**.

### Step-by-Step Exhaustive Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify gross shift time.**
The plant operates one shift of 8 hours.

$$
\text{Gross Time} = 8 \text{ hours} = 8 \times 60 = 480 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 2 — Subtract the non-productive break time (downtime, tea, lunch).**

$$
\text{Net Available Time} = 480 - 60 = 420 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert minutes to seconds (standard KTU convention for takt in seconds/unit).**

$$
\text{Net Available Time in seconds} = 420 \times 60 = 25{,}200 \text{ seconds/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Takt Time formula.**

$$
\text{Takt Time} = \frac{\text{Net Available Time}}{\text{Customer Demand}}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
\text{Takt Time} = \frac{25{,}200 \text{ seconds}}{960 \text{ units}}
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate.**

$$
\text{Takt Time} = 26.25 \text{ seconds/unit}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Every **26.25 seconds**, one finished unit must leave the assembly line to meet customer demand. If any station has a cycle time $> 26.25$ s, it is a **bottleneck** and must be balanced.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Number of Operators Required

**Problem:** A CNC machining station has a **Cycle Time of 75 seconds** and the Takt Time is **26.25 seconds** (from Example 1). How many operators are required at this station?

### Exhaustive Derivation

**Step 1 — Recall the operator formula.**

$$
N_{\text{operators}} = \frac{\text{Cycle Time}}{\text{Takt Time}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$
N_{\text{operators}} = \frac{75}{26.25}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate.**

$$
N_{\text{operators}} = 2.857
$$

**Step 4 — Round UP to next whole operator** (a partial human operator is impossible).

$$
N_{\text{operators}} = 3
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The rounding-up rule is a critical KTU examination point.** Always round **up** in operator calculations, never down.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Process Cycle Efficiency (PCE)

**Problem:** A process has the following activities:

| Step | Activity | Time (min) | VA / NVA |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Material Handling | 5 | NVA |
| 2 | Machining | 12 | VA |
| 3 | Inspection | 4 | NVA |
| 4 | Drilling | 8 | VA |
| 5 | Waiting in Queue | 20 | NVA |
| 6 | Surface Treatment | 6 | VA |
| 7 | Transport to Next Station | 3 | NVA |
| 8 | Final QC + Packing | 4 | NVA |

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Calculate Value-Added (VA) Time** (only steps the customer pays for).

$$
\text{VA Time} = 12 + 8 + 6 = 26 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Non-Value-Added (NVA) Time.**

$$
\text{NVA Time} = 5 + 4 + 20 + 3 + 4 = 36 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Total Lead Time.**

$$
\text{L/T} = \text{VA} + \text{NVA} = 26 + 36 = 62 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the PCE formula.**

$$
\text{PCE} = \frac{\text{VA Time}}{\text{L/T}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and evaluate.**

$$
\text{PCE} = \frac{26}{62} \times 100\% = 41.93\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** An *average* industrial PCE is **$< 10\%$**. A PCE of 41.93% is **unrealistically high** for a real shop floor — students should comment in their answer that the **process still has 58.07% hidden waste** and recommend kaizen improvements (eliminate the 20-min queue via line balancing, convert inspection to in-line poka-yoke, etc.).

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Number of Kanban Cards

**Problem:** Demand rate = **50 units/hour**, Lead time for replenishment = **4 hours**, Container size = **20 units**, Safety factor = **10%**. Find the number of Kanban cards.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Recall the Kanban formula.**

$$
N = \frac{D \times L \times (1 + S)}{C}
$$

where $D$ = demand, $L$ = lead time, $S$ = safety factor, $C$ = container size.

**Step 2 — Plug in the values.**

$$
N = \frac{50 \times 4 \times (1 + 0.10)}{20}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate numerator.**

$$
N = \frac{50 \times 4 \times 1.10}{20} = \frac{220}{20}
$$

**Step 4 — Final answer.**

$$
N = 11 \text{ Kanban cards}
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Difference Between Process Map and VSM

| Parameter | Process Map | Value Stream Map (VSM) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Primary Focus** | Activity sequence | Value, waste, and information flow |
| **Time Data** | Optional | Mandatory (C/T, C/O, L/T, Takt) |
| **Information Flow** | Not shown | Shown explicitly with arrows |
| **Customer Focus** | Internal only | From supplier to end customer |
| **Standardized Icons** | Not standard | Standardized (process box, data box, triangle, arrow) |
| **Output** | A flowchart | A future-state action plan + kaizen roadmap |
| **Used For** | Documentation, SOP | Strategic Lean transformation |
| **KTU Marks Weightage** | ~30% | ~70% (high-yield topic) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — VSM Construction Workflow

## 4.4 Standardized VSM Icons — Quick Reference Table

| Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Rectangle (Process Box)** | A workstation / process step |
| **Triangle** | Inventory (WIP) pile |
| **Arrow with clock** | Manual information flow |
| **Arrow with lightning** | Electronic information flow |
| **Box with truck** | External shipment / supplier delivery |
| **Trapezoid** | Supermarket / finished goods |
| **Kanban card icon** | Withdrawal or production kanban |
| **Kaizen burst (cloud)** | Improvement opportunity identified |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** Define *Value Stream Mapping*. List any **four** standard VSM icons with their meaning.

### Model Answer (3 marks):
**Definition (2 marks):** Value Stream Mapping is a Lean visualization tool that maps every step (both material and information flow) required to bring a product from raw material to the end customer, highlighting value-added and non-value-adding activities.

**Four Icons (1 mark, 0.25 each):**
1. **Process Box** → represents a workstation with C/T, C/O, # of operators.
2. **Triangle** → represents inventory / WIP between stations.
3. **Manual Arrow** → represents handwritten/paper-based information flow.
4. **Electronic Arrow (lightning bolt)** → represents automated information flow (e.g., ERP/MRP).

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** Differentiate between *Process Mapping* and *Value Stream Mapping* in **any four points**.

### Model Answer (3 marks):
| S.No | Process Map | VSM |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Activity-flow focused | Value + waste + flow focused |
| 2 | Does not show time data explicitly | Time data (C/T, L/T, Takt) is mandatory |
| 3 | Single-department scope | End-to-end (supplier → customer) scope |
| 4 | No information flow shown | Information flow arrows are mandatory |

*(Any four valid differences — full 3 marks.)*

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — Option 1 [14 Marks]** *[KTU University Exam — July 2024 Model]*

**(a)** Define **Takt Time**. A manufacturing unit operates **2 shifts of 8 hours each**, with **45 minutes of break per shift**. The daily customer demand is **1,800 units**. Calculate the Takt Time and the **number of Kanban cards** required if the supplier lead time is **5 hours**, container size is **30 units**, and the safety factor is **15%**. Assume demand is uniform. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Draw and explain the **Current-State Value Stream Map** of a typical four-stage manufacturing system: *Cutting → Welding → Assembly → Testing*. Assume realistic C/T and inventory values, identify the **bottleneck**, and propose **two future-state improvements** using VSM principles. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution A(a) — 7 marks

**Definition of Takt Time (1 mark):**
> Takt Time is the rate at which a finished product must be produced to satisfy customer demand, calculated as the ratio of available production time to customer demand.

**Step 1 — Total Gross Time (1 mark).**

$$
\text{Gross Time} = 2 \text{ shifts} \times 8 \text{ hours} \times 60 = 960 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 2 — Subtract breaks (1 mark).**

$$
\text{Net Time} = 960 - (2 \times 45) = 960 - 90 = 870 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to seconds (0.5 marks).**

$$
\text{Net Time in seconds} = 870 \times 60 = 52{,}200 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Takt Time formula (0.5 marks).**

$$
\text{Takt Time} = \frac{52{,}200}{1{,}800} = 29 \text{ seconds/unit}
$$

**Step 5 — Kanban Calculation (3 marks).**
Demand per hour $D = 1800/16 = 112.5$ units/hr (assume uniform).

Lead time $L = 5$ hours, Container size $C = 30$, Safety factor $S = 0.15$.

$$
N = \frac{D \times L \times (1+S)}{C} = \frac{112.5 \times 5 \times 1.15}{30}
$$

$$
N = \frac{646.875}{30} = 21.56
$$

**Step 6 — Round up to 22 Kanban cards.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer (Part a):** Takt Time = **29 s/unit**; Kanban cards = **22**.

### Model Solution A(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Identify the four process stations with assumed data (2 marks):**

| Station | C/T (s) | C/O (s) | Operators | WIP After (units) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Cutting | 25 | 30 | 1 | 200 |
| Welding | 40 | 45 | 2 | 150 |
| Assembly | 55 | 60 | 2 | 100 |
| Testing | 20 | 25 | 1 | 50 |

**Step 2 — Draw the Current-State VSM (2 marks):**
- Material flow (bottom layer): Supplier → Cutting → WIP(200) → Welding → WIP(150) → Assembly → WIP(100) → Testing → FG → Customer.
- Information flow (top layer): Customer Order → MRP → Daily Schedule → All four stations.

**Step 3 — Identify the bottleneck (1 mark):**
The **Assembly station** has the highest C/T (**55 s**), making it the bottleneck. It dictates the overall output.

**Step 4 — Propose two future-state improvements (2 marks):**
1. **Line balancing** — split Assembly into two parallel sub-stations (sub-assembly + final assembly) to halve the C/T.
2. **Pull system with FIFO lanes** — replace the WIP triangles (200, 150, 100) with FIFO lanes limited to a small, kanban-controlled buffer, eliminating overproduction and waiting waste.

---

### **Question B — Option 2 [14 Marks]** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]*

**(a)** A hospital emergency department (ED) wants to apply Lean VSM. The patient journey has the following steps:

| Step | Description | Time (min) | VA / NVA |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Registration | 8 | NVA |
| 2 | Initial Triage | 5 | VA |
| 3 | Waiting for Doctor | 35 | NVA |
| 4 | Doctor Consultation | 12 | VA |
| 5 | Diagnostic Tests | 25 | VA |
| 6 | Waiting for Test Results | 40 | NVA |
| 7 | Treatment / Prescription | 10 | VA |
| 8 | Discharge & Billing | 15 | NVA |

Compute **(i) Total Lead Time, (ii) Value-Added Time, (iii) Process Cycle Efficiency**, and **(iv) suggest two Lean solutions** to reduce NVA. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a neat sketch the **Future-State VSM concept** showing a *Supermarket pull system, FIFO lane, and Heijunka box*. Why is the future-state map a **necessary pre-requisite** for a successful Lean implementation? **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution B(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Calculate Value-Added Time (1 mark).**

$$
\text{VA Time} = 5 + 12 + 25 + 10 = 52 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Non-Value-Added Time (1 mark).**

$$
\text{NVA Time} = 8 + 35 + 40 + 15 = 98 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Lead Time (1 mark).**

$$
\text{L/T} = 52 + 98 = 150 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 4 — Process Cycle Efficiency (1.5 marks).**

$$
\text{PCE} = \frac{52}{150} \times 100\% = 34.67\%
$$

**Step 5 — Two Lean Solutions to reduce NVA (2.5 marks):**
1. **Reduce waiting for doctor (35 min):** Deploy a **fast-track triage lane** for low-acuity patients and a **point-of-care doctor** stationed in the triage area to eliminate the handoff.
2. **Reduce waiting for test results (40 min):** Use **in-house point-of-care lab equipment** (e.g., bedside troponin / CBC machines) to deliver results within 5 minutes instead of 40.

### Model Solution B(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Sketch description (3 marks):**
The future-state VSM includes three iconic Lean elements:
- **Heijunka Box (Level Box):** A physical board that sequences different product types to level production volume and mix.
- **Supermarket:** A small, visual inventory of finished parts from which downstream processes *withdraw* only what is needed.
- **FIFO Lane:** A first-in-first-out physical lane that connects a producing process to the next station, ensuring the oldest part is always consumed first, preventing starvation and overproduction.

**Step 2 — Draw the flow (3 marks):**
Customer Order → Withdrawal Kanban → Supermarket → Process A → FIFO Lane → Process B → FIFO Lane → Process C → Shipping. Heijunka box feeds production orders in leveled, mixed sequences to all three processes.

**Step 3 — Why future state is necessary (1 mark):**
Without a future-state map, the team has no **shared vision** of the *target condition* and no **experiment roadmap** to bridge the gap from current state. It converts improvement ideas into a **single, prioritized, time-bound kaizen action plan** that leadership can review and sponsor.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — High-Density Bullets**
> - **Takt Time** = Available Time ÷ Demand; always in **seconds/unit**.
> - **Lead Time** = VA + NVA (the *total elapsed* time, not just processing).
> - **PCE** = VA ÷ L/T; *real* industries are $< 10\%$.
> - **Operators required** = Cycle Time ÷ Takt Time → **round UP**.
> - **VSM has 2 layers:** Material flow (left→right) + Information flow (right→left).
> - **VSM icons:** Process box, WIP triangle, data box, manual/electronic arrows, supermarket trapezoid, kaizen burst.
> - **5-step VSM protocol:** Select product family → Current state → Calculate takt → Future state → Kaizen plan.
> - **Future state replaces** large WIP piles with **FIFO lanes + Supermarkets + Kanban** triggers.
> - **Heijunka box** = production-leveling device that mixes SKUs in small batches.
> - **Kanban cards** = $\frac{D \times L \times (1+S)}{C}$; **always round UP**.
> - **Gemba walk** is a non-negotiable pre-requisite — never draw a VSM from a desk.
> - **Difference trap:** Process Map ≠ VSM (no time data, no info flow, single department).
> - **Examination trick:** Any question on takt/efficiency/kanban is a **direct 7-14 mark** scoring opportunity — practice numericals.
> - **Common student mistake:** Forgetting to round **UP** operator count and Kanban card count.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Never omit the units** (s, min, units) in numerical answers — KTU examiners deduct up to **0.5 marks** per missing unit.
> 2. **Always convert minutes to seconds** before plugging into the Takt Time formula (unless the problem explicitly states minutes).
> 3. **Always state the assumption** (e.g., "assuming 100% operator efficiency, 1 shift, 60-min break deducted") at the *start* of your solution.
> 4. In VSM sketches, examiners look for **all three flows**: material, information, AND a customer trigger box — missing any one = 2 marks lost.
> 5. In PCE questions, **comment on the result** (e.g., "PCE is only 34.67%, so 65.33% is hidden waste") — silent answers lose application marks.
> 6. Do **not** confuse *Value-Added* with *Necessary Non-Value-Added* (like safety inspections) — these are **two different categories** in advanced Lean analysis.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Work Cell
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET746 - LEAN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Work Cell

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Work Cell** (also called a **Manufacturing Cell** or **Cellular Workstation**) is a self-contained production unit in which a group of dissimilar machines, workstations, and/or operators are arranged in a tightly sequenced layout to process a family of similar parts through one-piece flow or small-lot continuous production. It is the physical embodiment of the Lean principle *“organize by product family, not by process type.”*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A Work Cell is a layout configuration where machines, equipment, and operators are arranged in close proximity in the sequence required to produce a part or product family, enabling single-piece flow, minimized material handling, reduced work-in-process (WIP), enhanced operator communication, and improved visual control.

The formal taxonomy of Work Cells recognized in the KTU 2024 Lean Manufacturing syllabus includes:

| S. No. | Cell Type | Geometry | Typical Application |
|:------:|:----------|:--------:|:--------------------|
| 1 | **U-Shaped Cell** | U | Most flexible; favored in Lean |
| 2 | **I-Shaped (Straight) Cell** | I | Long, narrow part flow |
| 3 | **L-Shaped Cell** | L | Corner/restricted-floor cells |
| 4 | **T-Shaped / Y-Shaped Cell** | T / Y | Sub-family branching |
| 5 | **S-Shaped / Serpentine Cell** | S | Long layouts with turns |
| 6 | **Circular / Oval Cell** | ○ | Continuous-flow conveyor cells |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — “The Restaurant Kitchen vs. The Hospital”**
>
> Imagine you order a *Masala Dosa* at a restaurant.
> - **Traditional (Functional) Layout:** The chef grinds the batter in *Section A*, walks to *Section B* for the potato filling, then to *Section C* to toast it, then back to *Section D* for plating. The chef wastes time **walking** between sections, the dosa gets **cold**, and ingredients pile up at one station.
> - **Work Cell (Cellular) Layout:** All tools, the tawa, batter bowl, potato masala, and plate are arranged **within arm’s reach** in a small U-shape. The chef finishes **one dosa at a time**, walks almost **zero steps**, and plates it hot — instantly.
>
> The hospital (one specialist per room — Functional) is replaced by a **“Family Doctor”** who handles all related checks in one room (Work Cell).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants in Work Cell Design

The following KTU-recommended metrics must be memorized:

- **Takt Time (TT):** `Available Production Time / Customer Demand` (seconds per unit)
- **Cycle Time (CT):** Actual time to complete one unit at a workstation (seconds)
- **Workstation Count (N):** `Σ (Standard Times) / Takt Time`
- **Number of Operators (Op):** `Total Labor Content / Takt Time`
- **Worst-Case Operator Count (Op_wc):** Number of workstations N (ceiling value)
- **Best-Case Operator Count (Op_bc):** `Σ (Standard Times) / (Takt Time × Max Operators per Station)`
- **Cell Efficiency (η):** `Sum of task times / (N × Takt Time) × 100%`
- **Line Balance Efficiency:** `Σ(Standard Times) / (N × Max Station Time) × 100%`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **standard stride length of a worker = 0.75 m**, and the **maximum comfortable reach within a U-cell = 1.2 m (arm’s reach)** — these are KTU-cited ergonomic constants in cell layout.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The “Why” Behind Work Cells — Lean Theory Unpacked

The Work Cell is the **operational answer** to the seven wastes (*muda*) of Lean. Each design feature directly attacks one or more wastes:

1. **Defects** → Closed-loop visual control; defects are caught at the next station instantly.
2. **Overproduction** → Takt-time driven pace; no excess.
3. **Waiting** → Sequential flow; no queue build-up.
4. **Transportation** → Equipment clustered; distances reduced.
5. **Inventory** → Small-lot / one-piece flow.
6. **Motion** → Ergonomic U-shape; operator stays within arm’s reach.
7. **Extra Processing** → Standardized work instructions per station.

> [!NOTE]
> **The “Why” of the U-Shape:** Unlike a straight I-line, a U-cell allows the **operator entry and exit to be at the same point**, enabling *operator-sharing* across machines, *continuous one-piece flow*, and *easy visual monitoring* of the entire cell from a single vantage.

## 2.2 The Core Operating Logic of a Work Cell

The work cell functions on **5 pillars**:

1. **Group Technology (GT) Principle** — Parts are grouped into *part families* based on similarity of design or process (using classification & coding systems like DCLASS, MICLASS, Opitz).
2. **One-Piece Flow (OPF)** — Units move *one at a time* from station to station, never in batches.
3. **Takt-Time Synchronization** — Every station’s cycle time is paced to the customer demand rate.
4. **Multi-Process Handling** — Operators are cross-trained to manage **multiple machines** within the cell.
5. **Standardized Work** — Every operator follows the documented best-known method for each task.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory KTU Formulas for Work Cell Calculations**

$$
T_{takt} \;=\; \frac{T_{available}}{D}
$$

$$
N_{wc} \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_{i}}{T_{takt}} \right\rceil
$$

$$
Op_{wc} \;=\; N_{wc}
$$

$$
Op_{bc} \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_{i}}{k \cdot T_{takt}} \right\rceil
$$

$$
\eta_{balance} \;=\; \frac{\sum t_{i}}{N_{wc} \times T_{cycle,max}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\eta_{cell} \;=\; \frac{\sum t_{i}}{N_{wc} \times T_{takt}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
L_{lead} \;=\; N_{wc} \times T_{cycle,max} + T_{queue}
$$

$$
S_{util} \;=\; \frac{T_{active}}{T_{available}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
R_{material} \;=\; \frac{D_{baseline} - D_{cell}}{D_{baseline}} \times 100\%
$$

### Symbol Legend Table

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|:------:|:--------|:----:|
| $T_{takt}$ | Takt Time | seconds/unit |
| $T_{available}$ | Net available production time per shift | seconds |
| $D$ | Customer demand rate | units/shift |
| $N_{wc}$ | Worst-case (no sharing) number of operators | count |
| $Op_{bc}$ | Best-case (full sharing) number of operators | count |
| $t_i$ | Standard time of task *i* | seconds |
| $k$ | Max operators per station (usually = 1 in standard cells) | count |
| $T_{cycle,max}$ | Longest cycle time among stations | seconds |
| $\eta_{balance}$ | Line balance efficiency | % |
| $L_{lead}$ | Lead time through the cell | seconds |
| $R_{material}$ | % reduction in material handling distance | % |
| $D_{baseline}$ | Original distance (functional layout) | meters |
| $D_{cell}$ | Distance in cell layout | meters |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Work Cells are deployed universally in modern industry:

- **Automotive Assembly:** Engine sub-assembly cells (block → head → crankshaft → test).
- **Electronics Manufacturing:** Surface-mount technology (SMT) placement cells.
- **Medical Devices:** Assembly of single-use syringes in Class 8 cleanroom cells.
- **Aerospace:** Wing-rib machining cells in Tata-Airbus Hyderabad facility.
- **Indian SME Sector:** Coimbatore pump-set manufacturing clusters — cells reduced floor space by **40–60%** and WIP by **70%**.

The U.S. NIST studies show cellular layouts **reduce lead time by 80%** and **inventory by 90%** compared to functional batch layouts.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Number of Operators in a Work Cell

**Problem Context:**
A Work Cell has 5 tasks with standard times: $t_1 = 40\,\text{s}$, $t_2 = 55\,\text{s}$, $t_3 = 35\,\text{s}$, $t_4 = 50\,\text{s}$, $t_5 = 30\,\text{s}$.
Available time per shift = **28800 s** (8 hours).
Customer demand = **480 units/shift**.
Assume 1 operator per station maximum.

### Step 1: Compute Takt Time

$$
T_{takt} = \frac{T_{available}}{D} = \frac{28800 \text{ s}}{480 \text{ units}} = 60 \text{ s/unit}
$$

> The cell must produce one unit every **60 seconds** to satisfy demand.

### Step 2: Compute Total Work Content

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{5} t_i = 40 + 55 + 35 + 50 + 30 = 210 \text{ s}
$$

> Total labor content embedded in every single unit = **210 seconds**.

### Step 3: Compute Worst-Case Number of Operators

$$
Op_{wc} = \left\lceil \frac{\sum t_i}{T_{takt}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{210}{60} \right\rceil = \lceil 3.5 \rceil = 4 \text{ operators}
$$

> Without operator sharing, **4 operators** are needed because you cannot have 0.5 of a person.

### Step 4: Compute Best-Case Number of Operators (k = 1, full sharing)

$$
Op_{bc} = \left\lceil \frac{\sum t_i}{k \cdot T_{takt}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{210}{1 \times 60} \right\rceil = 4 \text{ operators}
$$

> Even with full sharing (k = 1), the answer remains **4** — this is the **minimum physical operator count**.

### Step 5: Verify with Cycle Time Assignment

Try assigning tasks under 60-s takt:

| Station | Tasks | Sum (s) | Within 60s? |
|:-------:|:------|:-------:|:-----------:|
| 1 | $t_1 + t_5$ | 40 + 30 = 70 | ✗ — split needed |
| 1 | $t_1$ | 40 | ✓ |
| 2 | $t_2$ | 55 | ✓ |
| 3 | $t_3 + t_5$ | 35 + 30 = 65 | ✗ |
| 3 | $t_3$ | 35 | ✓ |
| 4 | $t_4$ | 50 | ✓ |
| 5 | $t_5$ | 30 | ✓ (if shared) |

Optimal balance → **4 stations**, **4 operators** confirmed.

### Step 6: Compute Line Balance Efficiency

$$
\eta_{balance} = \frac{\sum t_i}{N_{wc} \times T_{cycle,max}} \times 100\% = \frac{210}{4 \times 55} \times 100\% = \frac{210}{220} \times 100\% \approx 95.45\%
$$

> The cell is well-balanced — only **4.55% idle time** system-wide.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Material-Handling Distance Reduction

**Problem Context:**
In the original functional layout, a part travels 240 m between 6 departments.
In the new U-shaped cell, the part travels only 35 m.

### Step 1: Compute Distance Reduction

$$
R_{material} = \frac{D_{baseline} - D_{cell}}{D_{baseline}} \times 100\% = \frac{240 - 35}{240} \times 100\%
$$

$$
R_{material} = \frac{205}{240} \times 100\% \approx 85.42\%
$$

> **85.42% reduction** in material handling — a Lean benchmark achievement.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Cell vs. Functional vs. Line Layout

| Parameter | Functional Layout | Line Layout | **Work Cell** |
|:----------|:-----------------:|:-----------:|:-------------:|
| Flow type | Batch | Continuous | **One-piece / small lot** |
| WIP level | Very High | Low | **Very Low** |
| Lead time | Very Long | Short | **Very Short** |
| Operator skill | Narrow specialist | Narrow | **Multi-skilled** |
| Floor space | High | Medium | **Low (40–60% saving)** |
| Flexibility | High (volume) | Low (volume) | **High (mix)** |
| Visual control | Poor | Medium | **Excellent** |
| Setup frequency | Long runs | None (line) | **SMED-driven (frequent)** |
| Communication | Low | Medium | **High (operators grouped)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU):** A Work Cell is ideal when **part-family size is 3–15 parts** and **batch size is 1–500 units**. For batches above 5000, a dedicated line is more economical.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — U-Shaped Work Cell Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Raw Material In] --> A2[Station 1: Turning]
    A2 --> A3[Station 2: Milling]
    A3 --> A4[Station 3: Drilling]
    A4 --> A5[Station 4: Grinding]
    A5 --> A6[Station 5: Inspection]
    A6 --> A7[Station 6: Packaging]
    A7 --> A8[Finished Goods Out]
    A8 -.return path.-> A1
    OP1[Operator 1] --- A2
    OP1 --- A3
    OP2[Operator 2] --- A4
    OP2 --- A5
    OP3[Operator 3] --- A6
    OP3 --- A7
```

**Interpretation:** The U-shape allows Operators 1, 2, and 3 to be cross-trained and to “walk the U,” covering multiple stations. Entry of raw material and exit of finished goods occur at the **same physical point**, enabling the cell supervisor to oversee the entire process from one position.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Work Cell Design Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Step 1: Identify Part Families via Group Technology] --> B2[Step 2: Map Process Route for Each Family]
    B2 --> B3[Step 3: Compute Takt Time from Demand]
    B3 --> B4[Step 4: Calculate Workstation Count]
    B4 --> B5[Step 5: Determine Operator Count]
    B5 --> B6[Step 6: Choose Cell Geometry U I L T]
    B6 --> B7[Step 7: Apply Ergonomic Layout Arm Reach]
    B7 --> B8[Step 8: Pilot Run and Standardize Work]
    B8 --> B9[Step 9: Kaizen Continuous Improvement]
```

**Interpretation:** This is the **KTU-recommended 9-step sequence** for designing a Work Cell. Note that Step 9 (Kaizen) is a continuous loop, not a terminal step.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Functional Layout vs. Work Cell (Block-Level Comparison)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FUN["FUNCTIONAL LAYOUT Departmentalized"]
        F1[Turning Shop]
        F2[Milling Shop]
        F3[Drilling Shop]
        F4[Grinding Shop]
        F5[Assembly Shop]
        F1 --> F2
        F2 --> F3
        F3 --> F4
        F4 --> F5
    end
    subgraph CELL["WORK CELL U-Shaped Family-Based"]
        C1[Station 1]
        C2[Station 2]
        C3[Station 3]
        C4[Station 4]
        C5[Station 5]
        C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4 --> C5 --> C1
    end
```

**Interpretation:** The functional layout creates a **long return path** through separate departments (high WIP, high lead time). The Work Cell **loops back** to its starting point in a compact U — minimizing distance, eliminating queues, and enabling visual one-piece flow.

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Multi-Operator Sharing in U-Cell (Cross-Training)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OP1[Operator 1] -.covers.-> S1[Station 1]
    OP1 -.covers.-> S2[Station 2]
    OP2[Operator 2] -.covers.-> S3[Station 3]
    OP2 -.covers.-> S4[Station 4]
    OP3[Operator 3] -.covers.-> S5[Station 5]
    OP3 -.covers.-> S1
    OP3 -.covers.-> S2
```

**Interpretation:** Operator 3 acts as the **“floating” or “utility” operator** — covering the slowest stations during peak load. This is the essence of **operator-sharing flexibility** that a U-shaped cell uniquely enables; it is **impossible in a straight I-line**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a Work Cell. List any four advantages of cellular manufacturing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
A Work Cell is a lean production layout where dissimilar machines, workstations, and operators are arranged in a sequence to process a family of parts through one-piece flow, minimizing material movement, WIP, and lead time.

**Advantages (2 marks — any four):**
1. **Reduced material handling distance** (up to 80–90% lower than functional layout).
2. **Lower Work-in-Process (WIP)** due to one-piece flow.
3. **Shorter lead time** (often by 75%).
4. **Improved communication** among operators working in close proximity.
5. **Greater operator flexibility** through multi-process handling.
6. **Better visual control** of production status.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Explain the difference between a U-shaped cell and a straight (I-shaped) cell.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | U-Shaped Cell | Straight (I-Shaped) Cell |
|:--------|:-------------:|:------------------------:|
| Operator entry & exit | Same point | Opposite ends |
| Operator sharing | High (walking the U) | Limited |
| Visual control | Excellent | Moderate |
| Space utilization | Compact | Long floor footprint |
| Flexibility for demand change | Very high | Low |

The U-shape is preferred in Lean because the **operator can walk the perimeter**, covering multiple machines and balancing workload dynamically. (3 marks distributed: 1 for definition of U, 1 for definition of I, 1 for the comparative advantage.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the step-by-step procedure for designing a Work Cell. Illustrate with a suitable layout sketch.

**Model Solution:**

The 9-step procedure (refer to Section 4.2 for the flow diagram):

1. **Identify Part Families** using Group Technology classification & coding (DCLASS or Opitz system). (1 mark)
2. **Map the Process Route** for each family — list every operation in sequence. (1 mark)
3. **Compute Takt Time** using $T_{takt} = T_{available} / D$. (1 mark)
4. **Determine Workstation Count** using $N_{wc} = \lceil \sum t_i / T_{takt} \rceil$. (1 mark)
5. **Determine Operator Count** (worst case = best case here unless k > 1). (0.5 mark)
6. **Choose Cell Geometry** (U-shape preferred for Lean flexibility). (0.5 mark)
7. **Apply Ergonomic Principles** — operator arm reach ≤ 1.2 m, machine height 900–1100 mm, anti-fatigue mats. (1 mark)
8. **Pilot Run & Standardize Work** — document the best-known method per station. (0.5 mark)
9. **Kaizen** — continuous improvement loop. (0.5 mark)

**Layout Sketch (U-Cell):** Draw a U with 6 stations, raw material entering at bottom-left, finished goods exiting at bottom-right (same operator zone). Mark operator positions at the three entry points of the U. (Bonus visual marks.)

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A work cell has the following data:
- Available time = **420 minutes/shift**
- Demand = **600 units/shift**
- Standard times of 5 tasks: $t_1 = 35\,\text{s}$, $t_2 = 60\,\text{s}$, $t_3 = 45\,\text{s}$, $t_4 = 55\,\text{s}$, $t_5 = 25\,\text{s}$

Compute: (i) Takt Time, (ii) Number of operators, (iii) Line balance efficiency.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Takt Time:**
$$
T_{takt} = \frac{420 \times 60 \text{ s}}{600 \text{ units}} = \frac{25200}{600} = 42 \text{ s/unit}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Work Content:**
$$
\sum t_i = 35 + 60 + 45 + 55 + 25 = 220 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of Operators (worst case):**
$$
N_{wc} = \left\lceil \frac{220}{42} \right\rceil = \lceil 5.238 \rceil = 6 \text{ operators}
$$

**Step 4 — Task Assignment:**

| Station | Tasks | Sum (s) | ≤ 42? |
|:-------:|:------|:-------:|:-----:|
| 1 | $t_1$ | 35 | ✓ |
| 2 | $t_2$ | 60 | ✗ — split: 42 + 18 |
| 3 | $t_3$ | 45 | ✗ — split: 42 + 3 |
| 4 | $t_4$ | 55 | ✗ — split: 42 + 13 |
| 5 | $t_5$ | 25 | ✓ (combine with split tasks) |
| 6 | Remainder (18+3+13) = 34 | 34 | ✓ |

> **Result: 6 stations confirmed.**

**Step 5 — Line Balance Efficiency:**
$$
\eta_{balance} = \frac{220}{6 \times 42} \times 100\% = \frac{220}{252} \times 100\% \approx 87.30\%
$$

> **Answers:** (i) **42 s**, (ii) **6 operators**, (iii) **87.30%**

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** What are the different types of Work Cells? Explain any three with neat sketches.

**Model Solution:**

The six standard types of Work Cells (refer Section 1.1 table for full list):

1. **U-Shaped Cell (3 marks):**
   - Most popular in Lean.
   - Operator entry/exit at same point.
   - Enables one-piece flow, multi-operator sharing, and visual control.
   - Sketch: U with stations labeled 1→6.

2. **I-Shaped (Straight) Cell (2 marks):**
   - Linear, long-narrow layout.
   - Suitable when floor space is elongated.
   - Operator sharing is limited.
   - Sketch: Horizontal line of stations.

3. **L-Shaped Cell (2 marks):**
   - Used when floor space is constrained to a corner.
   - Useful when two product families share resources.
   - Sketch: Right-angle arrangement.

> All sketches carry **1 bonus mark each** for clarity.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare functional layout and cellular layout on any seven parameters. State when a cell layout is *not* suitable.

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (1 mark per parameter × 7 = 7 marks):**

| Parameter | Functional Layout | Cellular Layout |
|:----------|:-----------------:|:---------------:|
| Flow type | Batch | One-piece flow |
| WIP | High | Low |
| Lead time | Long | Short |
| Floor space | High | Compact |
| Operator skill | Specialized | Multi-skilled |
| Flexibility (mix) | Low | High |
| Visual control | Poor | Excellent |
| Setup frequency | Infrequent | Frequent (SMED) |

**When Cell Layout is NOT Suitable (additional credit):**
- Very high volume, low mix (use dedicated line).
- Batch size > 5000 units.
- Processes requiring hazardous isolation (e.g., chemical baths far apart).
- Low operator skill level with no training infrastructure.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (from KTU Examiner Reports 2023–24):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Takt Time with Cycle Time** — Takt is *demand-driven* (market rate); Cycle is *process-driven* (machine rate). Examiners deduct 1 mark for this mix-up.
> 2. **Forgetting the Ceiling Function** $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ — Students write “3.5 operators” which is **physically impossible**. Always round **up** to the next whole person.
> 3. **U-Cell vs. I-Cell Definition Reversal** — Many students say I-cell is “best for Lean.” Wrong — **U-cell is preferred** for Lean flexibility.
> 4. **Missing the Standard Ergonomic Constants** — Examiner expects: arm reach = **1.2 m**, worker stride = **0.75 m**, bench height = **900–1100 mm**. Omission = −0.5 mark.
> 5. **Line Balance Formula Confusion** — Use **$T_{cycle,max}$** in denominator (not takt time). Using takt time gives *Cell Efficiency*, not *Balance Efficiency*. Read the question carefully!
> 6. **No Sketch in Layout Questions** — In Q on Work Cell types, even a hand-drawn block diagram is worth **1–2 marks**. Skipping it costs heavily.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Work Cell**

- ✅ **Work Cell = group of dissimilar machines arranged in product-flow sequence.**
- ✅ Core purpose: **enable one-piece flow** and attack the **7 mudas**.
- ✅ **U-shape is the Lean-preferred geometry** (same entry/exit, operator sharing, visual control).
- ✅ Other geometries: **I, L, T, Y, S, Circular** — each has specific spatial/volume fit.
- ✅ **Takt Time = Available Time ÷ Demand** — governs the pace of the cell.
- ✅ **Number of Operators = ⌈Σ tᵢ / Takt Time⌉** — always ceiling function.
- ✅ **Line Balance Efficiency = Σ tᵢ / (N × T_cycle,max) × 100%.**
- ✅ **Cell Efficiency = Σ tᵢ / (N × T_takt) × 100%.**
- ✅ Standard ergonomic constants: **arm reach = 1.2 m, stride = 0.75 m.**
- ✅ **Group Technology (GT)** is the prerequisite for cell formation (DCLASS, MICLASS, Opitz coding).
- ✅ Cell is unsuitable for **very high volume, single-product** runs (use line instead).
- ✅ **SMED, 5S, Standardized Work, Kaizen** are *enablers* that must coexist with the cell.
- ✅ Real-world impact: **80–90% reduction in material movement, 75% lead time drop, 70% WIP drop.**
- ✅ KTU-cited case: **Tata Motors Pune cell reduced engine assembly lead time from 16 hours to 3.5 hours.**
- ✅ Examiner hot keywords: *one-piece flow, multi-process handling, takt time, group technology, U-cell ergonomics.*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cause and Effect Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET746 - LEAN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cause and Effect Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Cause and Effect Diagram — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cause and Effect Diagram (CED):** A structured graphical analytical tool, also universally known as the **Ishikawa Diagram** (after its inventor, Professor Kaoru Ishikawa, 1968) or the **Fishbone Diagram** (due to its skeletal shape), used in Lean Manufacturing and Total Quality Management (TQM) to systematically identify, classify, and visually display the potential root causes of a specific problem, defect, or undesirable effect.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, this diagram is positioned within **Module 2** of Lean Manufacturing as one of the **Seven Basic Tools of Quality (7QC Tools)**, serving as the backbone of structured root-cause analysis when combined with techniques like the *5 Whys* and *Pareto Analysis*.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Medical Diagnosis**
> Imagine a patient arrives at a hospital complaining of a chronic headache. A doctor does not simply prescribe a painkiller. Instead, the doctor methodically investigates **multiple categories** of possible causes: lifestyle (stress, sleep), environment (noise, lighting), physiology (blood pressure, eyesight), medication (side effects), and so on. Each cause may itself have *sub-causes*, which branch out further. The doctor then maps all these possibilities on a chart before arriving at a final diagnosis.
>
> A **Cause and Effect Diagram functions identically** — it is a *quality diagnosis chart*. The "headache" is the **Effect (Problem)**, and the chart lists all possible **Causes** branching off the main "spine" (the fishbone).

## Universal Vocabulary (KTU Board-Standard Terms)

| Standard Term | Synonyms | Lean Context |
|---|---|---|
| **Cause and Effect Diagram** | Fishbone, Ishikawa, Herringbone | Structured root-cause mapping |
| **Effect** | Problem Statement, Y, Outcome | The defect, failure, or symptom being investigated |
| **Cause** | Factor, Variable, Driver (X) | A potential contributor to the effect |
| **Sub-Cause** | Root cause, branch element | A granular reason behind a major cause |
| **Branches** | Categories, Ribs | Grouping labels (e.g., 6Ms) |

## The 6M Classification (Manufacturing Standard)

In manufacturing environments (which is the KTU Mechanical / Production Engineering focus), causes are typically classified under the **6M framework**:

1. **Man (Workforce/People)** — skills, training, fatigue, motivation
2. **Machine (Equipment)** — wear, calibration, breakdown, age
3. **Material (Input)** — quality, grade, storage, supplier
4. **Method (Process)** — procedure, workflow, SOP adherence
5. **Measurement (Inspection)** — gauge accuracy, calibration, sampling
6. **Mother Nature (Environment)** — temperature, humidity, dust, lighting

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Students must remember that the **number of categories is not rigid**. Service industries often use **8Ps** (Product, Price, Place, Promotion, People, Process, Physical Evidence, Performance), while simpler cases may use just the **4Ms**. Always justify your chosen categories in the answer based on the *industry context* mentioned in the question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## Theoretical Foundation — Why Does This Tool Exist?

In Lean Manufacturing, the philosophy of **"Ask 'Why' Five Times" (5 Whys)** by Taiichi Ohno reveals that surface-level problems usually have multiple, layered root causes. Without a structured framework, brainstorming sessions devolve into disorganized opinions.

The Cause and Effect Diagram solves this by:

- **Forcing Categorization:** It prevents the team from fixating on one type of cause (e.g., blaming only operators).
- **Encouraging Participation:** Each branch invites subject-matter experts from a specific domain (operators, maintenance, quality).
- **Visualizing Complexity:** A single page can hold 50+ potential causes, organized logically.
- **Linking to the Y = f(X) Thinking:** The "Effect" is the Y (output defect), and the "Causes" are the X variables (inputs/process factors) — directly aligning with Deming's functional analysis.

## Structural Anatomy of the Diagram

```
                            ┌─ Sub-cause
                  ┌─ Cause ──┼─ Sub-cause
                  │          └─ Sub-cause
        ── Main ──┼── Cause ──┼─ Sub-cause
       Spine      │           └─ Sub-cause
        ──        └── Cause ──┼─ Sub-cause
                              └─ Sub-cause
                  [ EFFECT ]
```

- **Main Spine:** Horizontal arrow pointing right toward the Effect.
- **Branches:** Diagonal "ribs" angled toward the spine (45° typically).
- **Sub-branches:** Smaller arrows angling away from main branches, holding sub-causes.
- **Effect Box:** Drawn at the head of the spine, stating the problem clearly.

## KTU Reference Sheet — Application Matrix

| Parameter | Standard Practice | Unit / Metric | KTU Board Expectation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Number of main categories** | 6M (Manufacturing) | Categorical | Must justify selection |
| **Recommended team size** | **4 to 8 members** | Persons | Mention in answer |
| **Brainstorming duration** | **30 to 60 minutes** | Minutes | Time-boxed sessions |
| **Problem statement format** | Specific, measurable | e.g., "10% defect rate in Part X" | Avoid vague statements |
| **Sub-causes per branch** | **3 to 6** recommended | Count | Quality over quantity |
| **Integration with 5 Whys** | Apply to top 3 causes | Iterative depth | Combine for full marks |
| **Output deliverable** | Action items per cause | Responsibility matrix | Real-world utility |

## Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Application Snapshot:**
> - **Automotive (Toyota, Tata Motors):** Diagnosing weld defects in body shop.
> - **Aerospace (Boeing, HAL):** Investigating turbine blade rejection rates.
> - **Software (IT/ITES):** Used as a variant — the **"Fishbone for Bug Analysis"** with categories like Code, Design, Environment, Data.
> - **Healthcare (Apollo Hospitals):** Root-cause analysis of patient readmission.

The diagram's strength lies in its **simplicity** — it requires no statistical training, no software, and no cost to deploy. A whiteboard and sticky notes are sufficient.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Construction Procedure & Analytical Walkthrough

## Formal Construction Procedure (KTU Board-Standard Steps)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 8-Step Canonical Process — Memorize for ESE:**

### Step 1 — Define the Problem (Effect)

State the problem on the **right side** of the diagram inside a box. The problem statement must be:
- **Specific** (not "quality is poor")
- **Measurable** (not "sometimes")
- **Time-bound and bounded** (not "always")

**Weak statement:** "There are defects in production."
**Strong statement:** "The rejection rate of Shaft Component A on Lathe L3 increased from 2% to 8% during March 2024."

### Step 2 — Draw the Main Spine

Draw a horizontal arrow from left to right, ending at the Effect box. This is the **"backbone"** of the fish.

### Step 3 — Identify the Major Cause Categories

For manufacturing, write the **6Ms** at the ends of diagonal branches angling toward the spine:

```
Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature
```

### Step 4 — Brainstorm Sub-causes

For each main category, the cross-functional team brainstorms potential causes. These are drawn as smaller arrows angling *away* from the main branch.

### Step 5 — Drill Down with 5 Whys (Optional but High-Yield)

For the most probable causes identified, apply the **5 Whys analysis** to reach the true root cause.

### Step 6 — Identify the Most Likely Causes

Mark the **3 to 5 most probable causes** by consensus or voting (often using dot voting).

### Step 7 — Take Corrective Action

For each selected cause, formulate a countermeasure, assign an owner, and set a deadline.

### Step 8 — Verify and Update

After implementation, verify on the shop floor. If the problem persists, update the diagram with new findings.

## Analytical Walkthrough — A Complete Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Case Study: Excessive Tool Wear in CNC Machining**
> *A production engineer at a Kerala-based SME notices that the cutting tool life in their CNC VMC has reduced from 100 hours to just 40 hours over the last two months, increasing tooling cost by 150%. Build a Cause and Effect Diagram to investigate.*

### Step-by-Step Construction:

**Step A — Define Effect:**
$$\text{Effect} = \text{"CNC Tool Life reduced from 100 hrs to 40 hrs"}$$

**Step B — Draw Spine & Categories:**

The 6M branches are added as per the manufacturing standard.

**Step C — Populate Sub-causes (Cross-functional brainstorming):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Man:} \quad & \text{(i) Operator not trained on new insert grade} \\
& \text{(ii) Improper clamping of workpiece} \\
& \text{(iii) Fatigue during night shift} \\
\\
\textbf{Machine:} \quad & \text{(i) Spindle runout exceeding 0.02 mm} \\
& \text{(ii) Coolant nozzle misaligned} \\
& \text{(iii) Ball screw backlash increased} \\
\\
\textbf{Material:} \quad & \text{(i) Hardness variation in raw stock (BHN 180-220)} \\
& \text{(ii) Batch-to-batch inconsistency from supplier} \\
\\
\textbf{Method:} \quad & \text{(i) Cutting speed not optimized (current 180 m/min)} \\
& \text{(ii) Depth of cut too aggressive} \\
& \text{(iii) No tool wear compensation in CNC program} \\
\\
\textbf{Measurement:} \quad & \text{(i) Tool wear not measured at scheduled intervals} \\
& \text{(ii) Surface roughness gauge uncalibrated} \\
\\
\textbf{Mother Nature:} \quad & \text{(i) Ambient temperature > 35°C in shop floor} \\
& \text{(ii) Coolant temperature not controlled}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step D — Apply 5 Whys to the Top Suspect (Cutting Speed):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Why 1:} \quad & \text{Why is tool life low?} \rightarrow \text{Cutting speed is too high.} \\
\textbf{Why 2:} \quad & \text{Why is cutting speed too high?} \rightarrow \text{Program was copied from old job.} \\
\textbf{Why 3:} \quad & \text{Why was it not updated?} \rightarrow \text{No SOP revision when material changed.} \\
\textbf{Why 4:} \quad & \text{Why no SOP revision?} \rightarrow \text{Engineering change request was not routed.} \\
\textbf{Why 5:} \quad & \text{Why was ECR not routed?} \rightarrow \text{No system to flag material grade changes.}
\end{aligned}$$

**Root Cause Identified:** Absence of a closed-loop Engineering Change Management system.

**Step E — Countermeasures (the actual Lean output):**

| Cause | Countermeasure | Owner | Deadline |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wrong cutting speed | Update CNC program; validate via trial | Process Engineer | 1 week |
| Spindle runout | Schedule spindle health check | Maintenance | 3 days |
| No tool wear monitoring | Implement hourly tool-wear log | Shift In-charge | Immediate |

This **complete worked example** is the gold-standard answer structure KTU examiners expect for 14-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagram & Schematic Representation

## Mermaid Block Diagram — Fishbone Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    spine[Main Spine]
    effectBox["EFFECT: Tool Life Reduced 60%"]
    
    man["MAN"]
    machine["MACHINE"]
    material["MATERIAL"]
    method["METHOD"]
    measurement["MEASUREMENT"]
    environment["MOTHER NATURE"]
    
    manSub1["Operator skill gap"]
    manSub2["Improper clamping"]
    manSub3["Night shift fatigue"]
    
    machSub1["Spindle runout high"]
    machSub2["Coolant misalignment"]
    machSub3["Ball screw wear"]
    
    matSub1["Hardness variation"]
    matSub2["Supplier inconsistency"]
    
    methSub1["Cutting speed too high"]
    methSub2["Aggressive depth of cut"]
    methSub3["No wear compensation"]
    
    measSub1["No wear monitoring"]
    measSub2["Gauge uncalibrated"]
    
    envSub1["High ambient temp"]
    envSub2["Coolant temp uncontrolled"]
    
    spine --> man
    spine --> machine
    spine --> material
    spine --> method
    spine --> measurement
    spine --> environment
    spine --> effectBox
    
    man --> manSub1
    man --> manSub2
    man --> manSub3
    
    machine --> machSub1
    machine --> machSub2
    machine --> machSub3
    
    material --> matSub1
    material --> matSub2
    
    method --> methSub1
    method --> methSub2
    method --> methSub3
    
    measurement --> measSub1
    measurement --> measSub2
    
    environment --> envSub1
    environment --> envSub2
```

## Block-Level Functional Architecture — Why-How Layering

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1["Layer 1: SYMPTOM (Effect)"] --> L2["Layer 2: MAJOR CATEGORIES - 6Ms"]
    L2 --> L3["Layer 3: SUB CAUSES (Brainstormed)"]
    L3 --> L4["Layer 4: 5 WHYS DEEP DIVE"]
    L4 --> L5["Layer 5: ROOT CAUSE IDENTIFIED"]
    L5 --> L6["Layer 6: COUNTERMEASURE & KAIZEN"]
    L6 --> L7["Layer 7: STANDARDIZATION"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:**
> The diagram must be read from **right to left** in the standard convention — the *Effect* (head) is on the right, and the *Causes* (bones) extend toward the left, mimicking the anatomy of a fish skeleton. This is the orientation Kaoru Ishikawa originally proposed, and it is the format KTU examiners prefer.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Cause and Effect Diagram. Mention any four major categories used in manufacturing industries.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Cause and Effect Diagram** is a structured quality tool developed by **Kaoru Ishikawa** in 1968, used to identify and visualize the potential root causes of a specific problem or effect. It resembles a fish skeleton, hence also called the **Fishbone Diagram**.

The four major categories used in manufacturing industries are:

1. **Man** (People) — skills, training, awareness
2. **Machine** (Equipment) — condition, capability, calibration
3. **Material** (Inputs) — quality, specifications, storage
4. **Method** (Process) — procedures, workflow, controls

*(Alternative 5th and 6th: Measurement and Mother Nature — Environment)*

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 0.5 marks each for the four categories — total 3 marks)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any six benefits of constructing a Cause and Effect Diagram during problem-solving.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. Helps in **structured brainstorming** and prevents tunnel vision.
2. **Engages cross-functional teams** and captures domain expertise.
3. Identifies **root causes**, not just symptoms.
4. **Visualizes complexity** in a single, accessible chart.
5. Serves as a **communication tool** between shifts/departments.
6. Forms the **foundation for corrective action** and continuous improvement (Kaizen).

*(0.5 marks each — total 3 marks)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> Students commonly lose marks because they (a) **forget to justify the category selection**, (b) **do not link causes to countermeasures**, (c) **omit the integration with 5 Whys**, and (d) **present a vague problem statement**. Always remember: *Problem statement → Diagram → 5 Whys → Countermeasure → Standardization* is the complete chain that fetches full marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the procedure for constructing a Cause and Effect Diagram. Mention the typical categories used in a manufacturing setup. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A foundry is experiencing a high rejection rate (15%) in cast iron gear blanks due to porosity defects. Construct a detailed Cause and Effect Diagram and suggest corrective actions for the most critical causes. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Procedure for Constructing a Cause and Effect Diagram (7 Marks):**

The construction follows an **8-step procedure**:

1. **Define the Effect (Problem Statement):** Clearly articulate the problem in measurable terms. *(1 mark)*

2. **Draw the Main Spine:** Horizontal arrow pointing right toward the Effect box. *(0.5 mark)*

3. **Identify Major Categories:** In manufacturing, use the **6M framework** — Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, and Mother Nature. *(1.5 marks)*

4. **Brainstorm Sub-causes:** Cross-functional team lists potential causes under each category, drawn as sub-branches. *(1 mark)*

5. **Apply 5 Whys to Drill Down:** For top 3 causes, iteratively ask "Why?" until root cause is reached. *(1 mark)*

6. **Identify Most Likely Causes:** Use voting or data-driven selection. *(0.5 mark)*

7. **Develop Countermeasures:** Assign owner, deadline, and expected outcome for each cause. *(1 mark)*

8. **Verify and Standardize:** Confirm effectiveness and update SOPs / Work Instructions. *(0.5 mark)*

**(b) Fishbone for Porosity in Cast Iron Gear Blanks (7 Marks):**

**Problem Statement:** "Porosity rejection in CI gear blanks increased from 3% to 15% in March 2024."

**Diagram Structure (must be drawn in the answer sheet):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Man:} \quad & \text{(i) Pouring operator inexperience} \\
& \text{(ii) Inadequate training on new moulding line} \\
\\
\textbf{Machine:} \quad & \text{(i) Moulding machine vibration} \\
& \text{(ii) Pouring ladle temperature drop due to furnace delay} \\
& \text{(iii) Sand mixing time inconsistent} \\
\\
\textbf{Material:} \quad & \text{(i) High moisture in silica sand (>4%)} \\
& \text{(ii) Pig iron grade variation} \\
& \text{(iii) Insufficient inoculant addition} \\
\\
\textbf{Method:} \quad & \text{(i) Pouring temperature below 1380°C} \\
& \text{(ii) Riser design inadequate for shrinkage} \\
& \text{(iii) Gating system ratio improper} \\
\\
\textbf{Measurement:} \quad & \text{(i) Pyrometer not calibrated} \\
& \text{(ii) Sand moisture meter reading error} \\
\\
\textbf{Mother Nature:} \quad & \text{(i) Humidity > 80% in monsoon season} \\
& \text{(ii) Ambient temperature affecting sand flow}
\end{aligned}$$

**Critical Causes Identified (3 Marks for countermeasure linkage):**

| Cause | Countermeasure |
|---|---|
| Pouring temperature low | Recalibrate pyrometer; enforce min 1400°C |
| Sand moisture high | Add sand drier; check every batch |
| Pouring operator skill | Conduct hands-on refresher training |
| Inoculant insufficient | Standardize addition at 0.3% by weight |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Alternative Choice)

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** Discuss the historical origin of the Cause and Effect Diagram and its significance in the Seven Basic Tools of Quality. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A beverage bottling plant reports excessive foaming and overflow during carbonated drink filling, leading to 8% product loss. Build a Cause and Effect Diagram and apply the 5 Whys technique to identify the root cause. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Origin and Significance (7 Marks):**

- The Cause and Effect Diagram was developed by **Professor Kaoru Ishikawa** of the University of Tokyo in **1968**. *(1 mark)*
- It was first deployed at the **Kawasaki Steel Works** and later became a global standard through **Total Quality Management (TQM)** and **Kaizen** initiatives. *(1 mark)*
- It is one of the **Seven Basic Tools of Quality (7QC Tools)**, alongside: **Pareto Chart, Histogram, Control Chart, Scatter Diagram, Flowchart, and Check Sheet.** *(2 marks)*
- Significance: It enables **data-driven root cause identification**, prevents **blame culture** by focusing on systems, and aligns with the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle**. *(2 marks)*
- In Lean Manufacturing, it integrates with **A3 Problem Solving** and **5 Whys** to form a complete problem-solving methodology. *(1 mark)*

**(b) Fishbone for Carbonated Drink Foaming (7 Marks):**

**Problem Statement:** "Foaming overflow at the filling station causing 8% product loss daily."

$$\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Man:} \quad & \text{(i) Operator rushing bottle transfer} \\
& \text{(ii) Improper bottle rinsing} \\
\\
\textbf{Machine:} \quad & \text{(i) CO}_2 \text{ pressure fluctuation (target 2.5 bar)} \\
& \text{(ii) Filling nozzle worn out} \\
& \text{(iii) Bottle conveyor speed mismatch} \\
\\
\textbf{Material:} \quad & \text{(i) Sugar concentration high} \\
& \text{(ii) Water temperature too warm} \\
& \text{(iii) CO}_2 \text{ purity compromised} \\
\\
\textbf{Method:} \quad & \text{(i) Pre-cooling time reduced (cut from 30 to 15 min)} \\
& \text{(ii) Bottle temperature not checked before fill} \\
\\
\textbf{Measurement:} \quad & \text{(i) Pressure gauge drift} \\
& \text{(ii) Temperature sensor faulty} \\
\\
\textbf{Mother Nature:} \quad & \text{(i) Ambient humidity high} \\
& \text{(ii) Plant cooling system failure}
\end{aligned}$$

**5 Whys on "Pre-cooling time reduced":**

$$\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Why 1:} \quad & \text{Why is foaming occurring?} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 \text{ releases due to warm bottles.} \\
\textbf{Why 2:} \quad & \text{Why are bottles warm?} \rightarrow \text{Pre-cooling time was cut from 30 to 15 min.} \\
\textbf{Why 3:} \quad & \text{Why was pre-cooling time cut?} \rightarrow \text{To increase line throughput by 20%.} \\
