\textbf{Why 4:} \quad & \text{Why was throughput prioritized over cooling?} \rightarrow \text{Production target was set without consulting QA.} \\
\textbf{Why 5:} \quad & \text{Why was QA not consulted?} \rightarrow \text{No SOP for process change management.}
\end{aligned}$$

**Root Cause:** Absence of a process change management SOP requiring QA sign-off. **Countermeasure:** Restore 30-minute pre-cooling cycle, implement a process-change approval workflow.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Board Exam:**

- ✅ The Cause and Effect Diagram is also called the **Ishikawa Diagram** or **Fishbone Diagram**, invented by **Kaoru Ishikawa in 1968**.
- ✅ It is one of the **Seven Basic Tools of Quality (7QC Tools)**.
- ✅ In manufacturing, use the **6Ms**: Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature.
- ✅ In services, the **8Ps** are preferred; in simpler problems, the **4Ms** suffice.
- ✅ The **Effect** (problem) goes on the **right**, the **spine points right**, and **branches angle toward the spine**.
- ✅ The **problem statement must be specific, measurable, and time-bound**.
- ✅ Always **brainstorm with a cross-functional team** (4 to 8 members, 30–60 minutes).
- ✅ Integrate with the **5 Whys** technique to reach the true root cause.
- ✅ The diagram itself does **NOT solve the problem** — it only **identifies** causes. Corrective actions and standardization complete the cycle.
- ✅ A well-constructed diagram should lead to a **Countermeasure Matrix** with owner, deadline, and verification metric.
- ✅ Common board-exam trap: drawing the spine **in the wrong direction** (left to right with effect on the left) — **avoid this**.
- ✅ In 14-mark answers, always **draw the diagram AND justify the categories AND link to countermeasures** for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pareto Chart
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET746 - LEAN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pareto Chart

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Pareto Chart** is a specialized dual-axis graphical tool that combines a **bar graph** (with categories arranged in descending order of frequency/cost) and a **line graph** (representing the cumulative percentage of the total), used to visually identify the most significant factors among a set of contributing causes. It is a direct graphical application of the **Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule)**, which states that roughly **80% of the effects come from 20% of the causes**.

In the context of **Lean Manufacturing**, the Pareto Chart is a foundational **Quality Management** and **Problem-Solving** tool (one of the **7 Basic Quality Tools** defined by Kaoru Ishikawa) used to distinguish between the **"Vital Few"** (the dominant causes contributing to ~80% of the problem) and the **"Trivial Many"** (the remaining minor contributors).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** The Pareto Chart is explicitly listed as a **standalone statistical quality tool** under Module 2 of PEMET746. Students must be able to (i) state the Pareto Principle, (ii) construct a Pareto Chart from raw frequency data, and (iii) interpret the Vital Few vs. Trivial Many.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a hospital emergency room doctor. You notice that **80% of the patients coming in with severe injuries are involved in just 2 types of incidents** — say, road accidents and industrial mishaps — while the remaining 20% of injuries come from a long tail of miscellaneous causes (falls, burns, animal bites, etc.). If the hospital wants to save the most lives with limited resources, it should focus first on those **2 dominant causes**, not spread resources equally across all 10+ causes.

A **Pareto Chart works exactly the same way in a factory**:
- Instead of "patients," we have **defects** (scratches, dents, misalignments, color mismatches).
- Instead of "incident types," we have **defect categories**.
- The chart sorts these categories from largest to smallest and overlays a cumulative line.
- The "elbow" or steep drop in the cumulative line tells the production manager **where to focus first** to get the maximum defect reduction with minimum effort.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The chart operates on these key numerical benchmarks:

- **80/20 Rule (Pareto Principle):** A heuristic, not a rigid law; in practice, the "vital few" can range from **70%–90%** of the problem.
- **Cumulative Percentage Cut-off Line:** Conventionally drawn at **80%** on the secondary Y-axis to demarcate the Vital Few.
- **Standard Reference:** **80%** of the problem typically arises from the **top 20%** of the causes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Naming Origin:** The principle is named after the Italian economist **Vilfredo Pareto**, who in **1896** observed that approximately **80% of the land in Italy was owned by 20% of the population**. Dr. **Joseph M. Juran** later applied this observation to quality management in the 1950s, giving birth to the modern Pareto Chart.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundations

A Pareto Chart is built on three pillars of analytical logic:

**Pillar 1 — The Pareto Principle (Vilfredo Pareto, 1896)**
- States that a small number of causes (inputs) are responsible for a large majority of the effects (outputs).
- Mathematically expressed as an **asymmetric distribution** where the probability density function decays non-linearly (commonly modeled by a Power Law / Pareto Distribution: $P(X > x) = (x_m/x)^k$).

**Pillar 2 — Categorical Aggregation (Defect Stratification)**
- Raw observations are first **classified** into discrete, mutually exclusive categories (e.g., type of defect, machine, shift, supplier).
- This step converts chaotic raw data into **structured, actionable information** — a core Lean concept called *Muda* (waste) categorization.

**Pillar 3 — Descending Rank Ordering**
- Categories are sorted from **largest to smallest** frequency.
- This is the *defining feature* of a Pareto Chart — a regular bar chart of the same data would not yield the same insight because human eyes naturally read left-to-right and focus on the first bars.

### 2.2 The 80/20 Logic in Lean Manufacturing

In a Lean production environment, Pareto logic applies across multiple domains:

| **Lean Domain** | **80/20 Application** | **Vital Few Examples** |
|---|---|---|
| Defect Analysis | 80% of rejections from 20% of causes | Welding defects, dimensional errors |
| Inventory | 80% of stock value held in 20% of SKUs | Slow-moving raw materials, WIP |
| Downtime | 80% of stoppages from 20% of machines | One critical CNC center, one conveyor |
| Customer Complaints | 80% of returns from 20% of product lines | A single flagship model |
| Setup Time (SMED) | 80% of changeover time in 20% of activities | Bolt loosening, fixture adjustment |

### 2.3 Components of a Pareto Chart

1. **Left Vertical Axis (Y1):** Frequency / Count / Cost — measured in absolute units.
2. **Right Vertical Axis (Y2):** Cumulative Percentage — scaled from **0% to 100%**.
3. **Horizontal Axis (X):** The categories (cause types, defect types, machines, etc.) arranged in **descending order of frequency**. A final "Others" category may be added to the right.
4. **Bars (Primary Plot):** Rectangular bars, equal width, with heights proportional to the frequency of each category.
5. **Cumulative Line (Pareto Curve):** A line plot of cumulative percentage, starting at the top of the first bar and rising to 100% on the right. Dots mark the top of each bar; these are connected by line segments.
6. **80% Reference Line:** An optional horizontal dashed line at the 80% mark on Y2 to visually isolate the Vital Few.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Symbol** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Purpose / Utility** | **Standard Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Individual Percentage | $P_i = \dfrac{f_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} f_j} \times 100$ | Convert raw frequency to % share of total | Dimensionless (%) |
| Cumulative Percentage | $C_k = \sum_{j=1}^{k} P_j = \dfrac{\sum_{j=1}^{k} f_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} f_j} \times 100$ | Plot the Pareto curve (right Y-axis) | Dimensionless (%) |
| Total Frequency | $N = \sum_{j=1}^{n} f_j$ | Denominator of all percentage calculations | Count (units) |
| Pareto Vital Few Cut-off | $C_k \leq 80\% < C_{k+1}$ | Identifies the threshold category $k$ | Index (k) |
| Pareto Coefficient (Concentration) | $P_c = \dfrac{\text{Frequency of Top 20\% Categories}}{\text{Total Frequency}}$ | Measures strength of the 80/20 effect | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In Six Sigma DMAIC projects, the Pareto Chart is used in the **"Analyze"** phase to narrow the scope of root-cause investigation. In TPM (Total Productive Maintenance), it prioritizes machine improvement efforts. In FMEA, it ranks failure modes by occurrence frequency.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Automotive Assembly:** Ranking weld defects, paint runs, and torque misses to schedule the next Kaizen event.
- **Semiconductor Fabrication:** Stratifying wafer defects by tool, layer, or shift to identify the worst-performing equipment.
- **Supply Chain:** ABC analysis of inventory (A-items ≈ 20% of SKUs holding 80% of capital) is mathematically a Pareto decomposition.
- **Software Industry:** Bug triage — 80% of crashes caused by 20% of the code modules.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Example

### 3.1 Step-by-Step Construction Algorithm (KTU Board-Standard Procedure)

The following 7-step procedure is the **exact sequence** expected in KTU university exam answers for a "Construct a Pareto Chart" question:

1. **Step 1 — Define the Problem & Category:** Clearly state the problem (e.g., "defects in the Widget assembly line for the month of June 2024") and decide the categorization basis (defect type, machine, shift, operator, supplier).
2. **Step 2 — Collect Raw Data:** Gather all observations for the chosen time period using checklists, control charts, or rejection logs.
3. **Step 3 — Tally Frequencies per Category:** Count $f_i$ for each category $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$.
4. **Step 4 — Compute the Total:** $N = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i$.
5. **Step 5 — Sort in Descending Order:** Re-rank categories so that $f_1 \geq f_2 \geq \ldots \geq f_n$.
6. **Step 6 — Calculate Individual & Cumulative Percentages:** Apply the formulas in the cheat sheet.
7. **Step 7 — Draw the Chart:** Plot bars (left axis) and the cumulative curve (right axis); add the 80% reference line; label axes, title, and categories.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Numerical Worked Example

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU Exam Style):**

> A manufacturing unit producing electrical switches recorded the following defects over a one-week production run. Construct a Pareto Chart and identify the Vital Few defect categories.

**Raw Defect Data (Step 1 & 2):**

| **Defect Category** | **Raw Frequency Count ($f_i$)** |
|---|---|
| Loose Contact | 32 |
| Bent Terminal | 18 |
| Color Mismatch | 10 |
| Dimensional Error | 8 |
| Scratched Body | 7 |
| Missing Label | 3 |
| Other Minor Issues | 2 |

**Step 3 & 4 — Tally and Total:**

$$
N = 32 + 18 + 10 + 8 + 7 + 3 + 2 = 80 \text{ defects}
$$

**Step 5 — Sort in Descending Order:**

The data is already given in roughly descending order. We will sort it formally:

$$
f_1 = 32, \quad f_2 = 18, \quad f_3 = 10, \quad f_4 = 8, \quad f_5 = 7, \quad f_6 = 3, \quad f_7 = 2
$$

**Step 6 — Calculate Individual Percentages $P_i$:**

$$
P_1 = \frac{32}{80} \times 100 = 40.00\%
$$

$$
P_2 = \frac{18}{80} \times 100 = 22.50\%
$$

$$
P_3 = \frac{10}{80} \times 100 = 12.50\%
$$

$$
P_4 = \frac{8}{80} \times 100 = 10.00\%
$$

$$
P_5 = \frac{7}{80} \times 100 = 8.75\%
$$

$$
P_6 = \frac{3}{80} \times 100 = 3.75\%
$$

$$
P_7 = \frac{2}{80} \times 100 = 2.50\%
$$

**Step 6b — Calculate Cumulative Percentages $C_k$:**

$$
C_1 = P_1 = 40.00\%
$$

$$
C_2 = P_1 + P_2 = 40.00\% + 22.50\% = 62.50\%
$$

$$
C_3 = C_2 + P_3 = 62.50\% + 12.50\% = 75.00\%
$$

$$
C_4 = C_3 + P_4 = 75.00\% + 10.00\% = 85.00\%
$$

$$
C_5 = C_4 + P_5 = 85.00\% + 8.75\% = 93.75\%
$$

$$
C_6 = C_5 + P_6 = 93.75\% + 3.75\% = 97.50\%
$$

$$
C_7 = C_6 + P_7 = 97.50\% + 2.50\% = 100.00\%
$$

**Step 7 — Final Tabulated Data (for plotting):**

| **Rank** | **Defect Category** | **Frequency $f_i$** | **Percentage $P_i$ (%)** | **Cumulative % $C_k$** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Loose Contact | 32 | 40.00 | 40.00 |
| 2 | Bent Terminal | 18 | 22.50 | 62.50 |
| 3 | Color Mismatch | 10 | 12.50 | 75.00 |
| 4 | Dimensional Error | 8 | 10.00 | **85.00** |
| 5 | Scratched Body | 7 | 8.75 | 93.75 |
| 6 | Missing Label | 3 | 3.75 | 97.50 |
| 7 | Other Minor Issues | 2 | 2.50 | 100.00 |
| — | **Total** | **80** | **100.00** | — |

**Step 8 — Identify the Vital Few:**

Applying the cut-off rule $C_k \leq 80\% < C_{k+1}$:

$$
C_3 = 75.00\% \leq 80\% < 85.00\% = C_4
$$

Therefore, the **Vital Few** are the **top 3 categories**:
**Loose Contact, Bent Terminal, Color Mismatch**

These three categories together account for **75%** of all defects. Addressing them will deliver the maximum defect reduction. The remaining four categories are the **Trivial Many**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Cut-off:** If the cut-off were strictly 80% (i.e., the first category to *cross* 80%), we would include rank 4 as well (since $C_4 = 85\% > 80\%$). The standard KTU convention is to include all categories that contribute up to or just beyond 80% — the first 3 cover 75%, the 4th pushes us to 85%. Most examiners accept the first 3 as the Vital Few for this dataset.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Pareto Chart Construction

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **logical workflow** of constructing a Pareto Chart, including its dual-axis plotting architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Problem Scope] --> B[Collect Raw Defect Data]
    B --> C[Tally Frequency per Category]
    C --> D[Compute Total Frequency N]
    D --> E[Sort Categories in Descending Order]
    E --> F[Calculate Individual Percentage P_i]
    F --> G[Calculate Cumulative Percentage C_k]
    G --> H{Is Sum of P_i = 100%?}
    H -- No --> I[Recheck Arithmetic]
    I --> C
    H -- Yes --> J[Plot Primary Y1 Axis: Frequency Bars]
    J --> K[Plot Secondary Y2 Axis: Cumulative % Curve]
    K --> L[Draw 80% Reference Line on Y2]
    L --> M[Identify Vital Few Crossing 80% Threshold]
    M --> N[End: Interpret and Prioritize Lean Actions]
```

### 4.2 Component Anatomy of the Pareto Chart (Dual-Axis Structure)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph chartLayout[Pareto Chart Anatomy]
        XAxis[Horizontal Axis X: Categories sorted descending]
        Y1Axis[Left Vertical Axis Y1: Frequency 0 to N]
        Y2Axis[Right Vertical Axis Y2: Cumulative Percentage 0 to 100]
        Bars[Descending Bars: height equals f_i]
        Curve[Pareto Curve: line plot of C_k]
        RefLine[80% Dashed Reference Line]
        Title[Chart Title and Labels]
    end
    XAxis --- Bars
    Y1Axis --- Bars
    Y2Axis --- Curve
    Bars -.connects via dots.-> Curve
    RefLine --- Curve
    Title --- chartLayout
```

### 4.3 Visual Interpretation Map (Lean Decision Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Data[Raw Defect Data] --> Sort[Descending Sort]
    Sort --> Plot[Pareto Chart with 80% Line]
    Plot --> Decision{Which categories lie on or before the 80% line?}
    Decision -- Yes --> VitalFew[Vital Few - High Priority Kaizen]
    Decision -- No --> TrivialMany[Trivial Many - Monitor and Defer]
    VitalFew --> Action1[Root Cause Analysis: 5 Whys, Fishbone]
    VitalFew --> Action2[Implement Countermeasures via PDCA]
    TrivialMany --> Action3[Low-Cost Standardization]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
*State the Pareto Principle. How is it applied in Lean Manufacturing to differentiate between the Vital Few and the Trivial Many?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. The **Pareto Principle**, also known as the **80/20 Rule**, states that approximately **80% of the problems or effects arise from only 20% of the causes**. (1 mark)
2. In Lean Manufacturing, defect data is collected and classified into categories (e.g., types of defects, machine stoppages). These categories are sorted in **descending order of frequency** and plotted as a Pareto Chart. (1 mark)
3. The categories whose cumulative contribution reaches approximately **80%** are termed the **Vital Few** (high-priority causes for root-cause analysis), while the remaining long-tail categories are the **Trivial Many** (low priority, can be deferred). (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*List the essential components of a Pareto Chart and state the role of the secondary (right-hand) Y-axis.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. A Pareto Chart consists of: **a horizontal X-axis** (categories in descending order), **a left Y-axis** (frequency/cost), **a right Y-axis** (cumulative percentage from 0 to 100%), **descending bars**, and **a cumulative Pareto curve** drawn over the bars. (2 marks)
2. The **secondary right-hand Y-axis** carries the **cumulative percentage scale (0–100%)** and enables the viewer to read the cumulative contribution of each category and visually identify the 80% cut-off line for separating Vital Few from Trivial Many. (1 mark)

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (Choice 1) — [14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

> *(a)* Explain the construction procedure of a Pareto Chart with a suitable industrial example. State any two limitations of the tool. **[7 Marks]**

> *(b)* The following data represents the types and frequencies of defects observed in a CNC machining cell during a month. Construct a Pareto Chart by computing the cumulative percentages and identify the Vital Few causes.
>
> | Defect Type | Frequency |
> |---|---|
> | Burr | 42 |
> | Surface Roughness | 28 |
> | Oversize Diameter | 15 |
> | Tool Mark | 9 |
> | Undercut | 4 |
> | Others | 2 |
> **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

The construction of a Pareto Chart follows a systematic 7-step procedure: (2 marks for procedure)

1. **Define the Problem and Category** — Select the quality issue (e.g., rejection rate in CNC cell) and choose the basis of categorization (defect type).
2. **Collect Data** — Use inspection logs, SPC sheets, or operator check sheets for a defined period.
3. **Tally Frequencies** — Count $f_i$ for each category.
4. **Compute Total** — $N = \sum f_i$.
5. **Sort Descending** — Reorder categories so that $f_1 \geq f_2 \geq \ldots \geq f_n$.
6. **Calculate Percentages** — Compute $P_i = (f_i/N) \times 100$ and $C_k = \sum_{j=1}^{k} P_j$.
7. **Plot the Chart** — Bars on left Y-axis (frequency), cumulative curve on right Y-axis (cumulative %), and the 80% reference line.

**Two Limitations (1 mark each):**
- The Pareto Chart does not reveal the **root cause** of the dominant problems; it only shows their relative magnitude. Separate tools (Fishbone, 5 Whys) are needed for root cause identification.
- It cannot detect **interactions or correlations** between categories (e.g., whether Burr and Surface Roughness occur together on the same part). It is a univariate frequency tool only.
- (Optional 3rd limitation) The 80/20 split is a heuristic — the actual vital-few threshold may vary (70–90%) depending on the dataset, leading to subjective interpretation.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Total Frequency:**

$$
N = 42 + 28 + 15 + 9 + 4 + 2 = 100
$$

**Step 2 — Sort (already in descending order) and Calculate $P_i$, $C_k$:** (4 marks for table)

| **Rank** | **Defect Type** | **$f_i$** | **$P_i = (f_i/100) \times 100$ (%)** | **$C_k$ (%)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Burr | 42 | 42.00 | 42.00 |
| 2 | Surface Roughness | 28 | 28.00 | 70.00 |
| 3 | Oversize Diameter | 15 | 15.00 | **85.00** |
| 4 | Tool Mark | 9 | 9.00 | 94.00 |
| 5 | Undercut | 4 | 4.00 | 98.00 |
| 6 | Others | 2 | 2.00 | 100.00 |
| **—** | **Total** | **100** | **100.00** | — |

**Step 3 — Plotting Description (1 mark):**

The X-axis lists the six defect types from left (Burr) to right (Others). The left Y-axis (Y1) carries frequency from 0 to ~45. The right Y-axis (Y2) carries cumulative percentage from 0% to 100%. The bars descend from 42 down to 2. The cumulative curve starts at 42% above Burr, rises to 70% above Surface Roughness, reaches 85% above Oversize Diameter, then plateaus to 100% on the right. An 80% horizontal reference line crosses the curve between the 2nd and 3rd bars.

**Step 4 — Identification of Vital Few (2 marks):**

Applying the rule $C_k \leq 80\% < C_{k+1}$:

$$
C_2 = 70.00\% \leq 80\% < 85.00\% = C_3
$$

The **Vital Few** are the **top 2 categories: Burr and Surface Roughness**, which together account for **70%** of all defects. Oversize Diameter is the 3rd category and pushes cumulative to 85% — many practitioners include it in the Vital Few too. The remaining three categories (Tool Mark, Undercut, Others) are the Trivial Many.

**Conclusion:** The Lean team should prioritize **root-cause analysis and Kaizen on Burr and Surface Roughness** defects to achieve maximum defect reduction.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2) — [14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

> *(a)* Differentiate between a regular bar chart and a Pareto Chart. Why is the cumulative percentage curve essential to the interpretation of a Pareto Chart? **[7 Marks]**

> *(b)* A garment manufacturing unit recorded the following reasons for production delays in a week. Construct a Pareto Chart, calculate cumulative percentages, and identify the Vital Few. **[7 Marks]**
>
> | Reason for Delay | Hours Lost |
> |---|---|
> | Machine Breakdown | 36 |
> | Material Shortage | 24 |
> | Power Failure | 14 |
> | Absenteeism | 10 |
> | Quality Rework | 8 |
> | Changeover | 6 |
> | Others | 2 |

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

| **Aspect** | **Regular Bar Chart** | **Pareto Chart** | **Marks** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ordering of categories | Arbitrary (alphabetical, chronological, or any order) | Strictly in **descending order** of frequency | 1 |
| Secondary axis | Generally absent (single Y-axis) | Has a **second Y-axis on the right** for cumulative % | 1 |
| Additional curve | No curve | Includes a **cumulative percentage (Pareto) curve** | 1 |
| Purpose | General data visualization | Specifically designed for **priority-setting** and **Vital Few identification** | 1 |

**Why the cumulative curve is essential (3 marks):**
- The cumulative curve translates the discrete bar magnitudes into a **running total**, allowing the analyst to read the **combined contribution** of the top $k$ categories at a glance.
- The 80% reference line intersects the cumulative curve at a point that **mathematically defines the boundary** between the Vital Few (left of intersection) and the Trivial Many (right of intersection).
- Without the cumulative curve, the chart degenerates into a sorted bar chart and loses its **decision-making power** — the user would have to manually sum bars to find the 80% threshold.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Total Hours Lost:**

$$
N = 36 + 24 + 14 + 10 + 8 + 6 + 2 = 100 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $P_i$ and $C_k$:** (4 marks for table)

| **Rank** | **Reason for Delay** | **Hours ($f_i$)** | **$P_i$ (%)** | **$C_k$ (%)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Machine Breakdown | 36 | 36.00 | 36.00 |
| 2 | Material Shortage | 24 | 24.00 | 60.00 |
| 3 | Power Failure | 14 | 14.00 | **74.00** |
| 4 | Absenteeism | 10 | 10.00 | 84.00 |
| 5 | Quality Rework | 8 | 8.00 | 92.00 |
| 6 | Changeover | 6 | 6.00 | 98.00 |
| 7 | Others | 2 | 2.00 | 100.00 |

**Step 3 — Vital Few Identification (2 marks):**

$$
C_3 = 74.00\% \leq 80\% < 84.00\% = C_4
$$

The **Vital Few** are the **top 3 categories: Machine Breakdown, Material Shortage, and Power Failure**, which together account for **74%** of all delay hours. Absenteeism is the next contributor and pushes cumulative beyond 80% — depending on strict interpretation, it may also be included.

**Step 4 — Plotting Description (1 mark):**

The X-axis lists 7 reasons in descending order. Left Y-axis (Y1) ranges 0 to ~40 (hours). Right Y-axis (Y2) ranges 0% to 100%. Bars descend from 36 to 2. The cumulative curve starts at 36% above Machine Breakdown and rises monotonically to 100% above Others. The 80% reference line crosses the curve between Power Failure (74%) and Absenteeism (84%).

**Conclusion:** The Lean team should focus TPM efforts on **Machine Breakdown**, improve **supplier coordination for Material Shortage**, and install **backup power systems for Power Failure** to eliminate ~74% of all production delays.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to sort** the data in descending order before drawing the chart — this is the #1 mistake; if unsorted, the chart loses its Pareto identity and gets **2 marks deducted**.
> 2. **Not labelling both Y-axes** with units (frequency on left, cumulative % on right) — the KTU key explicitly awards **1 mark for axis labels and units**.
> 3. **Miscounting the Vital Few** — students often list too many categories. The correct interpretation is the **smallest set** whose cumulative % is closest to (but does not exceed) 80%, or just crosses it.
> 4. **Not showing the 80% reference line** on the chart — examiners deduct **1 mark** if it is missing.
> 5. **Calculation errors in cumulative %** — always recheck: the final cumulative % **must equal exactly 100%**. If it does not, the arithmetic is wrong.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A Pareto Chart is a **dual-axis bar + line chart** where bars are sorted in **descending order of frequency** and overlaid with a **cumulative percentage curve**.
- **Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule):** ~80% of effects come from ~20% of causes. Originated with **Vilfredo Pareto (1896)**; adapted to quality by **Juran (1950s)**.
- **Two Key Identifiers:**
  - **Vital Few** — categories whose cumulative contribution is ≤ 80% (high-priority action zone).
  - **Trivial Many** — the long tail of categories after the 80% cut-off (monitor and defer).
- **Core Formulas (memorize both):**
  - $P_i = \dfrac{f_i}{N} \times 100$
  - $C_k = \dfrac{\sum_{j=1}^{k} f_j}{N} \times 100$
- **Mandatory Chart Elements (each earns marks):** Title, X-axis labels, Left Y1 (Frequency) axis, Right Y2 (Cumulative %) axis, Bars, Cumulative curve with dots, 80% reference line, Vital Few identification.
- **Construction Sequence:** Define → Collect → Tally → Total → Sort → Calculate % → Calculate Cumulative % → Plot → Interpret.
- **In Lean Manufacturing:** Pareto Charts are used in the **Analyze phase of DMAIC**, for **TPM priority-setting**, **Kaizen event scoping**, and **ABC inventory classification**.
- **Limitations to remember:** Does **not** reveal root causes; cannot detect **interactions** between categories; the 80/20 split is a **heuristic** and not a fixed mathematical law.
- **Common Mistake to Avoid:** A Pareto Chart is **not** the same as a regular sorted bar chart — the **cumulative curve + dual axes** are what make it a Pareto Chart.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Poka-Yoke
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET746 - LEAN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Poka-Yoke

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: Poka-Yoke

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Poka-Yoke (ポカヨケ / 防錯)** is a Japanese quality management concept, formalized by **Shigeo Shingo** at Toyota Motor Corporation during the early 1960s. The term translates literally to **"mistake-proofing"** or **"error-proofing"** — derived from *yokeru* (to avoid) and *poka* (an inadvertent error made inadvertently by a worker). It is defined by KTU as a **lean manufacturing device, mechanism, or procedure designed to either prevent a defect from occurring in the first place, or immediately detect and signal a defect as soon as it occurs, thereby achieving 100% zero-defect production at the source.**

The central philosophy of Poka-Yoke is built upon the foundational lean belief that:

$$\text{Defects} = \text{Function(Worker Skill, Process Design, Mistake-Proofing Device)}$$

When the **process design** is engineered to be inherently incapable of producing errors (via Poka-Yoke), the dependency on operator skill is dramatically reduced — a critical tenet of the **Jidoka** (autonomation) pillar of the **Toyota Production System (TPS)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — The USB Connector:** Have you ever tried to insert a USB cable "upside down" and noticed it physically refuses to enter the port? That is **Poka-Yoke in action.** The asymmetric internal pin layout makes orientation-error impossible. You cannot make the mistake, no matter how hurried or inexperienced you are.
>
> **Analogy 2 — The Washing Machine Door:** When a front-load washing machine is mid-cycle, the door refuses to open until water drains and the drum stops spinning. The appliance has *detected* an unsafe condition and *physically prevented* the user error. This is detection-type Poka-Yoke.
>
> **Analogy 3 — The Car Gear Interlock:** A modern automatic car will not allow the key to be removed unless the transmission is in **'Park'**. The mechanical linkage physically stops the key release — a foolproof prevention device engineered directly into the design.

These everyday examples share one universal truth: **they shift the burden of perfection from the human (who is fallible) to the system (which is reliable).**

### 1.3 Historical Origin — Shigeo Shingo's Contribution

In the 1960s, Toyota workers were experiencing recurring assembly defects on the *Toyopet* and *Corona* lines. Shingo noticed that even highly trained workers occasionally inserted **two ignition-switch halves into the wrong side of the housing**, causing a **1% defect rate**. Rather than retraining workers or adding inspection checkpoints, Shingo installed a **small tray/hood** that physically held only enough parts for one assembly in only one orientation. The defects **dropped to zero overnight**, and the modern Poka-Yoke discipline was born.

### 1.4 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Poka** (ポカ) | Inadvertent mistake / blunders by humans |
| **Yoke** (ヨケ) | To prevent / to avoid (from *yokeru*) |
| **Defect** | A non-conformance to specification |
| **Source Inspection** | Detecting the error at the operation where it originates |
| **Informative Inspection** | Detecting downstream, requiring feedback loop |
| **Jidoka** | Toyota's principle of "automation with a human touch" |
| **100% Inspection** | Checking *every* unit produced (made possible by Poka-Yoke) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

## 2.1 The Two Fundamental Types of Poka-Yoke

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Poka-Yoke devices are classified into **two major categories** based on **when** they intervene in the defect-creation cycle. This is the single most heavily tested concept in KTU examinations.

### Type 1: Prevention Poka-Yoke (防錯 / Prevention Type)

$$\boxed{\text{Prevention Poka-Yoke} \rightarrow \text{Stops the error BEFORE it occurs}}$$

- The mistake is made **physically impossible** by the design itself.
- Examples: USB connector polarity, asymmetric gear teeth, torque-limiting wrenches that "click" at set value.
- **Zero defect by construction** — the operator cannot complete the erroneous action.
- Most preferred type in modern lean implementations.
- Aligned with Shingo's principle: *"The best inspection is the one that is not needed."*

### Type 2: Detection Poka-Yoke (検出 / Detection Type)

$$\boxed{\text{Detection Poka-Yoke} \rightarrow \text{Catches the error IMMEDIATELY after it occurs}}$$

- The defect is allowed to be made **once**, but a **sensor, alarm, or mechanical flag** immediately flags it before it progresses to the next workstation.
- Examples: Sensor that beeps if a screw is missing, pressure gauge that halts a press if no part is loaded, pin-count sensor on assembly line.
- Enables **immediate feedback (andon-style)** so the defect can be corrected at-source.
- The detected defect is **never passed downstream** to a customer.

### Comparative Matrix

| Parameter | Prevention Poka-Yoke | Detection Poka-Yoke |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **When it acts** | Before defect creation | Immediately after defect creation |
| **Defect allowed?** | No — impossible by design | Yes — but flagged instantly |
| **Reliance on operator** | Minimal | Moderate (operator must heed signal) |
| **Implementation cost** | Often higher (design change) | Often lower (sensor retrofit) |
| **Effectiveness** | Highest | High |
| **Example** | USB connector polarity | Missing-screw alarm sensor |
| **KTU mark weightage** | Frequently asked (7-8 marks) | Frequently asked (5-6 marks) |

## 2.2 The Three Implementation Methods of Poka-Yoke

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per Shigeo Shingo's original classification (and reproduced verbatim in KTU Module 2 textbooks), there are **three engineering methods** to physically realize Poka-Yoke. KTU expects students to know all three with real-world examples.

### Method 1: Contact Method (接触法 / *Sesshoku-hō*)

$$\boxed{\text{Contact Method} \rightarrow \text{Physical contact of part with a sensing device}}$$

- The part physically touches a **limit switch, jig, pin, sensor probe, or mechanical stop**.
- If the contact pattern is incorrect (wrong dimension, wrong orientation, missing feature), the device **triggers an alarm or stops the process**.
- **Example:** A locating pin in a welding jig that the part *must* sit against before the clamp closes. If the part is upside-down, the pin does not engage and the clamp will not close — the cycle is aborted.

### Method 2: Fixed-Value Method (定数法 / *Teisū-hō*)

$$\boxed{\text{Fixed-Value Method} \rightarrow \text{Counting moves, items, or signals to a preset number}}$$

- The system counts a discrete action (number of parts delivered, number of operations completed, number of holes drilled) and **automatically stops when the count equals the preset correct value**.
- Eliminates the classic **"forgot to add the fourth bolt"** or **"only loaded 3 springs, need 4"** errors.
- **Example:** An automatic parts feeder that holds exactly **4 springs**; after all 4 are dispensed, the next cycle cannot start. If one spring is missing from the bin, a sensor counts only 3 and halts the line.

### Method 3: Motion-Step Method (動作ステップ法 / *Dōsa Suteppu-hō*)

$$\boxed{\text{Motion-Step Method} \rightarrow \text{Verifies that EACH sequential step was completed correctly}}$$

- Each operation is treated as a **discrete step** in a sequence. The Poka-Yoke device checks that **Step N was completed before Step N+1 can begin**.
- **Example:** On a circuit board assembly, before the "soldering" step can begin, the system verifies that the "component insertion" sensor has fired. If the component is missing, the soldering station is locked out.

## 2.3 Source Inspection vs. Informative Inspection

This is the **Shingo distinction** that frequently appears in KTU 14-mark questions:

$$\text{Inspection Position} = \begin{cases} \text{Source Inspection} \rightarrow \text{At the producing operation} \\ \text{Informative Inspection} \rightarrow \text{Downstream of the producing operation} \end{cases}$$

**Shingo's First Ideal:** Inspection at the **source** (i.e., by the worker producing the part).
**Shingo's Second Ideal:** **100% self-inspection** by the operator under Poka-Yoke guidance.
**Shingo's Third Ideal:** **Successive checks** — the *next* operation inspects the *previous* one (relay inspection).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Quick-Reference Framework

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates all key analytical relationships, formulas, and decision logic that KTU expects students to reproduce in exams. No pipes `|` appear inside cells to preserve markdown table integrity.

| Concept | Formula / Relationship | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Defect-rate reduction | $\text{DPMO}_{\text{after}} = \text{DPMO}_{\text{before}} - \Delta_{\text{PY}}$ | Measure Poka-Yoke effectiveness |
| Cost of poor quality | $\text{COPQ} = C_{\text{scrap}} + C_{\text{rework}} + C_{\text{warranty}} + C_{\text{returns}}$ | Quantify savings from PY |
| Target defect level | $\text{Defect}_{\text{goal}} = 0$ (Zero Defects) | Philosophical aim of PY |
| First-time yield | $\text{FTY} = \frac{N_{\text{passes}}}{N_{\text{total}}}$ | Track yield after PY implementation |
| Rolled throughput yield | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{FTY}_i$ | Multi-station process improvement |
| Inspection reduction | $I_{\text{after}} \ll I_{\text{before}}$ since $100\%$ check is automated | Justify PY ROI |

## 2.5 Integration with TPS and Lean Pillars

Poka-Yoke is **not standalone** — it is the **technical enabler** of the *Jidoka* pillar within the Toyota Production System (TPS). The full lean context:

$$\text{TPS} = \underbrace{\text{Just-in-Time (JIT)}}_{\text{flow pillar}} + \underbrace{\text{Jidoka}}_{\text{quality pillar (enabled by Poka-Yoke)}}$$

Without Poka-Yoke, Jidoka collapses into "stop-the-line" relying on human vigilance — unreliable. **Poka-Yoke provides the mechanical / electronic intelligence** that makes Jidoka autonomous.

## 2.6 Real-World Industrial Utility

1. **Automotive Assembly:** Detecting missing bolts on engine blocks using vision sensors.
2. **Electronics Manufacturing:** Pick-and-place machines verify every component placement using vision + count sensors.
3. **Pharmaceutical Packaging:** Verifying leaflet presence in every carton before sealing.
4. **Medical Devices:** Single-use syringe plunger locks that physically prevent re-use (prevention PY).
5. **Aviation Maintenance:** Keyed fasteners where left-hand threads only fit left-hand holes (prevention PY).
6. **Food Industry:** Color-coded ingredient bins that physically cannot be installed in the wrong mixer port.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Implementation Derivation

## 3.1 The Four-Step Poka-Yoke Implementation Process

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently tests the **procedure / methodology** for designing a Poka-Yoke device. The following is the **Shingo-Correct Implementation Ladder** with full logical derivation.

**Step 1 — Identify the Defect (the *What*)**

$$\boxed{\text{Observation}} \longrightarrow \text{Defect list (4M: Man, Machine, Material, Method)}$$

- Use **Pareto analysis** to identify the *vital few* defects that consume the majority of cost.
- Apply the **5-Why analysis** repeatedly to reach the **root cause** of each defect.

**Step 2 — Analyze the Cause (the *Why*)**

$$\boxed{\text{5-Why}} \longrightarrow \text{Root cause identification}$$

The 5-Why technique proceeds by iterative questioning:

$$\text{Why}_1 \rightarrow \text{Why}_2 \rightarrow \text{Why}_3 \rightarrow \text{Why}_4 \rightarrow \text{Why}_5 = \text{Root Cause}$$

**Step 3 — Design the Poka-Yoke Function (the *How*)**

$$\boxed{\text{Root Cause}} \xrightarrow{\text{Device selection}} \text{Poka-Yoke Type \& Method}$$

| Root-Cause Category | Recommended PY Type | Recommended PY Method |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Wrong orientation | Prevention | Contact Method |
| Missing component | Detection | Fixed-Value Method |
| Skipped operation | Detection | Motion-Step Method |
| Wrong dimension | Prevention | Contact Method |
| Wrong sequence | Prevention | Motion-Step Method |

**Step 4 — Implement, Monitor, and Refine**

$$\boxed{\text{PY Implementation}} \xrightarrow{\text{PDCA Cycle}} \text{Sustained Improvement}$$

Apply **Plan-Do-Check-Act (Deming Cycle)** iteratively to validate, refine, and standardise the Poka-Yoke across the plant.

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation of Defect-Rate Reduction

Let us derive a quantitative model showing how Poka-Yoke mathematically reduces defects.

**Assumptions:**

- Let $N$ = total units produced per shift
- Let $p$ = probability of a single defect per operation (without PY)
- Let $n$ = number of operations in the assembly sequence

**Step 1 — Defects without Poka-Yoke:**

The expected number of defective units per shift without Poka-Yoke is:

$$E[\text{Defects}]_{\text{no PY}} = N \cdot \big(1 - (1-p)^{n}\big)$$

**Reasoning:** The probability that a unit passes all $n$ operations is $(1-p)^n$. The probability it fails at least one is $1 - (1-p)^n$. Multiplying by $N$ gives the expected defect count.

**Step 2 — Defects with Poka-Yoke (Detection Type):**

A Detection PY at the source station intercepts every defect, sending feedback to stop the line. Residual defect rate is $\varepsilon \approx 0$ (assuming perfect detection):

$$E[\text{Defects}]_{\text{with PY}} = N \cdot \varepsilon \quad \text{where } \varepsilon \rightarrow 0$$

**Step 3 — Defect-rate reduction ratio (the PY Effectiveness Index):**

$$\eta_{\text{PY}} = \frac{E[\text{Defects}]_{\text{no PY}} - E[\text{Defects}]_{\text{with PY}}}{E[\text{Defects}]_{\text{no PY}}} = 1 - \frac{\varepsilon}{1 - (1-p)^{n}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical illustration:**

Suppose $N = 1000$ units/shift, $p = 0.01$ (1% per-operation defect), $n = 5$ operations, $\varepsilon = 0.001$ (0.1% residual after PY).

$$\text{Defects without PY} = 1000 \times \big(1 - 0.99^{5}\big) = 1000 \times 0.0490 = 49.0 \text{ units}$$

$$\text{Defects with PY} = 1000 \times 0.001 = 1.0 \text{ unit}$$

$$\eta_{\text{PY}} = \frac{49 - 1}{49} = 0.9796 = \mathbf{97.96\% \text{ reduction}}$$

**Conclusion:** Poka-Yoke reduces defects by an order of magnitude, even when residual error $\varepsilon$ is small. KTU examiners value this kind of quantitative reasoning.

## 3.3 Decision Tree — Selecting PY Type and Method

A structured decision flow used by lean engineers:

$$\text{Is the error foreseeable from design?} \begin{cases} \text{Yes} \rightarrow \text{Prevention PY (asymmetric geometry, keying, fool-proofing)} \\ \text{No} \rightarrow \text{Detection PY — Contact, Fixed-Value, or Motion-Step?} \end{cases}$$

$$\text{Detection PY — which method?} \begin{cases} \text{Physical attribute mismatch} \rightarrow \text{Contact Method} \\ \text{Quantity / count mismatch} \rightarrow \text{Fixed-Value Method} \\ \text{Sequence / step mismatch} \rightarrow \text{Motion-Step Method} \end{cases}$$

This logic tree is frequently reproduced in KTU 14-mark answers for full marks.

## 3.4 Strategic Principles for Designing Effective Poka-Yoke

1. **Principle of Economy:** Use the *simplest*, *cheapest* device that solves the problem. A 50-cent plastic guide can replace a 50,000-rupee vision system.
2. **Principle of Function over Form:** A Poka-Yoke must *function* correctly under real shop-floor conditions (dust, vibration, oil). Aesthetic perfection is irrelevant.
3. **Principle of 100% Coverage:** A good Poka-Yoke inspects **every single unit**, not a sample. This is what makes 100% inspection economically feasible.
4. **Principle of Immediacy:** Feedback must be **instantaneous** — within seconds, not minutes. Delayed feedback defeats the purpose.
5. **Principle of Empowerment:** The worker who triggers the Poka-Yoke alarm must be *empowered* to stop the line. No management override.
6. **Principle of Source Inspection:** Detection should occur *at the producing station*, not 10 stations later.
7. **Principle of Self-Inspection:** Ideally, the *operator performing* the operation also performs the inspection under Poka-Yoke guidance — Shingo's *Second Ideal*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Poka-Yoke Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    RootA[POKA-YOKE] --> Type1[Prevention Type]
    RootA --> Type2[Detection Type]

    Type1 --> ExA1[Asymmetric Geometry]
    Type1 --> ExA2[Keyed Connectors]
    Type1 --> ExA3[Torque Limiters]

    Type2 --> Method1[Contact Method]
    Type2 --> Method2[Fixed-Value Method]
    Type2 --> Method3[Motion-Step Method]

    Method1 --> ExM1[Limit Switch Sensor]
    Method1 --> ExM2[Locating Pin Jig]
    Method2 --> ExM3[Parts Count Feeder]
    Method2 --> ExM4[Screw Counter]
    Method3 --> ExM5[Step Sequence Lock]
    Method3 --> ExM6[Operation Completion Sensor]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Implementation Flow (Four-Step Ladder)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Defect Detected on Shop Floor]) --> S1[Step 1: Identify the Defect Type]
    S1 --> S2[Step 2: Apply 5-Why Root Cause Analysis]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Select PY Type and Method]
    S3 --> Dec{Prevention or Detection?}
    Dec -- Prevention --> P1[Design Asymmetric Feature]
    Dec -- Detection --> P2[Choose Contact or Fixed-Value or Motion-Step]
    P1 --> Test
    P2 --> Test
    Test[Step 4: Pilot Test on Line] --> Check{Effectiveness Validated?}
    Check -- No --> S3
    Check -- Yes --> Std[Standardise Across Plant]
    Std --> PDCA[Apply PDCA for Continuous Refinement]
    PDCA --> Done([Zero Defect Operation Achieved])
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Integration with TPS and Lean Pillars

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TPS[Toyota Production System TPS] --> JIT[Just In Time Flow Pillar]
    TPS --> Jidoka[Jidoka Autonomation Pillar]

    JIT --> Pull[Pull Based Production]
    JIT --> Kanban[Kanban Signals]

    Jidoka --> PY[Poka Yoke Mistake Proofing]
    Jidoka --> Andon[Andon Visual Signal]
    Jidoka --> StopLine[Authority to Stop the Line]

    PY --> Type1[Prevention Type]
    PY --> Type2[Detection Type]

    Type1 --> Method1[Contact Method]
    Type1 --> Method2[Fixed Value Method]
    Type1 --> Method3[Motion Step Method]

    Type2 --> Method1
    Type2 --> Method2
    Type2 --> Method3

    Method1 --> Out1[Zero Defect by Construction]
    Method2 --> Out2[Immediate Defect Detection]
    Method3 --> Out3[Sequence Integrity Guaranteed]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Real-World Application Matrix

```mermaid
graph LR
    AppA[Automotive Assembly] --> PY1[Missing Bolt Sensor]
    AppB[Electronics SMT] --> PY2[Component Count Verification]
    AppC[Pharma Packaging] --> PY3[Leaflet Presence Sensor]
    AppD[Medical Devices] --> PY4[Single Use Lockout]
    AppE[Aviation MRO] --> PY5[Keyed Fastener Pattern]
    AppF[Food Industry] --> PY6[Color Coded Port Mismatch]

    PY1 --> RT[100 Percent Inspection Achieved]
    PY2 --> RT
    PY3 --> RT
    PY4 --> RT
    PY5 --> RT
    PY6 --> RT

    RT --> Goal[Zero Defect Output to Customer]
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Working Principle of a Detection Poka-Yoke

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Op[Operator Performs Operation] --> Part[Part Produced]
    Part --> Sens[Sensor or Limit Switch Activated]
    Sens --> Check{Output Matches Spec?}
    Check -- Yes --> Pass[Part Passes Downstream]
    Check -- No --> Alarm[Andon Alarm and Line Stop]
    Alarm --> Feed[Feedback to Operator]
    Feed --> Corr[Corrective Action at Source]
    Corr --> Op
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why no physical drawings?** Per the engine's safeguard rules, complex physical sketches (e.g., sensor housings, jigs) are abstracted into **functional block diagrams** to ensure rendering fidelity across all devices.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define Poka-Yoke. State its significance in the Toyota Production System.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Poka-Yoke is a Japanese term meaning "mistake-proofing" or "error-proofing," developed by **Shigeo Shingo** at Toyota during the 1960s. It refers to any device, mechanism, or procedure that either **prevents a defect from occurring** (prevention type) or **immediately detects and signals a defect** as soon as it occurs (detection type), thereby achieving **100% zero-defect production at the source.**

**Significance in TPS (1.5 marks):** Poka-Yoke is the **technical enabler** of the **Jidoka** (autonomation) pillar of TPS. It makes 100% inspection economically feasible, removes dependency on operator vigilance, and supports the philosophy of *"building quality in"* rather than inspecting it in. It is integral to lean manufacturing's pursuit of zero defects.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] Differentiate between Prevention and Detection types of Poka-Yoke with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Prevention Poka-Yoke | Detection Poka-Yoke |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Function** | Stops the error **before** it occurs | Catches the error **immediately after** it occurs |
| **Defect allowed?** | No — impossible by design | Yes — but flagged instantly |
| **Example** | USB connector (only fits one way) | Missing-bolt sensor that sounds an alarm |
| **Effectiveness** | Highest | High |

**Key distinction (1 mark):** Prevention is preferred because it eliminates the defect entirely; detection merely catches it before it propagates downstream.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **three implementation methods** of Poka-Yoke as classified by Shigeo Shingo, with one industrial example for each method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **four-step procedure for designing and implementing a Poka-Yoke device** in a manufacturing environment, citing a real case study. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Three Implementation Methods (7 marks):**

1. **Contact Method (2.5 marks):** In this method, the workpiece physically contacts a sensing element (limit switch, pin, jig, probe). If the contact pattern deviates from specification (wrong dimension, wrong orientation, missing feature), the sensor is *not* triggered, the cycle is aborted, and an alarm is raised.
   - *Example:* In a refrigerator compressor assembly, a locating pin in the welding fixture ensures the compressor shell can only be loaded in the correct orientation. If upside-down, the pin does not engage, and the clamp cannot close — preventing incorrect welds.

2. **Fixed-Value Method (2.5 marks):** The system counts discrete events (parts fed, operations performed, holes drilled) to a preset correct number. When the count reaches the preset value, the next cycle is enabled. Otherwise, the line halts.
   - *Example:* In an automotive airbag assembly, a sensor counts the 4 bolts required to secure the inflator module. After all 4 are detected torqued, the line moves. If only 3 are detected, the conveyor halts and an alarm lights up.

3. **Motion-Step Method (2 marks):** Each operation is treated as a discrete step in a sequence. The system verifies that Step $N$ was completed before Step $N+1$ can begin. This enforces the correct *order* of operations.
   - *Example:* On a printed circuit board line, the soldering station will not energize unless the prior station's sensor confirms component insertion has occurred.

**(b) Four-Step Implementation Procedure (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify the Defect (1.5 marks):** Use Pareto analysis on shop-floor defect logs to identify the *vital few* defects. Categorise them by 4M (Man, Machine, Material, Method).
- *Real case:* A two-wheeler manufacturer found that **42%** of all warranty returns were due to missing wheel-balancing weights — a *vital few* defect.

**Step 2 — Analyze the Cause (1.5 marks):** Apply the **5-Why technique** iteratively to reach the root cause.
- *Why 1:* Why are weights missing? — Operator forgot to attach one.
- *Why 2:* Why did the operator forget? — The bin holds more than one weight and the count is not enforced.
- *Why 3:* Why is the count not enforced? — No counting sensor exists.
- *Why 4:* Why no sensor? — Process was designed when defects were considered "acceptable."
- *Why 5:* Why acceptable? — Management did not link defect cost to warranty cost.
- *Root cause:* No systemic enforcement at the source; reliance on human vigilance.

**Step 3 — Design the Poka-Yoke (2.5 marks):** Choose type and method. A **Detection Poka-Yoke using Fixed-Value Method** is appropriate: install a **photoelectric counter sensor** on the weight-feed track that counts exactly **4 weights dispensed** per cycle; the indexing table only rotates if count = 4, else an andon lights up and the line halts.

**Step 4 — Implement, Monitor, Refine (1.5 marks):** Pilot on one line for 30 days. Track FTY, DPMO, and warranty returns. Apply **PDCA** continuously. Standardise across all assembly lines.
- *Result observed:* Warranty returns due to missing weights dropped from **42%** to **0.3%** within 3 months — an effectiveness ratio exceeding 99%.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU Model Paper 2024 – Set B]**

**(a)** With the help of a **block diagram**, explain how a Detection-type Poka-Yoke operates in a typical assembly workstation. Discuss the role of source inspection in this architecture. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** "Poka-Yoke is the engineering backbone of Jidoka in the Toyota Production System." Critically evaluate this statement with reference to TPS pillars and at least three real-world industrial examples. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Detection Poka-Yoke Block Diagram (7 marks):**

The operational architecture of a Detection-type Poka-Yoke can be represented as a closed feedback loop:

```
Operator → Operation → Part Produced → Sensor Check → Match?
                                                       ├── Yes → Pass downstream
                                                       └── No  → Andon Alarm
                                                                  ↓
                                                          Feedback to Operator
                                                                  ↓
                                                          Corrective Action at Source
```

**Working Description (4 marks):**
- The operator performs the operation (e.g., inserting a screw).
- The sensor (torque transducer or photo-eye) immediately verifies whether the operation was completed correctly (e.g., torque within 8 ± 0.5 Nm).
- If the verification **fails**, the sensor triggers an **Andon alarm** (visual: red light tower; auditory: buzzer), and the line is halted.
- The feedback loop returns control to the operator, who performs **corrective action at the source** — no defect ever propagates downstream.
- **Source inspection role (3 marks):** Inspection occurs *at the producing workstation*, not at a remote QC lab. This is Shingo's **First Ideal of Inspection.** The advantage is that the *same operator* who made the defect also catches it, enabling immediate learning. It eliminates the latency, transport cost, and information loss associated with downstream inspection.

**(b) Critical Evaluation — Poka-Yoke as the Backbone of Jidoka (7 marks):**

Jidoka (自働化), translated as **"automation with a human touch"**, is the second pillar of TPS. It赋予了 the machine or process the ability to *detect* abnormality and *autonomously stop*. Without Poka-Yoke, Jidoka collapses into mere "stop-on-demand" relying on human attention — which is unreliable. **Poka-Yoke is therefore the engineering mechanism that makes Jidoka technically feasible.**

**Three real-world examples supporting the statement:**

1. **Toyota Assembly Line — Missing Bolt Detection:** At Toyota's engine plant, a Poka-Yoke sensor verifies that all critical fasteners are torqued before the engine block is allowed to proceed to the next station. Without this Poka-Yoke, the Jidoka stop-the-line authority would rely on a worker visually inspecting every engine — error-prone and unscalable.

2. **Hirose Electric Connectors (Japan):** Mating connectors are designed with **asymmetric keying** (a Prevention Poka-Yoke) that physically prevents cross-mating between incompatible connectors. This is Poka-Yoke *enabling* Jidoka at the product-design level — eliminating an entire class of assembly error rather than relying on operator attention.

3. **Becton Dickinson Syringe Manufacturing:** Single-use syringes use a **plunger-breakaway tab** (Prevention Poka-Yoke) that physically prevents re-use. This Jidoka-equivalent design cannot be overridden by user action, embodying the philosophy of *quality built in*.

**Counter-argument (for balance):** Poka-Yoke alone is not sufficient — it must be paired with **Andon culture**, **Kaizen mindset**, and **management commitment** to sustain. Therefore, Poka-Yoke is the *engineering backbone*, but Jidoka also requires **cultural and organizational reinforcement**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Poka-Yoke Answers:**
> 1. **Confusing Poka-Yoke with Kaizen or 5S** — Poka-Yoke is a *device/ mechanism*, not a process improvement activity. Examiners deduct 2-3 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Writing only "Prevention" and "Detection" without examples** — every type/method must be accompanied by *at least one industrial example*. No example = -2 marks.
> 3. **Failing to attribute to Shigeo Shingo** — examiners expect the originator's name. Omission = -1 mark.
> 4. **Mixing up Contact, Fixed-Value, and Motion-Step** — students frequently assign the wrong example to the wrong method. Memorize one clear example per method.
> 5. **Ignoring quantitative reasoning** — when 14-mark questions ask for "explain with example," adding **numerical illustration** (e.g., DPMO reduction %) elevates the answer to full marks.
> 6. **Omitting the link to Jidoka** — any Poka-Yoke answer without reference to TPS/Jidoka is incomplete. Always close your answer with *"Thus, Poka-Yoke is the engineering enabler of Jidoka in TPS."*
> 7. **Spelling "Yoke" as "Yoka"** — standard spelling is **Poka-Yoke** (hyphenated). Lose half a mark for spelling errors.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision notes — memorize before KTU exams:**

- **Originator:** Shigeo Shingo (1960s, Toyota Motor Corporation, Japan).
- **Literal meaning:** *Poka* = inadvertent mistake; *Yoke* = to avoid (from *yokeru*). Together: "mistake-proofing."
- **Two main types:** **Prevention** (stops error before) and **Detection** (catches error immediately after).
- **Three implementation methods:** **Contact Method** (sensor + jig), **Fixed-Value Method** (counting to preset), **Motion-Step Method** (sequence verification).
- **100% inspection:** Poka-Yoke makes inspecting *every* unit economically viable — Shingo's ideal.
- **Source inspection vs Informative:** Source (at producing station) is preferred; informative (downstream) is inferior.
- **Shigeo Shingo's Three Ideals:** (1) Source inspection, (2) 100% self-inspection, (3) Successive (relay) checks.
- **Connection to TPS:** Poka-Yoke is the *technical enabler* of the **Jidoka** (autonomation) pillar.
- **Connection to Lean:** Supports *building quality in*, reduces *cost of poor quality (COPQ)*, and *Muda* (waste from rework/scrap).
- **First Toyota Case:** Ignition-switch halves; defect dropped from 1% → 0% using a parts-tray hood.
- **Prevention examples:** USB connector polarity, keyed fasteners, torque limiters, single-use syringe locks.
- **Detection examples:** Missing-bolt sensor, parts-counter feeder, sequence-lock on PCB soldering.
- **Implementation ladder:** Identify defect → 5-Why root cause → Choose PY type/method → Implement & PDCA.
- **Quantitative impact:** A well-designed Poka-Yoke can reduce defect rates by **95-99%** (per the derivation above).
- **Examiner's golden rule:** Always conclude with the link to **Jidoka + TPS + zero-defect philosophy** for full marks.
- **Memorize one example for each:** Prevention, Detection, Contact, Fixed-Value, Motion-Step — *5 examples total, no fewer.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kanban
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET746 - LEAN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Kanban

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 KANBAN — The Pulse of Lean & Just-In-Time Production

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kanban (Japanese: 看板, meaning "visual signal" or "signboard")** is a scheduling and information system for lean manufacturing and Just-In-Time (JIT) production that controls the flow of materials and work between stages of production by using **signals (cards, bins, lights, or electronic messages)** to authorize production or material movement only when it is actually needed.

In the strict KTU 2024 / Ohno sense, Kanban is *not* a production planning system — it is a **demand-pull signaling mechanism** that replaces forecast-driven "push" systems. It originated within the **Toyota Production System (TPS)** under **Taiichi Ohno** in the 1950s as the practical mechanism to operationalize JIT.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of TPS (per Toyota):**
> 1. Just-In-Time (made possible by Kanban)
> 2. Jidoka (Autonomation)
> 3. Kaizen (Continuous Improvement)

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you run a small restaurant 🍽️. The **waiter (customer)** finishes a dish and places the empty plate back on the counter. The **kitchen boy (Kanban signal)** sees the empty plate — that *empty plate is the Kanban* — and immediately refills it. The cook never pre-cooks 50 plates hoping someone will eat them. He cooks **only one plate, only when one is missing.**

That "missing plate signal" is exactly what a Kanban card does on a factory shop floor:
- **Empty bin at Station B** → triggers Kanban → Station A produces one container's worth.
- **No empty bin?** → Station A produces nothing. No overproduction, no inventory pile-up.

This is the essence of the **Pull System**.

## 1.3 Core Characteristics of Kanban

- **Visual Control**: The card/bin itself is a passive, glance-readable information system.
- **Pull (not Push)**: Work is pulled downstream; nothing is pushed upstream by forecasts.
- **WIP Limiting**: The number of Kanban in circulation **caps** the Work-In-Process.
- **Decentralized Authority**: Shop-floor workers make replenishment decisions without waiting for a planner.
- **Continuous Flow**: Smooth, level production (Heijunka) is reinforced.

## 1.4 Syllabus-Aligned Scope (KTU Module 2)

| Sub-Topic | KTU Coverage |
|---|---|
| Definition & Philosophy | Conceptual understanding |
| Two-Card System (Conveyance + Production) | System architecture |
| Kanban Rules (Toyota's 6 Rules) | Critical for 3-mark & 14-mark Qs |
| Number of Kanban Calculation | Mandatory numerical problem |
| Advantages & Limitations | Theory question |
| Implementation in TPS | Case-based questions |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📐 Deep Theoretical Analysis — How Kanban Operates

## 2.1 The Logic Flow of a Kanban System

The operation of a Kanban system can be decomposed into **6 sequential logic steps**:

1. **Downstream Demand Arises**: A customer order or next-process consumption creates demand at the consuming station.
2. **Empty Container / Withdrawal Trigger**: The downstream process sends a *Withdrawal Kanban* (Move Card) to the upstream storage area (the "supermarket").
3. **Authorization of Withdrawal**: The Withdrawal Kanban is the **only legal authorization** to take material from the supermarket.
4. **Production Authorization**: A *Production/Conveyance Kanban* is attached to the empty container and returned to the upstream process.
5. **Upstream Production**: The upstream process produces **exactly one standard container's worth** and attaches the Production Kanban.
6. **Supermarket Replenishment**: The newly produced container is placed in the supermarket; the cycle repeats.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight**: The Kanban does *not* carry an order quantity. The container size IS the order quantity. The number of Kanban in circulation IS the WIP cap.

## 2.2 Types of Kanban Cards

| Type | Japanese | Function | Attached To |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Withdrawal Kanban** | *Hikitori kanban* | Authorizes movement of parts from supermarket to consuming process | Full container in transit |
| **Production/Conveyance Kanban** | *Kadohiki kanban* | Authorizes upstream process to produce a container of parts | Empty container returning upstream |

In the classic **Two-Card System** of Toyota, these two circulate in opposite directions forming a closed loop. A simplified **Single-Card System** exists where withdrawal and production are coupled (only practical when a process feeds a single successor).

## 2.3 Toyota's Six Rules of Kanban (High-Yield for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These 6 rules are almost guaranteed to appear as a 3-mark or 14-mark question. Memorize verbatim.

1. **Do not send defective products to the downstream process.**
2. **The downstream process picks up only what is needed, when it is needed, in the amount needed.**
3. **Produce only the quantity withdrawn by the downstream process.**
4. **Level the production (Heijunka) — smooth, stable demand.**
5. **Kanban is a means of fine-tuning; do not use it to achieve large-scale production changes.**
6. **Stabilize and rationalize the process; continuously reduce the number of Kanban.**

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Number of Kanban Calculation

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL:** Never use the `|` (vertical pipe) character inside markdown tables — it breaks the table. Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute-value notation.

| Symbol | Parameter | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $N$ | Number of Kanban | Total cards in circulation | Dimensionless (integer) |
| $D$ | Average Demand Rate | Demand rate of downstream process | pieces / hour |
| $L$ | Lead Time | Total replenishment time | hours |
| $C$ | Container Capacity | Pieces per standard container | pieces / container |
| $S$ | Safety Stock Factor | Fractional safety allowance | dimensionless (e.g., 0.10) |
| $\alpha$ | Process Efficiency Factor | Usually $\alpha = 1$ in ideal Toyota case | dimensionless |

**Master Formula:**

$$
N = \frac{D \times L \times (1 + S)}{C}
$$

**Real-World Engineering Utility:**
- Determines **maximum WIP** in the system.
- Used to size **supermarket (Decoupling) inventory**.
- Lower $N$ ⇒ Tighter system, smaller inventory, faster problem detection.
- Used as a **lean maturity metric**: lean factories continually reduce $N$.

## 2.5 Other Useful Derived Expressions

**Demand During Lead Time (DLT):**

$$
\text{DLT} = D \times L
$$

**Safety Stock (in pieces):**

$$
\text{SS} = D \times L \times S = \text{DLT} \times S
$$

**Total Pieces in Pipeline:**

$$
\text{Total Inventory} = N \times C
$$

**Check:** $N$ must always be **rounded up** to the next whole integer; a fractional card is meaningless in practice.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Analysis

## 3.1 Derivation of the Number of Kanban Formula

We derive the master formula from first principles so that you understand *why* each term exists — examiners reward conceptual clarity.

### Step 1 — Define the Steady-State Demand

Let $D$ be the average consumption rate of the downstream process (pieces per hour). This demand is **fixed** in the short run under Heijunka-leveled production.

### Step 2 — Total Pieces Required During Lead Time

During the lead time $L$, the downstream process will consume:

$$
\text{DLT} = D \times L \quad \text{(pieces)}
$$

### Step 3 — Add Safety Stock

To guard against demand variability, lead-time variability, and quality rejections, we multiply by $(1 + S)$:

$$
\text{Pieces with safety} = D \times L \times (1 + S)
$$

### Step 4 — Convert Pieces to Kanban (Containers)

Each Kanban represents one container of $C$ pieces. So the number of containers (Kanban) is:

$$
N = \frac{D \times L \times (1 + S)}{C}
$$

Since $N$ must be a whole integer, the **final design value** is:

$$
N_{\text{final}} = \left\lceil \frac{D \times L \times (1 + S)}{C} \right\rceil
$$

where $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ denotes the **ceiling function**.

---

## 3.2 KTU-Style Solved Numerical Problem

> **[KTU University Exam - Sample Problem, Module 2, 14-mark adaptation]**

**Problem Statement:**
An assembly line consumes Part X at a rate of **50 pieces per shift (8 hours)**. The container holds **25 pieces**. The lead time for replenishment from the upstream process is **2 shifts**. Management wants a **10% safety stock**. Determine:
- (a) The number of Kanban required. *(7 marks)*
- (b) The total WIP in the system and comment on the result. *(7 marks)*

### Solution — Part (a)

**Given:**
- $D = 50$ pieces per shift
- $L = 2$ shifts
- $C = 25$ pieces per container
- $S = 0.10$ (10%)

**Step 1:** Compute demand during lead time.

$$
\text{DLT} = D \times L = 50 \times 2 = 100 \text{ pieces}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply safety factor.

$$
\text{Pieces with safety} = 100 \times (1 + 0.10) = 100 \times 1.10 = 110 \text{ pieces}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to number of Kanban.

$$
N = \frac{110}{25} = 4.4
$$

**Step 4:** Round up to next integer (ceiling function).

$$
\boxed{N_{\text{final}} = 5 \text{ Kanban}}
$$

### Solution — Part (b)

**Total WIP / Pipeline Inventory:**

$$
\text{Total Inventory} = N_{\text{final}} \times C = 5 \times 25 = 125 \text{ pieces}
$$

**Comment:**
With 5 Kanban in circulation, the system carries 125 pieces — equivalent to **2.5 shifts of demand**. This is a healthy lean level. Under Rule 6 of Toyota's Kanban rules, the company should aim to gradually **reduce N to 4** by cutting lead time (e.g., via SMED, layout improvement) or container size, thereby exposing problems and driving further improvement.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Parameter Variation

| Change | Effect on N | Lean Implication |
|---|---|---|
| $\uparrow D$ (demand rises) | $N \uparrow$ | Need more WIP → add cards, but investigate root cause |
| $\uparrow L$ (slower supplier) | $N \uparrow$ | Bad — indicates lead-time reduction is needed |
| $\uparrow C$ (bigger container) | $N \downarrow$ | Fewer cards but more inventory at risk |
| $\uparrow S$ (higher safety) | $N \uparrow$ | Hides variability — anti-lean |
| $\uparrow$ Process Quality | $N \downarrow$ | Less safety stock needed → leaner |

> [!NOTE]
> **Counter-Intuitive Truth:** In Kanban, a *defect* reveals itself immediately as an empty supermarket. **Larger N is a symptom of problems, not a solution.** Lean philosophy continuously drives N downward.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & System Schematics

## 4.1 The Two-Card Kanban Loop — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    procA[Upstream Process A - Production]
    superA[Supermarket A - Decoupling Store]
    procB[Downstream Process B - Assembly]
    cust[Customer Order - Demand Pull]

    cust -- Demand signal --> procB
    procB -- Empty container triggers Withdrawal Kanban --> superA
    superA -- Material withdrawn + Withdrawal Kanban --> procB
    procB -- Full container used by line --> cust
    procB -- Empty container + Production Kanban returned --> procA
    procA -- Produces one container of pieces --> superA

    wk[Withdrawal Kanban Card]
    pk[Production Kanban Card]

    procB -. Attached: Withdrawal Kanban .-> superA
    procB -. Attached: Production Kanban on empty bin .-> procA
```

```mermaid
    step9 -. Feedback Loop .-> step4

    style step1 fill:#E6F2FF
    style step2 fill:#E6F2FF
```

## 4.3 Toyota's Six Rules — Hierarchical Logic Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[Kanban System Discipline]

    r1[Rule 1: No Defects Downstream]
    r2[Rule 2: Take Only What Is Needed, When Needed]

    r4[Rule 4: Level the Production - Heijunka]
    r5[Rule 5: Kanban Fine Tunes - Not Redesigns]
    r6[Rule 6: Reduce the Number of Kanban Continuously]

    root --> r1

    root --> r3

    r2 --> r3
    r6 --> r5
    r1 --> r6
```
