<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Type: 3-mark definition]**

**Q: Define Kanban. List any four advantages of a Kanban system.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Kanban is a Japanese term meaning "visual signal" or "card." It is a **pull-based production control system** used in lean manufacturing to authorize and control the flow of materials and work-in-progress between processes. Each Kanban carries information that signals when to produce, what to produce, and how much to produce — typically equal to one standard container.

**Four Advantages:**
1. **Reduces Work-In-Process (WIP) inventory** — direct WIP cap.
2. **Eliminates overproduction** — only pull-driven output.
3. **Quick problem identification** — shortages surface immediately (visual control).
4. **Reduces lead time** and improves flow.
5. (Bonus) Empowers shop-floor workers through decentralized control.

---

### Question A.2
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Type: 3-mark conceptual]**

**Q: Differentiate between the Withdrawal Kanban and the Production Kanban.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Withdrawal Kanban | Production Kanban |
|---|---|---|
| Japanese name | *Hikitori kanban* | *Kadohiki kanban* |
| Function | Authorizes **movement** of parts from supermarket to consuming process | Authorizes **production** of a container at the upstream process |
| Attached to | Full container in transit | Empty container returning upstream |
| Direction | Downstream ← Upstream (Supermarket) | Upstream process receives it |
| Trigger | Empty bin at downstream | Empty bin returned by downstream |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full Descriptive / Numerical Questions (14 Marks)

> **[ESE KTU Module 2 — Internal Choice Pattern: Answer ANY ONE from Question 7A OR 7B]**

---

### 📌 Question 7A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2 K-level: Apply/Analyze]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the **operating procedure** of a two-card Kanban system in Just-In-Time production. State clearly the **six rules of Kanban** as prescribed by Toyota. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A machining cell produces Part M. The next process consumes **80 pieces per day**. The standard container holds **40 pieces**. The replenishment lead time is **3 days**. If the safety stock factor is **0.20 (20%)**, calculate the number of Kanban required. Comment on the lean implications. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Answer — 7A(a) [7 marks]

**Operating Procedure of a Two-Card Kanban System:**

1. A customer demand or downstream consumption creates the requirement for parts at the consuming station.
2. When the container at the consuming station becomes empty, the operator detaches the **Withdrawal Kanban** and sends it to the supermarket of the upstream process.
3. The Withdrawal Kanban is the only authorization to withdraw material from the supermarket. A full container is picked up and moved to the downstream process.
4. The empty container is attached with the **Production Kanban** and returned to the upstream process.
5. The upstream process uses the Production Kanban as authorization to produce **exactly one container's worth** of the part.
6. The produced container is sent to the supermarket, completing the closed loop.

**Toyota's Six Rules of Kanban:**

1. Do not send defective products to the downstream process.
2. Take only what is needed, when needed, in the amount needed.
3. Produce only the quantity withdrawn by the downstream process.
4. Level the production (Heijunka).
5. Kanban is a means of fine-tuning — not for major production changes.
6. Stabilize and rationalize the process; continuously reduce the number of Kanban.

---

#### Model Answer — 7A(b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $D = 80$ pieces/day
- $C = 40$ pieces/container
- $L = 3$ days
- $S = 0.20$

**Step 1: Demand during Lead Time**

$$
\text{DLT} = D \times L = 80 \times 3 = 240 \text{ pieces}
$$

**Step 2: Apply Safety Factor**

$$
\text{Pieces with safety} = 240 \times (1 + 0.20) = 240 \times 1.20 = 288 \text{ pieces}
$$

**Step 3: Number of Kanban**

$$
N = \frac{288}{40} = 7.2
$$

**Step 4: Round Up**

$$
\boxed{N_{\text{final}} = 8 \text{ Kanban}}
$$

**Lean Implication Comment:**
8 Kanban ⇒ WIP = $8 \times 40 = 320$ pieces = **4 days of demand** held in pipeline. This is reasonable but offers scope for lean improvement. Per Rule 6, the cell should engage in **Kaizen** to reduce lead time or container size, gradually reducing $N$ to 7 or 6 — exposing problems and tightening the system.

---

### 📌 Question 7B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2 — Cognitive: Understand/Apply]**

**Q: (a)** Describe the **types of Kanban** with neat sketches/schematic representation of each. Explain why Kanban is considered the **heart of the Toyota Production System**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A component flows through three processes. Daily demand is **600 pieces**, container size is **50 pieces**, lead time is **0.5 days**, and the safety factor is **10%**. Compute (i) Number of Kanban, (ii) Total WIP in pieces, (iii) If lead time reduces to 0.25 days with all else same, what is the new $N$? *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Answer — 7B(a) [7 marks]

**Types of Kanban (Two-Card System):**

**(i) Withdrawal Kanban (Hikitori Kanban):**
This card authorizes the **movement** of parts from the upstream supermarket to the downstream consuming process. It circulates between the supermarket and the downstream process. *(Schematic: Container attached to card moves ← from Supermarket → to Line)*

**(ii) Production/Conveyance Kanban (Kadohiki Kanban):**
This card authorizes the **upstream process to manufacture** a specified quantity (one container) of parts. It is attached to the empty container and travels upstream.

**Why Kanban is the Heart of TPS:**
- Kanban **operationalizes JIT** — the first pillar of TPS — by making demand visible and pulling production.
- It **caps WIP**, exposes variability, and triggers problem-solving (Jidoka + Kaizen).
- It decentralizes control — every worker holds pull authority.
- It drives continuous reduction of inventory, which is the strategic weapon of Toyota.

---

#### Model Answer — 7B(b) [7 marks]

**Given (Initial Case):**
- $D = 600$ pieces/day, $C = 50$, $L = 0.5$ day, $S = 0.10$

**(i) Number of Kanban:**

$$
N_1 = \frac{600 \times 0.5 \times (1 + 0.10)}{50} = \frac{330}{50} = 6.6
$$

$$
\boxed{N_1 = 7 \text{ Kanban}}
$$

**(ii) Total WIP:**

$$
\text{WIP}_1 = 7 \times 50 = 350 \text{ pieces}
$$

**(iii) Reduced Lead Time Case ($L = 0.25$ day):**

$$
N_2 = \frac{600 \times 0.25 \times 1.10}{50} = \frac{165}{50} = 3.3
$$

$$
\boxed{N_2 = 4 \text{ Kanban}}
$$

**Lean Insight:** Halving lead time from 0.5 → 0.25 days reduces Kanban from 7 → 4, a **42.9% reduction in WIP** — demonstrating Toyota's Rule 6 in action: shorter lead time = leaner Kanban system.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks on Kanban Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to round UP** $N$ to the next integer. A fractional Kanban is meaningless. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Confusing Withdrawal vs. Production Kanban.** Memorize the Japanese names and the direction of flow. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Skipping safety stock** in the calculation: must multiply by $(1 + S)$, not just $D \times L$. (–2 marks)
> 4. **Not stating the Toyota Six Rules** verbatim — examiners expect exact wording for Rule 1 and Rule 6. (–2 marks)
> 5. **Ignoring units** in numerical problems — always declare $D$ in pieces/hour or pieces/day consistently with $L$. (–1 mark)
> 6. **Confusing push vs. pull**: Kanban is *pull*. If your answer says "forecasts drive production," you have failed conceptually. (–2 marks)

---

## 5.3 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Kanban**

- ⭐ **Kanban = Visual Pull Signal** — it does NOT schedule; it AUTHORIZES.
- ⭐ **Two cards**: Withdrawal (movement) + Production (manufacture).
- ⭐ **Pull, not push** — downstream triggers upstream.
- ⭐ **6 Rules of Toyota** — memorize all six; expect a direct question.
- ⭐ **Master Formula**:

  $$
  N = \frac{D \times L \times (1 + S)}{C} \quad \text{(always round UP)}
  $$

- ⭐ WIP in pieces = $N \times C$.
- ⭐ **Lean philosophy**: continuously REDUCE $N$ by attacking $L$, $S$, and $C$.
- ⭐ Kanban requires **level production (Heijunka)** — without leveling, Kanban oscillates.
- ⭐ **Defects = immediate visibility** — Kanban exposes bad quality instantly (Jidoka link).
- ⭐ **Two-card vs. Single-card**: Two-card is for multi-customer processes; single-card for linear one-to-one flow.
- ⭐ Kanban is the **operational heart** of JIT, the first pillar of TPS.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET746 - LEAN MANUFACTURING > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Automation # 1. Automation — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Automation** in manufacturing is the integrated use of control systems, sensors, actuators, computer hardware, software, and information technologies to operate production equipment and processes **with minimal or no human intervention**, thereby achieving higher consistency, throughput, quality, and safety in a Lean Production environment. In the specific context of **Lean Manufacturing (PEMET746)**, automation is studied under its Japanese-origin philosophy called **Jidoka (自働化)** — also written as **Autonomation**. Jidoka is one of the two pillars of the Toyota Production System (TPS), the other being **Just-In-Time (JIT)**. > [!NOTE] > **Jidoka (Autonomation) — Core Definition** > "Automation with a human touch" — equipment is designed to **automatically detect defects, stop the process, and alert the operator**, preventing the production of defective items and exposing the root cause of the abnormality. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!TIP] > **Analogy — The Smoke Detector in Your Home** > Imagine a normal electric heater. If it overheats, it will keep running and may cause a fire. Now imagine a *smart* heater that has a built-in temperature sensor: when it detects overheating, it **automatically switches off** AND **rings a buzzer** to alert you, AND **displays a flashing light** showing where the fault occurred. The heater didn't just stop — it stopped intelligently and called for human help *only when needed*. **That intelligent stopping = Jidoka.** > > Now, contrast this with a *dumb* machine that just keeps producing defective parts because nobody noticed. Jidoka ensures: **No defect passes, no machine runs when abnormal, and the root cause gets solved.** ## 1.3 The Three Classical Types of Automation (KTU High-Yield) | # | Type of Automation | Core Idea | Typical Example | |---|--------------------|-----------|-----------------| | **1** | **Fixed (Hard) Automation** | Dedicated equipment for a fixed sequence of operations on a specific product | Automotive welding lines, bottling plants | | **2** | **Programmable Automation** | Sequence of operations can be changed by reprogramming; high changeover cost | CNC machines, industrial robots | | **3** | **Flexible (Soft) Automation** | Quick changeover between products with minimal downtime | FMS, AGVs, modular robotic cells | ## 1.4 Why Automation is Discussed Inside Lean Manufacturing Lean is often misunderstood as being "anti-automation." This is **FALSE**. Lean **embraces** automation — but insists on automation that: 1. **Empowers people** rather than replacing them blindly. 2. **Detects abnormalities** and stops the process. 3. **Exposes problems** so they can be solved at the **root cause** (a Lean principle). 4. Creates flow and pull (JIT) without producing waste (muda). > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Misconception (KTU Examiner Trap):** > Lean Manufacturing does NOT mean "low automation" or "no automation." Lean integrates automation strategically so that **variation and defects are prevented at the source**, in line with Poka-Yoke (mistake-proofing) and the 5S discipline. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Pillars (Functions) of Jidoka — Examinable Framework Jidoka is built on **four functional pillars** that examiners frequently ask as **5-mark or 7-mark** descriptive questions: 1. **Detect the abnormality** — Sensors, andon lights, SPC charts. 2. **Stop the process automatically** — Machine stops at the first sign of defect. 3. **Fix the immediate condition** — Operator responds to the andon call. 4. **Investigate the root cause and install a countermeasure** — 5-Why analysis, Kaizen, Poka-Yoke. ## 2.2 Automation Strategies — When to Automate (KTU Conceptual Question) Automation is justified when the following **four conditions** are simultaneously met: - **High Volume** — The operation is repeated many times. - **Low Variety** — Product design is stable (no frequent changes). - **High Danger / Hazardous Environment** — E.g., welding, painting, foundry. - **High Precision & Consistency Required** — Tolerances tighter than human capability. If these conditions are absent, Lean prefers **manual or semi-automatic** processes combined with **standardized work**. ## 2.3 Lean Automation Principles (TPS Foundation) | S.No. | Principle | Meaning | |-------|-----------|---------| | 1 | **Build a stop-function into every machine** | Any abnormality halts production. | | 2 | **Create a system to signal the abnormality (Andon)** | Visual & audible signal to operator/supervisor. | | 3 | **Empower every worker to stop the line** | Workers are owners of quality. | | 4 | **Separate machine work from human work** | Operator does value-added tasks; machine does repetitive work. | | 5 | **Pursue Kaizen on the production floor** | Continuous small improvements from the gemba. | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet The economic justification of automation in any Lean project is tested numerically. The following table compiles the **mandatory formulas**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mandatory Formulas for Automation Justification (Payback, ROI, Break-Even, OEE)** | # | Formula (LaTeX) | Variables Explained | Unit | |---|-----------------|---------------------|------| | 1 | $PB = \dfrac{C_{inv}}{S_{annual}}$ | $PB$ = Payback period, $C_{inv}$ = Capital investment, $S_{annual}$ = Annual savings | Years | | 2 | $ROI = \dfrac{S_{annual} - O_{annual}}{C_{inv}} \times 100\%$ | $ROI$ = Return on Investment, $O_{annual}$ = Annual operating cost | Percent | | 3 | $BEP_{units} = \dfrac{F}{(P - V)}$ | $BEP$ = Break-even point, $F$ = Fixed cost, $P$ = Price/unit, $V$ = Variable cost/unit | Units | | 4 | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | $A$ = Availability, $P$ = Performance, $Q$ = Quality (all decimal fractions) | Decimal (0 to 1) | | 5 | $T_{cycle} = T_{mach} + T_{handl} + T_{wait}$ | $T_{cycle}$ = Total cycle time of automated cell | Seconds / Minutes | | 6 | $C_{unit} = C_{fixed} + V_{unit} \cdot Q$ | $C_{unit}$ = Unit cost, $C_{fixed}$ = Total fixed cost, $V_{unit}$ = Variable cost/unit, $Q$ = Quantity | Currency | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Style Convention:** When writing $P - V$ inside a table row, use $\vert$ for absolute value references. Example: $\vert P - V \vert$ to avoid breaking markdown syntax. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of Jidoka-Based Automation - **Automotive Sector:** Toyota assembly lines use **Andon cords** — any worker can pull the cord to halt the line. - **Semiconductor Industry:** Equipment automatically stops on detection of wafer defect using SPC. - **Electronics Assembly:** Pick-and-place machines stop when component feed is wrong (Poka-Yoke integration). - **Pharmaceuticals:** Automatic rejection of faulty blister packs using vision systems. ## 2.6 Fixed vs Programmable vs Flexible Automation — Comparison Matrix | Parameter | Fixed Automation | Programmable Automation | Flexible Automation | |-----------|-----------------|------------------------|---------------------| | Initial Investment | **High** | Medium | **Very High** | | Flexibility | **Very Low** | Medium (requires reprogramming) | **High** | | Changeover Time | Hours–Days | Hours | **Minutes** | | Best Suited For | Mass production | Batch production | Mixed-model production | | Skilled Labour Required | Low | High | **Very High** | | Lean Fit | Limited (high WIP risk) | Moderate | **High (supports Heijunka)** | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Payback Period (PB) Formula **Starting Premise:** Payback period is the time required for cumulative cash savings to recover the initial capital outlay. Let: - $C_{inv}$ = Initial capital investment on the automated system (in currency). - $S_{annual}$ = Net annual cash saving (in currency per year) generated by the automation. - $PB$ = Payback period (in years). **Step 1 — Write the cumulative cash flow equation.** $$ C_{cum}(t) = S_{annual} \cdot t - C_{inv} $$ **Step 2 — Apply the boundary condition at payback.** At payback, the cumulative cash flow just equals zero (the investment is fully recovered, no more, no less). $$ C_{cum}(PB) = 0 $$ **Step 3 — Substitute the boundary condition into Step 1.** $$ S_{annual} \cdot PB - C_{inv} = 0 $$ **Step 4 — Solve algebraically for $PB$.** $$ S_{annual} \cdot PB = C_{inv} $$ $$ PB = \dfrac{C_{inv}}{S_{annual}} $$ This is the **final, examiner-ready form** of the Payback Period formula. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Return on Investment (ROI) **Starting Premise:** ROI measures the net annual profit (savings minus operating cost) per unit of capital invested, expressed as a percentage. Let: - $S_{annual}$ = Annual gross savings. - $O_{annual}$ = Annual operating cost of the automation (power, maintenance, software licence). - $C_{inv}$ = Capital investment. **Step 1 — Compute the net annual profit.** $$ P_{net} = S_{annual} - O_{annual} $$ **Step 2 — Express profit as a fraction of investment.** $$ r = \dfrac{P_{net}}{C_{inv}} = \dfrac{S_{annual} - O_{annual}}{C_{inv}} $$ **Step 3 — Convert to percentage.** $$ ROI = \dfrac{S_{annual} - O_{annual}}{C_{inv}} \times 100\% $$ ## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — Payback Period & ROI **Problem Statement (KTU Style):** > A manufacturing firm in Kerala is evaluating a Jidoka-based inspection cell. > - Capital investment $C_{inv}$ = Rs. 30,00,000 > - Annual labour savings $S_{lab}$ = Rs. 8,00,000 > - Annual defect-cost reduction $S_{def}$ = Rs. 4,00,000 > - Annual operating cost $O_{annual}$ = Rs. 2,00,000 > - Required payback ≤ 3 years > > *Find: Payback Period, ROI, and decide whether to automate.* **Step 1 — Compute total annual savings.** $$ S_{annual} = S_{lab} + S_{def} = 800000 + 400000 = 1200000 $$ **Step 2 — Compute Payback Period.** $$ PB = \dfrac{C_{inv}}{S_{annual}} = \dfrac{3000000}{1200000} = 2.5 \;\text{years} $$ **Step 3 — Compute Net Annual Profit.** $$ P_{net} = S_{annual} - O_{annual} = 1200000 - 200000 = 1000000 $$ **Step 4 — Compute ROI.** $$ ROI = \dfrac{1000000}{3000000} \times 100\% = 33.33\% $$ **Step 5 — Decision (Lean Justification Check).** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** Since $PB = 2.5 \;\text{years} < 3 \;\text{years}$ (acceptable) and $ROI = 33.33\%$ is attractive, the Jidoka-based automation is **economically justified**. Additionally, qualitative benefits (defect prevention, worker safety, Poka-Yoke integration) strengthen the Lean business case. ## 3.4 Break-Even Point Derivation **Step 1 — Define total cost and total revenue.** $$ C_{total} = F + V \cdot Q $$ $$ R_{total} = P \cdot Q $$ **Step 2 — Apply break-even condition $C_{total} = R_{total}$.** $$ F + V \cdot Q = P \cdot Q $$ **Step 3 — Isolate $Q$.** $$ F = P \cdot Q - V \cdot Q $$ $$ F = (P - V) \cdot Q $$ $$ BEP_{units} = \dfrac{F}{P - V} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall:** Do **not** substitute a negative value for $P - V$ in a break-even table row. If $P < V$, the business is structurally unprofitable and no break-even exists. Use the absolute value $\vert P - V \vert$ in tables to keep the formula technically well-defined for marginal analysis. ## 3.5 OEE Availability Derivation $$ A = \dfrac{T_{operational} - T_{down}}{T_{operational}} $$ $$ P = \dfrac{T_{ideal} \cdot N_{actual}}{T_{run}} $$ $$ Q = \dfrac{N_{good}}{N_{total}} $$ $$ OEE = A \times P \times Q $$ > [!NOTE] > **World-Class Benchmark (Lean Reference):** $OEE \geq 85\%$ is the world-class standard. Most Indian SMEs operate at $40\% - 60\%$, indicating massive scope for Lean Automation (Jidoka) projects. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Four Pillars of Jidoka (Autonomation) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Jidoka Cycle Triggered] --> B[Detect Abnormality
Sensor Vision SPC] B --> C[Automatic Stop
Machine Halt Andon Light ON] C --> D[Operator Response
Andon Call Immediate Fix] D --> E[Root Cause Analysis
5 Why Fishbone Kaizen] E --> F[Install Countermeasure
Poka Yoke Mistake Proofing] F --> G[Standardise and Restart
Updated Standard Work] G --> A ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Automation Hierarchy in Lean Manufacturing ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Manual Processes] --> B[Semi Automation] B --> C[Fixed Automation] C --> D[Programmable Automation] D --> E[Flexible Automation FMS] E --> F[Intelligent Automation
Jidoka with AI Sensors] F --> G[Fully Integrated Smart Factory
Industry 4.0] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Flow: When to Automate in a Lean Project ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[New Process to Design] --> Q1{Is Volume High?} Q1 -- No --> M1[Use Manual Work
with Standardised Work] Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is Variety Low?} Q2 -- No --> F1[Use Flexible Automation
FMS AGV] Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Is Process Hazardous?} Q3 -- Yes --> Auto1[Automate with Jidoka
Poka Yoke Sensors] Q3 -- No --> Q4{Is Precision Beyond Human?} Q4 -- No --> M2[Use Lean Manual Process] Q4 -- Yes --> Auto2[Automate with Jidoka
SPC Vision System] Auto1 --> Econ[Economic Justification
Payback ROI OEE] Auto2 --> Econ F1 --> Econ M1 --> Stop1[Stop - No Automation Needed] M2 --> Stop1 Econ --> Dec{PB and ROI Acceptable?} Dec -- Yes --> Implement[Implement with Andon System] Dec -- No --> Reject[Reject - Revisit Process Design] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Andon System in Jidoka-Based Production ```mermaid flowchart LR Worker[Operator at Workstation] -->|Pulls Andon Cord| Signal[Andon Light and Buzzer Activated] Signal --> Board[Andon Board Display Real Time Status] Board --> TL[Team Leader Rushes to Station] TL --> Diagnose[Diagnose Problem] Diagnose --> Fix[Apply Countermeasure] Fix --> Resume[Resume Production] Resume --> Log[Update Kaizen Log] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper] Define **Jidoka (Autonomation)**. Why is it considered one of the two pillars of the Toyota Production System? **Model Answer (3 marks):** Jidoka is a Japanese term that literally means **"automation with a human touch."** It is a Lean principle in which a machine is designed to **automatically detect defects or abnormalities in the process, stop the operation itself, and alert the operator** through a visual and audio signal (Andon). The process is paused so that the root cause of the problem can be identified and resolved before any defective product moves downstream. Jidoka is one of the **two pillars** of the Toyota Production System (TPS), the other being **Just-In-Time (JIT)**. While JIT ensures the right items are produced in the right quantity at the right time, **Jidoka ensures that whatever is produced is of the right quality** by preventing the propagation of defects. Together, they deliver a Lean production system that is **pull-based and built-in-quality**, rather than relying on end-of-line inspection. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary Exam] Differentiate between **Fixed Automation** and **Flexible Automation** in any three parameters relevant to Lean Manufacturing. **Model Answer (3 marks):** The three differentiating parameters are: 1. **Flexibility:** Fixed automation is designed for a single, dedicated product with very low flexibility. Flexible automation, on the other hand, can be quickly reconfigured to produce multiple product variants within the same system, making it ideal for mixed-model production. 2. **Changeover Time and Cost:** Fixed automation involves long, expensive changeovers (sometimes requiring new tooling). Flexible automation uses programmable logic controllers (PLCs), robots, and modular tooling that allow changeovers in minutes with minimal cost, which directly supports **Heijunka (level scheduling)** in Lean. 3. **Lean Suitability and WIP Risk:** Fixed automation tends to encourage high batch production and therefore generates **WIP (work-in-process) inventory**, which is a form of **muda (waste)**. Flexible automation supports small, frequent batches and one-piece flow, aligning with Lean goals. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Convention:** Part B questions in a module carry 14 marks and provide an **internal choice** between two alternatives. Each alternative is further divided into sub-parts (typically 7 marks + 7 marks). --- ### **Option A — Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Main Exam] #### (a) Explain in detail the **four pillars / functions of Jidoka (Autonomation)** with a suitable example. (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** Jidoka, when fully implemented, is supported by four functional pillars that ensure built-in quality and continuous improvement: **Pillar 1 — Detection of the Abnormality:** The first pillar requires that the production system be equipped with sensors, gauges, vision systems, or statistical process control (SPC) charts that can **detect any deviation** from the standard condition. This includes dimensional errors, temperature variations, abnormal sounds, or colour mismatches. The aim is to detect a defect at the moment of creation, not at the end of the line. *Example:* A CNC lathe is fitted with a digital probe that measures the diameter of every turned shaft. If the diameter deviates by more than $\pm 0.02$ mm from the standard, the probe signals an error. **Pillar 2 — Automatic Stop of the Process:** The second pillar converts the detected signal into an **automatic halt** of the machine. The line does not continue producing; it stops itself. This is the heart of Jidoka — *the machine does not produce defects.* *Example:* The CNC lathe above halts its spindle rotation and the conveyor feeding the next station, immediately stopping further production of suspect parts. **Pillar 3 — Immediate Fix at the Workstation:** The third pillar requires an **Andon system** (visual + audible signal) to summon an operator or team leader to the affected station. The operator performs a quick diagnosis and an immediate correction so that production can resume quickly without delay accumulating in the form of WIP. *Example:* The Andon light above the CNC station turns yellow, the team leader arrives within 60 seconds, and a minor tool-wear issue is corrected. **Pillar 4 — Root Cause Investigation and Countermeasure Installation:** The fourth pillar is the most strategically important. The team uses **5-Why analysis**, **Ishikawa (fishbone) diagrams**, and **Poka-Yoke (mistake-proofing)** to find the root cause of the abnormality and install a permanent countermeasure so that the same problem does not recur. This is where Jidoka connects directly with **Kaizen**. *Example:* The team discovers the tool was wearing out every 200 parts, so they install an **automatic tool-life monitor** and schedule a tool change at 180 parts — a Poka-Yoke device. These four pillars together transform automation from a mere cost-saving tool into a **lean philosophy of empowerment and learning**. --- #### (b) A company is planning to introduce an automated welding cell. The capital cost is Rs. 50,00,000. The annual savings are Rs. 15,00,000 and the annual operating cost is Rs. 3,00,000. Calculate the **Payback Period** and **ROI**, and justify whether the investment should be made. (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — Identify the given values.** $$ C_{inv} = 50{,}00{,}000 $$ $$ S_{annual} = 15{,}00{,}000 $$ $$ O_{annual} = 3{,}00{,}000 $$ **Step 2 — Calculate the Payback Period.** $$ PB = \dfrac{C_{inv}}{S_{annual}} = \dfrac{50{,}00{,}000}{15{,}00{,}000} = 3.33\;\text{years} $$ **Step 3 — Calculate the Net Annual Profit.** $$ P_{net} = S_{annual} - O_{annual} = 15{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000 = 12{,}00{,}000 $$ **Step 4 — Calculate the ROI.** $$ ROI = \dfrac{P_{net}}{C_{inv}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{12{,}00{,}000}{50{,}00{,}000} \times 100\% = 24\% $$ **Step 5 — Justification (Lean Decision).** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation for KTU Valuation:** > - Payback Period = 3.33 years is acceptable in most manufacturing industries (typical acceptable benchmark $\leq 4$ years). > - ROI = 24% is significantly higher than the typical bank fixed-deposit rate of 7%, indicating the investment yields far better returns than passive alternatives. > - **Qualitative benefits** in a Lean context (defect prevention, worker safety from welding fumes, Poka-Yoke integration, reduced WIP, support for Heijunka) further strengthen the case. > > **Conclusion:** The automated welding cell is **justified** and should be implemented with a Jidoka-based Andon system. --- ### **Option B — Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary Exam] #### (a) Compare the **three types of automation** (Fixed, Programmable, Flexible) with suitable engineering examples. Discuss which type best supports **Lean Manufacturing**. (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** **Type 1 — Fixed (Hard) Automation:** Fixed automation uses special-purpose equipment designed to produce a **single, standardised product at very high volume**. The sequence of operations is fixed by the design of the equipment itself. Once installed, it cannot be easily changed to another product. Examples include automotive engine-block machining transfer lines, glass-bottle manufacturing plants, and paper mills. The advantage is extremely **low unit cost at high volumes**; the disadvantage is the **complete lack of flexibility** and the risk of producing large quantities of defects if the dedicated process drifts. **Type 2 — Programmable Automation:** Programmable automation uses general-purpose equipment (such as CNC machines, industrial robots, or PLC-controlled systems) that can be **reprogrammed to produce different products or perform different sequences**. However, the reprogramming and changeover take a significant amount of time, making it best suited for **batch production**. Examples include CNC turning centres, robotic welding cells, and printed circuit board (PCB) assembly machines. The advantage is the ability to produce small batches of complex parts with consistent quality; the disadvantage is the **downtime during reprogramming**. **Type 3 — Flexible (Soft) Automation:** Flexible automation is an extension of programmable automation in which the changeover between products is **automated and very fast (often minutes)**, allowing a single system to produce a **mixed model of parts in random order**. Examples include Flexible Manufacturing Systems (FMS), Automated Guided Vehicles (AGV), and modular robotic assembly cells. The advantage is **maximum responsiveness** to customer demand variations; the disadvantage is the **very high initial investment** and the requirement of **highly skilled operators and maintenance staff**. **Comparison Summary Table:** | Parameter | Fixed | Programmable | Flexible | |-----------|-------|--------------|----------| | Volume | Very High | Medium | Medium-High | | Variety | Very Low | Medium | **High** | | Changeover | Days | Hours | **Minutes** | | Best Lean Fit | Weak | Moderate | **Strong** | **Which Type Best Supports Lean Manufacturing?** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Flexible Automation** best supports Lean Manufacturing because it directly enables **Heijunka (production levelling), one-piece flow, mixed-model production, and quick response to Takt time changes.** Fixed automation, in contrast, encourages large batches and inventory, which is the antithesis of Lean thinking. --- #### (b) Explain the role of the **Andon System** in Jidoka. How does it integrate with **Heijunka, Poka-Yoke, and 5-Why** to create a complete Lean Automation loop? (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** **Definition of Andon:** The Andon system is a **real-time visual and audible signalling system** used on a Lean production line to indicate the **status of production** — running normally, requiring assistance, or stopped due to an abnormality. The classic Andon is a board with coloured lights (commonly green for normal, yellow for warning, red for stop) positioned at every workstation and visible to operators, team leaders, and supervisors across the floor. A pull-cord or button allows any worker to summon help. **Role of Andon in Jidoka:** In the Jidoka philosophy, Andon is the **operational bridge** between the machine's automatic detection of an abnormality and the human's role in solving it. When the machine detects a defect or variation, it halts and **triggers the Andon**. The Andon immediately calls for a team leader (within a defined response time, typically 30–60 seconds) to come to the workstation, diagnose the issue, and either apply a quick fix or escalate the problem. This ensures that defects are **not allowed to propagate** to subsequent stations and that production resumes only when the abnormal condition is resolved. **Integration with Heijunka:** Heijunka is the practice of **levelling production volume and mix** over time to avoid the peaks and valleys of demand. The Andon system supports Heijunka by **exposing stoppages in real time**, allowing managers to level the workload, identify chronic imbalances between stations, and re-balance the line. Without Andon, stoppages would be hidden, and Heijunka planning would be based on guesswork rather than ground reality. **Integration with Poka-Yoke:** Poka-Yoke (mistake-proofing) refers to designing a process so that **an error becomes physically impossible** or is detected instantly. The Andon is the **signalling component of a Poka-Yoke device**. For example, a fixture that detects a missing part in an assembly triggers the Andon to stop the line, so the operator is immediately informed that the mistake-proofing has caught an error. **Integration with 5-Why:** When the Andon is activated repeatedly for the same type of abnormality, the team is expected to conduct a **5-Why analysis** — asking "why" iteratively until the root cause is reached. The Andon log provides the **historical data** of abnormalities, while 5-Why converts that data into **Kaizen improvements**. The result is a closed-loop system: **Detect (Jidoka) → Signal (Andon) → Solve (5-Why) → Prevent (Poka-Yoke) → Level (Heijunka)**. > [!NOTE] > **Complete Lean Automation Loop:** > $$\text{Jidoka} \rightarrow \text{Andon} \rightarrow \text{5-Why} \rightarrow \text{Poka-Yoke} \rightarrow \text{Heijunka} \rightarrow \text{Kaizen}$$ This integration is what makes Lean Automation fundamentally different from traditional mass-production automation, which often hides problems rather than exposing them. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Traps in the KTU 2024 Scheme (Automation Questions):** > 1. **Conflating Jidoka with simple automation:** Many students write "Jidoka means using robots." This is worth only partial credit. Jidoka specifically means **automation that detects, stops, and signals defects** — the **detect-and-stop** element is mandatory for full marks. > 2. **Skipping the Andon mention:** When describing Jidoka, omitting the **Andon system** results in a deduction of 1–2 marks in long-answer questions. > 3. **Payback Period formula units:** Always express the answer in **years** (not months) and verify the units of $C_{inv}$ and $S_{annual}$ match. Examiners will deduct marks for unit mismatch. > 4. **ROI expressed as a fraction:** Students often write ROI as a decimal (0.24) instead of a percentage (24%). KTU requires the **percentage form**. > 5. **Ignoring qualitative benefits:** In economic justification problems, the KTU rubric (under Outcome-Based Education) often awards **1–2 marks for qualitative Lean benefits** (defect prevention, safety, Heijunka, Poka-Yoke). Do not skip them. > 6. **Mermaid diagrams in exam:** Diagrams in the answer script must be **hand-drawn** for ESE; the printed Mermaid diagrams in these notes are for **learning and revision** only. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Automation (KTU 2024 Module 2):** > - **Jidoka = "Automation with a human touch"** — it is **detection + automatic stop + alert + root-cause fix**. > - Jidoka is one of the **two pillars of TPS**, the other being **JIT (Just-In-Time)**. > - **Three types of automation:** Fixed, Programmable, Flexible — Flexible best supports Lean. > - **Four pillars of Jidoka:** Detect, Stop, Fix-at-station, Root-cause & countermeasure (5-Why + Poka-Yoke). > - **Andon system** is the signalling mechanism of Jidoka (visual + audible). > - **Payback Period:** $PB = \dfrac{C_{inv}}{S_{annual}}$ (in years). > - **ROI:** $ROI = \dfrac{S_{annual} - O_{annual}}{C_{inv}} \times 100\%$. > - **OEE:** $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ (world-class benchmark $\geq 85\%$). > - **Lean Automation Loop:** Jidoka $\rightarrow$ Andon $\rightarrow$ 5-Why $\rightarrow$ Poka-Yoke $\rightarrow$ Heijunka $\rightarrow$ Kaizen. > - **When to automate:** High volume, low variety, hazardous process, or precision beyond human capability. > - Lean does **NOT** oppose automation — it demands **smart automation** that exposes problems rather than hides them. > - **Jidoka vs Ohno's distinction:** Taiichi Ohno deliberately wrote Jidoka using the kanji 働 (work) instead of 動 (motion) to convey that **machines should work intelligently, not merely move**. > - Always state **units** in numerical answers and **qualitative Lean benefits** in long-answer justifications.

topics.title: Single Minute Exchange of Die (SMED)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET746 - LEAN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Single Minute Exchange of Die (SMED)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Single Minute Exchange of Die (SMED)** is a lean manufacturing methodology developed by **Shigeo Shingo** at Toyota Production System (TPS) during the 1950s–1980s to drastically reduce machine **changeover (setup) time**. Despite its name, "Single Minute" does **not** mean one minute; it means a changeover time of **less than 10 minutes** (i.e., a single-digit minute duration, e.g., 9 minutes 59 seconds or less).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** SMED is a foundational tool of the **Toyota Production System (TPS)** under the umbrella of **Just-In-Time (JIT)** production. It directly targets waste (muda) of *waiting* and *overproduction* by enabling small lot sizes, reducing inventory, and improving **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)**.

The system was originally conceived to reduce die-change time on Toyota's stamping presses, but its principles have been generalized to **any manufacturing changeover** — mechanical, electrical, procedural, or administrative.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Misconception Clarifier:** SMED does NOT aim to eliminate changeovers. It aims to make changeovers so fast and standardized that they become a non-issue, enabling **economic lot size of one (1)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a busy South Indian restaurant (think a KTU canteen on a Monday morning):

- **Scenario A (No SMED):** The waiter takes 45 minutes to "reset" a table — clearing plates, wiping, arranging cutlery, fetching menus, making fresh coffee — *all while the customer is seated and waiting*. The kitchen is idle. The customer is frustrated. The next order cannot begin.

- **Scenario B (With SMED applied):** The waiter **pre-stages** clean plates, cutlery sets, and coffee powder on a side trolley *while the previous customer is still finishing* (External Setup). When the customer leaves, the actual table reset takes only 4 minutes (Internal Setup). A new customer is seated almost immediately.

**Translation to the factory floor:**
- The "customer at the table" = the machine's production run
- The "waiter resetting" = the operator performing the changeover
- The "side trolley" = pre-staged tools, dies, fixtures
- The "new customer" = the next production run of a different part

The shorter the table-reset (changeover), the more customers (production runs) you can serve (manufacture) per hour → higher equipment utilization, lower WIP, faster response to customer demand.

> [!TIP]
> **The Shingo Insight:** Most of the 45-minute reset in Scenario A was *internal* work being done *during* the customer's waiting time. Shingo's revelation was that **up to 70% of "internal" setup tasks are secretly convertible to "external" tasks** that can be done while the machine is still running.

## 1.3 Core Terminology & Standard Metrics

The following standardized SMED vocabulary is used in KTU textbooks and in shop-floor kaizen documentation:

- **Setup Time (S):** Total time from the last good part of run A to the first good part of run B, expressed in **seconds or minutes**.
- **Internal Setup Time ($S_I$):** Setup operations that **can only be performed** when the machine is **stopped** (e.g., removing a die, unclamping).
- **External Setup Time ($S_E$):** Setup operations that **can be performed** while the machine is **still running** (e.g., fetching the next die from the rack, preheating).
- **Changeover Time ($T_{CO}$):** Sum total: $T_{CO} = S_I + S_E$
- **Target Time ($T_{SMED}$):** $T_{SMED} \leq 9 \text{ min } 59 \text{ sec} = 599 \text{ seconds}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Single Digit Minutes" matters:** The original Toyota target was not arbitrary. A single-digit-minute changeover enables **flexible, demand-driven production** where small batches of varied products can be produced on the same line in a single shift, eliminating the need for massive inventories to "absorb" long changeovers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four (originally Three) Stage Theoretical Framework

Shigeo Shingo formalized SMED as a **progressive 4-stage methodology** (originally 3 stages, with Stage 0 added later to represent the pre-SMED baseline). Each stage builds on the previous, and the value gained at each step is cumulative.

### **Stage 0 — Initial State (Baseline Observation)**

> *"The starting point of all improvement is knowing the current condition."*

- No formal analysis is performed.
- The current changeover is performed exactly as it has always been done.
- The time is recorded using a video camera or stopwatch.
- This serves as the **baseline benchmark** against which all subsequent improvements are measured.

**Key Activity:** Videotape the entire changeover, watch it back, and write down *every* step in chronological order with timestamps.

### **Stage 1 — Separate Internal and External Setup Operations**

> *"Not all setup work requires the machine to be stopped."*

The primary intellectual work of Stage 1 is **classification**. Every step is scrutinized:

- **Question to ask:** *"Does this step absolutely require the machine to be stationary?"*
  - If **YES** → it is **Internal** ($S_I$)
  - If **NO** → it is **External** ($S_E$)

The first surprising finding is typically: **a large portion of so-called "internal" work is actually external** — it was merely being performed at the wrong time. For example, "fetching the next die" is external work that is *traditionally done while the machine is stopped*, waiting for the operator to walk to the die rack.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A common 3-mark question asks *"Differentiate between Internal and External Setup."* Always cite a **manufacturing example** — e.g., Internal: removing bolts on a die; External: transporting the next die to the machine.

### **Stage 2 — Convert Internal Setup to External Setup**

> *"The biggest leap in changeover reduction happens here."*

Once internal and external steps are clearly identified, engineers ask:

- *"Can this internal step be moved outside? Can it be performed before/after, while the machine is running?"*

Common Stage 2 conversion techniques include:

- **Pre-staging tools and dies** on roller conveyors beside the press
- **Pre-heating dies** in ovens while the previous run is in progress
- **Using standardized die carts** so dies can be moved in one motion
- **Clamping nuts replaced with single-lever clamps** (functional clamps)
- **Color-coding and pre-aligning connectors and hoses**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hallmark of Stage 2:** A 50–90% reduction in changeover time is typical — and this comes largely from *re-classification and re-timing*, not from expensive tooling.

### **Stage 3 — Streamline All Setup Operations**

> *"Eliminate, simplify, and standardize every remaining step."*

With the bulk of setup work now external, the remaining (and reduced) internal work is optimized. Standard SMED Stage 3 techniques include:

- **Elimination:** Remove unnecessary screws, adjustments, alignment steps.
- **Parallel Operations:** Two operators working simultaneously on different sub-assemblies.
- **Intermediate Jigs / Pre-positioning:** Use jigs that hold fasteners in a ready position so they don't have to be located by hand each time.
- **One-Touch Clamping:** Replace multi-bolt clamps with single-lever or hydraulic clamps.
- **Numerical Controls / Sensors:** Replace manual "trial and error" adjustments with limit switches and digital counters.
- **Standardized Work Instructions (SWI):** Document the new procedure so it cannot regress.

## 2.2 Mathematical Relationship Between SMED and Other Lean Metrics

SMED does not stand alone — it is a key enabler of other production metrics. The following relationships are **high-yield for KTU 14-mark questions** that link SMED to OEE, lead time, and inventory.

### **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) Decomposition**

$$OEE = A \times P \times Q$$

where:
- $A$ = Availability = (Operating Time) / (Planned Production Time)
- $P$ = Performance Efficiency = (Ideal Cycle Time $\times$ Total Pieces) / (Operating Time)
- $Q$ = Quality Rate = (Good Pieces) / (Total Pieces)

**SMED's direct impact is on $A$ (Availability):**

$$A = \frac{T_{planned} - T_{downtime}}{T_{planned}}$$

where **$T_{downtime}$ includes changeover time $T_{CO}$**. By reducing $T_{CO}$ via SMED, $T_{downtime}$ falls, and $A$ rises, thus lifting OEE.

### **Lot Size Reduction Triggered by SMED**

Traditional EOQ (Economic Order Quantity) formula:

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$$

The traditional objection to small lots was that "setup cost $S$ is too high." SMED reduces the **effective** setup time, which lowers setup cost $S$ per unit, allowing smaller $Q^*$ without losing economic viability. This is a **favourite KTU question linkage.**

### **KPI Reduction Targets (Typical Industry Benchmarks)**

| Metric | Pre-SMED | After Stage 1 | After Stage 2 | After Stage 3 |
|--------|----------|---------------|---------------|---------------|
| Changeover time (min) | 60 | 30 | 9 | 4 |
| Reduction (%) | 0 | 50 | 85 | 93 |
| WIP inventory (units) | 1000 | 600 | 200 | 50 |
| Lead time (days) | 15 | 9 | 4 | 2 |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition / Formula | Unit / Boundary | KTU Exam Relevance |
|---------------|----------------------|-----------------|-------------------|
| $T_{CO}$ | Total Changeover Time = $S_I + S_E$ | seconds (s) or minutes (min) | Direct |
| $S_I$ | Internal Setup Time | s or min | Direct |
| $S_E$ | External Setup Time | s or min | Direct |
| $T_{SMED}$ | Target Time (Single-Digit Minutes) | $\leq 599$ s | Direct |
| $OEE$ | $A \times P \times Q$ (0 to 1, or 0% to 100%) | Dimensionless ratio | High |
| $A$ | Availability = $\frac{T_{planned} - T_{downtime}}{T_{planned}}$ | 0 to 1 | High |
| $P$ | Performance = $\frac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Pieces}}{\text{Operating Time}}$ | 0 to 1 | Medium |
| $Q$ | Quality = $\frac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces}}$ | 0 to 1 | Medium |
| $Q^*$ | EOQ = $\sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$ | units | High (linkage) |
| $L$ | Lead Time reduction factor: $L \propto T_{CO}$ | days | Medium |
| WIP | Work-In-Process inventory (falls as $T_{CO}$ falls) | units | High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Hygiene Rule:** When writing absolute value or magnitude in your answer scripts, always use $\vert x \vert$ — never bare pipe `|x|` — to avoid table-rendering issues and to match the official textbook convention used by KTU board examiners.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

SMED is not just a Toyota historical artifact. It is actively deployed across:

- **Automotive stamping lines** (Toyota, Tata, Maruti Suzuki)
- **Injection molding** (every batch color changeover in plastic part plants)
- **CNC machining** (pallet changers, tool pre-staging)
- **Printing & packaging** (rotary press roll changes)
- **Aerospace** (wing-skin drilling template changes)
- **Pharmaceutical** (CIP/SIP line changeovers between products)

A well-executed SMED project typically yields **30–50% capacity increase without any capital investment** — purely through process re-engineering. This is its core strategic value: **free capacity from existing assets**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Applying the 4-Stage SMED Methodology to a Press Shop

**Scenario (typical KTU 14-mark problem statement):**

> A hydraulic press in a KTU-affiliated industry currently takes **45 minutes** for a die changeover. The changeover consists of **10 distinct steps**. The plant manager wants to apply SMED. Given the step data below, walk through Stages 0–3 and calculate the percentage reduction in changeover time at each stage.

| Step # | Step Description | Current Timing | Can it be moved external? |
|--------|------------------|----------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | Stop the press, lock-out tag-out (LOTO) | 1 min | No (Internal) |
| 2 | Fetch next die from die rack (10 m away) | 4 min | **Yes** (External) |
| 3 | Remove bolts (8 bolts) from current die | 6 min | No (Internal) |
| 4 | Lift current die out using overhead crane | 2 min | No (Internal) |
| 5 | Place new die on bolster (alignment trial) | 8 min | No (Internal) |
| 6 | Tighten 8 bolts (new die) | 6 min | No (Internal) |
| 7 | Connect hydraulic lines & sensors | 3 min | **Yes** (External) |
| 8 | First trial stroke & micro-adjustment | 8 min | No (Internal) |
| 9 | Run 5 sample pieces, inspect | 5 min | No (Internal) |
| 10 | Start production run | 2 min | No (Internal) |
| **Total** | | **45 min** | |

### **Stage 0 — Baseline Recording**

$$T_{CO,0} = 45 \text{ min}$$

This is the recorded baseline. No improvement has been made.

### **Stage 1 — Separate Internal from External**

By inspection, the steps that **can** be performed while the press is still running are Steps 2 and 7.

**Internal setup time after Stage 1:**

$$S_{I,1} = 1 + 6 + 2 + 8 + 6 + 8 + 5 + 2 = 38 \text{ min}$$

**External setup time after Stage 1:**

$$S_{E,1} = 4 + 3 = 7 \text{ min}$$

**Total time remains 45 min**, but now the work is correctly classified:

$$T_{CO,1} = S_{I,1} + S_{E,1} = 38 + 7 = 45 \text{ min}$$

> *Logic check:* Re-classification alone does **not** reduce total wall-clock changeover time yet — it just makes the operator aware that 7 minutes of work is being done *during* the "stopped" period when it could have been done *before* the press stopped.

### **Stage 2 — Convert Internal to External**

Now we re-time by **moving Steps 2 and 7 to before the press is stopped** (i.e., while the previous production run is in its final 7 minutes). This effectively **shifts those 7 minutes outside the stopped window**.

**New internal time after Stage 2:**

$$S_{I,2} = S_{I,1} - 7 = 38 - 7 = 31 \text{ min}$$

**External time after Stage 2:**

$$S_{E,2} = S_{E,1} + 7 = 7 + 7 = 14 \text{ min}$$

**New effective changeover time** (from last good part to first good part):

$$T_{CO,2} = 31 \text{ min}$$

**Percentage reduction after Stage 2:**

$$\Delta T_{2} = \frac{T_{CO,0} - T_{CO,2}}{T_{CO,0}} \times 100 = \frac{45 - 31}{45} \times 100$$

$$\Delta T_{2} = \frac{14}{45} \times 100 = 31.11\%$$

### **Stage 3 — Streamline (Apply Engineering Improvements)**

The maintenance team now applies Stage 3 techniques:

- **Step 3 (6 min):** Replace 8 bolts with a single-lever clamp → new time = **1 min**
- **Step 4 (2 min):** Use die-cart pre-positioning → new time = **1 min**
- **Step 5 (8 min):** Use pre-aligned guide pins so die self-locates → new time = **3 min**
- **Step 6 (6 min):** Clamping now done by new lever from Step 3 → new time = **1 min**
- **Step 8 (8 min):** Replace trial-and-error adjustment with sensor-based digital stop → new time = **3 min**

**New internal time after Stage 3:**

$$S_{I,3} = 1_{LOTO} + 1_{unclamp} + 1_{crane} + 3_{locate} + 1_{clamp} + 3_{adjust} + 5_{sample} + 2_{startup}$$

$$S_{I,3} = 1 + 1 + 1 + 3 + 1 + 3 + 5 + 2 = 17 \text{ min}$$

**External time** (mostly unchanged, but pre-staging refined):

$$S_{E,3} = 14 \text{ min} - 2 \text{ (further optimization of pre-stage)} = 12 \text{ min}$$

**Final changeover time:**

$$T_{CO,3} = 17 \text{ min}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **17 min > 9 min 59 sec target** — this is **not yet SMED-compliant**. The team must continue Stage 3 (eliminate sample inspection by using a sensor-based quality gate, eliminate the 5-minute sample run via statistical process control). Final realistic target: ~7 minutes.

**Percentage reduction after Stage 3:**

$$\Delta T_{3} = \frac{45 - 17}{45} \times 100 = \frac{28}{45} \times 100 = 62.22\%$$

### **3.2 Impact on OEE — Quantitative Derivation**

Assume the press operates **8 hours/day, 5 days/week, 50 weeks/year** = 120,000 minutes/year.

**Pre-SMED Availability calculation:**

- Originally, **3 changeovers/day** are performed.
- Total changeover time/year: $3 \times 45 \times 250 = 33{,}750 \text{ min/yr}$
- Planned production time: $120{,}000 \text{ min/yr}$
- $A_{pre} = \frac{120{,}000 - 33{,}750}{120{,}000} = 0.71875 = 71.88\%$

**Post-Stage-2 Availability:**

- $T_{CO} = 31$ min
- Total changeover time/year: $3 \times 31 \times 250 = 23{,}250 \text{ min/yr}$
- $A_{post2} = \frac{120{,}000 - 23{,}250}{120{,}000} = 0.80625 = 80.63\%$

**Post-Stage-3 Availability:**

- $T_{CO} = 17$ min
- Total changeover time/year: $3 \times 17 \times 250 = 12{,}750 \text{ min/yr}$
- $A_{post3} = \frac{120{,}000 - 12{,}750}{120{,}000} = 0.89375 = 89.38\%$

**OEE improvement** (assuming $P = 0.90$ and $Q = 0.98$ held constant):

| Stage | $A$ | $P$ | $Q$ | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ |
|-------|-----|-----|-----|------------------------------|
| Pre-SMED | 0.7188 | 0.90 | 0.98 | **0.6338 = 63.38%** |
| Post-Stage 2 | 0.8063 | 0.90 | 0.98 | **0.7111 = 71.11%** |
| Post-Stage 3 | 0.8938 | 0.90 | 0.98 | **0.7882 = 78.82%** |

> **Interpretation:** SMED alone lifted OEE by **~15.4 percentage points**, an enormous gain for a zero-capital change. World-class OEE is 85%, so SMED is a key step in that journey.

### **3.3 Master Analytical Matrix — Stage-Wise Deliverables**

| Stage | Primary Question | Main Action | Typical Time Reduction | Typical Cost |
|-------|------------------|-------------|------------------------|--------------|
| **0** | "What is our current state?" | Videotape, record baseline | 0% (baseline only) | Minimal (camera) |
| **1** | "What is truly internal?" | Re-classify all steps | 0% (no time saved yet, but clarity gained) | Zero |
| **2** | "Can internal be externalized?" | Move steps out of stopped window | 30–50% | Low |
| **3** | "Can we eliminate, simplify, parallelize?" | Engineering changes, clamping, jigs | 50–90% | Medium |
| **Beyond (4+)** | "Continuous improvement" | Standardize, train, kaizen | Sustained | Training only |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — SMED 4-Stage Implementation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 0: Baseline Recording] --> A1[Video tape entire changeover]
    A1 --> A2[Document every step with timestamps]
    A2 --> A3[Compute T_CO baseline = S_I + S_E]
    A3 --> B[Stage 1: Separate Internal vs External]
    B --> B1{Q1: Does step require machine to be stopped?}
    B1 -->|Yes| B2[Classify as Internal S_I]
    B1 -->|No| B3[Classify as External S_E]
    B2 --> C[Stage 2: Convert Internal to External]
    B3 --> C
    C --> C1{Q2: Can internal step be moved outside stopped window?}
    C1 -->|Yes| C2[Re-time and relocate to pre-stop phase]
    C1 -->|No| C3[Retain as internal, optimize in Stage 3]
    C2 --> D[Stage 3: Streamline All Operations]
    C3 --> D
    D --> D1[Eliminate unnecessary steps]
    D1 --> D2[Apply functional clamps and jigs]
    D2 --> D3[Parallelize remaining internal work]
    D3 --> E{Q3: Is T_CO less than 9 min 59 sec?}
    E -->|Yes| F[SMED Achieved - Standardize and Sustain]
    E -->|No| D1
    F --> G[Continuous Kaizen - Periodic Re-Validation]
    G --> A
```

> **Reading Guide:** The cycle returns to Stage 0 because SMED is *not a one-time project*; it is a continuous improvement discipline. Once the target is met, the team must standardize via SOPs and resume recording baselines for the next improvement cycle.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Internal vs External Setup Time Decomposition (Pre- and Post-SMED)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph preSMED[Pre-SMED: 45 min total]
        P1[Internal: 38 min]:::intStyle
        P2[External hidden inside: 7 min]:::extStyle
        P1 --> P2
    end
    subgraph postSMED[Post-SMED Stage 2: 31 min effective]
        Q1[Internal: 31 min]:::intStyle
        Q2[External performed upfront: 14 min]:::extStyle
        Q2 --> Q1
    end
    preSMED ==> postSMED
    classDef intStyle fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c33,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef extStyle fill:#dff0d8,stroke:#3a7,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> **Reading Guide:** Red boxes represent Internal Setup (machine stopped); green boxes represent External Setup (machine running). The post-SMED diagram shows that 14 minutes of external work is *moved before* the press stops, so the press is stopped for only 31 minutes — a 31% reduction in *effective* changeover time.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — How SMED Integrates with TPS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TPS[Toyota Production System TPS] --> JIT[Just In Time JIT]
    TPS --> JIDOKA[Autonomation Jidoka]
    JIT --> SMED[SMED - Quick Changeover]
    JIT --> HAI[Heijunka - Level Scheduling]
    JIT --> KO[Kanban - Pull System]
    SMED --> FLM[Flow - Continuous Flow Production]
    SMED --> OEE[Higher OEE Availability]
    SMED --> INV[Reduced WIP and Inventory]
    SMED --> EOD[Economic Order Quantity of 1]
    FLM --> RESP[Responsive to Customer Demand]
    OEE --> COST[Lower Unit Cost]
    INV --> COST
    EOD --> RESP
    RESP --> TPS
    COST --> TPS
```

> **Reading Guide:** This block diagram shows SMED's systemic position. It is a *JIT enabler*, not a stand-alone cost-cutting tool. Its true value is the chain reaction it triggers: smaller lots → less inventory → faster flow → responsive customer service → lower cost → stronger competitive position.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper PEMET746]`

**"What is meant by 'Single Minute' in the context of SMED? Is it necessary to achieve exactly one minute?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The phrase **"Single Minute"** in SMED refers to a changeover (setup) time that is expressible in **single-digit minutes** — that is, **less than 10 minutes** (specifically, 9 minutes 59 seconds or less). The word "single" modifies "digit" rather than "minute."

It is **not necessary** to achieve exactly one minute. The term originates from a target setting: any changeover that can be measured in single-digit minutes is considered "SMED-compliant" and good practice.

Shigeo Shingo introduced the concept at Toyota during the 1950s–1980s as part of the Toyota Production System, originally to reduce die-change time on stamping presses. The 10-minute threshold was chosen because once a changeover crosses into double-digit minutes, it is psychologically perceived as a long, expensive event, discouraging small-batch production. Single-digit-minute changeovers are perceived as routine and inexpensive, enabling flexible, demand-driven manufacturing.

*(Marking: 1 mark for stating "less than 10 min", 1 mark for clarifying "single digit", 1 mark for Shingo / Toyota context.)*

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**"Distinguish between Internal Setup Time and External Setup Time with one manufacturing example each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Internal Setup Time ($S_I$) | External Setup Time ($S_E$) |
|--------|------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Definition** | Setup operations that **can only be performed when the machine is stopped** | Setup operations that **can be performed while the machine is still running** |
| **Constraint** | Strictly bound to the stopped window | Independent of machine state |
| **Reduction strategy** | Stage 2 conversion + Stage 3 streamlining | Already optimum; further gain via parallelization |

**Example (Injection Molding):**
- **Internal ($S_I$):** Removing the current mold from the clamp and tightening the bolts of the new mold — *requires the machine to be open and idle.*
- **External ($S_E$):** Transporting the next mold from the heated storage cabinet to the machine on a preheated roller conveyor, and pre-purging the color feeder — *can be done while the previous shot is being produced.*

*(Marking: 1 mark for definitions, 1 mark for distinguishing constraint, 1 mark for manufacturing example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** *ESE Part B Module 2 questions will offer a Module 2 internal choice. Both options must be from Module 2 syllabus. Each option carries 14 marks, typically split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Modified Model]`**

**"With the help of a suitable example, explain the four stages of SMED methodology as proposed by Shigeo Shingo. Show the time-reduction achieved at each stage and explain the impact of SMED on the Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) of the equipment."**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Four Stages with Example**

The four stages of SMED, illustrated using a **hydraulic press die changeover** example, are:

**Stage 0 — Initial Baseline Observation:**
The current changeover is performed and video-recorded. No changes are made. The baseline time is established.
*Example:* A press shop records 60 minutes as the baseline changeover time for a stamping die.

**Stage 1 — Separate Internal and External Setup:**
Every step is examined to determine whether it strictly requires the machine to be stopped. Steps that do not are reclassified as *external*.
*Example:* Step "fetch next die from rack (5 min)" is found to be external — it had been performed *during* the stopped window, but it does not require the machine to stop. Reclassification alone does not yet reduce total time, but it reveals the hidden waste.

**Stage 2 — Convert Internal to External Setup:**
The internal steps identified in Stage 1 are moved to *before* or *after* the machine is stopped. Pre-staging, pre-heating, and parallel preparation are employed.
*Example:* The next die is now fetched and pre-heated *during the last 5 minutes of the previous production run*. Effective changeover drops from 60 min to ~35 min.

**Stage 3 — Streamline All Operations:**
Remaining internal operations are eliminated, simplified, or parallelized. Functional clamps, guide pins, and digital adjustment sensors replace manual trial-and-error.
*Example:* Eight bolts replaced by a one-touch hydraulic clamp; manual alignment replaced by guide pins. Changeover falls to **7 min 30 sec — SMED achieved**.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Impact on OEE**

The **OEE = Availability × Performance × Quality** framework reveals that SMED's primary impact is on **Availability**:

$$A = \frac{T_{planned} - T_{downtime}}{T_{planned}}$$

where $T_{downtime}$ includes changeover time $T_{CO}$. As $T_{CO}$ falls, $T_{downtime}$ falls, and $A$ rises.

**Numerical Illustration:**
Assume a press operates 480 min/day. Originally, 4 changeovers are performed, each 60 min, so total changeover = 240 min/day.
- $A_{pre} = (480 - 240) / 480 = 0.500$

After SMED (changeover reduced to 7.5 min), total changeover = 30 min/day.
- $A_{post} = (480 - 30) / 480 = 0.9375$

With Performance = 0.85 and Quality = 0.95 held constant:
- $OEE_{pre} = 0.500 \times 0.85 \times 0.95 = 0.404$ (40.4%)
- $OEE_{post} = 0.9375 \times 0.85 \times 0.95 = 0.757$ (75.7%)

SMED more than **doubled the OEE** without any new machine purchase. Beyond OEE, SMED also enables:
1. **Smaller lot sizes** — reducing work-in-process (WIP) inventory by 70–80%.
2. **Faster customer response** — economic lot size of one becomes viable.
3. **Lower unit cost** — fixed setup cost is amortized over fewer units but absorbed into the per-piece cost more easily.
4. **Better cash flow** — less inventory tied up in raw material, WIP, and finished goods.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers on Q-A:**
> 1. **Stating "SMED means 1 minute"** — *direct zero for that part*. Always clarify "single-digit minute."
> 2. **Skipping Stage 0** — students often jump straight to Stage 1, missing that baseline is mandatory for a kaizen project. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing OEE's three components** — many answers say SMED affects all three (A, P, Q). Strictly, SMED affects **only Availability**; any quality improvement is a side benefit, not the primary mechanism.
> 4. **Not showing the numerical calculation** — examiners award full OEE-linkage marks (typically 3 of 7) only when a numerical example is computed.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sample Paper]`**

**"Explain in detail the application of SMED principles to an injection-moulding machine. Discuss the conversion techniques used in Stage 2 and the engineering improvements applied in Stage 3. Comment on the relationship between SMED, lot size reduction, and Just-In-Time (JIT) production."**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: SMED Application to Injection Moulding**

In injection moulding, changeover includes mould swap, colour/material change, hot-runner adjustment, and trial-shot inspection. Applying SMED:

**Stage 0:** Baseline = 90 minutes per mould change (typical of a 200-tonne press).

**Stage 1 — Classification:**
- *External steps (discovered):* Mould pre-heating (20 min), colour masterbatch pre-mixing (8 min), hydraulic hoses pre-connection on a parallel manifold (10 min), operator kitting of ejector pins (5 min). **Total external = 43 min.**
- *Internal steps (true):* Clamp open, eject old mould, lift out, crane new mould, align, clamp, hot-runner purge, trial shots. **Total internal = 47 min.**

**Stage 2 — Conversion Techniques Used:**
1. **Mould pre-heating in oven** while previous run is in progress.
2. **Pre-positioned mould cart on rails** that rolls into position with one operator motion.
3. **Quick-disconnect hydraulic couplings** so hoses stay attached to the manifold.
4. **Pre-staged color feeder cartridges** loaded before the machine stops.
5. **Standardized clamp dimensions** so any mould fits the same receiver plate.

Effective changeover time post-Stage 2: **~20 minutes** (a 78% reduction).

**Stage 3 — Engineering Improvements:**
- **Hydraulic clamping** replacing manual toggle clamps.
- **Guide pins and self-aligning receivers** for instant mould registration.
- **Digital shot counter and pressure sensor** replacing trial-shot adjustment.
- **Hot-runner temperature controllers** with one-touch recall of saved recipes.
- **Statistical Process Control (SPC) for first-article** inspection, eliminating 10 minutes of manual sampling.

Final changeover time: **~8 minutes** — SMED achieved.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: SMED, Lot Size Reduction, and JIT**

The **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** formula:

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$$

where $S$ is the setup cost per changeover. **SMED reduces the effective $S$** (because the time-based cost of holding the machine idle drops), which mathematically allows a **smaller optimal $Q^*$**.

**Traditional paradigm:** Long changeovers → large lot sizes justified → high WIP inventory → long lead times → poor customer responsiveness.

**Post-SMED paradigm:** Short changeovers → small lot sizes viable → low WIP → short lead times → high customer responsiveness.

This is precisely the **foundation of Just-In-Time (JIT)** production. JIT's cardinal rule is *"produce only what is needed, only when it is needed, in the amount needed."* Without SMED, JIT is impossible — changeover costs would force large batches, destroying the very premise of pull-flow production.

In Toyota's system, SMED is therefore a **JIT enabler**, not an independent cost-cutting program. The Toyota 4P Model (Philosophy, Process, People, Problem-Solving) explicitly positions SMED under the *Process* pillar, supporting the higher-level *Philosophy* of continuous improvement (Kaizen) and the JIT goal of zero inventory.

**Quantitative linkage:**
- Pre-SMED: 4 changeovers/day → lot size 500 units/run → WIP = 4 × 500 = 2000 units.
- Post-SMED: 8 changeovers/day → lot size 50 units/run → WIP = 8 × 50 = 400 units (an 80% WIP reduction).
- Annual carrying cost savings: $H \times \overline{WIP} \times 365$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers on Q-B:**
> 1. **Forgetting to name JIT explicitly** — examiners look for the phrase "JIT enabler" or equivalent. Missing it costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Writing generic SMED points** without binding them to *injection moulding*. If your examples are about car-painting or punch-press without addressing the mould, marks drop sharply.
> 3. **Not mentioning guide pins / quick-disconnect couplings** — these are the *expected* Stage 2–3 technical answers for mould-changeover. Generic "use better tools" wording is treated as vague.
> 4. **Mixing up EOQ's three variables** — $D$ (demand), $S$ (setup), $H$ (holding). Always state them.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rapid Revision Checklist — SMED**

- **Definition (must be word-perfect):** SMED is a methodology to reduce machine changeover time to **less than 10 minutes (single-digit minutes)**, developed by **Shigeo Shingo** at Toyota.
- **Origin:** 1950s–1980s, Toyota Production System (TPS), originally for press-shop die changeovers.
- **Four Stages:**
  1. **Stage 0:** Record baseline (videotape, no changes).
  2. **Stage 1:** Separate Internal vs External setup (no time saved yet, only classification).
  3. **Stage 2:** Convert Internal to External (typically 30–50% time reduction).
  4. **Stage 3:** Streamline all operations (typically 50–90% cumulative reduction).
- **Key Formulas:**
  - $T_{CO} = S_I + S_E$
  - $OEE = A \times P \times Q$
  - $A = (T_{planned} - T_{downtime}) / T_{planned}$
  - $Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$ (EOQ)
- **Internal vs External:** Internal = machine must stop; External = can be done while running.
- **Goal is NOT 1 minute** — single-digit minute, i.e., 1–9 minutes 59 seconds.
- **SMED is a JIT enabler**, not a stand-alone program. It enables lot size of 1, reduces WIP, shortens lead time.
- **OEE impact is via Availability** only — do not claim SMED affects Performance or Quality directly (those are improved by other TPS tools like Jidoka and Poka-Yoke).
- **SMED is a continuous journey** — after target is met, baseline is reset for the next kaizen cycle.
- **Common conversion techniques (Stage 2):** Pre-staging, pre-heating, parallel kitting, standardized die carts.
- **Common streamlining techniques (Stage 3):** Functional clamps, guide pins, one-touch levers, digital adjustment, intermediate jigs, parallel operations.
- **Examiner's favourite 3-mark Q:** "Differentiate Internal vs External Setup" with a manufacturing example.
- **Examiner's favourite 14-mark Q:** Apply the 4-stage SMED to a real process and compute the OEE impact.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
