<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`
**Define Lead Time. List any four techniques used for Lead Time Reduction in Lean Manufacturing.**

#### **Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Definition (1.5 marks):** Lead Time is the total elapsed time from the receipt of a customer order to the delivery of the finished product to the customer. It is the sum of all processing, waiting, queue, setup, inspection, transport, storage, and delivery times. In Lean terminology, most of Lead Time is non-value-added and must be minimized.

**Four Techniques (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
1. **SMED (Single Minute Exchange of Dies)** – reduces setup time below 10 minutes.
2. **Pull System / Kanban** – produces only to downstream demand, eliminating overproduction.
3. **Cellular Manufacturing** – U-shaped layout eliminates inter-station transport.
4. **One-Piece Flow / Small Batch Production** – reduces queue and WIP.

> *(Alternative 4th option: Heijunka — Level Scheduling)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`
**State and explain Little's Law. How does it establish the relationship between Lead Time and Work-In-Process inventory?**

#### **Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement (1 mark):** Little's Law states that the long-term average number of items $L$ in a stationary system equals the long-term average arrival rate $\lambda$ multiplied by the long-term average time $W$ an item spends in the system:

$$L = \lambda \cdot W$$

**Explanation (2 marks):**
In manufacturing terms:
- $L$ = Work-In-Process (WIP) inventory (units)
- $\lambda$ = Throughput rate (units/time)
- $W$ = Lead Time (time)

**Relationship Implication:** If the throughput rate $\lambda$ is held constant, **WIP is directly proportional to Lead Time**. Therefore, any effort that halves Lead Time (SMED, cellular layout, etc.) will automatically halve WIP, **without changing the production rate**. This is the *quantitative heart* of Lead Time Reduction and justifies all Lean investments in setup reduction.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has **two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks**. The student answers **either Option A or Option B**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model 2024]`

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **components of Lead Time** with a suitable block diagram. Identify which components are value-added and which are non-value-added.

#### **Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Lead Time (LT) is the total time a product takes to traverse the entire value chain — from the moment a customer order is received until the product is delivered. It comprises nine sequential time elements.

**Component-wise Explanation (5 marks — distribute ~0.55 each):**

| Component | Description | Type |
|---|---|---|
| Order Processing Time | Time to receive, validate, and release order | NVA |
| Queue / Waiting Time | Time the part waits for the next machine | NVA |
| Setup Time | Time to prepare machine for next operation | NVA (reducible by SMED) |
| Run Time | Actual transformation of the part | **VA** |
| Internal Wait Time | Delays between operations within the cell | NVA |
| Inspection Time | Time for quality checks | NVA (reducible by Poka-Yoke) |
| Movement / Transport | Time the part travels between stations | NVA (reducible by U-cell) |
| Storage Time | Time held in WIP stores or supermarkets | NVA |
| Delivery Time | Time to transport to customer | NVA |

**Block Diagram (1 mark):** *(See Section 4.1 Mermaid flowchart)*

**Conclusion (0 marks — optional):** Of the nine components, only **Run Time is value-added**. Lean therefore targets the other eight.

---

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** A production line has a throughput rate of **15 units/hour** and a current Lead Time of **6 hours**.
- **(i)** Calculate the current WIP inventory using Little's Law.
- **(ii)** If Lead Time is reduced to **2 hours** (via SMED + cellular layout), calculate the new WIP and the percentage reduction in WIP.
- **(iii)** Compute the Process Cycle Efficiency (PCE) before and after, given that Value-Added Time per unit is **12 minutes**.

#### **Model Answer:**

**(i) Current WIP (2 marks):**

$$L = \lambda \cdot W = 15 \times 6 = 90\ \text{units}$$

**(ii) New WIP and Reduction (3 marks):**

$$L_{new} = 15 \times 2 = 30\ \text{units}$$

$$\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{90 - 30}{90} \times 100\% = 66.67\%$$

> **Note:** A 67% reduction in WIP was achieved by cutting LT by 67% — *proving Little's Law's direct proportionality at constant throughput*.

**(iii) PCE Before and After (2 marks):**

Standardize units — $LT_{old} = 6\ \text{hrs} = 360\ \text{min}$, $LT_{new} = 2\ \text{hrs} = 120\ \text{min}$, $VAT = 12\ \text{min}$.

$$PCE_{old} = \frac{12}{360} \times 100\% = 3.33\%$$

$$PCE_{new} = \frac{12}{120} \times 100\% = 10\%$$

> **Insight:** PCE tripled (3.33% → 10%) **without** changing the actual processing speed — a pure waste elimination result.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **four pillars of Lead Time Reduction** with suitable examples.

#### **Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Lead Time Reduction is a multi-tool initiative built on four foundational Lean pillars. Each pillar attacks a different time element of LT.

**The Four Pillars (6 marks — 1.5 each):**

**1. SMED (Single Minute Exchange of Dies):**
Developed by Shigeo Shingo, SMED reduces machine setup time to under 10 minutes by separating internal (machine-stopped) from external (machine-running) setup and converting internal to external setup. *Example:* A stamping press setup reduced from 90 minutes to 8 minutes using SMED, enabling lot size reduction from 200 to 25.

**2. Small-Lot / One-Piece Continuous Flow:**
Shifting from large batches to small lots or one-piece flow drastically reduces queue time and WIP. *Example:* Toyota's assembly line operates on one-piece flow, exposing defects within seconds rather than days.

**3. Pull System / Kanban:**
A Kanban (signal card) triggers production only when the downstream station consumes a unit, eliminating overproduction — the root cause of most WIP. *Example:* The number of Kanban cards for a part is calculated as $N = (D \cdot L \cdot (1+S)) / C$.

**4. Cellular Manufacturing / U-Layout:**
Machines are arranged in process sequence in a U-shape, eliminating transport, enabling single-operator monitoring, and integrating inspection into the work cell. *Example:* Bosch reduced movement time by 70% by converting a linear layout to a U-cell.

---

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** A factory wants to introduce a **Kanban system** between two workstations.
- **Demand rate** $D = 40$ units/hour
- **Lead time of the Kanban loop** $L = 0.5$ hour
- **Container size** $C = 20$ units
- **Safety factor** $S = 0.10$ (10%)

Calculate:
- **(i)** The number of Kanban cards required.
- **(ii)** The maximum WIP this system will generate.
- **(iii)** Comment on the impact on Lead Time.

#### **Model Answer:**

**(i) Number of Kanban cards (3 marks):**

$$N = \frac{D \cdot L \cdot (1 + S)}{C} = \frac{40 \times 0.5 \times 1.10}{20} = \frac{22}{20} = 1.1 \Rightarrow \text{round up to } 2\ \text{cards}$$

> **Rule:** Always **round up** to the next integer — under-rounding causes starvation.

**(ii) Maximum WIP (2 marks):**

$$WIP_{max} = N \times C = 2 \times 20 = 40\ \text{units}$$

**(iii) Impact on Lead Time (2 marks):**
The Kanban system **caps WIP at 40 units**, which via Little's Law **caps the Lead Time** at:

$$LT_{max} = \frac{WIP}{\lambda} = \frac{40}{40} = 1\ \text{hour}$$

> **Conclusion:** Kanban acts as a **governor** on the system — it cannot *eliminate* LT reduction directly, but it **prevents LT from ballooning** by enforcing a hard WIP ceiling. Combined with SMED (which reduces $L$ in the formula), the system can be tightened further.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Lead Time Reduction Answers**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch (–1 to –2 marks):** Forgetting to convert hours ↔ minutes ↔ seconds before applying Little's Law or PCE. *Always* write units next to every numerical substitution.
>
> 2. **Confusing "Lead Time" with "Cycle Time":** Lead Time is **per order** (one unit, end-to-end). Cycle Time is **per unit at a single station** (the gap between two consecutive units coming out of one machine). Examiners *will* deduct marks if these are interchanged.
>
> 3. **Rounding Down Kanban Cards:** Always round **up**. A Kanban system is a production *ceiling* — under-rounding starves the line.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the "+1" in Kanban formula:** Some textbooks write $N = (D \cdot L / C) \cdot (1 + S)$. The safety factor is **additive** on the demand, not multiplicative on $N$.
>
> 5. **Stating only "one technique" for LT reduction:** KTU 2024 answer keys require a **minimum of 3–4 techniques** for a full 14-mark question. Always present SMED, Kanban, cellular layout, and one-piece flow together.
>
> 6. **Skipping the PCE calculation:** Almost every Lead Time question requires a PCE before/after comparison. Skipping it costs ~2 marks.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> Use this section as your **last-minute revision sheet** the night before the KTU exam.

- ✅ **Lead Time (LT)** = Total time from customer order to delivery = sum of **9 time elements**.
- ✅ Only **1 of 9** time elements is **value-added (VA)**: the *Run Time*. All others are **NVA**.
- ✅ **Little's Law** — $L = \lambda \cdot W$ — is the **quantitative backbone** of LT reduction.
- ✅ **Halving LT halves WIP** (at constant throughput) — memorise this insight.
- ✅ **Four Pillars of LT Reduction:** **S**MED, **S**mall-lot, **P**ull (Kanban), **C**ellular (U-layout). Mnemonic: **"SSPC"**.
- ✅ **Process Cycle Efficiency (PCE)** = VAT / LT × 100%. World-class target ≥ 25–40%.
- ✅ **Takt Time** = Available Time / Customer Demand — used for flow pacing, *not* direct LT reduction.
- ✅ **Kanban formula** = $N = (D \cdot L \cdot (1+S)) / C$ — **round up** to nearest integer.
- ✅ **Setup time reduction** → enables **batch size reduction** → enables **one-piece flow** → **reduces queue time** → **reduces LT**.
- ✅ **Pull system prevents overproduction** — the #1 waste in the 7 Wastes framework.
- ✅ **U-cell layout** = single-operator multi-process cell = eliminates transport + integrates inspection.
- ✅ **Heijunka** (level scheduling) reduces demand variability → smoother flow → lower LT.
- ✅ **SMED** = Shigeo Shingo; goal is setup < **10 minutes** (single digit).
- ✅ **Pre- vs Post-Processing time** concepts: Pre-processing is queue before first operation; Post-processing is queue after last operation. Both are NVA.
- ✅ **Value Stream Mapping (VSM)** is the *diagnostic tool* to identify LT waste; LT reduction *tools* (SMED, Kanban, cells) are the *prescription*.
- ✅ **Cost of delay:** every day of LT = carrying cost + opportunity cost + obsolescence risk.
- ✅ **Examiner's favourite trio of questions:** (1) "Define LT + components" (3 marks), (2) "Little's Law numerical" (7 marks), (3) "Kanban design" (7 marks).

> 🎯 **Final KTU Pro-Tip:** Always end a Lead Time answer with a sentence on **continuous improvement (Kaizen)** — LT reduction is a *journey, not a destination*. This signals to the examiner that you understand Lean as a philosophy, not just a formula sheet.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optimum Utilization of Capital
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET746 - LEAN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Optimum Utilization of Capital

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Optimum Utilization of Capital** in the context of Lean Manufacturing refers to the systematic deployment of lean tools, techniques, and management philosophies to ensure that every unit of capital invested in machinery, equipment, infrastructure, and working capital generates the **maximum possible output, value, and return** with the **minimum possible waste (muda)**.

Under the KTU 2024 scheme, this concept is anchored on three pillars:

1. **Maximizing Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** — ensuring machines run at full designed capacity.
2. **Minimizing Capital Lock-up in Waste** — eliminating overproduction, excess inventory, and idle assets.
3. **Maximizing Return on Capital Employed (ROCE)** — generating higher throughput per rupee invested.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Approved):**
> *"Optimum Utilization of Capital is the lean principle of achieving the highest possible productivity, availability, and quality output from existing capital assets, while reducing the need for additional capital investment through waste elimination, process standardization, and continuous improvement (Kaizen)."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Taxi Driver's Mindset

Imagine a taxi driver who has bought a car (capital) for ₹8,00,000. If the taxi sits idle in the garage for 20 hours a day and runs only 4 hours, the **capital is severely under-utilized**, even though the driver technically "owns" a working taxi.

Now compare this to another taxi driver who runs the car **22 hours a day** through two shifts, maintains it perfectly (no breakdowns), ensures no customer complaints (no returns = quality), and uses GPS to take the shortest route (no wasted motion).

| Parameter | Idle Taxi Driver | Lean Taxi Driver |
|---|---|---|
| Capital (Car) | ₹8,00,000 | ₹8,00,000 |
| Running Hours/Day | 4 hrs | 22 hrs |
| Revenue/Month | ₹30,000 | ₹2,10,000 |
| ROI | Very Low | **Very High** |

The **lean driver achieves "Optimum Utilization of Capital"** — the same asset generates many times more value. This is exactly what lean manufacturing aims to do with factory machines, workstations, and capital equipment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Lean Insight:** Lean does **NOT** mean working machines until they break. It means **smart utilization** — running machines at optimal speed, with zero defects, zero breakdowns, and zero idle time, while maintaining employee well-being.

### 1.3 Core Metrics (Standard Industry Benchmarks)

The following metrics are used universally (and accepted by KTU examiners) to quantify capital utilization:

- **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness): World-Class Benchmark = 85%**
- **Capacity Utilization: World-Class Benchmark = 85–90%**
- **ROI (Return on Investment): Healthy Industry Benchmark = 15–25%**
- **ROCE (Return on Capital Employed): Healthy Industry Benchmark = 20%+**
- **TEEP (Total Effective Equipment Performance): World-Class = 100%**

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 explicitly maps this topic to the lean tools **TPM (Total Productive Maintenance)**, **SMED (Single Minute Exchange of Die)**, and **OEE Analysis** as the primary enablers of capital optimization.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Optimum Capital Utilization

Lean manufacturing approaches capital utilization through three interconnected pillars:

#### Pillar 1: **Eliminate the Six Big Losses** (Nakajima's TPM Framework)
Seiichi Nakajima (father of TPM) identified **Six Big Losses** that drain capital effectiveness. These must be systematically eliminated:

| Category | The Six Big Losses | Capital Impact |
|---|---|---|
| **Availability Losses** | 1. Equipment Failure (Breakdown) | Capital sits idle, no output |
| | 2. Setup & Adjustment Losses | Changeover time = lost production |
| **Performance Losses** | 3. Idling & Minor Stoppages | Machine on but not producing |
| | 4. Reduced Speed Losses | Machine running slower than design speed |
| **Quality Losses** | 5. Defect/Repair Losses | Scrap & rework consume capacity |
| | 6. Startup/Yield Losses | Rejects during warm-up |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When answering theory questions, always classify your answer into the **Six Big Losses framework** — examiners reward structured answers.

#### Pillar 2: **Improve Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)**
OEE is the gold standard metric. It is computed as:

$$OEE\% = Availability \times Performance \times Quality$$

Where each component is calculated as a percentage. The **multiplicative nature** is critical — even a small drop in one factor drastically reduces the overall OEE.

**Example impact:** If Availability = 90%, Performance = 95%, Quality = 99%:
$$OEE = 0.90 \times 0.95 \times 0.99 = 0.846 = 84.6\%$$

But if one factor drops to 80%:
$$OEE = 0.80 \times 0.95 \times 0.99 = 0.752 = 75.2\%$$

#### Pillar 3: **Reduce Capital Lock-up Through Lean Inventory Strategies**
Lean capital management demands:

- **Just-In-Time (JIT)** production to minimize Work-In-Process (WIP) inventory.
- **Kanban** systems to pull only what's needed.
- **Heijunka** (level scheduling) to avoid overproduction.
- **Value Stream Mapping (VSM)** to identify capital-blocking waste.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Benchmark |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Availability = \frac{Operating\ Time}{Planned\ Production\ Time} \times 100$ | Measures uptime excluding stoppages | % (Target: ≥90%) |
| 2 | $Performance = \frac{Ideal\ Cycle\ Time \times Total\ Pieces}{Operating\ Time} \times 100$ | Measures speed vs. design speed | % (Target: ≥95%) |
| 3 | $Quality = \frac{Good\ Pieces}{Total\ Pieces} \times 100$ | Measures first-pass yield | % (Target: ≥99%) |
| 4 | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | Composite capital effectiveness | % (World-Class: ≥85%) |
| 5 | $TEEP = \frac{Operating\ Time}{Calendar\ Time \ (24 \times 365)} \times 100$ | True asset utilization | % (Maximum: 100%) |
| 6 | $Capacity\ Utilization = \frac{Actual\ Output}{Maximum\ Possible\ Output} \times 100$ | Demand vs. capacity | % (Target: 85–90%) |
| 7 | $ROI = \frac{Net\ Profit}{Cost\ of\ Investment} \times 100$ | Profitability per ₹ invested | % (Industry: 15–25%) |
| 8 | $ROCE = \frac{EBIT}{Capital\ Employed} \times 100$ | Operating efficiency of capital | % (Healthy: >20%) |
| 9 | $Payback\ Period = \frac{Initial\ Investment}{Annual\ Cash\ Inflow}$ | Time to recover capital | Years (Shorter is better) |
| 10 | $Inventory\ Turnover = \frac{Cost\ of\ Goods\ Sold}{Average\ Inventory}$ | Capital freed from inventory | Times/Year (Higher = Lean) |
| 11 | $Machine\ Hour\ Rate = \frac{Total\ Machine\ Cost}{Effective\ Machine\ Hours}$ | Cost per productive hour | ₹/Hour (Lower = Leaner) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Formula Notation:** In all the above formulas, vertical bars have been replaced with `\vert` style notation to maintain KTU markdown rendering compatibility. Use $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value expressions in your own answer sheets.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles of optimum capital utilization are applied across:

- **CNC Machining Cells** — measuring OEE to identify hidden capacity.
- **Automotive Assembly Lines** — SMED to reduce changeover from hours to minutes.
- **Pharmaceutical Plants** — TPM to maintain GMP-compliant uptime.
- **Capital-Intensive Sectors** (Steel, Power, Petrochem) — ROCE drives investment decisions.
- **IT & Service Sectors** — Lean applied to server utilization and human capital.

> [!TIP]
> **Lean Philosophy Connection:** "Optimum Utilization of Capital" directly supports the lean principle of **"Creating More Value with Fewer Resources"** — the very definition of productivity per Taiichi Ohno, the father of the Toyota Production System.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: OEE Calculation (Comprehensive Derivation)

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
A CNC machining center in a lean manufacturing facility operates with the following data for a given week:

- Scheduled Production Time = 5 days × 8 hours = **2,400 minutes**
- Unplanned Downtime (breakdowns) = **120 minutes**
- Planned Stoppages (lunch, breaks) = **200 minutes**
- Setup & Changeover Time = **80 minutes**
- Ideal Cycle Time per part = **2.0 minutes/part**
- Total Parts Produced = **900 parts**
- Defective Parts = **45 parts**

**Calculate: (a) Availability, (b) Performance, (c) Quality, (d) OEE**

#### Step 1: Compute Operating Time

$$\begin{aligned}
Operating\ Time &= Scheduled\ Production\ Time \\
&\quad - Unplanned\ Downtime \\
&\quad - Planned\ Stoppages \\
&\quad - Setup\ \&\ Changeover\ Time
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
Operating\ Time &= 2400 - 120 - 200 - 80 \\
&= 2400 - 400 \\
&= 2000\ minutes
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Operating time is the actual time the machine was running and producing. All forms of stoppage are deducted to get the true productive window.

#### Step 2: Compute Availability

$$\begin{aligned}
Availability &= \frac{Operating\ Time}{Scheduled\ Production\ Time} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{2000}{2400} \times 100 \\
&= 83.33\%
\end{aligned}$$

> **Interpretation:** Out of 100% planned time, the machine was productive for 83.33% of it. The remaining 16.67% was lost to breakdowns, setup, and planned stoppages.

#### Step 3: Compute Performance

$$\begin{aligned}
Performance &= \frac{Ideal\ Cycle\ Time \times Total\ Pieces}{Operating\ Time} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{2.0 \times 900}{2000} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{1800}{2000} \times 100 \\
&= 90.00\%
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** If the machine had run at its design (ideal) speed, it would have produced 1800/2.0 = 900 parts in 1800 minutes. But it actually took 2000 minutes, so it was 10% slower than ideal.

#### Step 4: Compute Quality

$$\begin{aligned}
Good\ Pieces &= Total\ Parts - Defective\ Parts \\
&= 900 - 45 = 855\ parts
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
Quality &= \frac{Good\ Pieces}{Total\ Pieces} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{855}{900} \times 100 \\
&= 95.00\%
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Only 855 of the 900 produced parts were good — 45 were rejected. So 5% of production capacity was consumed by defects.

#### Step 5: Compute OEE

$$\begin{aligned}
OEE &= Availability \times Performance \times Quality \\
&= 83.33\% \times 90.00\% \times 95.00\% \\
&= 0.8333 \times 0.90 \times 0.95 \\
&= 0.7125 \\
&= 71.25\%
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conclusion:** The CNC center is operating at **71.25% OEE**, which is **below the world-class benchmark of 85%**. This signals an opportunity for lean intervention — likely through TPM, SMED, and Poka-Yoke.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: ROCE and Capital Utilization Analysis

**Problem Statement:**
A manufacturing company has the following financials for FY 2024-25:
- Total Capital Invested in Machinery = ₹5,00,00,000
- Working Capital (Inventory + Receivables - Payables) = ₹1,20,00,000
- EBIT (Earnings Before Interest & Tax) = ₹1,50,00,000
- Machine Book Value (Net) = ₹4,00,00,000
- Maximum Production Capacity = 1,20,000 units/year
- Actual Production = 96,000 units/year

**Calculate: (a) Capital Employed, (b) ROCE, (c) Capacity Utilization**

#### Step 1: Calculate Capital Employed

$$\begin{aligned}
Capital\ Employed &= Total\ Capital\ Invested + Working\ Capital \\
&= 5,00,00,000 + 1,20,00,000 \\
&= 6,20,00,000
\end{aligned}$$

> **Logic:** Capital Employed represents the total long-term and short-term capital used by the firm to generate profits.

#### Step 2: Calculate ROCE

$$\begin{aligned}
ROCE &= \frac{EBIT}{Capital\ Employed} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{1,50,00,000}{6,20,00,000} \times 100 \\
&= 24.19\%
\end{aligned}$$

> **Interpretation:** A ROCE of 24.19% is **healthy** by industry standards (target > 20%). This indicates efficient deployment of capital.

#### Step 3: Calculate Capacity Utilization

$$\begin{aligned}
Capacity\ Utilization &= \frac{Actual\ Production}{Maximum\ Capacity} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{96,000}{1,20,000} \times 100 \\
&= 80.00\%
\end{aligned}$$

> **Interpretation:** Only 80% of installed capacity is being used. The remaining 20% is **idle capital** — a lean opportunity to increase production without new investment.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Lean Tools Mapped to Capital Optimization

| Lean Tool | Capital Wastage Targeted | Impact on Metric | Typical Improvement |
|---|---|---|---|
| **TPM (Total Productive Maintenance)** | Equipment breakdowns, downtime | ↑ Availability | +15–25% |
| **SMED (Single Minute Exchange of Die)** | Long changeover times | ↑ Availability, ↑ Performance | Changeover reduced by 50–90% |
| **Poka-Yoke (Mistake Proofing)** | Defects, rework | ↑ Quality | Defects reduced by 80%+ |
| **JIT (Just-In-Time)** | Excess inventory, WIP | ↓ Working Capital | Inventory reduced 30–70% |
| **Kanban** | Overproduction | ↓ Inventory | WIP reduced 40–60% |
| **VSM (Value Stream Mapping)** | Non-value-added activities | ↑ Overall OEE | +10–20% OEE improvement |
| **5S + Visual Management** | Searching, motion waste | ↑ Effective time | +10–15% productivity |
| **Kaizen (Continuous Improvement)** | All forms of muda | ↑ ROCE, ↑ ROI | Sustained 5–10%/year |
| **Heijunka (Level Scheduling)** | Uneven production, idle time | ↑ Capacity Utilization | Mura elimination |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Lean Capital Optimization Framework (Block-Level Flow)

This block diagram maps the structural flow of how lean tools systematically improve capital utilization, from input capital to output value.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Capital Investment : Machinery, Equipment, Infrastructure] --> B[Asset Deployment in Production]
    B --> C{Six Big Losses Present?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Loss Identification via OEE Analysis]
    C -- No --> K[World Class Performance Achieved]
    D --> D1[Availability Losses : Breakdowns, Setup]
    D --> D2[Performance Losses : Idling, Reduced Speed]
    D --> D3[Quality Losses : Defects, Rework]
    D1 --> E[Apply Lean Tools]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> E1[TPM : Eliminate Breakdowns]
    E --> E2[SMED : Reduce Changeover]
    E --> E3[PokaYoke : Prevent Defects]
    E --> E4[JIT and Kanban : Reduce Inventory Lockup]
    E1 --> F[Improved OEE Availability]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G[OEE Improved to World Class 85 percent]
    G --> H[Higher Throughput per Machine]
    H --> I[Improved ROCE and ROI]
    I --> J[Optimum Utilization of Capital Achieved]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Continuous Kaizen Cycle Restarts]
```

### 4.2 OEE Component Decomposition Diagram

This diagram visually decomposes the OEE multiplication into its three sub-metrics, showing how each factor contributes to capital effectiveness.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OEE[OEE Overall Equipment Effectiveness] --> A1[Availability 90 percent]
    OEE --> A2[Performance 95 percent]
    OEE --> A3[Quality 99 percent]
    A1 --> A1a[Operating Time vs Planned Time]
    A1 --> A1b[Loss: Breakdowns and Setup]
    A2 --> A2a[Ideal Cycle Time vs Actual]
    A2 --> A2b[Loss: Idling and Reduced Speed]
    A3 --> A3a[Good Pieces vs Total Pieces]
    A3 --> A3b[Loss: Defects and Rework]
    A1a --> R[OEE Equals 85 percent World Class]
    A1b --> R
    A2a --> R
    A2b --> R
    A3a --> R
    A3b --> R
```

### 4.3 Capital Utilization Decision Flow

This schematic shows the decision-making flow a lean manager follows when evaluating whether capital is optimally utilized.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Evaluate Current Capital Performance] --> T[Measure OEE]
    T --> Q{OEE Greater than 85 percent?}
    Q -- No --> U[Identify Loss Category]
    U --> U1{Breakdown Loss?}
    U --> U2{Setup Loss?}
    U --> U3{Defect Loss?}
    U1 --> V1[Deploy TPM Autonomous Maintenance]
    U2 --> V2[Deploy SMED Single Minute Exchange of Die]
    U3 --> V3[Deploy PokaYoke Mistake Proofing]
    V1 --> M[Re-measure OEE]
    V2 --> M
    V3 --> M
    M --> Q
    Q -- Yes --> W[Measure ROCE and ROI]
    W --> X{ROCE Greater than 20 percent?}
    X -- No --> Y[Optimize Working Capital with JIT]
    Y --> W
    X -- Yes --> Z[Optimum Capital Utilization Confirmed]
    Z --> KA[Initiate Kaizen for Continuous Improvement]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node identifiers used above are alphanumeric (e.g., `A1a`, `U2`, `V3`) and labels are kept raw and uppercase to ensure successful rendering on the KTU digital learning platform. No reserved keywords like `end` or `subgraph` are used as node IDs.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) and state the world-class benchmark value.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
OEE is a composite metric used in lean manufacturing to evaluate how effectively a manufacturing operation is utilized, compared to its full potential. It is the product of three factors: **Availability, Performance, and Quality**.

$$OEE = Availability \times Performance \times Quality$$

The world-class benchmark for OEE, as defined by SEMI (Semiconductor Equipment and Materials International), is **85%** (or greater). A typical manufacturing plant operates at 40–60% OEE, indicating significant scope for lean improvement.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for benchmark value)*

---

#### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List the Six Big Losses as identified by Seiichi Nakajima in TPM.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Six Big Losses, classified into three categories, are:

1. **Availability Losses:**
   - (i) Equipment Failure / Breakdown
   - (ii) Setup and Adjustment Losses

2. **Performance Losses:**
   - (iii) Idling and Minor Stoppages
   - (iv) Reduced Speed Losses

3. **Quality Losses:**
   - (v) Defect and Repair Losses
   - (vi) Startup / Yield Losses

*(1 mark for category names, 2 marks for listing the six losses)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each module in the End Semester Examination carries a 14-mark question with **internal choice** (i.e., students answer EITHER "Question A" OR "Question B" — both are from the same module). The 14 marks are typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Module 3 Choice 1]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the concept of Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE). Derive the formulas for its three components — Availability, Performance, and Quality — and discuss the world-class benchmark.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A plastic injection molding machine operates for 2 shifts of 8 hours each, 6 days a week. The data for a particular week is as follows:
- Unplanned downtime = 90 minutes
- Changeover time = 60 minutes
- Planned breaks = 240 minutes
- Ideal cycle time = 1.5 minutes per part
- Total parts produced = 2,400
- Defective parts = 96

**Calculate the OEE and identify the weakest component. Suggest two lean tools to improve it.**

**Model Solution (Question A):**

**(a) Concept and Derivation of OEE [7 Marks]:**

OEE was developed by Seiichi Nakajima as a comprehensive measure of manufacturing productivity. It identifies the percentage of planned production time that is truly productive. An OEE score of 100% represents **perfect production** — manufacturing only good parts, at full speed, with no stoppage.

**The three components are derived as follows:**

**1. Availability — measures Uptime:**

$$Availability = \frac{Operating\ Time}{Planned\ Production\ Time} \times 100$$

Where $Operating\ Time = Planned\ Production\ Time - Downtime - Setup\ Time$

**2. Performance — measures Speed Efficiency:**

$$Performance = \frac{Ideal\ Cycle\ Time \times Total\ Pieces\ Produced}{Operating\ Time} \times 100$$

**3. Quality — measures First-Pass Yield:**

$$Quality = \frac{Good\ Pieces}{Total\ Pieces\ Produced} \times 100$$

The final OEE is:

$$OEE\% = A \times P \times Q$$

The world-class benchmark is **85%**, as established by SEMI. A typical Indian manufacturing plant operates between 40–60% OEE.

*(2 marks for concept explanation, 3 marks for derivations, 2 marks for benchmark and interpretation)*

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1: Compute Planned Production Time**
$$\begin{aligned}
Planned\ Time &= 2\ shifts \times 8\ hrs \times 6\ days \times 60\ min \\
&= 5760\ minutes
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Compute Operating Time**
$$\begin{aligned}
Operating\ Time &= 5760 - 90 - 60 - 240 \\
&= 5370\ minutes
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Compute Availability**
$$Availability = \frac{5370}{5760} \times 100 = 93.23\%$$

**Step 4: Compute Performance**
$$Performance = \frac{1.5 \times 2400}{5370} \times 100 = \frac{3600}{5370} \times 100 = 67.04\%$$

**Step 5: Compute Quality**
$$Quality = \frac{2400 - 96}{2400} \times 100 = \frac{2304}{2400} \times 100 = 96.00\%$$

**Step 6: Compute OEE**
$$OEE = 0.9323 \times 0.6704 \times 0.9600 = 0.6000 = 60.00\%$$

**Step 7: Identification of Weakest Component**
Performance (67.04%) is the weakest factor, indicating the machine is running significantly slower than its design speed.

**Step 8: Lean Recommendations**
1. **TPM (Total Productive Maintenance)** — to eliminate minor stoppages and idling that reduce effective speed.
2. **SMED (Single Minute Exchange of Die)** — although setup is not the worst factor, optimizing it frees more operating time for productive running at design speed.

*(1 mark for Step 1, 1 mark each for Steps 2–6, 1 mark for weakest component identification, 2 marks for lean tool recommendations)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Module 3 Choice 2]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the role of TPM (Total Productive Maintenance) and SMED (Single Minute Exchange of Die) in achieving optimum utilization of capital. How do they impact OEE and ROCE?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A company invested ₹10 crore in a manufacturing facility. The annual EBIT is ₹2.5 crore. The working capital is ₹2 crore. Calculate the ROCE. If the company implements lean TPM practices that improve OEE from 55% to 78% (with proportional EBIT improvement), what is the new ROCE? Comment on the lean impact.

**Model Solution (Question B):**

**(a) TPM and SMED for Optimum Capital Utilization [7 Marks]:**

**TPM (Total Productive Maintenance):**
TPM is a proactive and preventive maintenance system for capital equipment, developed by Seiichi Nakajima in 1971. It involves operators in routine maintenance (autonomous maintenance) along with maintenance engineers.

- **Role in Capital Utilization:** Eliminates breakdowns, reduces unplanned downtime, increases equipment life.
- **Impact on OEE:** Improves the **Availability** component significantly (typically +15–25%).
- **Impact on ROCE:** Higher uptime → higher production → higher EBIT → higher ROCE.

**SMED (Single Minute Exchange of Die):**
SMED, developed by Shigeo Shingo at Toyota, is a methodology to reduce machine changeover time to under 10 minutes (single-digit minutes).

- **Role in Capital Utilization:** Reduces setup time, increases production capacity, enables small-batch production.
- **Impact on OEE:** Boosts both **Availability** and **Performance** by reclaiming productive hours.
- **Impact on ROCE:** Higher effective capacity → more output from same capital → better return.

**Combined Lean Impact:** TPM + SMED together transform a capital asset from being a cost center into a value-generating engine, directly supporting the lean goal of "more output, less capital."

*(2 marks each for TPM and SMED explanation, 2 marks for OEE/ROCE impact, 1 mark for integrated conclusion)*

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1: Compute Original Capital Employed**
$$Capital\ Employed = 10\ crore + 2\ crore = 12\ crore$$

**Step 2: Compute Original ROCE**
$$ROCE = \frac{2.5\ crore}{12\ crore} \times 100 = 20.83\%$$

**Step 3: Compute OEE Improvement Ratio**
$$\begin{aligned}
OEE\ Improvement\ Factor &= \frac{New\ OEE}{Old\ OEE} = \frac{78\%}{55\%} = 1.418
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Compute New EBIT (Proportional Improvement)**
$$\begin{aligned}
New\ EBIT &= Old\ EBIT \times 1.418 \\
&= 2.5\ crore \times 1.418 \\
&= 3.545\ crore
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Compute New ROCE**
$$New\ ROCE = \frac{3.545\ crore}{12\ crore} \times 100 = 29.54\%$$

**Step 6: Comment on Lean Impact**
- ROCE increased from **20.83% → 29.54%** — a jump of **8.71 percentage points**.
- This is achieved **without any new capital investment** — purely through lean process improvement.
- The same capital now generates **42% more earnings**, validating lean as a capital-multiplier philosophy.

*(1 mark for original capital, 1 mark for original ROCE, 2 marks for OEE ratio and new EBIT, 1 mark for new ROCE, 2 marks for qualitative lean impact analysis)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls (Examiner-Verified):**
>
> 1. **Formula Misapplication (–2 marks):** Students often write $OEE = A + P + Q$ (addition) instead of **multiplication**. Always remember — OEE is **multiplicative**, and even a small drop in one factor drastically reduces the total.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to Subtract Downtime from Operating Time (–1 mark):** Operating Time ≠ Scheduled Time. Always deduct unplanned downtime, planned stoppages, and setup/changeover time.
>
> 3. **Confusing OEE with TEEP (–1 mark):** OEE uses *Planned Production Time* as the denominator. TEEP uses *24 × 365 = Calendar Time*. They are not interchangeable.
>
> 4. **No Units in Numerical Answers (–0.5 to –1 mark):** Always mention "%" with OEE, ROCE, ROI values.
>
> 5. **Skipping "Why" in Theory Answers (–1 to –2 marks):** Don't just list the Six Big Losses — explain how each one reduces capital effectiveness. Examiners reward the "So What?" reasoning.
>
> 6. **Missing Lean Tool Mapping (–1 mark):** When asked how to improve OEE, students often give generic answers. Always map to specific lean tools: TPM, SMED, Poka-Yoke, Kaizen.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Block — Memorize for KTU ESE**

- ✅ **Optimum Utilization of Capital** = Maximum output + Maximum value + Minimum waste from existing capital.
- ✅ **OEE Formula:** $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ (multiplicative, not additive).
- ✅ **World-Class OEE Benchmark:** **85%** (per SEMI standards).
- ✅ **Six Big Losses:** Breakdowns, Setup, Idling, Reduced Speed, Defects, Startup Yield.
- ✅ **Three OEE Components:**
  - **Availability** (target ≥ 90%) — eliminate downtime.
  - **Performance** (target ≥ 95%) — run at design speed.
  - **Quality** (target ≥ 99%) — produce right the first time.
- ✅ **Lean Tools for Capital Optimization:** TPM, SMED, JIT, Kanban, Poka-Yoke, VSM, 5S, Kaizen, Heijunka.
- ✅ **Key Financial Metrics:**
  - $ROCE = \frac{EBIT}{Capital\ Employed} \times 100$ (target > 20%)
  - $ROI = \frac{Net\ Profit}{Investment} \times 100$ (target 15–25%)
  - $Capacity\ Utilization = \frac{Actual\ Output}{Maximum\ Output} \times 100$ (target 85–90%)
- ✅ **TPM** → improves **Availability** of OEE.
- ✅ **SMED** → reduces setup/changeover → improves **Availability & Performance**.
- ✅ **Poka-Yoke** → prevents defects → improves **Quality**.
- ✅ **JIT & Kanban** → reduce working capital lock-up → improves ROCE.
- ✅ **Core Lean Philosophy:** "Doing more with less" — capital efficiency is at the heart of lean.
- ✅ **Critical KTU Insight:** Lean improves capital utilization **without new investment** — it extracts more value from existing assets.
- ✅ **Exam Mantra:** Always state the **benchmark values** (85% OEE, 90% A, 95% P, 99% Q) — examiners reward numerical anchoring.
- ✅ **Taiichi Ohno Quote (use in answers for full marks):** *"The most dangerous kind of waste is the waste we do not recognize."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optimum Utilization of People
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET746 - LEAN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Optimum Utilization of People

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optimum Utilization of People** is defined as the strategic alignment of human capital—skills, creativity, knowledge, and problem-solving abilities—within a Lean enterprise to maximize value-added activities while simultaneously developing employees' capabilities, ensuring their active participation in continuous improvement (Kaizen), and fostering a culture of mutual respect.

In the **Toyota Production System (TPS)** and the broader **Lean Manufacturing** framework, people are considered the most valuable resource (not machines). The principle asserts that **every employee is a problem-solver**, not merely an operator. The optimization is achieved by removing non-value-added activities, standardizing work, providing training, empowering teams, and engaging workers in decision-making through structured systems.

### The Two Pillars of the Toyota Way
| Pillar | Japanese Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Continuous Improvement** | *Kaizen* | Process optimization |
| **Respect for People** | *Ningen Soncho* | Dignity, empowerment, teamwork |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric for KTU Board Exams:** The most cited metric is **Value-Added Ratio (VAR)**:
> $$VAR = \frac{\text{Value-Added Time}}{\text{Total Lead Time}} \times 100\%$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **kitchen in a busy restaurant** during dinner rush. 

- A **poorly utilized chef** spends 60% of his time washing dishes, fetching ingredients from storage, cleaning spills, and waiting for the oven to preheat. Only 40% is actual cooking.
- An **optimally utilized chef** works in a **mise-en-scène** (everything in its place) kitchen: ingredients pre-measured, surfaces clean, tools arranged, and a clear order sequence. His hands never stop cooking—**100% value-added activity**.

In Lean terms, the restaurant has applied **5S (Sort, Set, Shine, Standardize, Sustain)**, removed the **7 Wastes (Muda)**, and empowered the chef to suggest menu improvements. This is **Optimum Utilization of People**—transforming the worker from a pair of hands into an engaged problem-solver.

## 1.3 The Three Dimensions of People Utilization

1. **Physical Dimension** — Eliminating physical waste (motion, transportation, waiting).
2. **Cognitive Dimension** — Engaging the mind in problem-solving (Kaizen, suggestions).
3. **Emotional Dimension** — Respect, recognition, and purpose (Morale, motivation).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Insight:** Questions on this topic almost always test whether the student understands that *Lean treats people as assets, not costs*. Use the phrase *"human capital as a source of competitive advantage"* in your answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework (The Five Pillars)

### Pillar I: Work Standardization
Standardized work is the **foundation** for optimum utilization. Without a baseline, improvement cannot be measured. Standardized work consists of:
- **Takt Time** (pace of customer demand)
- **Work Sequence** (specific order of tasks)
- **Standard Work-In-Process (SWIP)**
- **Time per piece / Cycle Time**

> [!NOTE]
> **Standardized work is not a rigid prescription; it is a living document** that evolves through Kaizen.

### Pillar II: Work Balancing (Heijunka of Labor)
Load balancing across operators and workstations prevents overburden (*Muri*) and unevenness (*Mura*). The principle: **Match the workforce to Takt Time**.

**Line Balancing Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{LB} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i}{n \times T_{cycle}} \times 100\%$$

Where:
- $\eta_{LB}$ = Line balancing efficiency (%)
- $t_i$ = Standard time for task $i$ (seconds)
- $n$ = Number of workstations
- $T_{cycle}$ = Cycle time / Takt time (seconds)

### Pillar III: Skill Development & Multi-Skilling
A worker trained on **multiple operations** is more flexible. The skill matrix tracks this:

| Operator \ Operation | Op 1 | Op 2 | Op 3 | Op 4 |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Worker A | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | – |
| Worker B | ✓ | ✓ | – | ✓ |
| Worker C | – | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |

### Pillar IV: Empowerment & Suggestion Systems
Workers closest to the process have the **best information** for improvements. Formal mechanisms include:
- **Quality Circles** (small groups, voluntary, problem-solving)
- **Kaizen Teian** (suggestion system)
- **Autonomous Maintenance** (operators maintain their machines)
- **Andon** (visual signaling for help)

### Pillar V: Job Enrichment (Herzberg's Two-Factor Theory)
Unlike *job enlargement* (adding more tasks of the same level), *job enrichment* adds **vertical depth**—planning, control, and evaluation.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Unit | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Takt Time | $TT = \frac{T_{available}}{D}$ | sec/unit | Pace matching |
| 2 | Line Balancing Efficiency | $\eta_{LB} = \frac{\sum t_i}{n \cdot T_{cycle}}$ | % | Operator utilization |
| 3 | Value-Added Ratio | $VAR = \frac{VA\ Time}{Lead\ Time}$ | % | Waste identification |
| 4 | Operator Downtime Ratio | $ODR = \frac{Downtime}{Available\ Time}$ | % | Equipment/personnel |
| 5 | Suggestion Acceptance Rate | $SAR = \frac{Accepted}{Total\ Suggestions} \times 100$ | % | Engagement quality |
| 6 | Cross-Training Ratio | $CTR = \frac{Skills\ per\ Worker}{Total\ Operations}$ | % | Workforce flexibility |
| 7 | Labor Productivity | $LP = \frac{Output}{Labor\ Hours}$ | units/hr | Efficiency metric |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Avoid using the vertical pipe `|` symbol** in formulas. For absolute values, use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$. (Critical for LaTeX rendering on KTU digital platforms.)

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In automotive assembly (Toyota, Tata Motors), electronics manufacturing (Foxconn), and even software engineering (Spotify squads), the principle remains identical: **engage people, eliminate waste, develop skills, and respect individuality**. The economic impact is measurable: companies with high employee engagement scores show **21% higher productivity** (Gallup, 2023 meta-analysis).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Optimum Number of Operators from Takt Time

### Problem Statement
A manufacturing cell has a total available production time of **7.5 hours per shift (27,000 seconds)**. The daily demand is **450 units**. The cell has **4 sequential operations** with the following standard times:

| Operation | Standard Time (sec) |
| :--- | :---: |
| Op-A (Loading) | 45 |
| Op-B (Machining) | 90 |
| Op-C (Inspection) | 30 |
| Op-D (Unloading) | 15 |

**Required:** Calculate (i) Takt Time, (ii) Number of operators, (iii) Line Balancing Efficiency.

### Step 1: Calculate Takt Time (TT)

$$
\begin{aligned}
TT &= \frac{T_{available}}{D} \\
&= \frac{27{,}000\ \text{seconds}}{450\ \text{units}} \\
&= 60\ \text{seconds per unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** We have 27,000 seconds available to satisfy customer demand of 450 units. To meet demand, one unit must roll off the line every 60 seconds.

### Step 2: Determine Workstations Required

The total work content per unit is:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum t_i &= t_A + t_B + t_C + t_D \\
&= 45 + 90 + 30 + 15 \\
&= 180\ \text{seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

The theoretical minimum number of workstations:
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{min} &= \left\lceil \frac{\sum t_i}{TT} \right\rceil \\
&= \left\lceil \frac{180}{60} \right\rceil \\
&= \left\lceil 3 \right\rceil \\
&= 3\ \text{workstations}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Assign Operations to Workstations (Using Largest Candidate Rule)

To balance the line, sort operations by time (descending): B(90), A(45), C(30), D(15).

| Workstation | Assigned Ops | Total Time (sec) | Idle Time (sec) |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | B (90) | 90 | $60 - 90 = -30$ ❌ |
| 1 | B + D (90 + 15) | 105 | $60 - 105 = -45$ ❌ |
| 1 | B + D + C (90 + 15 + 30) | 135 | $60 - 135 = -75$ ❌ |
| 1 | A + C + D (45 + 30 + 15) | 90 | $60 - 90 = -30$ ❌ |
| 1 | A + C (45 + 30) | 75 | ❌ |
| 1 | B alone | 90 | ❌ (over takt) |

**Reconsider** — the largest task (B = 90 sec) **exceeds Takt Time (60 sec)**. This indicates either:
- The operation must be split into sub-tasks, or
- The Takt Time must be increased (impossible if demand is fixed), or
- The operation requires equipment assistance.

**Solution:** Split Op-B into B1 (45 sec) and B2 (45 sec) with a sub-station. Revised assignment:

| Workstation | Assigned Ops | Total Time (sec) | Idle Time (sec) |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | B1 + C (45 + 30) | 75* | — |
| 2 | B2 + A (45 + 45) | 90* | — |
| 3 | D (15) | 15 | $60 - 15 = 45$ |

*Even with splitting, WS1 and WS2 exceed takt. Practical adjustment: add helpers OR reduce demand.*

**Final feasible assignment with helpers (rebalancing):**

| Workstation | Ops | Time (sec) | Idle (sec) |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | A (45) + D (15) | 60 | 0 |
| 2 | B1 (45) + C (30) | 75* | — |
| 3 | B2 (45) | 45 | 15 |

Assume WS2 is shared with a helper. Total operators = **3** (one per WS) + **1 helper** = **4**.

### Step 4: Calculate Line Balancing Efficiency

Using 3 effective workstations (with helper counted fractionally):
$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{LB} &= \frac{\sum t_i}{n \cdot TT} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{180}{3 \times 60} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{180}{180} \times 100\% \\
&= 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

If helper counted as a full operator (n=4):
$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{LB} &= \frac{180}{4 \times 60} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{180}{240} \times 100\% \\
&= 75\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretive Note:** 75% efficiency is acceptable for high-variability cells; 100% is the theoretical ideal but rarely sustainable. KTU answers should acknowledge **real-world constraints** (operator fatigue, equipment reliability, variation in demand).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Motivation Theories Applied to Lean

| Theory | Author | Core Idea | Lean Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Hierarchy of Needs | Maslow (1943) | 5-tier pyramid (physiological → self-actualization) | Pay (base), safety (stable job), belonging (teams), esteem (recognition), growth (Kaizen leadership) |
| Two-Factor | Herzberg (1959) | Hygiene factors vs. Motivators | Hygiene: pay, conditions; Motivators: achievement, responsibility, growth |
| Theory X & Y | McGregor (1960) | X: workers lazy; Y: workers self-motivated | Lean adopts **Theory Y**: trust workers, empower teams |
| Expectancy Theory | Vroom (1964) | Motivation = Expectancy × Instrumentality × Valence | Link bonuses to Kaizen results |
| Job Characteristics Model | Hackman & Oldham (1976) | 5 core job dimensions → 3 critical psychological states | Job rotation, enrichment, autonomy |

---
