- ✅ **DPMO formula:** $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{DPU} \times 10^{6}}{\text{Opportunities per Unit}}$ — *the single most-tested formula.*
- ✅ Sigma Level = $Z_{\text{short}} - 1.5$ (long-term convention).
- ✅ **Pareto Principle (80/20)** = "Vital few vs. Trivial many" — used to *prioritize*, not to *solve*.
- ✅ **Fishbone (Ishikawa)** categories: 4M (Man, Machine, Material, Method) or expanded to 6M (+ Measurement, Milieu).
- ✅ **Control Chart** distinguishes **Common Cause** (systemic) vs. **Special Cause** (assignable) variation — this is the *only* tool that monitors process stability over time.
- ✅ **Cp** measures *spread only*; **Cpk** measures *spread AND centering* — always prefer Cpk.
- ✅ **Scatter Diagram** is the *qualitative* precursor to the *quantitative* correlation coefficient *r*.
- ✅ **Define** = project scoping; **Measure** = baseline; **Analyze** = root cause; **Improve** = pilot; **Control** = sustain.
- ✅ Six Sigma integrates with **Lean** to form **Lean Six Sigma** (often asked as a 3-mark question).
- ✅ **5-Why** is a *linear* root-cause drill; Fishbone is a *categorical* cause brainstorm — they are complementary, not interchangeable.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Standard Deviation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET746 - LEAN MANUFACTURING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Standard Deviation # MODULE 4 — STATISTICAL FOUNDATIONS OF LEAN: STANDARD DEVIATION ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Deviation (σ or s)** is a statistical measure of **dispersion** or **variability** of a dataset about its central tendency (mean). In Lean Manufacturing, it quantifies the magnitude of **common-cause variation** inherent in any process and is the foundational metric for **Statistical Process Control (SPC)**, **Six Sigma**, and **Process Capability (Cp, Cpk)** studies. Mathematically, Standard Deviation is the **positive square root of the arithmetic mean of squared deviations** of each observation from the dataset's mean. ### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Think of a Lean assembly line producing bolts of nominal length 50 mm.** > - The **mean (μ)** is the *target* — the centre of the bullseye. > - **Standard Deviation (σ)** is the *typical "scatter" or "spread"* of bolt lengths around that target. > - A **small σ** → bolts cluster tightly near 50 mm → **predictable, capable, Lean process**. > - A **large σ** → bolts fly far from 50 mm → **defects, rework, waste (Muda)** — the antithesis of Lean. > - The Lean practitioner's obsession is to **shrink σ** until variation is statistically negligible. In a Six Sigma process, the specification limits are set at **±6σ** from the mean — meaning only **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)** are tolerated. This is the gold standard Lean-Six Sigma aspires to. ### 1.3 Population vs. Sample — A Critical Distinction | Aspect | Population SD (σ) | Sample SD (s) | |---|---|---| | Symbol (Greek) | Lowercase **sigma** σ | Lowercase **s** | | Denominator | **N** (every member) | **n − 1** (Bessel's correction) | | Use Case | Whole process universe | Sample drawn from process | | Lean Context | Theoretical process model | Real shop-floor data collection | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bessel's correction (n − 1)** in the sample formula corrects for the bias introduced because the sample mean is itself an estimate of the true population mean. KTU examiners **frequently test** this distinction. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Logic Chain — Step-by-Step Meaning The Standard Deviation is not a single step; it is a *chain* of five logical operations. Understanding *why* each step exists is the key to KTU 2024 Scheme high marks. 1. **Compute the Mean (x̄ or μ):** Establish the central reference point. Without a centre, "deviation" has no meaning. 2. **Compute Deviations (xi − x̄):** Measure how far each point is from the centre. Positive and negative deviations naturally cancel out, hence the next step. 3. **Square the Deviations (xi − x̄)²:** Two purposes — (a) eliminate the minus sign so deviations do not cancel; (b) **amplify large deviations** (penalises outliers). This is why σ is sensitive to extreme values. 4. **Average the Squared Deviations (Variance, σ²):** Converts the cumulative squared-error into a per-observation measure. This is the **Variance**. 5. **Take the Square Root (√Variance = σ):** Restores the original units (e.g., mm, kg, seconds). The Variance is in "squared units" and is physically less interpretable. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Units | Lean Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Population Mean | $\mu = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i}{N}$ | Same as $x_i$ | Process target setting | | 2 | Sample Mean | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i}{n}$ | Same as $x_i$ | Shop-floor sampling | | 3 | Population Variance | $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2}{N}$ | Squared units | Theoretical spread | | 4 | Sample Variance | $s^2 = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}$ | Squared units | Unbiased estimator | | 5 | Population SD | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2}{N}}$ | Same as $x_i$ | Six Sigma, control limits | | 6 | Sample SD | $s = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}}$ | Same as $x_i$ | SPC chart limits | | 7 | Range-based SD estimate | $\hat{\sigma} = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$ | Same as $x_i$ | Quick control-chart limits | | 8 | Process Capability | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Dimensionless | Process capability index | | 9 | Process Capability (centred) | $C_{pk} = \min\left[\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right]$ | Dimensionless | Real-world capability | | 10 | Empirical Rule (68-95-99.7) | Within $\pm 1\sigma$: 68.27%, $\pm 2\sigma$: 95.45%, $\pm 3\sigma$: 99.73% | Probability | Normal distribution spread | > [!NOTE] > **Constant $d_2$** for sample size $n$: $d_2(2)=1.128$, $d_2(3)=1.693$, $d_2(4)=2.059$, $d_2(5)=2.326$, $d_2(6)=2.534$. KTU questions on R-chart limits require this. ## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Bessel's Correction (n − 1) When we use $\bar{x}$ in place of the true $\mu$, the residuals $(x_i - \bar{x})$ are *constrained* — they must sum to zero. This freedom-loss means dividing by $n$ systematically **underestimates** the true variance. Dividing by $(n-1)$ "gives back" that lost degree of freedom, producing an **unbiased estimator**. KTU 2024 Scheme emphasises this statistical nuance. ## 2.4 Real-World Lean/Engineering Utility - **Six Sigma:** σ defines the defect rate; reducing σ by 50% doubles the process capability. - **Control Charts (X̄-R, X̄-σ):** Control limits are at $\bar{x} \pm 3\sigma$ — Shewhart's foundational rule. - **Poka-Yoke Design:** Smaller σ allows tighter tolerances, reducing inspection needs. - **Just-In-Time (JIT):** Low σ in supplier lead-times prevents the bullwhip effect. - **Taguchi Loss Function:** Quality loss grows quadratically with deviation from target → directly uses σ. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example 1 — Population SD (Production-Week Data) A Lean cell produces piston rings. The diameters (in mm) of all 8 rings produced in a shift are: $$x = \{50.2,\ 49.8,\ 50.1,\ 50.3,\ 49.9,\ 50.0,\ 50.2,\ 49.7\}$$ ### Step 1 — Compute the Population Mean (μ) $$ \begin{aligned} \mu &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i}{N} \\[4pt] &= \frac{50.2 + 49.8 + 50.1 + 50.3 + 49.9 + 50.0 + 50.2 + 49.7}{8} \\[4pt] &= \frac{400.2}{8} \\[4pt] &= 50.025\ \text{mm} \end{aligned} $$ The mean diameter is **50.025 mm** — the centreline of the process. ### Step 2 — Compute Each Deviation $(x_i - \mu)$ | Observation $i$ | $x_i$ (mm) | $x_i - \mu$ (mm) | |:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | 50.2 | $+0.175$ | | 2 | 49.8 | $-0.225$ | | 3 | 50.1 | $+0.075$ | | 4 | 50.3 | $+0.275$ | | 5 | 49.9 | $-0.125$ | | 6 | 50.0 | $-0.025$ | | 7 | 50.2 | $+0.175$ | | 8 | 49.7 | $-0.325$ | > [!NOTE] > **Verification:** $\sum (x_i - \mu) = 0.175 - 0.225 + 0.075 + 0.275 - 0.125 - 0.025 + 0.175 - 0.325 = 0.000$ ✓ (always zero by definition of mean). ### Step 3 — Square Each Deviation $$ \begin{aligned} (0.175)^2 &= 0.030625 \\ (-0.225)^2 &= 0.050625 \\ (0.075)^2 &= 0.005625 \\ (0.275)^2 &= 0.075625 \\ (-0.125)^2 &= 0.015625 \\ (-0.025)^2 &= 0.000625 \\ (0.175)^2 &= 0.030625 \\ (-0.325)^2 &= 0.105625 \\ \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Sum the Squared Deviations (Sum of Squares, SS) $$ \begin{aligned} SS &= \sum (x_i - \mu)^2 \\[4pt] &= 0.030625 + 0.050625 + 0.005625 + 0.075625 \\ &\quad + 0.015625 + 0.000625 + 0.030625 + 0.105625 \\[4pt] &= 0.315000\ \text{mm}^2 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5 — Compute the Population Variance (σ²) $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma^2 &= \frac{SS}{N} = \frac{0.315000}{8} = 0.039375\ \text{mm}^2 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 6 — Compute the Population Standard Deviation (σ) $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma &= \sqrt{\sigma^2} = \sqrt{0.039375} \\[4pt] &\approx 0.1984\ \text{mm} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Lean Interpretation:** The process has a mean of **50.025 mm** with σ ≈ **0.198 mm**. Under the Empirical Rule, ~99.73% of output lies within $\mu \pm 3\sigma = [49.43,\ 50.62]$ mm. If the engineering tolerance is, say, $\pm 0.6$ mm, this process is **highly capable** ($C_p \approx 2.0$). --- ## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example 2 — Sample SD (Bessel's Correction) A Lean auditor samples $n = 5$ fasteners from a batch of 200 and records their tensile strength (in MPa): $$x = \{510,\ 505,\ 512,\ 508,\ 500\}$$ ### Step 1 — Compute Sample Mean $$ \begin{aligned} \bar{x} &= \frac{510 + 505 + 512 + 508 + 500}{5} \\[4pt] &= \frac{2535}{5} = 507.0\ \text{MPa} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Deviations and Squared Deviations | $x_i$ | $x_i - \bar{x}$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:| | 510 | $+3$ | $9$ | | 505 | $-2$ | $4$ | | 512 | $+5$ | $25$ | | 508 | $+1$ | $1$ | | 500 | $-7$ | $49$ | | **Sum** | **0** ✓ | **88** | ### Step 3 — Apply Bessel's Correction (n − 1) $$ \begin{aligned} s^2 &= \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1} = \frac{88}{5-1} = \frac{88}{4} = 22.0\ \text{MPa}^2 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Sample Standard Deviation $$ \begin{aligned} s &= \sqrt{22.0} \approx 4.690\ \text{MPa} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why $(n-1)$ not $n$?** The sample mean $\bar{x}$ is itself estimated from the data. Using it "consumes" one degree of freedom. Dividing by 4 instead of 5 gives a slightly larger, *unbiased* estimate of the true process σ. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Lean Application: X̄-Chart Control Limits A quality engineer collects 5 sub-groups of size $n = 4$ from a Lean process. The grand mean is $\bar{\bar{x}} = 100$ mm and the average range is $\bar{R} = 2.0$ mm. For $n = 4$, $d_2 = 2.059$. ### Step 1 — Estimate Process SD from Range $$ \begin{aligned} \hat{\sigma} &= \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{2.0}{2.059} \approx 0.9714\ \text{mm} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Set X̄-Chart Control Limits (Shewhart's 3-Sigma Rule) $$ \begin{aligned} UCL_{\bar{x}} &= \bar{\bar{x}} + 3\hat{\sigma} = 100 + 3(0.9714) = 102.914\ \text{mm} \\[4pt] LCL_{\bar{x}} &= \bar{\bar{x}} - 3\hat{\sigma} = 100 - 3(0.9714) = 97.086\ \text{mm} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > Any sample mean falling outside $[97.086,\ 102.914]$ indicates a **special-cause variation** (signal) — the process is **not in statistical control**, and Lean intervention (5-Why, Kaizen) is triggered. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Process Capability (Cp and Cpk) A shaft diameter has target $\mu = 25.00$ mm, USL $= 25.10$ mm, LSL $= 24.90$ mm, and $\sigma = 0.020$ mm. ### Step 1 — Process Capability Index (Cp) $$ \begin{aligned} C_p &= \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{25.10 - 24.90}{6 \times 0.020} = \frac{0.20}{0.12} \approx 1.667 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Process Capability Index (Cpk) — accounts for centring $$ \begin{aligned} C_{pk,\ upper} &= \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{25.10 - 25.00}{3 \times 0.020} = \frac{0.10}{0.06} = 1.667 \\[4pt] C_{pk,\ lower} &= \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{25.00 - 24.90}{3 \times 0.020} = \frac{0.10}{0.06} = 1.667 \\[4pt] C_{pk} &= \min(1.667,\ 1.667) = 1.667 \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ → **Lean Six Sigma "Capable"** process. $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ → **Highly capable / Six Sigma world-class**. Our process is exceptional. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Formula | Scenario in Lean | Correct Formula | Reason | |---|---|---| | All process data available (Census) | Population SD ($\sigma$, divide by $N$) | No estimation needed | | Sample drawn for SPC | Sample SD ($s$, divide by $n-1$) | Unbiased estimator | | R-chart with sub-group ranges | $\hat{\sigma} = \bar{R}/d_2$ | Quick on-line estimate | | X-bar chart from sub-groups | $A_3 \bar{R}$ or $3\hat{\sigma}$ | Shewhart limits | | Process centred at target | $C_{pk} = C_p$ | Perfect centring | | Process off-centre | $C_{pk} < C_p$ | Centring loss | | Non-normal distribution | Use Median, IQR, or transform | Empirical Rule fails | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Standard Deviation in a Lean Quality System ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input Data from Process
xi observations] --> B[Step 1 Compute Mean
mu or xbar] B --> C[Step 2 Deviations
xi minus mu] C --> D[Step 3 Square Deviations
xi minus mu squared] D --> E[Step 4 Sum of Squares
SS equals sum of squared deviations] E --> F{Data Type?} F -- Population --> G[Divide by N
Variance equals SS over N] F -- Sample --> H[Divide by n minus 1
Variance equals SS over n minus 1] G --> I[Step 5 Square Root
sigma or s] H --> I I --> J[Standard Deviation Output] J --> K[Lean Tool 1 SPC Control Charts
Limits at mu plus or minus 3 sigma] J --> L[Lean Tool 2 Six Sigma
Defect Rate Mapping] J --> M[Lean Tool 3 Process Capability
Cp and Cpk Indices] J --> N[Lean Tool 4 Kaizen Trigger
Special Cause Identification] K --> O[Continuous Improvement Loop
Reduce sigma shrink waste] L --> O M --> O N --> O ``` ## 4.2 Functional Flow — Empirical Rule and Defect Mapping ```mermaid flowchart LR P[Process Mean mu] --> Q[Within 1 sigma
68.27 percent] P --> R[Within 2 sigma
95.45 percent] P --> S[Within 3 sigma
99.73 percent] P --> T[Beyond 3 sigma
0.27 percent Defects] Q --> U[Common Cause
Inherent Variation] R --> U S --> U T --> V[Special Cause
Investigate via 5 Why] U --> W[Lean Action
Standardise and Reduce] V --> X[Lean Action
Root Cause Elimination] W --> Y[Lower sigma Better Quality] X --> Y ``` ## 4.3 Schematic — Population vs. Sample SD Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD start[You have collected data] --> q1{Is data every
unit produced?} q1 -- Yes Census --> pop[Use Population SD
sigma equals sqrt SS over N] q1 -- No Sample --> samp[Use Sample SD
s equals sqrt SS over n minus 1] pop --> appl1[Apply to Six Sigma
True process sigma] samp --> appl2[Apply to SPC Control Charts
Unbiased estimate of sigma] appl1 --> master[Feed into Cp Cpk
Process Capability Study] appl2 --> master master --> lean[Lean Decision
Accept Improve or Reject] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set A]** **Q1.** Define Standard Deviation. Distinguish between population standard deviation (σ) and sample standard deviation (s). Why is $(n-1)$ used in the denominator of the sample formula? **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** Standard Deviation is the positive square root of the mean of squared deviations of observations from their mean. It measures the dispersion of data about the central value. - **Distinction (1 mark):** Population SD (σ) uses every member of the population and divides by $N$. Sample SD ($s$) is computed from a subset and divides by $n-1$. - **Bessel's Correction (1 mark):** The $(n-1)$ denominator removes the bias caused by using the sample mean $\bar{x}$ (an estimate) instead of the true $\mu$. It provides an unbiased estimator of the population variance. --- > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]** **Q2.** State the Empirical (68-95-99.7) Rule for a normal distribution. Mention its significance in a Six Sigma quality environment. **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Statement (2 marks):** For a normal (bell-shaped) distribution: approximately 68.27% of data lies within $\mu \pm 1\sigma$; 95.45% within $\mu \pm 2\sigma$; 99.73% within $\mu \pm 3\sigma$. - **Six Sigma Significance (1 mark):** A Six Sigma process places specification limits at $\pm 6\sigma$ from the mean, so that even if the mean shifts by 1.5σ, defect rate is only 3.4 DPMO — defining world-class Lean quality. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **(a)** The following data represent the waiting time (in minutes) of 10 customers at a Lean service cell: $$8,\ 9,\ 7,\ 10,\ 8,\ 11,\ 6,\ 9,\ 8,\ 7$$ Compute the **mean**, **variance**, and **standard deviation** of the dataset. **Comment on the spread** with respect to the mean. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the role of Standard Deviation in **Statistical Process Control (SPC)**. With a neat sketch, describe the **X-bar control chart** and explain how $\pm 3\sigma$ control limits are derived using the range-based estimate $\hat{\sigma} = \bar{R}/d_2$. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Question A **(a) Computation (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Mean:** $$ \begin{aligned} \bar{x} &= \frac{8 + 9 + 7 + 10 + 8 + 11 + 6 + 9 + 8 + 7}{10} \\[4pt] &= \frac{83}{10} = 8.3\ \text{minutes} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Deviations and Squared Deviations:** | $x_i$ | $x_i - \bar{x}$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:| | 8 | $-0.3$ | $0.09$ | | 9 | $+0.7$ | $0.49$ | | 7 | $-1.3$ | $1.69$ | | 10 | $+1.7$ | $2.89$ | | 8 | $-0.3$ | $0.09$ | | 11 | $+2.7$ | $7.29$ | | 6 | $-2.3$ | $5.29$ | | 9 | $+0.7$ | $0.49$ | | 8 | $-0.3$ | $0.09$ | | 7 | $-1.3$ | $1.69$ | | **Sum** | **0.0** ✓ | **20.10** | **Step 3 — Variance (treating as sample, $n=10$):** $$ \begin{aligned} s^2 &= \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1} = \frac{20.10}{9} \approx 2.233\ \text{min}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Standard Deviation:** $$ \begin{aligned} s &= \sqrt{2.233} \approx 1.494\ \text{minutes} \end{aligned} $$ > **Comment (1 mark within part a):** The mean wait time is 8.3 min, and σ ≈ 1.49 min. The coefficient of variation $CV = \sigma/\bar{x} = 1.494/8.3 \approx 18\%$ indicates moderate variation — a Lean improvement opportunity (e.g., value-stream redesign). --- **(b) Role of SD in SPC and X-bar Chart (7 marks):** **Role of SD in SPC (3 marks):** 1. **Quantifies common-cause variation:** Shewhart distinguished *common cause* (inherent, statistical) from *special cause* (assignable, signal). SD measures the former. 2. **Sets control limits:** Control limits on X-bar and R charts are at $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ — these define whether a process is *in control*. 3. **Triggers improvement:** Falling within limits → standardise. Falling outside → investigate via 5-Why, Ishikawa. **X-bar Chart Sketch and Limit Derivation (4 marks):** The X-bar chart plots sub-group means over time with three horizontal lines: **UCL (Upper Control Limit)**, **Centre Line (CL) = $\bar{\bar{x}}$**, and **LCL (Lower Control Limit)**. **Range-based SD estimate:** For sub-group size $n$, the standard deviation is estimated as $$ \begin{aligned} \hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} \end{aligned} $$ where $\bar{R}$ is the average range of sub-groups and $d_2$ is a tabulated constant (e.g., $d_2 = 2.059$ for $n = 4$). **Control Limits:** $$ \begin{aligned} UCL_{\bar{x}} &= \bar{\bar{x}} + 3 \cdot \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \bar{\bar{x}} + A_2 \bar{R} \\[4pt] LCL_{\bar{x}} &= \bar{\bar{x}} - 3 \cdot \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \bar{\bar{x}} - A_2 \bar{R} \end{aligned} $$ where $A_2 = 3/d_2$ is the X-bar chart factor. A point outside the limits indicates a **special-cause variation** requiring Lean intervention. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget the $3\sigma$ multiplier, confuse $A_2$ (for X-bar) with $D_3/D_4$ (for R chart), or omit Bessel's correction when computing sample SD. **Always state the sample size $n$ and the table of $d_2$ values you are using.** --- > ### Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a)** Define **Six Sigma** quality level. A manufacturing process produces bearings with diameter normally distributed, mean $\mu = 20.00$ mm and standard deviation $\sigma = 0.05$ mm. Specification limits are USL = 20.15 mm and LSL = 19.85 mm. Calculate the **process capability indices $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$** and comment on the process capability. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With the help of a flow diagram, explain how Standard Deviation is used in the **DMAIC** methodology of Lean Six Sigma. List the statistical tools used at each phase that involve σ. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Question B **(a) Six Sigma and Process Capability (7 marks):** **Six Sigma Definition (1 mark):** Six Sigma is a data-driven quality philosophy aiming for **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**. It is achieved when specification limits are placed at $\pm 6\sigma$ from the process mean (allowing for a 1.5σ long-term mean shift). Standard Deviation is the central metric. **Step 1 — $C_p$ Calculation (Process Spread vs Specification Spread):** $$ \begin{aligned} C_p &= \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{20.15 - 19.85}{6 \times 0.05} \\[4pt] &= \frac{0.30}{0.30} = 1.000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — $C_{pk}$ Calculation (incorporating centring):** $$ \begin{aligned} C_{pk,\ upper} &= \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{20.15 - 20.00}{3 \times 0.05} = \frac{0.15}{0.15} = 1.000 \\[4pt] C_{pk,\ lower} &= \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{20.00 - 19.85}{3 \times 0.05} = \frac{0.15}{0.15} = 1.000 \\[4pt] C_{pk} &= \min(1.000,\ 1.000) = 1.000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Interpretation (2 marks):** - $C_p = C_{pk} = 1.000$ → Process is **on target** and just **minimally capable** (industry minimum acceptable). - The process meets the **3σ** level (99.73% within specs), but is **far from Six Sigma** ($C_{pk} = 2.0$ needed for Six Sigma world-class). - **Lean Recommendation:** Reduce σ to **0.025 mm** (i.e., halve variation) to achieve $C_{pk} = 2.0$ and enter the Six Sigma regime. --- **(b) DMAIC Methodology and the Role of SD (7 marks):** **DMAIC Flow Diagram (3 marks):** ```mermaid flowchart LR d1[Define
CTQ and Problem] --> d2[Measure
Collect Data and Compute sigma] --> d3[Analyze
Identify Variation Sources] --> d4[Improve
Reduce sigma] --> d5[Control
Sustain via Control Charts] --> d1 ``` **Statistical Tools Involving σ in Each Phase (4 marks):** | DMAIC Phase | Statistical Tool | Role of σ | |---|---|---| | **Define** | Project Charter, SIPOC | Voice of Customer sets tolerance $\rightarrow$ target σ | | **Measure** | Histogram, Run Chart, Gage R&R | Initial estimate of σ to baseline process | | **Analyze** | Pareto, Cause-and-Effect, Hypothesis tests | Decompose σ into common-cause and special-cause | | **Improve** | DOE, Regression, Kaizen | Action to **shrink σ**; target $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ | | **Control** | X-bar R chart, p-chart, FMEA | Monitor σ to prevent regression | > **Key Insight:** Standard Deviation is the *single thread* that runs through all five DMAIC phases. Lean Six Sigma's central pursuit is the **systematic reduction of σ** to compress variation and eliminate waste. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write $C_p$ but forget to interpret it. Marks are awarded for: (i) showing the formula, (ii) the numerical answer, and (iii) **the qualitative judgement** (capable / not capable / Six Sigma / 3σ level). Skipping (iii) costs 2-3 marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > ### Rapid-Revision Checklist — Standard Deviation - **Definition:** σ (or s) = √(average of squared deviations from the mean). Quantifies **dispersion**. - **Two flavours:** **Population SD** → divide by $N$. **Sample SD** → divide by $(n-1)$ (Bessel's correction for unbiasedness). - **Computation recipe:** Mean → Deviations → Square → Sum → Average → Square Root. **Five-step chain.** - **Always verify:** $\sum (x_i - \bar{x}) = 0$ — a quick self-check. - **Empirical Rule (Normal):** 68% within 1σ, 95% within 2σ, 99.73% within 3σ. Foundation of **Shewhart 3σ control limits**. - **Range estimate:** $\hat{\sigma} = \bar{R}/d_2$ — used to quickly build X-bar and R charts in SPC. - **Process Capability:** $C_p = (USL-LSL)/(6\sigma)$; $C_{pk}$ adds centring. Benchmarks: $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ (capable), $\geq 1.67$ (Six Sigma). - **Lean Significance:** Reducing σ is the statistical equivalent of **Muda (waste) elimination**. Smaller σ → tighter tolerances → fewer defects → JIT stability → customer satisfaction. - **DMAIC:** σ is the unifying metric across Define → Measure → Analyze → Improve → Control. - **Six Sigma:** Specs at ±6σ → 3.4 DPMO after 1.5σ shift allowance. - **Common KTU Errors:** (1) Dividing sample SD by $n$ instead of $n-1$. (2) Forgetting the square root (giving variance, not SD). (3) Mixing up $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$. (4) Not stating units in the final answer. (5) Misapplying the Empirical Rule to non-normal data. ---

topics.title: Normal Distribution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET746 - LEAN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Normal Distribution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Normal Distribution** (also known as the **Gaussian Distribution** or **Laplace-Gauss Distribution**) is a continuous probability distribution that is symmetrical about its mean, depicting that data near the mean are more frequent in occurrence than data far from the mean. In the context of Lean Manufacturing and Statistical Process Control (SPC), it is the foundational probability model used to characterize the natural variability of in-control processes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A random variable $X$ is said to follow a Normal Distribution with parameters $\mu$ (mean) and $\sigma^2$ (variance) if its probability density function (PDF) is given by the bell-shaped curve. It is denoted as $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$.

Mathematically, a continuous random variable $X$ follows a Normal Distribution if its PDF is:

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}, \quad -\infty < x < +\infty$$

Where:
- $\mu$ = **Mean** of the distribution (location parameter, determines the center)
- $\sigma^2$ = **Variance** of the distribution (scale parameter, determines the spread)
- $\sigma$ = **Standard Deviation**
- $\pi \approx 3.14159$ and $e \approx 2.71828$ are mathematical constants
- The factor $\frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}$ is the **normalization constant** that ensures the total area under the curve equals **1**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Coffee Shop Queue"**
>
> Imagine you are observing the waiting times of customers at a coffee shop during morning rush hour.
> - Most customers wait around **3 minutes** (the mean).
> - A few customers get lucky and wait only **1 minute** (left tail).
> - A few unlucky ones wait **6 minutes** (right tail).
> - Very rarely does anyone wait **10 minutes** (extreme tail).
>
> If you plot the frequency of waiting times, you get a **bell-shaped curve** — short on the extremes, tall in the middle. This is exactly what the Normal Distribution looks like.

In Lean Manufacturing, every machine has a **natural variation**. When you measure a critical dimension (like the diameter of a shaft) thousands of times:
- Most measurements cluster tightly around the target value.
- Very few measurements deviate dramatically.
- The resulting histogram forms a beautiful bell curve.

This is the cornerstone of **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** — understanding "voice of the process" through the Normal Distribution.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Parameters (Bolded for emphasis):**
> - **Mean ($\mu$)**: Central tendency — measured in the same unit as $X$.
> - **Standard Deviation ($\sigma$)**: Spread — measured in the same unit as $X$.
> - **Variance ($\sigma^2$)**: Squared spread — measured in squared units.
> - **Skewness = 0**: Perfect symmetry about the mean.
> - **Kurtosis = 3**: Mesokurtic (normal peakedness).
> - **Total Area under the curve = 1** (since it is a probability distribution).
> - **Mode = Median = Mean = $\mu$** (all three measures of central tendency coincide).

---

## 1.4 Historical Context (Background)

The Normal Distribution was first introduced by **Abraham de Moivre** in 1733 as an approximation to the binomial distribution. It was later formalized by **Pierre-Simon Laplace** and independently by **Carl Friedrich Gauss** (who used it to analyze astronomical data). This is why it carries the dual name "Gaussian Distribution" in engineering literature.

> [!NOTE]
> In Six Sigma and Lean contexts, the Normal Distribution is the mathematical backbone of the **DMAIC** methodology (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control), particularly during the "Measure" and "Analyze" phases where process capability indices like $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ are calculated assuming normality.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Properties of the Normal Distribution

The Normal Distribution exhibits a unique set of mathematical properties that make it the most important distribution in statistical quality control. Let us break these down into structured logic steps:

### Property 1: Symmetry
- The curve is **perfectly symmetric** about the vertical line $x = \mu$.
- This means $f(\mu + k) = f(\mu - k)$ for any constant $k$.
- **Why?** Because the exponent $(x-\mu)^2$ is symmetric around $\mu$.

### Property 2: Bell Shape (Unimodal)
- The curve has **exactly one peak** at $x = \mu$.
- This peak value is the maximum of the PDF: $f(\mu) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}$.
- **Why?** The exponential function decays as $(x-\mu)^2$ increases, but the normalization constant ensures the curve never touches zero.

### Property 3: Asymptotic Behavior
- The tails of the curve **approach but never touch** the horizontal axis.
- As $x \to \pm\infty$, $f(x) \to 0$.
- **Why?** The exponential term dominates the polynomial denominator in the limit.

### Property 4: Inflection Points
- The curve changes concavity at $x = \mu - \sigma$ and $x = \mu + \sigma$.
- Between these two points, the curve is **concave down** (shaped like a frown).
- Outside these points, the curve is **concave up** (shaped like a smile).

### Property 5: Total Area = 1
- $\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} f(x) \, dx = 1$
- This is guaranteed by the normalization constant $\frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}$.

### Property 6: Mean, Median, and Mode Coincide
- For a Normal Distribution, Mean = Median = Mode = $\mu$.
- This is a direct consequence of symmetry and unimodality.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all high-yield formulas** you need for KTU examinations on Normal Distribution. Memorize these before attempting any problem.

| **Formula / Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Description / Use Case** |
|---|---|---|
| PDF of Normal Distribution | $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}$ | Probability Density Function |
| CDF (Cumulative) | $F(x) = P(X \leq x) = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t) \, dt$ | No closed-form elementary expression |
| Standardization (Z-score) | $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Converts $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ to $Z \sim N(0,1)$ |
| Standard Normal PDF | $\phi(z) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-\frac{z^2}{2}}$ | PDF of $Z$ when $\mu=0, \sigma=1$ |
| Standard Normal CDF | $\Phi(z) = P(Z \leq z)$ | Tabulated in KTU statistical tables |
| Empirical Rule (68%) | $P(\mu - \sigma < X < \mu + \sigma) \approx 0.6827$ | Within 1 standard deviation |
| Empirical Rule (95%) | $P(\mu - 2\sigma < X < \mu + 2\sigma) \approx 0.9545$ | Within 2 standard deviations |
| Empirical Rule (99.7%) | $P(\mu - 3\sigma < X < \mu + 3\sigma) \approx 0.9973$ | Within 3 standard deviations |
| Symmetry Property | $\Phi(-z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$ | Critical for tail calculations |
| Linear Transformation | If $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$, then $aX + b \sim N(a\mu+b, a^2\sigma^2)$ | Used in Six Sigma scaling |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL PITFALL:** When using the Z-score formula, always remember:
> - $Z = 0$ corresponds to the mean.
> - Positive $Z$ → right tail (above mean).
> - Negative $Z$ → left tail (below mean).
> - Never confuse the population parameters ($\mu, \sigma$) with sample statistics ($\bar{x}, s$).

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

The Normal Distribution is not just a textbook concept — it is the **workhorse** of industrial statistics. Key applications include:

1. **Statistical Process Control (SPC):** Control charts (X-bar, R-charts) rely on the assumption that process variation follows a normal distribution. Control limits are typically set at $\mu \pm 3\sigma$.

2. **Six Sigma Quality:** The goal of Six Sigma is to reduce defects to **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**. This corresponds to a process operating within $\pm 6\sigma$ of the target, with $\pm 1.5\sigma$ shift accounted for.

3. **Process Capability Indices:**
   - $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ — measures process capability.
   - $C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ — measures process centering.

4. **Measurement System Analysis (MSA):** Gage Repeatability and Reproducibility (GR&R) studies assume normal distribution of measurement errors.

5. **Tolerance Stack-Up Analysis:** When many small independent tolerances add up, the resulting distribution approaches a normal distribution by the **Central Limit Theorem**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Normal Distribution PDF

The Normal Distribution can be derived from multiple foundational principles. We will use the **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)** approach, which is the most intuitive and KTU-relevant.

### Step 1: Start with a Random Error Model
Consider a physical measurement $X$ that is subject to many small, independent, random errors. Let each error be denoted $E_i$, so:

$$X = \mu + E_1 + E_2 + E_3 + \cdots + E_n$$

Where $\mu$ is the true value and each $E_i$ is an independent random error with:
- $E(E_i) = 0$ (errors average out to zero)
- $\text{Var}(E_i) = \sigma_i^2$ (each has finite variance)

### Step 2: Apply the Central Limit Theorem
As $n \to \infty$, the sum $S_n = E_1 + E_2 + \cdots + E_n$ approaches a normal distribution with mean 0 and variance $\sigma^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma_i^2$.

$$S_n \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$$

### Step 3: Write the Limiting Form
The probability density of $S_n$ as $n \to \infty$ becomes:

$$f(s) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-\frac{s^2}{2\sigma^2}}$$

### Step 4: Substitute Back $X = \mu + S_n$
Since $X - \mu = S_n$, we replace $s$ with $x - \mu$:

$$\boxed{f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}}$$

This is the complete derivation of the Normal PDF from first principles.

---

## 3.2 Verification of Total Area = 1

To confirm the PDF is properly normalized, we need to show:

$$I = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}} \, dx = 1$$

### Step 1: Substitution
Let $u = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$, so $du = \dfrac{1}{\sigma} \, dx$, hence $dx = \sigma \, du$.

When $x = -\infty$, $u = -\infty$. When $x = +\infty$, $u = +\infty$.

$$I = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-\frac{u^2}{2}} \cdot \sigma \, du$$

### Step 2: Simplify
The $\sigma$ cancels out:

$$I = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} e^{-\frac{u^2}{2}} \, du$$

### Step 3: Use the Gaussian Integral Identity
The famous Gaussian integral evaluates to:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} e^{-\frac{u^2}{2}} \, du = \sqrt{2\pi}$$

### Step 4: Conclude
Substituting back:

$$I = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{2\pi} = 1 \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Maximum Value of the PDF

The maximum of $f(x)$ occurs at $x = \mu$. Let us derive this rigorously.

### Step 1: Differentiate the PDF
Using the chain rule on the exponential:

$$\frac{df}{dx} = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \cdot e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}} \cdot \left(-\frac{2(x-\mu)}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

### Step 2: Simplify
$$\frac{df}{dx} = -\frac{(x-\mu)}{\sigma^3\sqrt{2\pi}} \cdot e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}$$

### Step 3: Set to Zero
Setting $\dfrac{df}{dx} = 0$:

$$-\frac{(x-\mu)}{\sigma^3\sqrt{2\pi}} \cdot e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}} = 0$$

Since the exponential term is never zero, we must have:

$$x - \mu = 0 \implies x = \mu$$

### Step 4: Compute the Maximum Value
Substituting $x = \mu$ into the PDF:

$$f(\mu) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \cdot e^{0} = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}$$

So the peak height of the bell curve is $\dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}$. Note that as $\sigma$ increases, the peak decreases (the curve becomes flatter and wider).

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Inflection Points

The inflection points occur where $\dfrac{d^2f}{dx^2} = 0$.

### Step 1: Second Derivative
Differentiating $\dfrac{df}{dx}$ again and setting it to zero (after algebraic manipulation), we obtain:

$$\frac{d^2f}{dx^2} = 0 \implies (x-\mu)^2 = \sigma^2$$

### Step 2: Solve
Taking square roots:

$$x - \mu = \pm\sigma \implies x = \mu \pm \sigma$$

### Step 3: Interpretation
The curve changes concavity at $x = \mu - \sigma$ and $x = \mu + \sigma$. Between these points, the curve is concave down (frown shape); outside, it is concave up (smile shape). This is critical for sketching the bell curve in KTU examinations.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: PDF for Various Parameter Values

> [!NOTE]
> The following analytical matrix helps you visualize how the Normal Distribution changes with parameters.

| **Case** | **$\mu$** | **$\sigma$** | **Peak Height $\frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}$** | **Behavior** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Standard Normal | 0 | 1 | $\approx 0.3989$ | Reference case |
| Wider Spread | 0 | 2 | $\approx 0.1995$ | Flatter, wider curve |
| Narrower Spread | 0 | 0.5 | $\approx 0.7979$ | Taller, narrower curve |
| Shifted Right | 5 | 1 | $\approx 0.3989$ | Same shape, shifted by 5 units |
| Shifted Left | $-3$ | 1 | $\approx 0.3989$ | Same shape, shifted left by 3 units |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Changing $\mu$ shifts the curve horizontally (no shape change). Changing $\sigma$ alters the spread (taller peak means smaller $\sigma$, wider curve means larger $\sigma$).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define the Normal Distribution and state any four of its properties.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Normal Distribution is a continuous probability distribution defined by the probability density function:

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}, \quad -\infty < x < +\infty$$

**Four Properties (½ mark each):**
1. **Symmetry:** The curve is perfectly symmetric about the mean $\mu$.
2. **Bell-shaped / Unimodal:** It has a single peak at $x = \mu$ with maximum value $\frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}$.
3. **Mean = Median = Mode:** All three measures of central tendency coincide at $\mu$.
4. **Total Area = 1:** The area under the curve over the entire real line equals 1, making it a valid probability distribution.
5. **Empirical Rule:** Approximately 68.27% of data lies within $\mu \pm \sigma$, 95.45% within $\mu \pm 2\sigma$, and 99.73% within $\mu \pm 3\sigma$.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**What is the Standard Normal Distribution? Write its PDF and explain the Z-score transformation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Standard Normal Distribution is a special case of the Normal Distribution with **mean $\mu = 0$** and **standard deviation $\sigma = 1$**. It is denoted $Z \sim N(0, 1)$.

**PDF of Standard Normal (1 Mark):**

$$\phi(z) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-\frac{z^2}{2}}, \quad -\infty < z < +\infty$$

**Z-Score Transformation (1 Mark):**
Any normal random variable $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ can be transformed to the standard normal using:

$$Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$$

After transformation, $Z \sim N(0, 1)$. This allows probability calculations using standard normal tables.

**Significance (1 Mark):**
The Z-score measures how many standard deviations $X$ is from the mean. It enables comparison across different normal distributions and simplifies table lookups for probability calculations.

---

## PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, Part B questions carry **internal choice**. Solve **either Question A or Question B**. Each sub-part is worth **7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

#### Part (a) – 7 Marks
**The diameters of shafts produced by a CNC machine follow a Normal Distribution with mean $\mu = 50$ mm and standard deviation $\sigma = 0.5$ mm. The specification limits are $LSL = 49$ mm and $USL = 51$ mm.**

**Calculate:**
**(i)** The probability that a randomly selected shaft has a diameter between 49 mm and 51 mm. **(3 Marks)**
**(ii)** The probability that a shaft diameter exceeds 50.7 mm. **(2 Marks)**
**(iii)** The process capability index $C_p$. **(2 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $\mu = 50$ mm, $\sigma = 0.5$ mm
- $LSL = 49$ mm, $USL = 51$ mm

**(i) Probability between 49 mm and 51 mm (3 Marks):**

Step 1 — Standardize the lower limit:

$$Z_1 = \frac{LSL - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{49 - 50}{0.5} = \frac{-1}{0.5} = -2.00$$

Step 2 — Standardize the upper limit:

$$Z_2 = \frac{USL - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{51 - 50}{0.5} = \frac{1}{0.5} = +2.00$$

Step 3 — Compute the probability using standard normal tables:

$$P(49 < X < 51) = P(-2.00 < Z < +2.00) = \Phi(2.00) - \Phi(-2.00)$$

Using the symmetry property $\Phi(-z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$:

$$P = \Phi(2.00) - [1 - \Phi(2.00)] = 2\Phi(2.00) - 1$$

From standard normal tables: $\Phi(2.00) = 0.9772$

$$P = 2(0.9772) - 1 = 1.9544 - 1 = 0.9544$$

$$\boxed{P(49 < X < 51) = 0.9544 \text{ or } 95.44\%}$$

This matches the **Empirical Rule** for $\pm 2\sigma$.

---

**(ii) Probability that diameter exceeds 50.7 mm (2 Marks):**

Step 1 — Standardize:

$$Z = \frac{50.7 - 50}{0.5} = \frac{0.7}{0.5} = +1.40$$

Step 2 — Compute the right-tail probability:

$$P(X > 50.7) = P(Z > 1.40) = 1 - \Phi(1.40)$$

From tables: $\Phi(1.40) = 0.9192$

$$P(X > 50.7) = 1 - 0.9192 = 0.0808$$

$$\boxed{P(X > 50.7) = 0.0808 \text{ or } 8.08\%}$$

---

**(iii) Process Capability Index $C_p$ (2 Marks):**

$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{51 - 49}{6 \times 0.5} = \frac{2}{3} = 0.667$$

$$\boxed{C_p = 0.667}$$

**Interpretation:** Since $C_p < 1.00$, the process is **not capable** — the natural variation ($\pm 3\sigma = \pm 1.5$ mm) exceeds the specification width (2 mm). This signals a need for process improvement.

---

#### Part (b) – 7 Marks
**A Six Sigma project measures the viscosity of a lubricant. The data follow a Normal Distribution with $\mu = 100$ cP and $\sigma = 4$ cP.**

**Find:**
**(i)** The values that bound the central 95% of the distribution. **(3 Marks)**
**(ii)** If the upper specification limit is $USL = 108$ cP, calculate the **defect rate in parts per million (PPM)**. **(4 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $\mu = 100$ cP, $\sigma = 4$ cP

**(i) Central 95% bounds (3 Marks):**

The central 95% corresponds to $Z = \pm 1.96$ (since $\Phi(1.96) = 0.975$).

Step 1 — Lower bound:

$$X_{\text{lower}} = \mu - 1.96\sigma = 100 - 1.96(4) = 100 - 7.84 = 92.16 \text{ cP}$$

Step 2 — Upper bound:

$$X_{\text{upper}} = \mu + 1.96\sigma = 100 + 1.96(4) = 100 + 7.84 = 107.84 \text{ cP}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Central 95% lies between } 92.16 \text{ cP and } 107.84 \text{ cP}}$$

---

**(ii) Defect Rate in PPM (4 Marks):**

Step 1 — Standardize the USL:

$$Z_{USL} = \frac{USL - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{108 - 100}{4} = \frac{8}{4} = +2.00$$

Step 2 — Calculate the probability of exceeding USL:

$$P(X > 108) = P(Z > 2.00) = 1 - \Phi(2.00)$$

From tables: $\Phi(2.00) = 0.9772$

$$P(X > 108) = 1 - 0.9772 = 0.0228$$

Step 3 — Convert to PPM:

$$\text{Defect Rate} = 0.0228 \times 1{,}000{,}000 = 22{,}800 \text{ PPM}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Defect Rate} = 22{,}800 \text{ PPM}}$$

**Interpretation:** A defect rate of 22,800 PPM corresponds to roughly **2.28%** defects — this is far from the Six Sigma goal of 3.4 PPM. The process needs significant improvement (e.g., reducing $\sigma$ or re-centering the mean).

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

#### Part (a) – 7 Marks
**The weights of components manufactured in a lean production line follow a Normal Distribution with mean $\mu = 250$ g and variance $\sigma^2 = 9$ g².**

**Determine:**
**(i)** The probability that a component weighs between 247 g and 253 g. **(3 Marks)**
**(ii)** The weight $X$ such that $P(X < x) = 0.90$. **(2 Marks)**
**(iii)** If only 5% of components are allowed to be below a threshold, find that threshold weight. **(2 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $\mu = 250$ g, $\sigma^2 = 9$, hence $\sigma = 3$ g.

**(i) Probability between 247 g and 253 g (3 Marks):**

Step 1 — Standardize:

$$Z_1 = \frac{247 - 250}{3} = -1.00$$

$$Z_2 = \frac{253 - 250}{3} = +1.00$$

Step 2 — Apply symmetry:

$$P(247 < X < 253) = P(-1 < Z < +1) = 2\Phi(1.00) - 1$$

From tables: $\Phi(1.00) = 0.8413$

$$P = 2(0.8413) - 1 = 1.6826 - 1 = 0.6826$$

$$\boxed{P(247 < X < 253) = 0.6826 \text{ or } 68.26\%}$$

This confirms the **Empirical Rule for $\pm 1\sigma$**.

---

**(ii) Find $X$ such that $P(X < x) = 0.90$ (2 Marks):**

Step 1 — From the standard normal table, find $z$ such that $\Phi(z) = 0.90$:

$$z = 1.28 \quad (\text{since } \Phi(1.28) = 0.8997 \approx 0.90)$$

Step 2 — Reverse the standardization:

$$x = \mu + z\sigma = 250 + 1.28(3) = 250 + 3.84 = 253.84 \text{ g}$$

$$\boxed{x = 253.84 \text{ g}}$$

---

**(iii) Threshold below which 5% of components lie (2 Marks):**

Step 1 — Find $z$ such that $\Phi(z) = 0.05$:

$$z = -1.645 \quad (\text{since } \Phi(-1.645) = 0.05)$$

Step 2 — Reverse the standardization:

$$x = \mu + z\sigma = 250 + (-1.645)(3) = 250 - 4.935 = 245.065 \text{ g}$$

$$\boxed{x = 245.065 \text{ g}}$$

**Interpretation:** 5% of components weigh less than 245.065 g. In a lean system, this threshold could be used for **poka-yoke** (error-proofing) — components below this weight are rejected automatically.

---

#### Part (b) – 7 Marks
**In a manufacturing cell, the cycle time of an operation follows a Normal Distribution with mean $\mu = 45$ seconds and standard deviation $\sigma = 3$ seconds. The takt time is 50 seconds.**

**Calculate:**
**(i)** The probability that the cycle time exceeds the takt time. **(3 Marks)**
**(ii)** The probability that the cycle time is within $\pm 5$ seconds of the mean. **(2 Marks)**
**(iii)** Comment on the lean implications if the defect probability in (i) is considered the rate of missed takt time. **(2 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $\mu = 45$ s, $\sigma = 3$ s, Takt Time = 50 s.

**(i) Probability of exceeding takt time (3 Marks):**

Step 1 — Standardize:

$$Z = \frac{50 - 45}{3} = \frac{5}{3} = +1.667$$

Step 2 — Calculate tail probability:

$$P(X > 50) = P(Z > 1.667) = 1 - \Phi(1.667)$$

From tables: $\Phi(1.67) \approx 0.9525$

$$P(X > 50) = 1 - 0.9525 = 0.0475$$

$$\boxed{P(X > 50) = 0.0475 \text{ or } 4.75\%}$$

---

**(ii) Probability within $\pm 5$ seconds of the mean (2 Marks):**

Step 1 — Bounds: Lower = $45 - 5 = 40$ s, Upper = $45 + 5 = 50$ s.

Step 2 — Standardize:

$$Z_{\text{lower}} = \frac{40 - 45}{3} = -\frac{5}{3} = -1.667$$

$$Z_{\text{upper}} = \frac{50 - 45}{3} = +1.667$$

Step 3 — Compute:

$$P(40 < X < 50) = P(-1.667 < Z < +1.667) = 2\Phi(1.667) - 1$$

$$P = 2(0.9525) - 1 = 0.9050$$

$$\boxed{P(40 < X < 50) = 0.9050 \text{ or } 90.50\%}$$

---

**(iii) Lean Implications (2 Marks):**

A 4.75% miss rate against takt time indicates that the process is **borderline capable**. Lean implications:

1. **Cycle Time Reduction:** Although mean is 45 s, the tail risk causes **inventory pile-ups** at downstream stations. Implement **Kaizen** to reduce $\mu$.

2. **Variation Reduction:** The standard deviation $\sigma = 3$ s is too high. Use **5S and standardized work** to reduce $\sigma$, making the process more predictable.

3. **Buffer Strategy:** Even with improvements, a small contingency buffer (e.g., **10% safety stock**) may be needed to absorb the 4.75% tail risk.

4. **Heijunka:** Implement **production leveling** to smooth demand, reducing the impact of cycle time variability on takt adherence.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $\sigma$ and $\sigma^2$ in Z-score formula:** The Z-score uses **standard deviation $\sigma$**, NOT variance $\sigma^2$. Using $\sigma^2 = 9$ instead of $\sigma = 3$ gives incorrect answers. **(Lose 2-3 marks)**
>
> 2. **Wrong direction of inequality:** Always double-check whether the question asks for $P(X > x)$, $P(X < x)$, or $P(a < X < b)$. Many students flip the inequality and use $\Phi$ instead of $1 - \Phi$. **(Lose 2 marks)**
>
> 3. **Forgetting symmetry property:** When calculating $P(Z < -z)$, use $\Phi(-z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$. Do NOT look up $\Phi(-z)$ directly in most tables. **(Lose 1-2 marks)**
>
> 4. **Incorrect process capability formula:** $C_p$ uses $6\sigma$ in the denominator (full $\pm 3\sigma$ spread), NOT $3\sigma$ or $4\sigma$. **(Lose 2 marks)**
>
> 5. **Unit mismatch:** Ensure that $\mu$, $\sigma$, and specifications are all in the same units (e.g., all in mm or all in seconds). **(Lose 1 mark)**
>
> 6. **Not interpreting results:** KTU examiners award marks for **practical interpretation**. Always end your solution with a sentence explaining what the numerical result means in a manufacturing/quality context.

---
