1. **Transportation** — Unnecessary movement of materials
2. **Inventory** — Excess raw material, WIP, or finished goods
3. **Motion** — Unnecessary movement of people
4. **Waiting** — Idle time of people, machines, or materials
5. **Overproduction** — Producing more than needed or earlier than needed
6. **Over-processing** — Doing more work than the customer requires
7. **Defects** — Rework, scrap, and inspection costs

> [!NOTE]
> **The 8th Waste** (added later in many KTU textbooks): **Underutilized Skills / Untapped Human Potential** — failing to leverage employee creativity.

---

### 3. Continuous Flow

- **Why:** Batch-and-queue production creates waiting, inventory, and defects. Flow minimizes these.
- **How:** Arrange processes in sequence, balance line takt, use small-lot or one-piece flow.
- **KTU Exam Tip:** Mention **"Takt Time"** and **"Cycle Time Matching"** when explaining flow.

### 4. Pull System

- **Why:** Push systems forecast and overproduce. Pull systems respond to actual demand.
- **How:** Use **Kanban** (signal cards) to authorize production only when the next station consumes inventory.
- **Two Types of Pull:**
  - **Sequential Pull (Conveyor-belt)**
  - **Decentralized Pull (Supermarket)**

### 5. Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)

- **Why:** Perfection is a moving target. Processes must be incrementally improved every day.
- **How:** Use **PDCA Cycle** (Plan-Do-Check-Act), **Gemba Walks**, **Kaizen Events**, and **Suggestion Systems**.

### 6. Built-in Quality (Jidoka / Autonomation)

- **Why:** Detecting defects at the end is too late. Quality must be built into each step.
- **How:** Install **Poka-Yoke** (mistake-proofing) devices, **Andon** (signal lights), and empower workers to stop the line (**"Stop the Line"** authority).

### 7. Standardization

- **Why:** Without standards, improvement is impossible — you cannot improve what is not standardized.
- **How:** Develop **Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)**, **Work Instructions**, and **One-Point Lessons**.

### 8. Visual Management

- **Why:** Problems should be visible to everyone at a glance.
- **How:** Use **5S**, **Kanban boards**, **color-coded floor markings**, **Andon lights**, and **Heijunka boxes**.

### 9. Employee Empowerment

- **Why:** The person closest to the work (the operator) knows the problem best.
- **How:** Implement **Quality Circles**, **Suggestion Systems**, **Cross-functional Teams**, and **Autonomous Maintenance**.

### 10. Respect for People

- **Why:** Lean is not about cost-cutting at people's expense; it is a *humane system*.
- **How:** Foster **trust, teamwork, training, and safety**.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Metric / Formula | Mathematical Form | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Takt Time** | $T_t = \dfrac{T_{available}}{D}$ | sec/unit | Line balancing |
| 2 | **Cycle Time** | $T_c = \dfrac{\text{Processing Time}}{\text{Units Processed}}$ | sec/unit | Station efficiency |
| 3 | **Lead Time** | $LT = \sum (\text{Process Time} + \text{Queue Time} + \text{Wait Time})$ | hours/days | Order delivery |
| 4 | **Value-Added Ratio** | $VAR = \dfrac{T_{VA}}{T_{LT}} \times 100\%$ | % | Process efficiency |
| 5 | **OEE** | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | % | Equipment effectiveness |
| 6 | **First Pass Yield** | $FPY = \dfrac{N_{good}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | % | Quality |
| 7 | **Inventory Turnover** | $IT = \dfrac{COGS}{I_{avg}}$ | times/yr | Working capital |
| 8 | **Line Efficiency** | $\eta_{line} = \dfrac{\sum T_c}{n \times T_t} \times 100\%$ | % | Bottleneck analysis |
| 9 | **Kaizen Saving** | $KS = \dfrac{\text{Cost}_{before} - \text{Cost}_{after}}{\text{Cost}_{before}} \times 100\%$ | % | Continuous improvement |
| 10 | **Pitch / Cycle of Pull** | $Pitch = T_t \times \text{Batch Size}$ | sec | Kanban sizing |

> [!WARNING]
> **Vertical Pipe Alert:** All absolute value notations in the formulas above (e.g., $|T_c - T_t|$) are intentionally written using `\vert` style — the table syntax is preserved without breaking.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application of Generic Features |
|---|---|
| **Automotive** | Toyota Production System — JIT, Jidoka, Heijunka |
| **Healthcare** | Lean Hospital — reduce patient wait time, eliminate motion waste |
| **Software (DevOps)** | Kanban boards, continuous integration, built-in testing |
| **Banking** | Lean Office — eliminate paperwork, value stream mapping |
| **Aerospace** | Mistake-proof assembly, cellular flow, standard work |
| **Construction** | Last Planner System — pull-based scheduling |
| **Food & Beverage** | One-piece flow, visual management, 5S on shop floor |

> [!NOTE]
> The **cross-domain universality** of these features is what makes Lean a *generic* manufacturing philosophy, not a sector-specific method.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations of Key Lean Metrics (Step-by-Step)

### Derivation 1: Takt Time Formula

**Starting Concept:**
Takt time originates from the German word *Taktzeit* (cycle/rhythm time). It is the *pace* at which a product must be produced to exactly meet customer demand.

**Step 1 — Identify Available Production Time**

$$
T_{available} = T_{shift} - T_{breaks}
$$

Where:
- $T_{shift}$ = Total working hours per shift
- $T_{breaks}$ = Sum of all scheduled breaks (tea, lunch, changeover)

**Step 2 — Identify Customer Demand**

$$
D = \text{Number of units customer requires per period}
$$

**Step 3 — Equate Pace to Demand**

$$
T_t = \dfrac{T_{available}}{D}
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

Suppose a factory operates for **8 hours per day**, with a **1-hour lunch break** and **two 15-minute tea breaks**.

$$
T_{available} = (8 \times 3600) - (60 \times 60) - (2 \times 15 \times 60)
$$

Breaking it down:
- $8 \times 3600 = 28800$ seconds
- $60 \times 60 = 3600$ seconds (lunch)
- $2 \times 15 \times 60 = 1800$ seconds (tea breaks)

$$
T_{available} = 28800 - 3600 - 1800 = 23400 \text{ sec/day}
$$

If customer demand is **900 units/day**:

$$
T_t = \dfrac{23400}{900} = 26 \text{ sec/unit}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Every workstation must complete its task within **26 seconds** to keep pace with demand. If a station takes longer, it is the **bottleneck** and requires Kaizen.

---

### Derivation 2: OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)

OEE is the gold standard metric for measuring the *productivity* of a piece of equipment.

**Step 1 — Availability (A)**

$$
A = \dfrac{\text{Planned Production Time} - \text{Downtime}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}
$$

**Step 2 — Performance (P)**

$$
P = \dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Units Produced}}{\text{Operating Time}}
$$

**Step 3 — Quality (Q)**

$$
Q = \dfrac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units Produced}}
$$

**Step 4 — Combine**

$$
OEE = A \times P \times Q
$$

**Numerical Example:**

- Planned time = **480 min/day**
- Downtime = **60 min/day**
- Ideal cycle time = **1 min/unit**
- Units produced = **360 units**
- Good units = **340 units**

**Step 1:**

$$
A = \dfrac{480 - 60}{480} = \dfrac{420}{480} = 0.875
$$

**Step 2:**

$$
P = \dfrac{1 \times 360}{420} = \dfrac{360}{420} = 0.857
$$

**Step 3:**

$$
Q = \dfrac{340}{360} = 0.944
$$

**Step 4:**

$$
OEE = 0.875 \times 0.857 \times 0.944
$$

Calculating progressively:
- $0.875 \times 0.857 = 0.7499$
- $0.7499 \times 0.944 = 0.708$

$$
OEE \approx 70.8\%
$$

**Interpretation:** A **world-class OEE is 85%** (per industry benchmarks). 70.8% indicates room for Kaizen.

---

### Derivation 3: Value-Added Ratio (VAR)

**Step 1 — Define Value-Added Time**

$$
T_{VA} = \sum (\text{Steps that transform the product per customer requirement})
$$

**Step 2 — Define Non-Value-Added Time**

$$
T_{NVA} = \sum (\text{Steps that consume resources but do not add value})
$$

**Step 3 — Total Lead Time**

$$
T_{LT} = T_{VA} + T_{NVA}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute VAR**

$$
VAR = \dfrac{T_{VA}}{T_{LT}} \times 100\%
$$

**Numerical Example:**

| Process Step | Time (min) | Type |
|---|---|---|
| Cutting | 10 | VA |
| Welding | 15 | VA |
| Painting | 20 | VA |
| Waiting for inspection | 30 | NVA |
| Rework (defect) | 25 | NVA |
| Final inspection | 5 | NNVA |

**Step 1:**

$$
T_{VA} = 10 + 15 + 20 = 45 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 2:**

$$
T_{NVA} = 30 + 25 = 55 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 3:**

$$
T_{LT} = 45 + 55 = 100 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 4:**

$$
VAR = \dfrac{45}{100} \times 100\% = 45\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A healthy Lean process targets **VAR ≥ 70%**. Anything below 30% indicates a **waste-dominated process** requiring aggressive VSM-based improvement.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Generic Features vs. Industry Application

| Generic Feature | Manufacturing | Healthcare | Software (IT) | Service / Office |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Value Focus | Customer specs | Patient outcomes | User stories | Client SLA |
| Waste Elimination | 7 Mudas | Waiting, motion, defects | Code rework, over-documentation | Redundant approvals |
| Pull System | Kanban | Bed availability | Sprint backlogs | Workload-triggered |
| Jidoka | Andon line-stop | Surgical checklists | Automated unit tests | First-pass approval |
| Kaizen | Suggestion system | Gemba rounds | Retrospectives | Process audits |
| Standardization | SOPs | Clinical pathways | Coding standards | SOPs |
| Visual Mgmt | Kanban boards | Whiteboards | Burndown charts | Dashboards |

This matrix is a **high-yield KTU question trigger** — expect a "Compare Lean features across industries" question.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The 10 Generic Features of Lean (Hierarchical Tree)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Generic Features of Lean Manufacturing] --> B[Value Stream Focus]
    A --> C[Waste Elimination Muda]
    A --> D[Continuous Flow]
    A --> E[Pull System Kanban]
    A --> F[Continuous Improvement Kaizen]
    A --> G[Built in Quality Jidoka]
    A --> H[Standardization]
    A --> I[Visual Management 5S]
    A --> J[Employee Empowerment]
    A --> K[Respect for People]

    B --> B1[Customer Value Definition]
    C --> C1[7 Wastes Transportation Inventory Motion Waiting Overproduction Overprocessing Defects]
    D --> D1[One Piece Flow Line Balancing Takt Time]
    E --> E1[Kanban Cards Heijunka Level Scheduling]
    F --> F1[PDCA Cycle Gemba Walks Kaizen Events]
    G --> G1[Poka Yoke Andon Stop the Line]
    H --> H1[Standard Work SOPs One Point Lessons]
    I --> I1[5S Color Coding Andon Lights Kanban Boards]
    J --> J1[Quality Circles Suggestion Systems Autonomous Maintenance]
    K --> K1[Trust Training Safety Teamwork]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Lean Generic Features

```mermaid
graph LR
    IN[INPUT: Customer Demand] --> P1[Process 1: Value Identification]
    P1 --> P2[Process 2: Value Stream Mapping]
    P2 --> P3[Process 3: Flow Design]
    P3 --> P4[Process 4: Pull Establishment]
    P4 --> P5[Process 5: Standardization]
    P5 --> P6[Process 6: Quality at Source]
    P6 --> P7[Process 7: Kaizen Loop]
    P7 --> OUT[OUTPUT: Customer Value Delivered]

    P7 -.-> P2
    P7 -.-> P3
    P7 -.-> P4

    EM[Employee Empowerment Layer] --> P1
    EM --> P2
    EM --> P3
    EM --> P4
    EM --> P5
    EM --> P6
    EM --> P7

    VM[Visual Management Layer] --> P4
    VM --> P5
    VM --> P6
```

**Interpretation:**
- The **outer loop** (P7 → P2, P3, P4) represents the **Kaizen feedback cycle** — continuous improvement.
- The **EM layer** (Employee Empowerment) acts as a *transverse enabler* across all processes.
- The **VM layer** (Visual Management) supports pull, standardization, and quality.
- This diagram demonstrates the **systems thinking** nature of Lean — features are *interconnected*, not isolated.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: 7 Wastes + 1 (DOWNTIME Mnemonic)

```mermaid
graph TD
    W[7 Wastes Plus 1 of Lean Muda] --> W1[D: Defects]
    W --> W2[O: Overproduction]
    W --> W3[W: Waiting]
    W --> W4[N: Non utilized talent]
    W --> W5[T: Transportation]
    W --> W6[I: Inventory]
    W --> W7[M: Motion]
    W --> W8[E: Extra processing]
```

> [!TIP]
> **DOWNTIME** is the standard English mnemonic used in KTU textbooks. Memorize it for the **3-mark definition question** in the university exam.

---

## 4.4 Diagram Safety Notes

> [!NOTE]
> All Mermaid diagrams use **alphanumeric node IDs only** (e.g., `W1`, `P1`, `EM`) — no reserved keywords are used. Labels are plain uppercase text without markdown formatting to ensure flawless rendering.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: 2-Mark / 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> [!NOTE]
> Part A carries 3 marks each as per KTU 2024 ESE pattern. Answers should be concise (3–4 sentences).

---

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Similar Pattern)]

**Define Generic Features of Lean Manufacturing. List any four.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Generic features of Lean Manufacturing refer to the **universal, industry-independent characteristics** that define any Lean system. These features are *transferable* across manufacturing, service, healthcare, and software domains. The four generic features are:

1. **Customer Value Focus** — defining value from the customer's perspective
2. **Waste Elimination (Muda)** — removing non-value-added activities
3. **Continuous Flow** — ensuring uninterrupted movement of products
4. **Pull System** — producing only in response to actual demand

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for listing, 1 mark for brief explanation — total 3 marks)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Similar Pattern)]

**Explain the 7 wastes (Muda) of Lean with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| # | Waste | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Transportation | Unnecessary movement of materials | Moving parts between distant warehouses |
| 2 | Inventory | Excess raw material or WIP | Storing 6 months of stock |
| 3 | Motion | Unnecessary movement of people | Worker walking 50 m for a tool |
| 4 | Waiting | Idle time | Machine stopped for a tool change |
| 5 | Overproduction | Producing too much/too early | Making 1000 parts when 500 needed |
| 6 | Over-processing | Extra work beyond requirement | Polishing surfaces that are painted |
| 7 | Defects | Rework/scrap | Welding defects causing 5% rejection |

*(½ mark each × 6 = 3 marks. Cover all 7 wastes within 3 marks — use tabular format for compactness.)*

---

## Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern as per KTU 2024 ESE)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE Part B offers **internal choice** between two questions on the same module. Each 14-mark question has **two sub-parts (a) and (b), each 7 marks**. Both Question A and Question B are given below — students answer **either A or B**.

---

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Modeled)]

**Q.A. (a) Explain in detail the 10 generic features of Lean Manufacturing with relevant examples. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

The 10 generic features of Lean Manufacturing are:

1. **Customer Value Focus (1 mark):**
   - Every process must be evaluated from the customer's standpoint.
   - Example: In an automotive plant, the customer pays for a *defect-free, fuel-efficient car*, not for a beautifully painted engine bay.

2. **Waste Elimination (1 mark):**
   - Seven wastes (transportation, inventory, motion, waiting, overproduction, over-processing, defects) + 8th waste: *untapped human potential*.
   - Example: Eliminating excess WIP between two stations reduces holding cost.

3. **Continuous Flow (1 mark):**
   - One-piece or small-lot flow without interruptions.
   - Example: Assembly line where each station completes work in 26 sec (Takt).

4. **Pull System (1 mark):**
   - Kanban-based authorization triggers production only when downstream consumes.
   - Example: Toyota's supermarket replenishment system.

5. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen) (½ mark):**
   - Daily incremental improvements via PDCA cycles.
   - Example: Workers suggesting 5S-driven layout changes.

6. **Built-in Quality (Jidoka) (½ mark):**
   - Stop-the-line authority plus Poka-Yoke.
   - Example: Sensor that stops a press when a part is misaligned.

7. **Standardization (½ mark):**
   - SOPs and standard work instructions.
   - Example: Color-coded standard work chart at every station.

8. **Visual Management (½ mark):**
   - Andon lights, Kanban boards, floor markings.
   - Example: Red light on a malfunctioning station triggers immediate response.

9. **Employee Empowerment (½ mark):**
   - Suggestion systems, quality circles.
   - Example: Toyota's 1 million suggestions per year from employees.

10. **Respect for People (½ mark):**
    - Humane treatment, training, safety, and job security.
    - Example: Lifetime employment philosophy at Toyota (cultural context).

**Conclusion:** These 10 features are *interconnected* and together form the *Lean DNA* of any organization.

---

**Q.A. (b) A workstation has a planned production time of 480 minutes per day. Downtime is 75 minutes. Ideal cycle time is 1.2 minutes/unit. Total units produced = 300. Good units = 270. Compute OEE and interpret the result. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Availability (1 mark):**

$$
A = \dfrac{480 - 75}{480} = \dfrac{405}{480} = 0.84375
$$

**Step 2 — Performance (2 marks):**

$$
\text{Operating Time} = 480 - 75 = 405 \text{ min}
$$

$$
P = \dfrac{1.2 \times 300}{405} = \dfrac{360}{405} = 0.8889
$$

**Step 3 — Quality (1 mark):**

$$
Q = \dfrac{270}{300} = 0.90
$$

**Step 4 — OEE (2 marks):**

$$
OEE = A \times P \times Q = 0.84375 \times 0.8889 \times 0.90
$$

Progressive multiplication:
- $0.84375 \times 0.8889 = 0.7500$
- $0.7500 \times 0.90 = 0.675$

$$
OEE = 0.675 = 67.5\%
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation (1 mark):**

A **world-class OEE** is **≥ 85%**. The current 67.5% indicates significant losses, particularly in:
- **Quality losses (10%)** — high defect rate
- **Performance losses (11.1%)** — minor stops / reduced speed
- **Availability losses (15.6%)** — equipment breakdowns

**Recommendations:** Implement TPM (Total Productive Maintenance), Poka-Yoke, and standard work to push OEE above 85%.

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Modeled)]

**Q.B. (a) Discuss the role of Takt Time, Cycle Time, and Lead Time in establishing Lean flow. Compute Takt Time for a plant working 2 shifts of 8 hours each, with 1 hour of break per shift, and a customer demand of 1500 units/day. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Theoretical Discussion (4 marks):**

- **Takt Time** — the *pace* at which a product must be produced to satisfy customer demand. Acts as the *heartbeat* of Lean.
- **Cycle Time** — the *actual* time a workstation takes to complete one unit. Must be **≤ Takt Time** at every station.
- **Lead Time** — the *total* time from customer order to delivery. The objective of Lean is to reduce Lead Time by eliminating Non-Value-Added steps.

**Relationship:**

$$
T_t \geq T_c \text{ (at every station)}
$$

If $T_c > T_t$, the station is a **bottleneck** and needs Kaizen.

**Numerical Computation (3 marks):**

**Step 1:** Total working time per day:

$$
T_{total} = 2 \times 8 = 16 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 2:** Total breaks per day:

$$
T_{breaks} = 2 \times 1 = 2 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 3:** Net available time:

$$
T_{available} = 16 - 2 = 14 \text{ hours} = 14 \times 3600 = 50400 \text{ sec}
$$

**Step 4:** Takt Time:

$$
T_t = \dfrac{50400}{1500} = 33.6 \text{ sec/unit}
$$

**Interpretation:** Every workstation must complete its task within 33.6 seconds.

---

**Q.B. (b) Explain Value-Added Ratio (VAR) with a worked example. A process has the following steps: Cutting (8 min, VA), Drilling (12 min, VA), Assembly (15 min, VA), Inspection (10 min, NNVA), Waiting (40 min, NVA), Rework (25 min, NVA). Calculate VAR and comment on the process health. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Theory (2 marks):**
Value-Added Ratio (VAR) measures the proportion of lead time that actually creates value as perceived by the customer. It is a key indicator of process leanness.

**Step 1 — Identify Value-Added Time (1 mark):**

$$
T_{VA} = 8 + 12 + 15 = 35 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify Non-Value-Added Time (1 mark):**

$$
T_{NVA} = 40 + 25 = 65 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Lead Time (1 mark):**

$$
T_{LT} = 35 + 65 = 100 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 4 — VAR (1 mark):**

$$
VAR = \dfrac{35}{100} \times 100\% = 35\%
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation (1 mark):**
A VAR of **35%** is **low** (target ≥ 70%). The process is **waste-dominated**. Immediate Lean interventions required:
- Eliminate *Waiting* through line balancing.
- Reduce *Rework* through Poka-Yoke.
- Re-examine whether *Inspection* is necessary (move toward *built-in quality*).

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes in Lean Generic Features Questions:**
>
> 1. **Listing features without explanation** — KTU examiners deduct **1–2 marks** if you list without a one-line description.
> 2. **Confusing Takt Time with Cycle Time** — Takt is *demand-driven*; Cycle is *process-driven*. Examiners will mark this as a *conceptual error*.
> 3. **Omitting units in numerical answers** — Always write `sec/unit`, `min`, `%`, etc. Missing units = ½ mark deduction.
> 4. **Forgetting the 8th waste** (Untapped Human Talent) — Examiners love this catch. Add it for a bonus ½ mark in Part A.
> 5. **Writing "Lean = cost-cutting"** — Lean is **value creation with less work**, not just cost reduction. This misframing loses marks in 14-mark answers.
> 6. **No diagram in 14-mark answers** — A small Mermaid/flowchart of the 7 wastes or 10 features earns 1–2 extra marks.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Generic features are the *universal, cross-industry* characteristics of Lean systems.
- **10 Pillars:** Value, Waste, Flow, Pull, Kaizen, Jidoka, Standardization, Visual Mgmt, Empowerment, Respect.
- **7 Wastes (DOWNTIME):** Defects, Overproduction, Waiting, Non-utilized talent, Transportation, Inventory, Motion, Extra-processing.
- **8th Waste:** Untapped human potential.
- **Takt Time:** $T_t = T_{available} / D$ — the heartbeat of Lean.
- **OEE:** $A \times P \times Q$ — world-class ≥ 85%.
- **VAR:** Target ≥ 70% for a healthy Lean process.
- **Pull ≠ Push:** Pull reacts to demand; Push forecasts and overproduces.
- **Jidoka = Autonomation:** Automation with a *human touch* — detect and stop defects.
- **Kaizen = Continuous, Incremental, Daily:** Not a one-time event.
- **5S:** Sort, Set in Order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain — foundation of Visual Management.
- **Cross-Industry Application:** Lean works in hospitals, banks, IT, and construction — not just factories.
- **Mnemonic for 7 Wastes:** **DOWNTIME** (memorize for instant 3-mark question).
- **Mnemonic for 5S:** **Sort, Set, Shine, Standardize, Sustain** (SSSSS).
- **Respect for People** is the *most often forgotten* generic feature in exams — write it explicitly.
- **Standardization comes *before* improvement** — *"You cannot improve what is not standardized."*
- **Visual Management** = Make problems *visible at a glance* — Andon, Kanban, 5S.
- **Employee Empowerment** ≠ delegation; it is *authority + capability* to stop and fix.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Approaches to Enhance Ability in Manufacturing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET746 - LEAN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Approaches to Enhance Ability in Manufacturing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Lean Manufacturing (PEMET746)** under the KTU 2024 Scheme, **"Approaches to Enhance Ability in Manufacturing"** refers to the integrated set of **operational techniques, managerial philosophies, and engineering tools** that systematically upgrade a manufacturing system's inherent capability to deliver **right-quality, right-quantity, right-time output** at the lowest possible cost while eliminating all forms of *muda* (waste), *mura* (unevenness), and *muri* (overburden).

These approaches transform a fragile, error-prone production line into a **robust, responsive, and self-correcting** value stream. They are the practical "muscle" of the Toyota Production System (TPS) and constitute the strategic backbone of any Lean transformation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** The objective of Module 4 is to expose students to **proven industrial engineering techniques** that improve equipment effectiveness, changeover agility, defect prevention, and workforce capability — the four pillars of manufacturing ability.

## 1.2 The Five Pillars of Enhanced Manufacturing Ability

The KTU curriculum emphasizes five mutually reinforcing approaches:

| Pillar | Japanese Term | English Equivalent | Primary Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Quick Changeover | SMED | Single Minute Exchange of Dies | Reduce setup time drastically |
| Mistake Proofing | Poka-Yoke | Error-Proofing / Fool-Proofing | Prevent defects at source |
| Autonomous Maintenance | Jidoka (partial) | Autonomation | Empower operators to maintain equipment |
| Total Productive Maintenance | TPM | Total Productive Maintenance | Maximize Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) |
| Standardized Work | Standard Work | Standardized Operations | Create a stable, repeatable baseline |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Marathon Runner" Intuition

Imagine a **car assembly plant** as a **marathon runner**:

- **SMED** is like a runner practicing **rapid shoe-changeovers** at pit stops — finishing the race is not just about speed, but about losing minimal time during transitions.
- **Poka-Yoke** is like a **car's reverse gear alarm** — it doesn't let the driver make a costly mistake; the design itself prevents the error.
- **TPM** is like **daily stretching and oiling** of joints — without preventive care, the runner collapses before the finish line.
- **Jidoka / Autonomation** is like the runner **listening to his own body** and stopping at the first sign of cramp, instead of running until he breaks down.
- **Standardized Work** is the **coach's training schedule** — every day, the same warm-up, pace, and recovery — it builds the consistency that wins races.

Just as a champion runner combines all five habits, a **Lean factory combines all five approaches** to convert ordinary operations into a world-class manufacturing system.

## 1.4 Definition of "Ability" in Manufacturing

> [!NOTE]
> **Manufacturing Ability (A)** can be quantified as a multiplicative function of **four sub-capabilities**:
> $$A = f(Q,\, F,\, S,\, R)$$
> where $Q$ = **Quality capability** (defect-free rate), $F$ = **Flexibility** (changeover speed), $S$ = **Stability** (uptime/reliability), and $R$ = **Reliability of human processes** (standardization & skill). Each approach in Module 4 enhances one or more of these sub-capabilities.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2.1 Approach 1 — SMED (Single Minute Exchange of Dies)

### 2.1.1 Origin and Definition
**SMED** was developed by **Shigeo Shingo** at Toyota in the 1950s. The "Single Minute" does **not** mean one minute — it means a setup time expressible in **single-digit minutes** (i.e., less than 10 minutes).

> [!NOTE]
> A setup is everything that occurs between the **last good part of the previous run** and the **first good part of the next run**.

### 2.1.2 Theoretical Foundation
Shingo classified all setup activities into two categories:

1. **Internal Setup (I):** Activities that **can only be performed** while the machine is stopped.
2. **External Setup (E):** Activities that **can be performed** while the machine is still running.

The **fundamental theorem of SMED** is to convert as many internal activities as possible into external ones, and then to refine the remaining internal activities.

### 2.1.3 The Four Stages of SMED Implementation
1. **Stage 0 — Baseline Study:** Observe and document every micro-step of the current setup. Classify each step as I or E.
2. **Stage 1 — Separation:** Move all possible internal setup activities to the external stage (machine running).
3. **Stage 2 — Conversion:** Convert remaining internal activities into external ones through clever tooling, parallel operations, or preparatory work.
4. **Stage 3 — Streamlining:** Smooth the remaining internal activities using quick fasteners, standardized heights, function checklists, and visual controls.

### 2.1.4 Engineering Utility
SMED enables **small lot production**, which in turn enables **just-in-time delivery**, **reduced inventory**, and **faster customer response**. It is the prerequisite for **one-piece flow** and **mixed-model production**.

---

## 2.2 Approach 2 — Poka-Yoke (Mistake Proofing)

### 2.2.1 Definition
**Poka-Yoke** is any **device, mechanism, or procedure** that either **prevents an error from occurring** (prevention type) or **detects an error immediately after it occurs and stops the process** (detection type), before defective products move downstream.

### 2.2.2 Theoretical Classification
Poka-Yoke devices are classified along three dimensions:

| Dimension | Type 1 | Type 2 |
|---|---|---|
| **Function** | Prevention Method | Detection Method |
| **Mechanism** | Contact Method | Fixed-Value (Count) Method |
| **Control Action** | Warning Method | Control Method (stops process) |

### 2.2.3 Underlying Psychology
Poka-Yoke operates on the principle that **humans err by nature**; therefore, the system should be designed so that errors are **physically impossible** or **immediately visible**. It is a direct engineering response to the **zero-defect** philosophy of the Toyota Production System.

### 2.2.4 Engineering Examples
- A SIM card tray that fits only one way into a phone (prevention).
- A dishwasher that beeps if the door is not fully closed (detection).
- A pin on an assembly fixture that physically blocks a missing part (prevention).

---

## 2.3 Approach 3 — Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)

### 2.3.1 Definition
**TPM** is a **company-wide, proactive, and preventive maintenance system** for equipment and machinery, designed to maximize **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** by involving **all employees** — from top management to shop-floor operators.

### 2.3.2 The Eight Pillars of TPM (also called the Eight Activities of TPM)
1. **Autonomous Maintenance (Jishu Hozen):** Operators clean, lubricate, and inspect their own machines.
2. **Planned Maintenance:** Scheduled, systematic maintenance based on equipment history.
3. **Quality Maintenance:** Defect prevention through equipment condition management.
4. **Focused Improvement (Kobetsu Kaizen):** Cross-functional teams solve specific chronic losses.
5. **Early Management / Early Equipment Management:** Designing new equipment for high reliability, maintainability, and operability from day one.
6. **Training and Education:** Building multi-skilled operators and maintenance craftsmen.
7. **Office TPM:** Extending TPM to administrative and support functions.
8. **Safety, Health, and Environment (SHE):** Zero-accident and zero-pollution workplace.

### 2.3.3 The Six Big Losses Addressed by TPM
TPM targets the **six big losses** that degrade equipment effectiveness:
1. **Breakdowns** (availability loss)
2. **Setup and Adjustment Losses** (availability loss)
3. **Small Stops** (availability loss)
4. **Reduced Speed** (performance loss)
5. **Defects and Rework** (quality loss)
6. **Startup Losses** (performance loss)

---

## 2.4 Approach 4 — Jidoka (Autonomation)

### 2.4.1 Definition
**Jidoka** (also called **Autonomation**) is the principle of **building quality into the process** by giving machines and operators the **authority and ability to detect abnormalities and stop production immediately**, rather than producing defective goods that propagate downstream.

### 2.4.2 The "Human Touch" of Automation
Jidoka distinguishes itself from Western automation by adding a **human judgment layer**:
- The machine has the **intelligence to detect** an abnormal condition.
- The machine (or operator) has the **authority to halt** the line.
- A clear **signal (Andon)** is raised to summon help.
- The **root cause** is addressed before the line is restarted (the **Five-Why** analysis).

### 2.4.3 The Four Rules of Jidoka
1. **Detect the abnormality.**
2. **Stop the work.**
3. **Fix or correct the immediate condition.**
4. **Investigate the root cause and install a countermeasure.**

---

## 2.5 Approach 5 — Standardized Work

### 2.5.1 Definition
**Standardized Work** is the **most efficient combination of operators, machines, and materials** known at a given point in time, documented precisely enough to be repeatable, trainable, and improvable. It is the **foundation for Kaizen**.

### 2.5.2 The Three Elements of Standardized Work
1. **Takt Time:** The pace at which a unit must be produced to meet customer demand.
2. **Work Sequence:** The exact ordered steps the operator must follow, without variation.
3. **Standard In-Process Stock:** The minimum inventory required to keep the sequence running smoothly.

### 2.5.3 Why Standardization Enhances Ability
Without a standard, there is **no baseline for improvement**. Taiichi Ohno famously said: *"Without standards, there can be no Kaizen."*

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **mandatory** for KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination) numerical problems on Module 4.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness) | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | $A$ = Availability, $P$ = Performance, $Q$ = Quality |
| 2 | Availability | $A = \dfrac{\text{Planned Production Time} - \text{Downtime}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}$ | Expressed as a percentage |
| 3 | Performance | $P = \dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces}}{\text{Operating Time}}$ | Measures speed losses |
| 4 | Quality | $Q = \dfrac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces Produced}}$ | Measures defect losses |
| 5 | Takt Time | $T_{takt} = \dfrac{\text{Available Production Time}}{\text{Customer Demand}}$ | Determines the heartbeat of production |
| 6 | SMED Reduction Ratio | $\eta_{SMED} = \dfrac{T_{old} - T_{new}}{T_{old}} \times 100\%$ | % reduction in setup time |
| 7 | MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) | $MTBF = \dfrac{\text{Total Uptime}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | Higher is better |
| 8 | MTTR (Mean Time To Repair) | $MTTR = \dfrac{\text{Total Downtime}}{\text{Number of Repairs}}$ | Lower is better |
| 9 | First Pass Yield (FPY) | $FPY = \dfrac{\text{Good Units at First Pass}}{\text{Total Units Started}}$ | Direct quality indicator |
| 10 | Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) | $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | Product of yields across $n$ process steps |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units reminder:** All ratios (A, P, Q, FPY, RTY) are dimensionless. Times are in **seconds (s)** or **minutes (min)**. MTBF and MTTR carry the unit of hours ($\text{h}$).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example 1 — OEE Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU-style):** A CNC machine has the following daily data:
> - Planned Production Time: **480 minutes**
> - Downtime (breakdowns + setup): **60 minutes**
> - Ideal Cycle Time: **1.5 minutes per part**
> - Total Parts Produced: **280**
> - Defective Parts: **20**
>
> Calculate the **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** and identify the dominant loss category.

### Step 1: Compute Operating Time
Operating time is the time during which the machine is actually running (i.e., planned production time minus all downtime).

$$
T_{op} = T_{planned} - T_{down} = 480 - 60 = 420 \text{ min}
$$

This is the *denominator* for both availability and performance.

### Step 2: Compute Availability ($A$)
Availability measures **stop-time losses** (breakdowns, setups, changeovers).

$$
A = \dfrac{T_{planned} - T_{down}}{T_{planned}} = \dfrac{480 - 60}{480} = \dfrac{420}{480} = 0.875
$$

Converting to percentage: $A = 87.5\%$.

### Step 3: Compute Performance ($P$)
Performance measures **speed losses** (running slower than ideal cycle time).

$$
P = \dfrac{T_{cycle, ideal} \times N_{total}}{T_{op}} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 280}{420} = \dfrac{420}{420} = 1.000
$$

So $P = 100.0\%$. This means the machine is running at its theoretical ideal speed — no small stops or speed losses.

### Step 4: Compute Quality ($Q$)
Quality measures **defect losses** (parts that had to be reworked or scrapped).

$$
Q = \dfrac{N_{good}}{N_{total}} = \dfrac{280 - 20}{280} = \dfrac{260}{280} \approx 0.9286
$$

So $Q \approx 92.86\%$.

### Step 5: Compute OEE
$$
OEE = A \times P \times Q = 0.875 \times 1.000 \times 0.9286 \approx 0.8125
$$

So $OEE \approx 81.25\%$.

### Step 6: Identify the Dominant Loss
- Availability loss: $1 - 0.875 = 0.125$ (12.5 percentage points lost)
- Performance loss: $1 - 1.000 = 0.000$ (no loss)
- Quality loss: $1 - 0.9286 = 0.0714$ (7.14 percentage points lost)

**Dominant loss = Availability (12.5 pp)**. The corrective action is therefore **TPM-based downtime reduction** and **SMED-based setup reduction**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** World-class OEE is considered to be **≥ 85%**. Our calculated 81.25% is good but indicates room for improvement, especially in availability.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 2 — SMED Reduction

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A press machine had an original setup time of **90 minutes**. After applying the **four stages of SMED**, the new setup time is **12 minutes**.
>
> Calculate: (a) The percentage reduction in setup time. (b) If the press previously produced **10 batches per shift**, how many additional batches can now be produced per shift (assume 1 batch fits in the saved time, and the new time allows 2 batches in the time of 1 old)?

### Step 1: Apply the SMED Reduction Formula

$$
\eta_{SMED} = \dfrac{T_{old} - T_{new}}{T_{old}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{90 - 12}{90} \times 100\% = \dfrac{78}{90} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\eta_{SMED} = 86.67\%
$$

### Step 2: Compute the Batches per Shift
Let the cycle time per batch be $T_c$. Then per shift of duration $T_{shift}$:

- **Before SMED:** Number of batches = $\dfrac{T_{shift}}{T_c + T_{old}}$ = $\dfrac{T_{shift}}{T_c + 90}$
- **After SMED:** Number of batches = $\dfrac{T_{shift}}{T_c + 12}$

Assume for simplicity $T_c = 30$ min and $T_{shift} = 600$ min (10 hours):

- Before: $\dfrac{600}{30 + 90} = \dfrac{600}{120} = 5$ batches
- After: $\dfrac{600}{30 + 12} = \dfrac{600}{42} \approx 14.28 \rightarrow 14$ batches

So the additional batches = $14 - 5 = 9$ batches per shift, an increase of **180%** in throughput.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 3 — Takt Time and Operator Count

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A factory operates **2 shifts of 8 hours each**, with **60 minutes of break time per shift**. Daily customer demand is **960 units**. Calculate: (a) Takt time. (b) Number of operators required if the standard work content per unit is **8 minutes**.

### Step 1: Total Available Production Time
$$
T_{avail} = (\text{Shift time} - \text{Break time}) \times \text{Number of shifts}
$$
$$
T_{avail} = (480 - 60) \times 2 = 420 \times 2 = 840 \text{ min/day}
$$

### Step 2: Takt Time
$$
T_{takt} = \dfrac{T_{avail}}{D} = \dfrac{840}{960} = 0.875 \text{ min/unit} = 52.5 \text{ seconds/unit}
$$

### Step 3: Number of Operators
The standard work content per unit is $W_c = 8$ min. The total work content per day is:
$$
W_{total} = W_c \times D = 8 \times 960 = 7680 \text{ min/day}
$$

The number of operators required (assuming 100% utilization, rounded up):
$$
N_{op} = \left\lceil \dfrac{W_{total}}{T_{avail}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{7680}{840} \right\rceil = \lceil 9.14 \rceil = 10 \text{ operators}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Even though the *math* suggests 9.14 operators, KTU expects the **ceiling function** to be applied because a fraction of an operator cannot exist in reality. The 10th operator handles minor auxiliary tasks.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Approaches to Manufacturing "Ability"

| Approach | Enhances $Q$ (Quality) | Enhances $F$ (Flexibility) | Enhances $S$ (Stability/Uptime) | Enhances $R$ (Reliability of Human Process) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SMED** | ✗ | ★★★ | ★★ (less setup downtime) | ★ (standardized setup steps) |
| **Poka-Yoke** | ★★★ | ✗ | ✗ | ★★ (less rework, less operator stress) |
| **TPM** | ★★ | ★ | ★★★ | ★★ (operator ownership) |
| **Jidoka** | ★★★ | ✗ | ★★ (avoids cascade failure) | ★★★ (operator judgment) |
| **Standardized Work** | ★★ | ★ | ★★ | ★★★ (creates baseline) |

Where **★★★** = strong contribution, **★★** = moderate, **★** = minor, and **✗** = not directly addressed.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.1 Conceptual Architecture — How the Five Approaches Interlock

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef pillar fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef enab fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef outcome fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff

    Goal["Enhanced Manufacturing Ability (A = f Q F S R)"]:::outcome
    SMED["SMED - Single Minute Exchange of Dies"]:::pillar
    Poka["Poka-Yoke - Mistake Proofing"]:::pillar
    TPM["TPM - Total Productive Maintenance"]:::pillar
    Jido["Jidoka - Autonomation"]:::pillar
    Std["Standardized Work"]:::pillar

    Kaizen["Kaizen - Continuous Improvement Engine"]:::enab
    Q["Quality Capability Q"]:::outcome
    F["Flexibility F"]:::outcome
    S["Stability S"]:::outcome
    R["Reliability of Human Process R"]:::outcome

    Goal --> Q
    Goal --> F
    Goal --> S
    Goal --> R

    SMED --> F
    SMED --> S
    Poka --> Q
    TPM --> S
    TPM --> R
    Jido --> Q
    Jido --> R
    Std --> R
    Std --> Q

    Kaizen --> SMED
    Kaizen --> Poka
    Kaizen --> TPM
    Kaizen --> Jido
    Kaizen --> Std
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each of the five approaches feeds a specific sub-capability. **Kaizen** is the continuous-improvement engine that *re-tunes* all five. Together, they raise the overall **Manufacturing Ability $A$**.

---

## 4.2 SMED Implementation Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef step fill:#0d47a1,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef decision fill:#ff6f00,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef done fill:#1b5e20,stroke:#fff,color:#fff

    Start(["Current Setup T old minutes"]):::step
    S0["Stage 0: Document every step of setup"]:::step
    Classify["Classify each step as Internal or External"]:::decision
    S1["Stage 1: Separate Internal from External"]:::step
    CanConv{"Can remaining Internal be done while machine is running"}:::decision
    S2["Stage 2: Convert Internal to External"]:::step
    S3["Stage 3: Streamline remaining Internal steps"]:::step
    NewTime["New Setup Time T new minutes less than 10 min target"]:::done
    Repeat{"T new greater than 9 minutes"}:::decision
    Done(["Single Minute Setup Achieved"]):::done

    Start --> S0 --> Classify --> S1 --> CanConv
    CanConv -- Yes --> S2 --> S3
    CanConv -- No --> S3
    S3 --> NewTime --> Repeat
    Repeat -- Yes --> S0
    Repeat -- No --> Done
```

---

## 4.3 Poka-Yoke Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef node fill:#4a148c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef branch fill:#e65100,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef endnode fill:#1b5e20,stroke:#fff,color:#fff

    Q1["Has the error already occurred in the process?"]:::branch
    P1["Prevention Poka-Yoke: Design the process so the error CANNOT happen"]:::endnode
    D1["Detection Poka-Yoke: Detect the error the moment it happens"]:::branch
    Q2["What is the consequence severity?"]:::branch
    W["Warning Method: Light buzzer, lamp, flag"]:::endnode
    C["Control Method: Stop the machine or line"]:::endnode
    Q3["Detection trigger type?"]:::branch
    M1["Contact Method: Physical sensor / limit switch / pin"]:::endnode
    M2["Fixed-Value Method: Counter, timer, sequencer"]:::endnode

    Q1 -- No --> P1
    Q1 -- Yes --> D1 --> Q2
    Q2 -- Minor --> W
    Q2 -- Major --> C
    C --> Q3
    Q3 -- Physical --> M1
    Q3 -- Numerical --> M2
```

---

## 4.4 TPM — The Eight Pillars Architecture


## 4.5 Jidoka — The "Stop and Fix" Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef op fill:#0d47a1,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef det fill:#e65100,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef fix fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#fff,color:#fff

    Op["Operator or Machine working on next unit"]:::op
    Det{"Abnormality detected?"}:::det
    Stop["STOP production - Pull Andon cord"]:::det
    Help["Team Leader or Maintenance arrives"]:::fix
    Why["Five-Why Root Cause Analysis"]:::fix
    Counter["Install Countermeasure Poka-Yoke if possible"]:::fix
    Resume["Resume production only after root cause addressed"]:::op

    Op --> Det
    Det -- No --> Op
    Det -- Yes --> Stop --> Help --> Why --> Counter --> Resume --> Op
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define SMED. What is the difference between "Internal" and "External" setup activities?**

**Model Answer:**
SMED, or **Single Minute Exchange of Dies**, is a Lean manufacturing technique developed by **Shigeo Shingo** to reduce machine setup time to less than 10 minutes (single-digit minutes).

- **Internal Setup Activities:** These are the operations that **can only be performed when the machine is stopped** (e.g., removing dies, adjusting bolts, mounting fixtures).
- **External Setup Activities:** These are the operations that **can be performed while the machine is still running** (e.g., bringing the next die, pre-heating, preparing tools, transporting material).

The core principle of SMED is to **convert internal activities into external activities** and to streamline the remaining internal ones.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**List any three types of Poka-Yoke devices with one industrial example each.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Contact Method:** A physical pin or sensor detects the presence/absence of a part. *Example:* A pin on a welding fixture that blocks the welding gun if a sheet-metal component is missing.
2. **Fixed-Value (Count) Method:** A counter or sequencer ensures the right number of operations. *Example:* A pill-bottle capper that counts exactly 30 tablets before capping.
3. **Motion-Sequence Method:** Detects if operations are performed in the correct order. *Example:* An assembly tool that will not release a screw unless the previous step's bolt is fully tightened.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adapted]**

**(a)** Discuss the **four stages of SMED implementation** with an example from an actual industrial press machine. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compute the **OEE** of a stamping press from the following data and suggest two approaches from Module 4 to improve it. (7 marks)

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Shift length | 8 hours = 480 min |
| Breaks (lunch + tea) | 60 min |
| Unplanned downtime | 45 min |
| Setup / changeover time | 30 min |
| Ideal cycle time | 30 seconds per part |
| Total parts produced in shift | 420 |
| Defective parts | 30 |

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Four Stages of SMED (7 marks)

1. **Stage 0 — Baseline Observation and Recording (1.5 marks):**
   The team observes and video-records the entire setup of a 200-ton press. Every micro-activity is logged — entering safety cage, removing old die (8 min), cleaning bolster (3 min), bringing new die (12 min), aligning die (15 min), tightening bolts (20 min), trial part (5 min). Total = 63 min. The activities are then classified as Internal (must be done with press stopped) or External (can be done while press is running).

2. **Stage 1 — Separating Internal and External (1.5 marks):**
   The team discovers that "bringing the new die" can be done during the previous run. The new die is pre-positioned on a roller table. This converts 12 min from Internal to External. New Internal time ≈ 51 min.

3. **Stage 2 — Converting Internal to External (2 marks):**
   The team designs a quick-clamp system, eliminating the need to enter the safety cage for bolt tightening. Now, only 5 min of internal time remains. New Internal time ≈ 12 min.

4. **Stage 3 — Streamlining the Internal (2 marks):**
   Standardize die heights (use a die-height gauge), use color-coded quick connectors, and implement a setup checklist. The remaining 12 min is reduced to **6 min** — well within "single-digit minutes".

---

#### Part (b) — OEE Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1: Planned Production Time**
$$
T_{planned} = 480 - 60 = 420 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 2: Downtime**
$$
T_{down} = 45 + 30 = 75 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 3: Operating Time**
$$
T_{op} = 420 - 75 = 345 \text{ min} = 345 \times 60 = 20{,}700 \text{ sec}
$$

**Step 4: Availability**
$$
A = \dfrac{420 - 75}{420} = \dfrac{345}{420} = 0.8214 = 82.14\%
$$

**Step 5: Performance**
$$
P = \dfrac{30 \text{ s} \times 420}{20{,}700 \text{ s}} = \dfrac{12{,}600}{20{,}700} \approx 0.6087 = 60.87\%
$$

**Step 6: Quality**
$$
Q = \dfrac{420 - 30}{420} = \dfrac{390}{420} = 0.9286 = 92.86\%
$$

**Step 7: OEE**
$$
OEE = 0.8214 \times 0.6087 \times 0.9286 \approx 0.4642 = 46.42\%
$$

**Step 8: Two Approaches to Improve OEE (2 marks):**

1. **SMED** — reduce the 30-min changeover to less than 10 min, directly increasing $A$ (availability) and indirectly improving $P$ by allowing more actual production time.
2. **TPM (Autonomous Maintenance)** — reduce unplanned downtime of 45 min by implementing daily cleaning, lubrication, and inspection rituals, thereby raising $A$ closer to 95%.
3. (Bonus) **Poka-Yoke** — reduce defects from 30 to near zero by installing a sensor that detects mis-stamped parts, raising $Q$ to ~100%.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common mistake #1:** Students often use the *cycle time* as the denominator for Performance. The correct denominator is **Operating Time** ($T_{op}$), not cycle time.
> - **Common mistake #2:** Do not include **break time** as downtime in the Availability formula. Breaks are *non-planned production time* and are subtracted from **shift length**, not from **downtime**.
> - **Common mistake #3:** Always express OEE as a **percentage**. Writing "0.4642" instead of "46.42%" is a typical 0.5-mark loss.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **eight pillars of TPM** with a one-line description of each. (7 marks)

**(b)** A plant has a daily demand of **720 units**. Each operator works **2 shifts of 7 hours each**, with **45 min of break per shift**. The standard work content per unit is **6 minutes**. Calculate the **takt time** and the **number of operators required** to meet demand. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Eight Pillars of TPM (7 marks — 0.875 marks each, rounded)

1. **Autonomous Maintenance (Jishu Hozen):** Operators take ownership of routine maintenance of their own machines (clean, lubricate, inspect).
2. **Planned Maintenance:** Scheduled, systematic maintenance based on equipment history to prevent breakdowns.
3. **Quality Maintenance:** Ensuring equipment conditions are such that no defect-producing conditions arise.
4. **Focused Improvement (Kobetsu Kaizen):** Cross-functional teams tackle specific chronic losses in equipment.
5. **Early Equipment Management:** Designing new equipment for reliability, maintainability, and operability from the start.
6. **Training and Education:** Building multi-skilled operators and skilled maintenance craftsmen.
7. **Office TPM:** Extending TPM principles to administrative and support functions (planning, HR, procurement).
8. **Safety, Health, and Environment:** Achieving a zero-accident, zero-pollution workplace.

---

#### Part (b) — Takt Time & Operator Count (7 marks)

**Step 1: Available Production Time**
$$
T_{avail} = (7 \text{ hr} \times 60 - 45) \times 2 = (420 - 45) \times 2 = 375 \times 2 = 750 \text{ min/day}
$$

**Step 2: Takt Time**
$$
T_{takt} = \dfrac{T_{avail}}{D} = \dfrac{750}{720} = 1.0417 \text{ min/unit} \approx 62.5 \text{ s/unit}
$$

**Step 3: Total Work Content per Day**
$$
W_{total} = W_c \times D = 6 \times 720 = 4320 \text{ min/day}
$$

**Step 4: Number of Operators**
$$
N_{op} = \left\lceil \dfrac{4320}{750} \right\rceil = \lceil 5.76 \rceil = 6 \text{ operators}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common mistake #1:** Forgetting to apply the **ceiling function** for number of operators (you cannot have 0.76 of an operator).
> - **Common mistake #2:** Mixing up **Takt Time** (a *demand-driven* pace) with **Cycle Time** (a *capability-driven* pace).
> - **Common mistake #3:** Not converting hours to minutes. Always do so explicitly to avoid unit errors.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE Module 4**

- **SMED** = Shigeo Shingo's technique to bring setup time to **single-digit minutes** (<10 min). Four stages: **Observe → Separate → Convert → Streamline**.
- **Poka-Yoke** = "Mistake Proofing". Two main types: **Prevention** (error cannot occur) and **Detection** (error caught immediately). Two control actions: **Warning** and **Control (Stop)**.
- **TPM** has **8 pillars**. The first pillar is **Autonomous Maintenance** — the most visible and most often asked in exams.
- **Six Big Losses** of TPM: Breakdowns, Setup Losses, Small Stops, Reduced Speed, Defects/Rework, Startup Losses.
- **Jidoka** = "Autonomation". The four rules: **Detect → Stop → Fix → Investigate Root Cause**. It is the human-judgment extension of automation.
- **Standardized Work** has **3 elements**: Takt Time + Work Sequence + Standard In-Process Stock.
- **OEE Formula**: $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ — always expressed as a **percentage**.
  - **A = (Planned Time − Downtime) / Planned Time**
  - **P = (Ideal Cycle Time × Total Pieces) / Operating Time**
  - **Q = Good Pieces / Total Pieces**
- **Takt Time Formula**: $T_{takt} = \dfrac{\text{Available Production Time}}{\text{Customer Demand}}$ — drives the **heartbeat of Lean production**.
- **SMED Reduction**: $\eta = \dfrac{T_{old} - T_{new}}{T_{old}} \times 100\%$.
- **Number of Operators** (with ceiling): $N_{op} = \left\lceil \dfrac{W_{total}}{T_{avail}} \right\rceil$.
- **World-class benchmark**: OEE ≥ 85%, SMED < 10 min, Poka-Yoke coverage 100% of critical defects.
- The five approaches are **mutually reinforcing** — SMED enables JIT, TPM enables SMED, Jidoka enables Poka-Yoke, and Standardized Work enables all of them.
- Always remember: **Kaizen** is the *engine* that drives all five approaches — without continuous improvement, gains erode.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Managing People in Agile Organization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET746 - LEAN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Managing People in Agile Organization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Agile Organization** is a structured entity (typically a manufacturing or service enterprise) that has built-in capabilities to rapidly adapt, evolve, and respond to dynamic market conditions, customer demands, and operational disruptions. From a **Lean Manufacturing and People Management** perspective (KTU PEMET746 Module 4), *Managing People in an Agile Organization* refers to the systematic approach of aligning Human Resource (HR) strategies, leadership philosophies, team structures, and cultural practices to foster **flexibility, empowerment, continuous learning, and rapid decision-making** at every organizational level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "An agile organization views its workforce as a strategic asset capable of self-organization, cross-functional collaboration, and continuous improvement, eliminating rigid hierarchies in favor of empowered, multi-skilled teams."

The core people-management pillars in an agile manufacturing enterprise include:
- **Self-Directed Work Teams (SDWTs)**
- **Empowerment and Decentralized Decision-Making**
- **Cross-Functional Skill Development (Multi-Skilling)**
- **Continuous Learning Culture (Kaizen of People)**
- **Servant Leadership and Agile Coaching**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Modern Kitchen Brigade vs. The Old Assembly Line**
> 
> Imagine a restaurant kitchen. In a **traditional assembly-line kitchen** (old bureaucratic organization), the chef (manager) issues every command — "cut the onions," "stir the pot," "plate the dish." If the chef falls sick or the customer changes the order, the kitchen halts.
> 
> In an **agile kitchen brigade** (modern agile organization), every cook knows multiple stations (multi-skilled), can swap roles when needed (flexibility), decides independently when a sauce needs adjusting (empowerment), and continuously experiments with new recipes (continuous learning). Even if one cook leaves, the kitchen keeps running smoothly.
> 
> **The cook = the employee.** The **kitchen = the agile organization.** The **brigade leader = the servant leader** (who serves, not commands).

### 1.3 Key Characteristics of People in Agile Organizations

| **S.No** | **Characteristic** | **Behavioral Indicator** |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Self-Organizing** | Teams plan, execute, and control their own work |
| 2 | **Multi-Skilled / Cross-Functional** | Operators can run 3–5 different machines |
| 3 | **Empowered** | Decision authority pushed to shop-floor level |
| 4 | **Continuous Learners** | Daily Kaizen, TWI (Training Within Industry) participation |
| 5 | **Customer-Focused** | Every action tied to internal/external customer value |
| 6 | **Collaborative** | Open communication, visual management, daily stand-ups |
| 7 | **Adaptive / Resilient** | Rapid response to change without panic |

### 1.4 Standard HR Metrics in Agile Manufacturing (Bold Constants)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key People Metrics Used in Agile Organizations:**
> - **Employee Engagement Index (EEI):** Typically maintained above **75%** in world-class agile factories
> - **Multi-Skill Ratio (MSR):** Target $\geq 4$ skills per operator
> - **Absenteeism Rate (AR):** Best-in-class below **2%**
> - **First-Time-Right (FTR) by People:** Target $\geq 95\%$
> - **Suggestion Implementation Rate (SIR):** Target $\geq 70\%$ of employee ideas
> - **Training Hours per Employee per Year:** World-class **40–80 hours**

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of Managing People in Agile Organizations

#### **Pillar 1: Empowerment and Decentralization**

In lean-agile organizations, decision-making authority is pushed downward to the **Gemba** (the actual workplace). This is the opposite of Taylor's classical "command-and-control" model.

- **Why?** Frontline workers have the most contextual information about a process problem.
- **How?** Through *standardized work*, *visual management*, and *obeya (war room)* meetings.
- **Boundary Condition:** Empowerment works only when combined with **skill development** and **clear decision boundaries** (Heijunka-based authority matrix).

#### **Pillar 2: Self-Directed Work Teams (SDWTs)**

A SDWT is a small group of employees (typically **5–12 members**) who collectively manage their own work, schedule, problem-solving, and continuous improvement activities.

- **Team Maturity Stages:** Forming → Storming → Norming → Performing (Tuckman Model)
- **Characteristics of High-Performing SDWTs:**
  - Shared goals and metrics (e.g., OEE, FTR, cycle time)
  - Rotational leadership
  - Joint accountability
  - Internal customer-supplier relationships

#### **Pillar 3: Multi-Skill Development (Cross-Training)**

Multi-skilling ensures workforce flexibility to absorb variability — a core lean-agile principle (Mura elimination).

- **Skill Matrix (Yamazumi Chart):** A visual tool mapping operator skills to machine/process requirements.
- **Training Systems Used:** TWI (Training Within Industry), JIT Training, OJT (On-the-Job Training), Job Rotation, Job Enrichment, Job Enlargement.

#### **Pillar 4: Servant Leadership**

A leader in an agile organization **serves the team** by removing impediments, providing resources, and protecting the team from organizational noise.

> [!NOTE]
> **Robert Greenleaf's Servant Leadership Principle:** "The servant-leader focuses first on the growth and well-being of people and the communities to which they belong."

**Behaviors of Agile Servant Leaders:**
- Active listening
- Empathy
- Stewardship
- Building community
- Foresight (anticipating change)

#### **Pillar 5: Continuous Learning Culture (Kaizen Mindset)**

Learning is treated as a daily, integrated activity — not a periodic event.

- **Mechanisms:** PDCA cycles, A3 problem solving, Genba walks, Gemba Walks, Reflection workshops.
- **Knowledge Management Tools:** Yoki (Yokoten) — horizontal deployment of best practices; Lessons Learned databases.

### 2.2 Agile HRM (Human Resource Management) Functions

| **HRM Function** | **Traditional Approach** | **Agile Approach** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Recruitment** | Hire for one specific job | Hire for adaptability and learning agility |
| **Training** | Annual classroom sessions | Continuous, on-the-job, just-in-time |
| **Performance Appraisal** | Annual, top-down rating | Continuous, 360° feedback, team-based |
| **Compensation** | Fixed job-based pay | Skill-based pay, team incentives, Kaizen bonuses |
| **Career Path** | Vertical ladder | Horizontal, lattice, multi-disciplinary |
| **Communication** | Memo, formal letters | Visual boards, daily stand-ups, huddles |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — People Management in Agile Organizations**

| **S.No** | **Metric / Concept** | **Formula** | **Standard Unit** | **Target / Benchmark** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | **Employee Engagement Index (EEI)** | $\displaystyle EEI = \frac{\text{Engaged Employees}}{\text{Total Employees}} \times 100$ | Percent (%) | $\geq 75\%$ |
| 2 | **Multi-Skill Ratio (MSR)** | $\displaystyle MSR = \frac{\text{Total Skills Acquired by Workforce}}{\text{Total Workforce}}$ | Skills/Person | $\geq 4$ |
| 3 | **Absenteeism Rate (AR)** | $\displaystyle AR = \frac{\text{Unplanned Absent Days}}{\text{Total Available Workdays}} \times 100$ | Percent (%) | $\leq 2\%$ |
| 4 | **Training Coverage Ratio (TCR)** | $\displaystyle TCR = \frac{\text{Employees Trained}}{\text{Total Employees}} \times 100$ | Percent (%) | $\geq 90\%$ |
| 5 | **Suggestion Implementation Rate (SIR)** | $\displaystyle SIR = \frac{\text{Ideas Implemented}}{\text{Ideas Submitted}} \times 100$ | Percent (%) | $\geq 70\%$ |
| 6 | **First-Time-Right (FTR)** | $\displaystyle FTR = \frac{\text{Defect-Free Units}}{\text{Total Units Produced}} \times 100$ | Percent (%) | $\geq 95\%$ |
| 7 | **Workforce Productivity (WP)** | $\displaystyle WP = \frac{\text{Output (Good Units)}}{\text{Direct Labor Hours}}$ | Units/Hour | Increasing trend |
| 8 | **Skill Coverage Index (SCI)** | $\displaystyle SCI = \frac{\text{Operations Covered by Trained Operators}}{\text{Total Operations}} \times 100$ | Percent (%) | $100\%$ |
| 9 | **Team Autonomy Index (TAI)** | $\displaystyle TAI = \frac{\text{Decisions Made at Team Level}}{\text{Total Decisions}} \times 100$ | Percent (%) | $\geq 60\%$ |
| 10 | **Kaizen Participation Rate (KPR)** | $\displaystyle KPR = \frac{\text{Employees Submitting Kaizens}}{\text{Total Employees}} \times 100$ | Percent (%) | $\geq 80\%$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Notation Tip:** When writing absolute values in tables, use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ — never the bare pipe symbol `|x|` — to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Automotive Sector (Toyota Production System):** Shop-floor teams make real-time *Heijunka* (leveling) decisions; multi-skilled operators run 4–5 stations.
- **Aerospace (Boeing, Airbus):** Agile cells with cross-certified technicians handle mixed-model assembly.
- **Electronics (Foxconn, Samsung):** Skill-matrix-based rostering to absorb demand variability.
- **Healthcare Lean-Agile:** Cross-trained nursing teams adapt to patient-load surges (COVID-19 response).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Computing Multi-Skill Ratio (MSR) for a Production Cell

**Problem Statement:** A manufacturing cell has **10 operators**. The total skills acquired (across all multi-skilling programs) are as follows:

| **Operator** | **Number of Skills Acquired** |
|:---:|:---:|
| O1 | 5 |
| O2 | 4 |
| O3 | 6 |
| O4 | 3 |
| O5 | 5 |
| O6 | 4 |
| O7 | 7 |
| O8 | 4 |
| O9 | 5 |
| O10 | 3 |

**Compute the Multi-Skill Ratio (MSR) and comment on workforce flexibility.**

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the formula from the KTU Formula Sheet.

$$\begin{aligned}
MSR &= \frac{\text{Total Skills Acquired by Workforce}}{\text{Total Workforce}} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Sum the skills acquired by all 10 operators.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Skills} &= 5 + 4 + 6 + 3 + 5 + 4 + 7 + 4 + 5 + 3 \\
&= (5+4+6+3+5) + (4+7+4+5+3) \\
&= 23 + 23 \\
&= 46 \text{ skills}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the formula. The total workforce is 10 operators.

$$\begin{aligned}
MSR &= \frac{46}{10} \\
&= 4.6 \text{ skills per operator}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Interpret the result against the KTU benchmark.

> [!NOTE]
> **Benchmark Comparison:** The world-class / KTU-recommended MSR is $\geq 4$ skills/operator. The computed value of **4.6** **exceeds** the benchmark, indicating **HIGH workforce flexibility** and strong resistance to variability (Mura).

**Step 5:** Engineering recommendation. Operators O4 and O10 (with only 3 skills) should be prioritized for the next training cycle to lift the floor of the skill distribution.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Employee Engagement Index (EEI) for an Agile Plant

**Problem Statement:** A medium-scale agile manufacturing plant has **500 employees**. A recent pulse survey reveals:
- **Engaged employees:** 360
- **Disengaged employees:** 90
- **Actively disengaged employees:** 50

**Compute the EEI and the Net Engagement Score (NES).**

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Recall the EEI formula.

$$\begin{aligned}
EEI &= \frac{\text{Engaged Employees}}{\text{Total Employees}} \times 100 \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the EEI.

$$\begin{aligned}
EEI &= \frac{360}{500} \times 100 \\
&= 0.72 \times 100 \\
&= 72\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Recall the Net Engagement Score (NES) formula, which adjusts for actively disengaged employees.

$$\begin{aligned}
NES &= \frac{\text{Engaged} - \text{Actively Disengaged}}{\text{Total Employees}} \times 100 \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the NES.

$$\begin{aligned}
NES &= \frac{360 - 50}{500} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{310}{500} \times 100 \\
&= 62\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Interpret against KTU benchmarks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EEI = 72%** is **slightly below** the world-class target of **75%**, signaling a **marginal improvement opportunity**.
> 
> **NES = 62%** indicates a positive but improvable engagement climate. The 50 actively disengaged employees should be the focus of structured 1-on-1 servant-leader coaching.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Skill Coverage Index (SCI) for Line Balancing

**Problem Statement:** A mixed-model production line has **8 workstations** and **6 operators** during a shift. The required skill-station matrix is:

| **Operator** | **W1** | **W2** | **W3** | **W4** | **W5** | **W6** | **W7** | **W8** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| A | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | – | – | – | – | – |
| B | – | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | – | – | – | – |
| C | – | – | – | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | – | – |
| D | – | – | – | – | – | ✓ | ✓ | – |
| E | ✓ | – | – | – | – | – | ✓ | ✓ |
| F | – | – | ✓ | – | – | – | – | ✓ |

**Compute the SCI and identify the most critical bottleneck station.**

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Recall the SCI formula.

$$\begin{aligned}
SCI &= \frac{\text{Operations Covered by $\geq 1$ Trained Operator}}{\text{Total Operations}} \times 100 \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Count trained operators per workstation.

- W1: A, E → **2 operators** ✓ Covered
- W2: A, B → **2 operators** ✓ Covered
- W3: A, B, F → **3 operators** ✓ Covered
- W4: B, C → **2 operators** ✓ Covered
- W5: C → **1 operator** ✓ Covered (but vulnerable)
- W6: C, D → **2 operators** ✓ Covered
- W7: D, E → **2 operators** ✓ Covered
- W8: E, F → **2 operators** ✓ Covered

**Step 3:** All 8 workstations are covered by at least one trained operator.

$$\begin{aligned}
SCI &= \frac{8}{8} \times 100 \\
&= 100\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Identify the bottleneck. Although SCI = 100%, station W5 has only 1 trained operator (C). If operator C is absent, **W5 halts the line**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Risk:** A 100% SCI does NOT guarantee resilience. Stations with single-coverage (here, W5) are **single points of failure**. The agile manager must immediately initiate cross-training to bring SCI redundancy to $\geq 2$ operators per station.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative HRM Models in Agile vs. Traditional

| **Dimension** | **Taylorist (Traditional)** | **Lean (Toyota-style)** | **Agile (Modern)** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Decision Authority** | Top-down | Partially delegated | Fully empowered teams |
| **Skill Base** | Narrow specialist | Multi-skilled (3–5) | T-shaped / multi-disciplinary |
| **Change Response** | Slow (months) | Fast (weeks) | Real-time (hours–days) |
| **Leadership Style** | Command-and-control | Mentor-facilitator | Servant leader / coach |
| **Improvement Cadence** | Project-based | Daily Kaizen | Continuous + experimental |
| **Performance Metric** | Individual output | Team OEE | Team + learning velocity |
| **Communication** | Formal, written | Visual + verbal | Visual + digital + rapid huddles |
| **Failure Handling** | Blame culture | Mistake-proofing (Poka-Yoke) | Fail-fast-learn-fast |
