## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**"Define the term 'Self-Directed Work Team' (SDWT) and list any four of its key characteristics in the context of an agile manufacturing organization."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** A *Self-Directed Work Team (SDWT)* is a small group of employees (typically 5–12 members) in an agile organization who are collectively responsible for planning, executing, controlling, and continuously improving their own work processes with minimal direct supervision.

> **Four Key Characteristics (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**
> 1. **Shared Goals & Metrics:** The team collectively tracks KPIs such as OEE, First-Time-Right, and cycle time.
> 2. **Rotational Leadership:** Leadership responsibilities rotate among team members based on the situation.
> 3. **Internal Customer–Supplier Linkage:** Each member treats the next station as an internal customer.
> 4. **Joint Problem-Solving:** The team conducts daily Kaizen and PDCA cycles without awaiting managerial approval.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**"What is 'Servant Leadership' and how does it differ from traditional command-and-control leadership in lean-agile organizations?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Servant Leadership Definition (1.5 Marks):** Coined by Robert K. Greenleaf (1970), servant leadership is a philosophy where the leader's primary role is to *serve* the team — removing impediments, providing resources, and nurturing the growth and well-being of people, rather than exercising authority over them.

> **Three Key Differences from Traditional Leadership (1.5 Marks — 0.5 each):**
> 1. **Authority Direction:** Traditional leaders command downward; servant leaders support upward.
> 2. **Decision Making:** Traditional = centralized; Servant = decentralized and team-driven.
> 3. **Success Metric:** Traditional = output volume; Servant = people development + sustainable results.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]*

**(a)** Explain in detail the **five pillars of managing people in an agile organization**. Discuss how each pillar supports workforce flexibility, empowerment, and continuous improvement. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A flexible manufacturing cell employs **8 operators**. The recorded skills per operator are: **5, 6, 4, 7, 5, 6, 4, 5**. The cell has **6 workstations**, and the daily shift has **480 minutes**. The cell produced **240 good units** in the shift with **2 minor defects** out of total production.
**Compute the following and comment on each result:**
- (i) Multi-Skill Ratio (MSR)
- (ii) Skill Coverage Index (SCI) — *given that all 6 stations are covered by at least 2 operators*
- (iii) First-Time-Right (FTR) percentage
- (iv) Workforce Productivity (WP) in units/hour, assuming 1 operator per station. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

**Part (a) — Five Pillars of Managing People in Agile Organizations (7 Marks):**

**Introduction (1 Mark):** Agile organizations recognize that *people, not machines, are the primary source of competitive advantage*. Managing them requires a holistic, people-centric model built on five interdependent pillars.

**Pillar 1 — Empowerment & Decentralized Decision-Making (1.5 Marks):**
In agile organizations, decision authority is pushed to the Gemba (shop floor). Frontline workers, who possess the deepest contextual knowledge, are authorized to stop the line (Andon), modify processes, and resolve abnormalities in real time. This eliminates the *Muda* of waiting for managerial approval. *Example:* In Toyota's Andon system, any operator can halt production upon detecting a defect.

**Pillar 2 — Self-Directed Work Teams (SDWTs) (1.5 Marks):**
SDWTs are autonomous groups of 5–12 multi-skilled employees who jointly own planning, scheduling, quality control, and improvement. They evolve through Tuckman's stages (Forming → Storming → Norming → Performing) and use team boards to track performance. SDWTs foster *ownership* and *psychological safety*.

**Pillar 3 — Multi-Skill Development (1 Mark):**
Through TWI (Training Within Industry), Job Rotation, and OJT, operators are trained on multiple machines/processes. The skill matrix (Yamazumi) is the visual tracking tool. Multi-skilling eliminates *Mura* (unevenness) and *over-reliance* on individual workers.

**Pillar 4 — Servant Leadership (1 Mark):**
Agile leaders serve the team by removing obstacles, providing resources, and shielding the team from organizational noise. They practice active listening, empathy, and stewardship — directly enabling the team's autonomy.

**Pillar 5 — Continuous Learning Culture (1 Mark):**
Learning is integrated into daily work via PDCA cycles, A3 problem-solving, Genba walks, and Yokoten (horizontal deployment of best practices). The mindset is *fail-fast-learn-fast* — failures are treated as learning opportunities, not blame events.

**Conclusion (0.5 Mark):** Together, these five pillars create a **resilient, adaptive, and continuously improving** agile workforce.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computations (7 Marks):**

**(i) Multi-Skill Ratio (MSR) (2 Marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Skills} &= 5 + 6 + 4 + 7 + 5 + 6 + 4 + 5 \\
&= 42 \text{ skills}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
MSR &= \frac{42}{8} \\
&= 5.25 \text{ skills per operator}
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment (0.5 Mark):** MSR = **5.25** exceeds the world-class benchmark of **4**, indicating **excellent workforce flexibility**.

---

**(ii) Skill Coverage Index (SCI) (1.5 Marks):**

Since all 6 workstations are covered by at least 2 trained operators:

$$\begin{aligned}
SCI &= \frac{6}{6} \times 100 \\
&= 100\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment (0.5 Mark):** SCI = **100%** with redundancy of 2 operators per station provides **strong resilience** against absenteeism.

---

**(iii) First-Time-Right (FTR) (1.5 Marks):**

Total production = 240 good + 2 defects = 242 units.

$$\begin{aligned}
FTR &= \frac{240}{242} \times 100 \\
&= 99.17\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment (0.5 Mark):** FTR = **99.17%** far exceeds the world-class target of 95%, indicating **excellent operator skill and process discipline**.

---

**(iv) Workforce Productivity (WP) (1 Mark):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Labor Hours} &= \frac{8 \text{ operators} \times 480 \text{ minutes}}{60} \\
&= 64 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
WP &= \frac{240 \text{ good units}}{64 \text{ hours}} \\
&= 3.75 \text{ units/hour}
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment (0.5 Mark):** The team produces **3.75 good units per labor hour**, a strong productivity metric for a flexible cell.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]*

**(a)** Discuss the role of **Human Resource Management (HRM)** in transitioning a traditional manufacturing organization to an agile one. Cover recruitment, training, performance appraisal, and compensation strategies. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An agile plant has **300 employees**. The annual HR audit reports the following:
- **Total training hours delivered** = 9,000 hours
- **Kaizen suggestions submitted** = 1,200
- **Kaizen suggestions implemented** = 900
- **Engaged employees (survey)** = 240
- **Unplanned absent days in the year** = 1,500
- **Total available workdays per employee** = 240

**Compute:**
- (i) Training Hours per Employee per Year
- (ii) Suggestion Implementation Rate (SIR)
- (iii) Employee Engagement Index (EEI)
- (iv) Absenteeism Rate (AR)

**Comment on each result against standard KTU / world-class benchmarks.** **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

**Part (a) — Role of HRM in Agile Transition (7 Marks):**

**Introduction (1 Mark):** Transforming a traditional organization into an agile one is fundamentally a *people transformation*. HRM acts as the **architect of this cultural shift**, redesigning every people-process to align with agility.

**1. Recruitment Strategy (1.5 Marks):**
Traditional HR hires for *narrow, job-specific* skills. Agile HR recruits for:
- **Learning Agility** — willingness and ability to acquire new skills quickly.
- **Adaptability** — comfort with ambiguity and change.
- **Collaborative Mindset** — ability to work in cross-functional teams.
Selection tools shift from rigid interviews to *behavioral simulations* and *agility assessments*.

**2. Training & Development (2 Marks):**
- Move from *annual classroom sessions* to *continuous, on-the-job, just-in-time* learning.
- Deploy **TWI (Training Within Industry)** — Job Instruction, Job Methods, Job Relations.
- Establish **Skill Matrix (Yamazumi)** for visible, dynamic tracking.
- Implement **Job Rotation** and **Job Enrichment** to build T-shaped employees (depth + breadth).

**3. Performance Appraisal (1.5 Marks):**
- Replace annual top-down ratings with **continuous 360° feedback**.
- Introduce **team-based metrics** (OEE, FTR, Kaizen velocity) alongside individual learning metrics.
- Emphasize *learning from failure* — failures are documented in *Lessons Learned* databases, not penalized.

**4. Compensation Strategy (1 Mark):**
- Shift from *job-based fixed pay* to **skill-based pay** (more skills → higher pay).
- Introduce **team incentives** (gain-sharing) and **Kaizen bonuses**.
- Reward *knowledge sharing* and *Yokoten* (horizontal deployment of improvements).

**Conclusion (0.5 Mark):** HRM is the *strategic enabler* of the lean-agile transformation — without HR redesign, technical lean tools remain unused.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computations (7 Marks):**

**(i) Training Hours per Employee per Year (1.5 Marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{THY} &= \frac{9{,}000}{300} \\
&= 30 \text{ hours per employee per year}
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment (0.5 Mark):** The world-class benchmark is **40–80 hours/year**. The plant at **30 hours** is **below benchmark** — HR must scale up training programs.

---

**(ii) Suggestion Implementation Rate (SIR) (1.5 Marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
SIR &= \frac{900}{1{,}200} \times 100 \\
&= 75\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment (0.5 Mark):** SIR = **75%** exceeds the world-class target of **70%**, indicating a **mature Kaizen culture** where employee ideas are acted upon.

---

**(iii) Employee Engagement Index (EEI) (1.5 Marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
EEI &= \frac{240}{300} \times 100 \\
&= 80\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment (0.5 Mark):** EEI = **80%** exceeds the world-class benchmark of **75%** — a strong indicator of a **healthy agile culture**.

---

**(iv) Absenteeism Rate (AR) (1.5 Marks):**

Total available workdays = $300 \times 240 = 72{,}000$ days.

$$\begin{aligned}
AR &= \frac{1{,}500}{72{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 2.083\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment (0.5 Mark):** AR = **2.08%** is **marginally above** the world-class target of **≤ 2%** — the plant is **on the edge** of best-in-class and should investigate root causes (e.g., ergonomics, shift design, morale).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
> 
> 1. **Conflating "Lean" with "Agile":** Lean = eliminating waste; Agile = adapting rapidly to change. They overlap but are NOT synonymous. (Loss: 1–2 marks)
> 
> 2. **Confusing Multi-Skill Ratio (MSR) with Skill Coverage Index (SCI):** MSR measures *skills per operator*; SCI measures *stations covered by trained operators*. Students often interchange them. (Loss: 1–2 marks)
> 
> 3. **Forgetting the Formula Units:** Always mention the unit (%, skills/person, units/hour) — bare numbers lose 0.5 marks.
> 
> 4. **Skipping Comments:** KTU explicitly requires *interpretation against benchmark* after every numerical. A correct number with no comment = **half credit only**.
> 
> 5. **Writing "Empowered" Without Explaining the Mechanism:** Empowerment must be tied to *decision authority, Andon, and visual management* — not just stated as a slogan.
> 
> 6. **Ignoring Servant Leadership:** It is a **favourite KTU question**. Students who only write about Kaizen and miss Greenleaf's framework lose 2–3 marks.
> 
> 7. **Using Taylor / Ford as Positive Examples:** They are *anti-agile* in this context. Use Toyota, Toyota Production System, or Bosch as positive references.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Summary — KTU Module 4: Managing People in Agile Organization**
> 
> ✅ Agile Organization = adaptability, empowerment, learning velocity, customer focus.
> 
> ✅ 5 Pillars = Empowerment, SDWTs, Multi-Skilling, Servant Leadership, Continuous Learning.
> 
> ✅ SDWT = 5–12 members, self-managing, joint accountability, Tuckman model.
> 
> ✅ Servant Leadership = Greenleaf (1970) — leader serves the team, removes impediments.
> 
> ✅ Multi-Skill Ratio target = **≥ 4 skills/operator**; formula = total skills ÷ total workforce.
> 
> ✅ Skill Coverage Index target = **100%**; check single-point-of-failure stations.
> 
> ✅ Employee Engagement Index target = **≥ 75%**.
> 
> ✅ Absenteeism Rate target = **≤ 2%**.
> 
> ✅ First-Time-Right target = **≥ 95%**.
> 
> ✅ Suggestion Implementation Rate target = **≥ 70%**.
> 
> ✅ Training Hours/Employee/Year target = **40–80 hours**.
> 
> ✅ Skill Matrix (Yamazumi Chart) = visual tool for tracking operator-to-station coverage.
> 
> ✅ TWI = Training Within Industry (Job Instruction, Job Methods, Job Relations).
> 
> ✅ Yokoten = horizontal deployment of best practices across teams/plants.
> 
> ✅ Andon = visual/audible signal allowing any operator to halt the line.
> 
> ✅ Obeya Room = "war room" for cross-functional visual management.
> 
> ✅ T-Shaped Employee = deep expertise in one area + broad knowledge across many.
> 
> ✅ Performance Appraisal shift = annual top-down → continuous 360° + team-based.
> 
> ✅ Compensation shift = job-based → skill-based + Kaizen bonuses + gain-sharing.
> 
> ✅ Recruitment shift = narrow skills → learning agility + adaptability + collaboration.
> 
> ✅ Key Risk = 100% SCI with single-coverage stations = *single point of failure*.
> 
> ✅ Lean ≠ Agile, but **Lean-Agile** integrates both philosophies for modern manufacturing.
> 
> ✅ Real-world references: Toyota, Bosch, Boeing, Foxconn, Samsung — use these in answers for extra marks.
